name: Idli
description: Soft steamed rice and lentil cakes, a classic South Indian breakfast served with chutney and sambar.
instructions: Soak rice and urad dal separately for 6 hours.
Grind to a smooth batter, mix, add salt.
Ferment overnight.
Pour into idli moulds, steam for 10-12 minutes.
tips: Fermentation is key — keep in a warm spot.

name: Rava Idli
description: Soft semolina idli made without fermentation, quick and fluffy.
instructions: Roast rava lightly, cool. Mix with curd, water to a thick batter.
Temper mustard seeds, curry leaves, cashews in ghee, add to batter.
Add ENO, mix gently, pour into moulds, steam 10 min.
tips: Add ENO just before steaming for fluffy texture.

name: Oats Idli
description: Healthy idli made with oats instead of rice batter, steamed and light.
instructions: Dry roast oats and rava. Mix with curd and water.
Add grated carrots and salt.
Add ENO, pour into moulds, steam 10 min.
tips: Use instant oats for softer texture.

name: Ragi Idli
description: Nutritious finger millet idli, high in calcium and iron.
instructions: Soak urad dal 4 hours, grind smooth.
Mix with ragi flour and salt.
Ferment 8 hours. Pour into moulds, steam 12 min.
tips: Ragi idli is denser than regular — slightly thinner batter helps.

name: Masala Dosa
description: Crispy rice and lentil crepe filled with spiced potato masala.
instructions: Boil and mash potatoes. Temper mustard seeds, add onions, turmeric, mashed potatoes.
Spread dosa batter thin on hot tawa, drizzle oil.
Place potato filling, fold and serve with chutney and sambar.
tips: Use a well-seasoned cast iron tawa for crispiest dosa.

name: Plain Dosa
description: Thin crispy rice and lentil crepe, a staple South Indian breakfast.
instructions: Heat tawa, spread batter in thin circle.
Drizzle oil, cook until golden and crispy.
Fold and serve with chutney and sambar.
tips: Batter should be slightly thin for crispy dosa.

name: Rava Dosa
description: Crispy semolina crepe with cumin and pepper, no fermentation needed.
instructions: Mix all dry ingredients, add water to thin batter.
Pour on hot tawa in lacy pattern.
Cook until crisp, fold and serve.
tips: Batter should be very thin and pourable.

name: Moong Dal Dosa
description: Protein-rich crepe made from ground moong dal, no fermentation.
instructions: Soak moong dal and rice 4 hours. Grind to smooth batter.
Add ginger, cumin, chili. Spread on tawa, cook both sides.
tips: Soak longer for easier grinding.

name: Brown Rice Dosa
description: Healthy dosa made with brown rice for added fiber.
instructions: Soak brown rice and urad dal 6 hours.
Grind, ferment overnight.
Spread on tawa and cook until golden.
tips: Brown rice takes longer to grind — soak extra hour.

name: Wheat Dosa
description: Instant dosa made with whole wheat flour, no fermentation.
instructions: Mix flour with water, add chopped onion, cumin, chili.
Spread on tawa like dosa, cook both sides.
tips: Keep batter thin for crispy result.

name: Oats Dosa
description: Healthy instant dosa made with oats, onions, and spices.
instructions: Blend oats coarsely. Mix with rice flour, water, spices.
Spread on tawa, cook until crispy.
tips: Don't make batter too thick.

name: Mixed Dal Dosa
description: Protein-packed dosa from mixed lentils, no rice needed.
instructions: Soak all dals 4 hours. Grind to smooth batter.
Spread on tawa and cook until golden on both sides.
tips: Mix in a little rice for crispier texture.

name: Pesarattu
description: Andhra-style green gram dosa, high in protein.
instructions: Soak moong 4 hours. Grind with ginger, chilies to thick batter.
Add cumin. Spread on tawa, top with onion, cook until crisp.
tips: Serve with ginger chutney for authentic flavor.

name: Neer Dosa
description: Delicate lacy rice crepes from Karnataka, paper-thin.
instructions: Soak rice 4 hours. Grind with coconut to thin batter.
Pour on hot tawa, swirl for lacy pattern. Cover and steam 1 min.
tips: Do not flip — cook only one side.

name: Set Dosa
description: Soft spongy dosa that's thick and fluffy, served in sets of 2-3.
instructions: Add baking soda to well-fermented batter.
Pour thick rounds on tawa, don't spread too thin.
Cover and cook on low heat until set on top. Do not flip.
tips: Use extra-fermented batter for best results.

name: Millet Dosa
description: Nutritious dosa made with pearl millet (bajra), gluten-free.
instructions: Mix flours with water, add cumin, chopped onion.
Spread on hot tawa and cook both sides until golden.
tips: Millet dosa is best served hot.

name: Ragi Dosa
description: Healthy finger millet crepe rich in calcium and iron.
instructions: Mix flours with water, spices, diced onion.
Spread on tawa and cook until crispy.
tips: Add grated carrots for extra nutrition.

name: Quinoa Dosa
description: Modern healthy dosa made with quinoa and urad dal.
instructions: Soak quinoa and urad dal 4 hours.
Grind to smooth batter, ferment 6 hours.
Spread on tawa and cook until golden.
tips: Quinoa gives a nuttier flavor than rice dosa.

name: Adai Dosa
description: Thick lentil and rice crepe with mixed dals and spices.
instructions: Soak rice and all dals 2 hours.
Grind coarsely with chilies and curry leaves.
Spread thick on tawa, cook both sides with oil.
tips: Coarse grinding is key — don't make it smooth like regular dosa.

name: Uttapam
description: Thick savory rice and lentil pancake topped with vegetables.
instructions: Pour thick batter on tawa.
Top with diced onion, tomato, chili, coriander.
Cook on medium heat until bottom is golden. Flip and cook other side.
tips: Use thick, well-fermented batter for best uttapam.

name: Rava Uttapam
description: Quick semolina pancake with vegetables, no fermentation needed.
instructions: Mix rava with curd and water to thick batter. Rest 10 min.
Pour on tawa, top with veggies.
Cook both sides until golden.
tips: Let batter rest to absorb moisture.

name: Medu Vada
description: Crispy deep-fried urad dal doughnuts, a South Indian classic.
instructions: Soak urad dal 4 hours. Grind to fluffy batter (don't add much water).
Add ginger, chilies, curry leaves.
Shape into donuts, deep fry until golden brown.
tips: Batter should be thick and fluffy — the key to crispy vada.

name: Paniyaram
description: Round savory dumplings made from dosa batter in a special pan.
instructions: Temper mustard seeds and curry leaves, add to batter with diced onion.
Heat paniyaram pan, grease wells with oil.
Pour batter, cook turning until golden all around.
tips: Use well-fermented batter for fluffy paniyaram.

name: Appam
description: Lacy bowl-shaped rice crepe with soft fluffy center, from Kerala.
instructions: Soak and grind rice. Add coconut milk, yeast, sugar. Ferment 6 hours.
Pour in appam pan, swirl for thin edges.
Cover and cook until center is fluffy and edges are lacy.
tips: The pan must be very hot for lacy edges.

name: Podi Idli
description: Leftover idli tossed with spicy podi (gun powder) and ghee.
instructions: Cut idlis into quarters.
Heat ghee and oil, toss idli pieces.
Add podi powder, toss well until coated.
tips: Use day-old idlis for best texture.

name: Punugulu
description: Quick deep-fried dosa batter fritters, crispy outside and soft inside.
instructions: Add diced onion, chili, curry leaves to thick batter.
Drop spoonfuls into hot oil.
Fry until golden brown and crispy.
tips: Batter should be thick for round shape.

name: Akki Roti
description: Karnataka-style rice flour flatbread with vegetables.
instructions: Mix rice flour with finely chopped vegetables, salt, and water.
Pat into thin rounds on banana leaf or plastic.
Cook on tawa with oil until both sides are golden.
tips: Add sesame seeds for extra flavor.

name: Ven Pongal
description: Comforting South Indian rice-lentil porridge tempered with pepper and cumin.
instructions: Dry roast moong dal. Cook rice and dal together until mushy.
Temper pepper, cumin, curry leaves, cashews in ghee.
Pour tempering over pongal, mix well.
tips: Cook until creamy consistency — add more water if needed.

name: Idli Upma
description: Leftover idli crumbled and tempered with spices — zero waste breakfast.
instructions: Crumble idlis by hand.
Temper mustard seeds, curry leaves, add onion and chili.
Add crumbled idli, toss well. Squeeze lemon juice.
tips: Great way to use day-old idlis.

name: Coconut Chutney
description: Fresh coconut chutney with tempering, essential South Indian accompaniment.
instructions: Grind coconut, chana dal, chili, ginger with water.
Temper mustard seeds and curry leaves in oil.
Pour tempering over chutney, mix.
tips: Add a small piece of raw mango for tang.

name: Rava Kesari
description: Sweet semolina halwa with ghee, cashews, and saffron.
instructions: Roast rava in ghee until golden.
Add hot water carefully, stir to avoid lumps.
Add sugar, saffron, cardamom. Cook until thick. Garnish with cashews.
tips: Use generous ghee for authentic taste.

name: Aloo Paratha
description: Stuffed potato flatbread cooked with ghee, a Punjabi breakfast staple.
instructions: Knead dough. Boil, mash potatoes with spices.
Stuff dough balls with potato mixture, roll out.
Cook on tawa with ghee until golden on both sides.
tips: Roll evenly to prevent filling from bursting out.

name: Gobi Paratha
description: Spiced cauliflower stuffed whole wheat flatbread.
instructions: Grate cauliflower, squeeze out water. Mix with spices.
Stuff dough, roll out.
Cook on tawa with ghee until golden.
tips: Squeeze cauliflower well to prevent soggy parathas.

name: Mooli Paratha
description: Spiced grated radish stuffed flatbread.
instructions: Grate radish, squeeze water. Mix with spices.
Stuff dough, roll, cook with ghee.
tips: Salt the radish and drain to remove excess moisture.

name: Palak Paratha
description: Spinach-infused green flatbread, nutritious and flavorful.
instructions: Blanch spinach, puree. Knead into dough with spices.
Roll out and cook on tawa with ghee.
tips: Don't add too much spinach puree or dough gets sticky.

name: Methi Paratha
description: Fenugreek leaf flatbread, aromatic and nutritious.
instructions: Chop methi finely. Knead into dough with spices.
Roll and cook on tawa with ghee.
tips: Add a pinch of sugar to reduce bitterness of methi.

name: Pumpkin Paratha
description: Sweet and savory stuffed pumpkin flatbread.
instructions: Grate pumpkin, cook until dry. Season with spices.
Stuff dough with pumpkin mixture, roll and cook with ghee.
tips: Cook filling until completely dry.

name: Mix Veg Paratha
description: Flatbread stuffed with mixed vegetables and spices.
instructions: Finely chop and sauté vegetables with spices.
Stuff dough, roll, cook on tawa with ghee.
tips: Chop veggies very fine for easy stuffing.

name: Plain Paratha
description: Layered whole wheat flatbread cooked with ghee.
instructions: Knead soft dough. Roll, apply ghee, fold into layers.
Roll again, cook on tawa with ghee until golden layers form.
tips: Fold and roll multiple times for flaky layers.

name: Thepla
description: Gujarati spiced fenugreek flatbread, great for travel.
instructions: Mix flour with chopped methi, curd, turmeric, salt. Knead soft dough.
Roll thin, cook on tawa with oil until light brown spots appear.
tips: Stays fresh for days — ideal travel food.

name: Poori
description: Deep-fried puffed whole wheat bread, festive breakfast.
instructions: Knead firm dough with flour, salt, a little oil.
Roll into small circles.
Deep fry in hot oil until they puff up and turn golden.
tips: Oil must be very hot for pooris to puff.

name: Bhatura
description: Deep-fried leavened bread, served with chole.
instructions: Mix flour, curd, baking soda, sugar, salt. Knead soft dough, rest 2 hours.
Roll into oval shapes.
Deep fry until puffed and golden.
tips: Resting the dough is essential for soft bhatura.

name: Chole Bhature
description: Spiced chickpea curry with deep-fried bread, a Delhi classic.
instructions: Soak and pressure cook chickpeas. Make chole gravy with onion-tomato-spice base.
Knead bhatura dough, rest, roll and deep fry.
Serve hot bhatura with chole.
tips: Soak chickpeas overnight and add tea bags while boiling for dark color.

name: Poha
description: Spiced flattened rice with mustard seeds, onions, and peanuts.
instructions: Rinse poha, drain, let soften 5 min.
Temper mustard seeds, fry peanuts, add curry leaves and onion.
Add poha, turmeric, salt. Toss gently, squeeze lemon.
tips: Don't over-soak or it becomes mushy.

name: Upma
description: Savory semolina porridge tempered with spices and vegetables.
instructions: Dry roast rava until fragrant.
Temper mustard seeds, curry leaves, add onion and chili.
Add water, salt, bring to boil. Add rava, stir continuously until thick.
tips: Stir continuously to prevent lumps.

name: Bread Upma
description: Quick breakfast made with bread cubes tempered with spices.
instructions: Cut bread into cubes.
Temper mustard seeds, curry leaves. Add onion, chili.
Add bread cubes, toss with turmeric and salt.
tips: Use day-old bread for best results.

name: Semiya Upma
description: Vermicelli upma, a quick and light South Indian breakfast.
instructions: Roast vermicelli in ghee until golden.
Temper mustard seeds, peanuts, curry leaves. Add onion.
Add water, salt. Add roasted vermicelli, cover and cook 5 min.
tips: Roast vermicelli well to prevent mushy texture.

name: Oats Upma
description: Healthy upma made with oats instead of semolina.
instructions: Dry roast oats 2 min.
Temper mustard seeds, curry leaves, add vegetables.
Add water, oats, cook 3-4 min until thick.
tips: Don't over-cook or oats become gummy.

name: Tomato Upma
description: Tangy tomato-flavored semolina upma.
instructions: Roast rava. Temper spices, add onion and tomato.
Add water, salt. Add rava, stir until thick.
tips: Use ripe red tomatoes for best flavor.

name: Aval Upma
description: Upma made with flattened rice (poha) and spices.
instructions: Rinse thick poha, drain.
Temper spices, add onion and peanuts.
Add poha, turmeric, salt. Toss and cook 3 min.
tips: Use thick poha variety for this.

name: Besan Chilla
description: Savory gram flour pancake with onions and spices.
instructions: Mix besan with water, spices, onion to smooth batter.
Pour on hot tawa, spread thin.
Cook both sides with a little oil until golden.
tips: Add finely grated vegetables for extra nutrition.

name: Moong Dal Chilla
description: Protein-rich savory pancake from ground moong lentils.
instructions: Soak moong dal 4 hours. Grind to smooth batter.
Add ginger, chili, cumin.
Spread on tawa, cook both sides until golden.
tips: Soak overnight for easiest grinding.

name: Masoor Dal Chilla
description: Savory red lentil pancakes, quick and protein-packed.
instructions: Soak masoor dal 2 hours. Grind to batter.
Mix in diced onion, cumin, coriander.
Spread on tawa and cook both sides.
tips: Masoor grinds faster than other dals.

name: Oats Chilla
description: Healthy savory pancake made with oats and besan.
instructions: Blend oats. Mix with besan, water, veggies, spices.
Spread on tawa, cook both sides.
tips: Great for kids — hide vegetables in the batter.

name: Egg Bhurji
description: Indian scrambled eggs with onions, tomatoes, and spices.
instructions: Beat eggs. Sauté onion, tomato, chili with turmeric.
Add beaten eggs, scramble until cooked.
Garnish with coriander.
tips: Don't overcook — keep eggs slightly soft.

name: Sabudana Khichdi
description: Fluffy sago pearls with peanuts and potato, a fasting breakfast.
instructions: Soak sabudana 4-6 hours. Drain well.
Roast and crush peanuts. Cube and fry potato.
Temper cumin, add sabudana, peanuts, potato. Toss gently.
tips: Sabudana must be soaked properly — each pearl should be separate.

name: Dalia
description: Broken wheat porridge cooked with vegetables and mild spices.
instructions: Dry roast dalia until nutty.
Temper cumin, sauté onion, tomato, peas.
Add water, dalia, cook until soft and fluffy.
tips: Roasting dalia enhances flavor and keeps grains separate.

name: Savory Masala French Toast
description: Indian-spiced French toast with chili and coriander.
instructions: Beat eggs with finely chopped chili, onion, coriander, salt.
Dip bread slices in egg mixture.
Pan fry on both sides until golden.
tips: Use thick bread for better absorption.

name: Bread Pakora
description: Bread slices dipped in spiced gram flour batter and deep fried.
instructions: Make potato stuffing with spices. Sandwich between bread slices.
Make besan batter. Dip sandwiches in batter.
Deep fry until golden and crispy.
tips: Squeeze bread slightly before dipping so it doesn't absorb too much oil.

name: Bombay Sandwich
description: Layered vegetable sandwich with chutneys, a Mumbai street classic.
instructions: Spread chutney on bread. Layer sliced veggies and potato.
Press together, toast on tawa with butter.
Cut diagonally and serve.
tips: Use thick-sliced bread for sturdier sandwich.

name: Kolkata Egg Roll
description: Egg-wrapped paratha roll with onions and chutney, street food style.
instructions: Cook paratha on tawa. Pour beaten egg on top, flip to set.
Add sliced onion, chutney, lemon juice.
Roll up tightly and serve.
tips: Use a hot tawa so egg sets quickly.

name: Handvo
description: Gujarati savory rice and lentil cake with vegetables.
instructions: Soak rice and dals, grind to batter, ferment overnight.
Add grated bottle gourd, spices, baking soda.
Pour in greased pan, top with sesame seeds. Bake or cook on stovetop.
tips: Can be baked in oven at 375°F for 30 min.

name: Dhokla
description: Steamed fermented gram flour cake, light and spongy Gujarati snack.
instructions: Mix besan, curd, turmeric, water to smooth batter.
Add ENO, mix gently, pour into greased plate, steam 12 min.
Temper mustard seeds and curry leaves, pour over dhokla.
tips: Add ENO just before steaming.

name: Masala Chai
description: Indian spiced milk tea with ginger and cardamom.
instructions: Boil water with crushed ginger and cardamom.
Add tea leaves, simmer 2 min.
Add milk and sugar, bring to boil twice, strain and serve.
tips: Crush cardamom and ginger for maximum flavor.

name: Overnight Oats
description: No-cook oats soaked in milk overnight with fruits and nuts.
instructions: Mix oats, milk, yogurt, chia seeds, honey in a jar.
Top with berries.
Refrigerate overnight, eat cold in the morning.
tips: Prepare 2-3 jars at once for easy weekday breakfasts.

name: Baked Oatmeal
description: Warm oven-baked oatmeal with banana and cinnamon, feeds a crowd.
instructions: Mash bananas. Mix all ingredients.
Pour into greased baking dish.
Bake at 375°F for 30 min until set and golden.
tips: Make ahead — reheat portions through the week.

name: Classic Oatmeal
description: Simple stovetop oatmeal with honey and banana.
instructions: Bring liquid to boil, add oats.
Cook 5 min stirring occasionally.
Top with sliced banana, honey, cinnamon.
tips: Use steel-cut oats for chewier texture (cook longer).

name: Savory Oatmeal
description: Oatmeal topped with a fried egg, cheese, and herbs — savory twist.
instructions: Cook oats in water with salt.
Fry an egg separately.
Top oatmeal with egg, cheese, pepper, herbs.
tips: Add sautéed spinach for extra nutrition.

name: Homemade Granola
description: Crunchy oven-baked oat clusters with nuts and honey.
instructions: Mix oats, nuts, melted coconut oil, honey, vanilla, cinnamon.
Spread on baking sheet, press flat.
Bake at 325°F for 25 min, stir once. Cool completely for clusters.
tips: Don't stir after baking for big crunchy clusters.

name: Cinnamon Quinoa Breakfast Bowl
description: Warm quinoa cooked like oatmeal with cinnamon and fruit.
instructions: Cook quinoa in milk with cinnamon.
Sweeten with maple syrup.
Top with berries and sliced almonds.
tips: Rinse quinoa well to remove bitterness.

name: Chia Seed Pudding
description: Creamy chia pudding soaked in milk overnight with vanilla.
instructions: Mix chia seeds, milk, vanilla, honey.
Stir well, refrigerate 4 hours or overnight.
Top with fresh fruit before serving.
tips: Stir after 10 min to prevent clumping.

name: Mango Chia Pudding
description: Tropical chia pudding layered with fresh mango puree.
instructions: Mix chia seeds with coconut milk and honey.
Refrigerate 4 hours.
Blend mango, layer with chia pudding.
tips: Use ripe Alphonso mango for best sweetness.

name: Classic Omelette
description: Fluffy folded omelette with cheese and herbs.
instructions: Beat eggs with salt and pepper.
Melt butter in pan, pour eggs.
When nearly set, add cheese and herbs, fold in half.
tips: Use medium-low heat for fluffy omelette.

name: Scrambled Eggs
description: Soft creamy scrambled eggs, a breakfast essential.
instructions: Beat eggs with milk, salt, pepper.
Melt butter on low heat, add eggs.
Stir gently with spatula, remove when just set.
tips: Low heat and gentle stirring = creamiest scramble.

name: Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in spiced tomato-pepper sauce, a Middle Eastern classic.
instructions: Sauté onion and pepper in olive oil. Add spices.
Add crushed tomatoes, simmer 10 min.
Make wells, crack eggs in, cover and cook until set.
tips: Serve with crusty bread for dipping.

name: Frittata
description: Italian open-faced omelette baked with vegetables and cheese.
instructions: Sauté vegetables in oven-safe pan.
Pour beaten eggs over, top with cheese.
Cook on stove 3 min, then broil 5 min until puffed and golden.
tips: Great for using up leftover vegetables.

name: Egg Muffins
description: Mini egg cups baked with vegetables, perfect for meal prep.
instructions: Beat eggs, mix in chopped vegetables and cheese.
Pour into greased muffin tin.
Bake at 375°F for 18-20 min.
tips: Freeze for up to 1 month — reheat in microwave.

name: Crustless Quiche
description: Light egg and vegetable quiche without the pastry crust.
instructions: Sauté vegetables. Beat eggs with milk.
Layer veggies and cheese in baking dish. Pour egg mixture.
Bake at 375°F for 25-30 min until set.
tips: Let rest 5 min before cutting.

name: Egg Toast
description: Simple fried or scrambled egg on buttered toast.
instructions: Toast bread. Fry or scramble eggs with butter.
Place eggs on toast, season.
tips: Runny yolk on toast is chef's kiss.

name: Strawberry Banana Smoothie
description: Classic fruit smoothie, naturally sweet and creamy.
instructions: Add all ingredients to blender.
Blend until smooth.
Pour and serve immediately.
tips: Use frozen fruit for thicker smoothie.

name: Avocado Smoothie
description: Creamy avocado smoothie with banana and spinach.
instructions: Blend all ingredients until creamy.
Adjust thickness with more milk if needed.
tips: Avocado makes it incredibly creamy.

name: Peanut Butter Banana Smoothie
description: Protein-rich smoothie with peanut butter and banana.
instructions: Blend all ingredients until smooth.
tips: Add a scoop of protein powder for post-workout breakfast.

name: Green Smoothie
description: Nutrient-packed smoothie with spinach, banana, and fruits.
instructions: Blend spinach and water first until smooth.
Add remaining fruits and ginger, blend again.
tips: Spinach flavor is undetectable once blended.

name: Mango Smoothie
description: Tropical mango smoothie with yogurt, perfect summer breakfast.
instructions: Blend mango, yogurt, milk, honey until smooth.
tips: Use frozen mango for thicker texture.

name: Blueberry Smoothie
description: Antioxidant-rich blueberry smoothie with banana.
instructions: Blend all ingredients until smooth.
tips: Frozen blueberries work just as well.

name: Coffee Smoothie
description: Energizing breakfast smoothie with coffee and banana.
instructions: Blend all ingredients until smooth and frothy.
tips: Freeze coffee in ice cube trays for thicker smoothie.

name: Classic Pancakes
description: Fluffy American-style pancakes with maple syrup.
instructions: Mix dry ingredients. Whisk wet ingredients separately.
Combine, don't overmix (lumps are fine).
Cook on griddle until bubbles form, flip, cook until golden.
tips: Don't press down on pancakes while cooking.

name: Banana Pancakes
description: Naturally sweet pancakes made with mashed banana.
instructions: Mash bananas. Mix with eggs, oats, cinnamon, baking powder.
Cook small pancakes on medium heat.
Flip when bubbles form.
tips: Riper bananas = sweeter pancakes.

name: Blueberry Pancakes
description: Fluffy pancakes studded with fresh blueberries.
instructions: Mix dry ingredients. Add wet, fold in blueberries.
Cook on griddle, flip when bubbles form.
tips: Don't smash blueberries — fold gently.

name: Oatmeal Pancakes
description: Hearty whole grain pancakes made with oats.
instructions: Blend oats into flour. Mix with remaining ingredients.
Cook on medium heat, flip when set.
tips: Blend oats fine for smooth pancakes, coarse for rustic.

name: French Toast
description: Bread slices soaked in egg-milk mixture, pan-fried golden.
instructions: Beat eggs with milk, cinnamon, vanilla.
Dip bread slices to coat evenly.
Cook in butter until golden on both sides.
tips: Use thick bread like brioche for best results.

name: Homemade Waffles
description: Crispy on the outside, fluffy inside — classic waffles.
instructions: Mix dry ingredients. Whisk wet ingredients.
Combine gently. Pour into preheated waffle iron.
Cook until steam stops and waffle is golden.
tips: Separate eggs, whip whites for extra fluffy waffles.

name: Avocado Toast
description: Smashed avocado on toast with lemon, chili flakes, and salt.
instructions: Toast bread. Mash avocado with lemon, salt, chili.
Spread on toast.
tips: Top with a poached egg for extra protein.

name: Breakfast Sandwich
description: Egg and cheese sandwich on toasted bread.
instructions: Toast bread. Fry egg.
Layer egg, cheese, spinach on toast.
tips: Add avocado or tomato for extra flavor.

name: Healthy Banana Bread
description: Moist banana bread made with whole wheat and less sugar.
instructions: Mash bananas. Mix wet ingredients.
Fold in dry ingredients.
Bake at 350°F for 45 min.
tips: Use very ripe (spotty) bananas for best sweetness.

name: Zucchini Bread
description: Moist quick bread with grated zucchini and warm spices.
instructions: Grate zucchini, squeeze water.
Mix wet and dry ingredients separately, combine.
Bake at 350°F for 50 min.
tips: Don't skip squeezing the zucchini moisture.

name: Blueberry Muffins
description: Classic fluffy muffins bursting with blueberries.
instructions: Mix dry ingredients. Combine wet ingredients.
Fold together, add blueberries.
Fill muffin cups 2/3 full, bake 375°F for 22 min.
tips: Toss blueberries in flour to prevent sinking.

name: Healthy Banana Muffins
description: Naturally sweetened banana muffins with oats.
instructions: Blend oats into flour. Mash bananas.
Mix all ingredients. Spoon into muffin tin.
Bake 350°F for 18-20 min.
tips: These are lower sugar — sweetened by banana and a little honey.

name: Apple Muffins
description: Warm spiced apple muffins with cinnamon.
instructions: Dice apple. Mix dry ingredients.
Combine wet, fold in apples.
Bake 375°F for 22 min.
tips: Granny Smith apples hold up best in baking.

name: Granola Bars
description: Chewy homemade granola bars with oats, honey, and nuts.
instructions: Heat honey and peanut butter until smooth.
Mix with oats, nuts, cranberries.
Press into pan, bake 325°F for 20 min. Cool and cut.
tips: Press firmly for bars that hold together.

name: Energy Balls
description: No-bake protein bites with oats, peanut butter, and chocolate.
instructions: Mix all ingredients.
Roll into balls.
Refrigerate 30 min before serving.
tips: Make a big batch — keeps in fridge for a week.

name: Blueberry Scones
description: Buttery tender scones with blueberries.
instructions: Cut butter into flour. Add sugar, baking powder.
Fold in blueberries, add cream until dough forms.
Shape, cut into wedges, bake 400°F for 15 min.
tips: Cold butter is essential for flaky scones.

name: Sweet Potato Hash
description: Crispy cubed sweet potatoes with peppers and onions.
instructions: Cube sweet potatoes small.
Cook in oil on medium-high until crispy, 12-15 min.
Add peppers and onion, cook 5 more min. Season.
tips: Don't overcrowd the pan for crispiest hash.

name: Breakfast Tacos
description: Soft tortillas filled with scrambled eggs and salsa.
instructions: Scramble eggs. Warm tortillas.
Fill with eggs, beans, cheese, avocado, salsa.
tips: Corn tortillas for gluten-free option.

name: Breakfast Burrito
description: Rolled flour tortilla filled with eggs, beans, and cheese.
instructions: Scramble eggs. Warm tortilla.
Layer beans, eggs, cheese, salsa, sour cream.
Roll up tightly.
tips: Wrap in foil for on-the-go breakfast.

name: Farmers Market Breakfast Bowl
description: Grain bowl with roasted vegetables, greens, and egg.
instructions: Cook grain. Roast cubed sweet potato 20 min.
Sauté kale. Fry egg.
Assemble bowl with all components.
tips: Prep grains and roast veggies ahead for quick assembly.

name: Yogurt Bowl
description: Creamy yogurt topped with granola, fruits, and honey.
instructions: Spoon yogurt into bowl.
Top with granola, sliced fruit, drizzle honey.
tips: Layer for best texture — eat immediately so granola stays crunchy.

name: Fruit Salad
description: Fresh seasonal fruit salad with a squeeze of lime and mint.
instructions: Chop all fruits into bite-sized pieces.
Toss with lime juice.
Garnish with mint leaves.
tips: Use seasonal fruits for best flavor and price.

name: Kale Potato Paratha
description: Nutritious stuffed paratha with kale and potato filling.
instructions: Blanch and chop kale. Mash boiled potatoes, mix with kale and spices.
Stuff dough, roll, cook on tawa with ghee.
tips: Blanching kale removes bitterness.

name: Thalipeeth
description: Maharashtrian multi-grain savory flatbread.
instructions: Mix flour with chopped onion, coriander, cumin, water.
Pat directly on tawa into thick round.
Make a hole in center, add oil. Cook both sides.
tips: Use multi-grain flour mix for authentic taste.

name: Kothu Parotta
description: Shredded flaky parotta tossed with eggs and spices, South Indian street food.
instructions: Shred parotta into small pieces.
Scramble eggs separately. Sauté onion, chili, curry leaves.
Add shredded parotta, eggs, soy sauce. Toss on high heat.
tips: Use leftover parotta for best results.

name: Sheera
description: Sweet semolina dessert with ghee, nuts, and saffron — same as Rava Kesari.
instructions: Roast rava in ghee. Add hot milk carefully.
Cook until thick, add sugar, cardamom.
Garnish with fried cashews.
tips: Ghee and sugar make this rich — best as occasional treat.

name: Appe
description: Round savory dumplings cooked in a special pan, like mini uttapam.
instructions: Mix finely diced veggies into dosa batter.
Heat appe pan, add oil in each cavity.
Pour batter, cook on low heat, turn for even cooking.
tips: Use a well-seasoned appe pan for non-stick results.

name: Ragi Roti
description: Nutritious finger millet flatbread, high in calcium.
instructions: Mix flour with diced onion, cumin, coriander, water.
Pat into rounds on tawa.
Cook both sides until done.
tips: Press directly on tawa — ragi dough doesn't roll well.

name: Dudhi Muthiya
description: Steamed bottle gourd dumplings from Gujarat.
instructions: Grate bottle gourd, mix with flours, spices.
Shape into logs, steam 15 min.
Slice and temper with mustard seeds and sesame.
tips: Squeeze excess water from gourd.

name: Kothimbir Vadi
description: Crispy coriander fritters from Maharashtra.
instructions: Mix chopped coriander with flours, spices, water to thick batter.
Steam 15 min, cool, cut into squares.
Shallow fry until crispy.
tips: Steam first then fry for best texture.

name: Cheese Dosa
description: Crispy dosa topped with grated cheese, a kids' favorite.
instructions: Spread dosa batter thin on hot tawa.
Sprinkle grated cheese on top.
Cook until crispy and cheese melts. Fold and serve.
tips: Use a blend of mozzarella and cheddar.

name: Oats Egg Omelette
description: Quick protein-rich omelette with oats mixed in.
instructions: Beat eggs with oats, chopped onion, chili.
Pour on heated pan, cook until set.
Flip and cook other side.
tips: Oats add fiber without changing taste much.

name: Spinach Omelette
description: Eggs cooked with fresh spinach and onions.
instructions: Beat eggs. Sauté spinach and onion briefly.
Pour eggs over, cook until set, fold.
tips: Wilt spinach first so it doesn't release water into the eggs.

name: Poha
description: Flattened rice tempered with mustard seeds, curry leaves, onions, and peanuts.
instructions: Rinse poha in water, drain, and set aside for 5 minutes until softened.
Heat oil in a pan, add mustard seeds and let them splutter, then add peanuts and fry until golden.
Add curry leaves, green chilies, and chopped onion; sauté until onion is translucent.
Add turmeric powder and salt, mix well, then add the softened poha and toss gently.
Cook on low heat for 2-3 minutes, squeeze lemon juice, and serve garnished with fresh coriander.
tips: Do not over-soak the poha or it will become mushy.
Add a pinch of sugar for the traditional Maharashtrian sweet-savory balance.

name: Upma
description: Savory semolina porridge cooked with vegetables, mustard seeds, and curry leaves.
instructions: Dry roast semolina in a pan on medium heat until light golden and aromatic, then set aside.
In the same pan, heat ghee, add mustard seeds, urad dal, and curry leaves; let them splutter.
Add chopped onion, green chili, carrot, and peas; sauté for 3-4 minutes.
Add water and salt, bring to a boil, then slowly add the roasted semolina while stirring continuously.
Cook on low heat for 3-4 minutes until water is absorbed and upma reaches a fluffy consistency.
tips: Stir continuously while adding semolina to prevent lumps.
Roasting the semolina well is the key to fluffy upma.

name: Idli Sambar
description: Steamed rice and lentil cakes served with a spiced lentil and vegetable stew.
instructions: Pressure cook toor dal with 2 cups water for 3 whistles until soft, then mash and set aside.
Grease idli moulds, pour batter into each mould, and steam for 10-12 minutes.
For sambar, heat oil, add mustard seeds and curry leaves, then add chopped onion and tomato; sauté until soft.
Add drumstick pieces, sambar powder, tamarind paste, cooked dal, and water; simmer for 10 minutes.
Serve hot idlis with the sambar and coconut chutney on the side.
tips: Well-fermented batter gives the softest idlis.
Let the sambar rest for 5 minutes before serving for deeper flavors.

name: Masala Dosa
description: Crispy fermented rice and lentil crepe filled with spiced potato filling.
instructions: Boil potatoes until tender, peel, and roughly mash them.
Heat oil, add mustard seeds, curry leaves, and green chilies; let them splutter.
Add chopped onion, sauté until translucent, then add turmeric and mashed potatoes; mix well and season with salt.
Heat a flat griddle, pour a ladleful of dosa batter, and spread in a thin circle; drizzle oil around the edges.
Once the dosa is golden and crisp, place the potato filling on one half, fold over, and serve with coconut chutney and sambar.
tips: The griddle must be hot enough that water droplets sizzle and evaporate immediately.
Spread batter quickly in one motion for an even, thin dosa.

name: Aloo Paratha
description: Whole wheat flatbread stuffed with spiced mashed potato filling, pan-fried with butter.
instructions: Knead whole wheat flour with water and a pinch of salt into a soft dough; rest for 15 minutes.
Boil and mash potatoes, mix with chopped green chili, cumin, amchur, coriander, and salt.
Divide dough into balls, roll out, place a portion of filling in the center, seal, and roll out again gently.
Cook on a hot tawa, applying butter on both sides until golden brown spots appear.
Serve hot with yogurt, pickle, and butter.
tips: Use dry potato mash to prevent the paratha from tearing.
Roll gently and evenly to keep the filling from breaking through.

name: Gobi Paratha
description: Whole wheat flatbread stuffed with spiced grated cauliflower filling.
instructions: Grate cauliflower finely, squeeze out excess moisture using a clean cloth.
Mix grated cauliflower with chopped green chili, garam masala, ajwain, coriander, and salt.
Knead flour with water and salt into a soft dough; rest for 10 minutes.
Roll out dough balls, stuff with cauliflower mixture, seal, and roll out gently.
Cook on a hot tawa with ghee until golden and crispy on both sides.
tips: Squeezing out all moisture from the cauliflower is crucial to avoid soggy parathas.
Adding ajwain aids digestion and gives a distinctive flavor.

name: Methi Paratha
description: Whole wheat flatbread infused with fresh fenugreek leaves and spices.
instructions: Wash and finely chop fenugreek leaves, sprinkle with salt and let sit for 10 minutes to reduce bitterness.
Mix flour, chopped methi, cumin, turmeric, red chili powder, and salt; knead into a soft dough using water.
Divide into balls, roll out into circles, and cook on a hot tawa with ghee until golden on both sides.
Serve hot with yogurt and pickle.
tips: Salting the methi leaves beforehand reduces their natural bitterness.
The dough may need less water since methi releases moisture.

name: Besan Chilla
description: Savory gram flour pancake with onions, tomatoes, and green chilies.
instructions: Mix besan, water, salt, turmeric, and cumin seeds into a smooth, lump-free batter.
Fold in finely chopped onion, tomato, green chili, and coriander.
Heat a non-stick pan, pour a ladle of batter, and spread into a thin circle.
Drizzle oil around edges, cook until the bottom is golden, flip, and cook the other side.
Serve hot with green chutney or ketchup.
tips: Keep batter consistency like dosa batter — not too thick, not too thin.
A non-stick pan makes flipping much easier.

name: Medu Vada
description: Crispy deep-fried lentil doughnuts made from urad dal batter.
instructions: Soak urad dal for 4 hours, drain, and grind into a thick, fluffy batter without adding too much water.
Mix in finely chopped onion, green chili, ginger, curry leaves, pepper, and salt.
Wet your hands, take a portion of batter, shape into a round with a hole in the center.
Deep fry in hot oil on medium heat until golden brown and crispy on both sides.
Drain on paper towels and serve with coconut chutney and sambar.
tips: The batter must be thick and airy — do not add excess water while grinding.
Test oil temperature by dropping a small bit of batter; it should rise immediately.

name: Uttapam
description: Thick rice and lentil pancake topped with onions, tomatoes, and vegetables.
instructions: Finely chop onion, tomato, capsicum, green chili, and coriander.
Heat a tawa, pour a thick ladle of batter and do not spread too thin — uttapam should be thicker than dosa.
Immediately scatter chopped vegetables on top and press gently into the batter.
Drizzle oil around the edges, cover and cook on medium heat for 2-3 minutes until bottom is golden.
Flip carefully, cook for another minute, and serve with coconut chutney and sambar.
tips: Use slightly thick batter for the best texture.
Cover while cooking to ensure the top vegetables set properly.

name: Sabudana Khichdi
description: Tapioca pearls stir-fried with peanuts, potatoes, and cumin — a popular fasting dish.
instructions: Soak sabudana in water for 4-5 hours or overnight; drain completely.
Roast peanuts, cool, and coarsely crush them.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds, green chilies, and diced potato; cook until potato is tender.
Add soaked sabudana, crushed peanuts, salt, and sugar; stir gently on low heat for 5-7 minutes.
Squeeze lemon juice, garnish with coriander, and serve warm.
tips: Sabudana must be soaked long enough that each pearl is soft but still separate.
Cook on low heat and avoid over-stirring to prevent it from becoming sticky.

name: Moong Dal Chilla
description: Savory crepe made from ground green moong dal batter with onions and spices.
instructions: Soak moong dal for 3-4 hours, drain, and grind into a smooth batter with ginger, green chili, and minimal water.
Mix in cumin seeds, chopped onion, coriander, and salt.
Heat a non-stick pan, pour batter, and spread into a thin circle.
Drizzle oil, cook until golden on the bottom, flip, and cook the other side.
Serve with green chutney or yogurt.
tips: Do not make the batter too thin; it should be spreadable but thick.
These are high in protein and make a great healthy breakfast.

name: Rava Idli
description: Steamed semolina cakes with cashews and spices — a quick alternative to traditional idli.
instructions: Mix semolina with yogurt, water, and salt; let the batter rest for 15 minutes to absorb moisture.
Heat ghee, add mustard seeds, curry leaves, cashews, green chili, and carrot; sauté briefly.
Add the tempering to the batter, mix, then fold in baking soda just before steaming.
Pour into greased idli moulds and steam for 10-12 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean.
Serve hot with coconut chutney and sambar.
tips: Add baking soda only just before steaming for the fluffiest results.
Do not over-mix after adding baking soda.

name: Bread Omelette
description: Indian street-style omelette with onions and spices sandwiched between toasted bread.
instructions: Beat eggs with salt, pepper, chopped onion, tomato, green chili, and coriander.
Heat butter in a pan, pour the egg mixture, and cook until partially set.
Place bread slices on top of the omelette, press gently, and flip carefully.
Cook until the bread is toasted and the egg is fully set.
Serve hot with ketchup.
tips: Use slightly stale bread for better texture and less sogginess.
Flip when the egg is set enough to hold the bread.

name: Curd Rice
description: Creamy yogurt mixed with cooked rice, tempered with mustard seeds and curry leaves.
instructions: Mash cooked rice slightly, mix with yogurt, milk, and salt until creamy.
Heat oil, add mustard seeds, urad dal, curry leaves, ginger, and green chili; let them splutter.
Pour the tempering over the rice-yogurt mixture and mix well.
Refrigerate for 30 minutes for best flavor, or serve at room temperature.
Garnish with pomegranate seeds before serving.
tips: Add milk to keep the curd rice creamy even after refrigerating.
Best served cold — a classic South Indian comfort food.

name: Ven Pongal
description: South Indian rice and moong dal porridge flavored with black pepper, cumin, and ghee.
instructions: Dry roast moong dal until fragrant, wash both rice and dal together.
Pressure cook rice and dal with water for 4 whistles until very soft and mushy.
Heat ghee in a pan, add cumin, peppercorns, curry leaves, ginger, and cashews; fry until cashews are golden.
Pour the tempering over the cooked rice-dal mixture, add salt, and mix well.
Serve hot with coconut chutney and sambar.
tips: Use more water than usual — pongal should be creamy, not dry.
Generous ghee is the key to authentic pongal flavor.

name: Dal Tadka
description: Yellow lentils cooked until creamy and finished with a sizzling temper of ghee, garlic, and spices.
instructions: Wash toor dal, pressure cook with turmeric and water for 3-4 whistles until soft, then mash well.
Add chopped tomato and salt to the dal, simmer on low for 5 minutes.
In a separate small pan, heat ghee, add cumin seeds, dried red chilies, and sliced garlic; fry until garlic is golden.
Pour the sizzling tadka over the dal, cover immediately to trap the aroma.
Garnish with coriander and serve with steamed rice or chapati.
tips: The tadka must be poured while sizzling hot for the best flavor.
Mash the dal well for a creamy consistency.

name: Dal Makhani
description: Rich and creamy black lentils slow-cooked with butter, cream, and aromatic spices.
instructions: Soak urad dal and rajma overnight, then pressure cook with water for 5-6 whistles until very soft.
In a heavy pan, melt butter, add chopped onion, and sauté until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste, cook for a minute, then add tomato puree and spices; cook until oil separates.
Add the cooked dal, mix well, and simmer on very low heat for 30-40 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Stir in cream, adjust salt, and serve garnished with a swirl of cream and a pat of butter.
tips: Low and slow cooking is the secret — never rush dal makhani.
Overnight soaking ensures the lentils cook to a creamy consistency.

name: Chana Masala
description: Chickpeas simmered in a tangy, spiced tomato and onion gravy.
instructions: Heat oil, add finely chopped onions, and sauté until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste, cook for a minute, then add chopped tomatoes and cook until they break down.
Add turmeric, red chili powder, chole masala, and salt; cook the masala until oil separates.
Add chickpeas with half a cup of water, mix well, and simmer for 15 minutes.
Finish with amchur powder, garnish with coriander, and serve with bhature or rice.
tips: For deeper flavor, use tea-soaked water when cooking dried chickpeas from scratch.
Mash a few chickpeas against the pan to thicken the gravy.

name: Rajma
description: Punjabi-style kidney beans cooked in a thick, spiced tomato-onion gravy.
instructions: Soak rajma overnight, then pressure cook with water and salt for 6-7 whistles until soft.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add finely chopped onions and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste, chopped tomatoes, and all dry spices; cook until the masala is well blended.
Add the cooked rajma with its cooking liquid, simmer for 15-20 minutes until the gravy thickens.
Finish with garam masala, garnish with coriander, and serve with steamed basmati rice.
tips: Always soak rajma for at least 8 hours for even cooking.
Mashing a few beans helps thicken the curry naturally.

name: Palak Paneer
description: Cottage cheese cubes in a vibrant pureed spinach gravy with garlic and spices.
instructions: Blanch spinach in boiling water for 2 minutes, immediately transfer to ice water, then blend into a smooth puree.
Heat butter, add cumin seeds, garlic, and green chilies; sauté for a minute.
Add chopped onion and tomato; cook until soft.
Add spinach puree, salt, and garam masala; simmer for 5 minutes.
Add paneer cubes, stir in cream, cook for 2-3 minutes, and serve with naan or roti.
tips: Blanching and ice-bathing the spinach preserves its bright green color.
Do not overcook the spinach puree or it will turn dull.

name: Paneer Butter Masala
description: Soft paneer cubes in a rich, creamy tomato-cashew gravy with butter and spices.
instructions: Boil tomatoes and cashews together until soft, cool, and blend into a smooth puree.
Heat butter, add ginger-garlic paste, and sauté for a minute.
Add the tomato-cashew puree, red chili powder, salt, and sugar; cook on medium heat for 10 minutes.
Add paneer cubes and cream, simmer for 5 minutes on low heat.
Crush kasuri methi and sprinkle on top, finish with a pat of butter, and serve with naan.
tips: Soaking cashews in warm water for 15 minutes makes blending easier.
Adding sugar balances the acidity of the tomatoes.

name: Aloo Gobi
description: Dry-style potato and cauliflower stir-fry with turmeric, cumin, and coriander.
instructions: Cut potato and cauliflower into similar-sized florets and pieces.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add chopped onion and sauté until translucent.
Add tomato, turmeric, coriander powder, and red chili powder; cook for 2-3 minutes.
Add potato and cauliflower, toss well, cover, and cook on low heat for 15-18 minutes until tender.
Sprinkle garam masala, garnish with coriander, and serve with roti.
tips: Cut vegetables to uniform size for even cooking.
Do not stir too often — let the vegetables develop a light crust.

name: Bhindi Masala
description: Crispy okra stir-fried with onions, tomatoes, and aromatic spices.
instructions: Wash okra, dry thoroughly, and cut into 1-inch pieces.
Heat oil in a wide pan, add cumin seeds and chopped onion; sauté until golden.
Add okra pieces and stir-fry on medium-high heat without covering for 8-10 minutes until crispy.
Add chopped tomato, turmeric, coriander, red chili powder, and salt; cook for 3-4 minutes.
Finish with amchur powder and serve with roti or paratha.
tips: The okra must be completely dry before cutting to prevent sliminess.
Do not cover the pan while cooking okra — it needs dry heat to crisp up.

name: Baingan Bharta
description: Smoky roasted eggplant mash cooked with onions, tomatoes, and green chilies.
instructions: Roast the whole eggplant directly over a gas flame, turning frequently, until the skin is charred and the flesh is soft.
Peel off the charred skin under running water, mash the smoky flesh, and set aside.
Heat mustard oil, add cumin seeds, garlic, and green chilies; sauté briefly.
Add chopped onions and cook until golden, then add tomatoes and spices; cook until oil separates.
Add mashed eggplant, mix well, cook for 5 minutes, garnish with coriander, and serve with roti.
tips: Charring directly over flame gives the authentic smoky flavor — do not skip this step.
Mustard oil is traditional and adds pungent depth.

name: Matar Paneer
description: Cottage cheese and green peas in a mildly spiced tomato-onion gravy.
instructions: Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add chopped onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste, cook for a minute, then add pureed tomatoes and all spice powders.
Cook the masala until oil separates, about 5-7 minutes.
Add green peas and half a cup of water; simmer for 8 minutes until peas are tender.
Add paneer cubes, simmer for 3-4 minutes, finish with garam masala, and serve with roti or rice.
tips: Use frozen peas for convenience — they cook perfectly in the gravy.
Do not overcook paneer or it becomes rubbery.

name: Kadai Paneer
description: Paneer and bell peppers cooked in a spicy tomato gravy with freshly ground kadai masala.
instructions: Dry roast coriander seeds and dried red chilies, then coarsely grind into kadai masala.
Heat oil, add chopped onion and garlic; sauté until light golden.
Add pureed tomatoes, salt, and cook until thick and oil separates.
Add the kadai masala, capsicum strips, and julienned ginger; cook for 3-4 minutes.
Add paneer cubes, toss gently, sprinkle kasuri methi, and serve hot with naan.
tips: Freshly ground kadai masala is essential — pre-made spice mixes lack the depth.
Add capsicum late to retain its crunch.

name: Sambar
description: South Indian lentil and vegetable stew flavored with tamarind and sambar powder.
instructions: Pressure cook toor dal with turmeric and water until soft, then mash well.
Boil chopped vegetables (drumstick, carrot, onion) separately until tender.
In a pan, heat oil, add mustard seeds and curry leaves; let them splutter.
Add tomato, sambar powder, tamarind paste, cooked vegetables, and mashed dal; simmer for 10 minutes.
Adjust salt and consistency, and serve with rice, idli, or dosa.
tips: Use fresh sambar powder for the best flavor.
Adding a small piece of jaggery balances the tamarind sourness.

name: Rasam
description: Tangy South Indian soup made with tamarind, tomatoes, pepper, and cumin.
instructions: Boil chopped tomatoes with tamarind paste, rasam powder, and water until tomatoes are soft.
Add mashed cooked toor dal and salt; bring to a rolling boil.
In a small pan, heat ghee, add mustard seeds, cumin, crushed garlic, and curry leaves; let them splutter.
Pour the tempering into the rasam, garnish with coriander.
Serve hot with steamed rice or drink as a soup.
tips: Rasam should be thin and soupy, not thick like sambar.
Turn off heat as soon as it comes to a frothy boil — do not over-boil.

name: Malai Kofta
description: Deep-fried paneer and potato dumplings served in a rich, creamy tomato-cashew gravy.
instructions: Boil and mash potatoes, crumble paneer, mix with cornstarch and salt; shape into round koftas.
Deep fry koftas on medium heat until golden brown; drain and set aside.
For gravy: blend boiled tomatoes and soaked cashews into a smooth puree.
Heat butter, add ginger-garlic paste, pour in the tomato-cashew puree with spices; cook for 10 minutes.
Add cream and kasuri methi, place koftas in the gravy just before serving to keep them crispy.
tips: Add koftas to gravy only while serving — soaking makes them soggy.
If koftas break during frying, add more cornstarch to the mixture.

name: Chole
description: North Indian spiced chickpea curry with a dark, tangy gravy made with tea and spices.
instructions: Soak chickpeas overnight, then pressure cook with a tea bag and water for 5-6 whistles until soft; remove tea bag.
Heat oil, add finely sliced onions, and fry until deep brown for the base.
Add ginger-garlic paste, chopped tomatoes, and all spice powders; cook until oil separates.
Add cooked chickpeas with their dark cooking liquid; simmer for 15 minutes until thick.
Finish with amchur, garnish with ginger juliennes and coriander, serve with bhature or rice.
tips: The tea bag gives chole their characteristic dark color.
Frying onions until very dark brown is essential for authentic flavor.

name: Dal Fry
description: Mixed lentils cooked until creamy, tempered with cumin, garlic, and tomatoes.
instructions: Wash all dals together, pressure cook with turmeric and water for 3-4 whistles until soft.
Mash the cooked dal to a smooth consistency.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds and sliced garlic; fry until garlic turns golden.
Add chopped onion, sauté until translucent, then add tomato and red chili powder; cook until soft.
Add the mashed dal, simmer for 5 minutes, adjust salt, and serve with rice or roti.
tips: Using a mix of dals gives a richer texture than a single variety.
A squeeze of lemon at the end brightens the flavor.

name: Lauki Sabzi
description: Mild bottle gourd curry cooked with tomatoes and simple spices.
instructions: Peel and dice bottle gourd into small cubes.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add chopped onion and sauté until translucent.
Add tomato, green chili, turmeric, and coriander powder; cook for 2 minutes.
Add lauki cubes, salt, cover, and cook on low heat for 12-15 minutes until tender.
Garnish with coriander and serve with roti or dal-rice.
tips: Lauki releases a lot of water, so no need to add extra water.
Choose a young, firm lauki for the best texture.

name: Tinda Masala
description: Indian baby round gourd cooked in a simple onion-tomato masala.
instructions: Peel tinda, cut into quarters or halves depending on size.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then chopped onion; sauté until golden.
Add tomato and spice powders; cook until masala thickens.
Add tinda pieces, salt, and a splash of water; cover and cook for 15-18 minutes.
Garnish with coriander and serve with roti.
tips: Choose tender, small tinda for best results — large ones can be seedy.
Do not add too much water; tinda has high moisture content.

name: Arhar Dal
description: Simple everyday pigeon pea dal tempered with cumin, garlic, and ghee.
instructions: Wash toor dal, pressure cook with turmeric and water for 3 whistles until soft.
Mash the dal, add chopped tomato and salt; simmer for 5 minutes.
Heat ghee in a small pan, add cumin seeds, dried red chili, and sliced garlic; fry until golden.
Pour the tadka over the simmering dal, cover for a minute to let flavors meld.
Serve with steamed rice and a side of pickle.
tips: This is a staple everyday dal — keep it simple and let the tadka do the work.
Consistency should be thinner than dal makhani but thicker than rasam.

name: Moong Dal
description: Light and comforting yellow moong dal with a simple garlic and cumin tadka.
instructions: Wash moong dal, pressure cook with turmeric and water for 2-3 whistles until very soft.
Mash lightly with a whisk, add salt, and bring to a simmer.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds, sliced garlic, and green chili; fry until golden.
Pour the tadka over the dal, garnish with coriander.
Serve with steamed rice — this is one of the lightest, most digestible dals.
tips: Moong dal cooks quickly and does not need soaking.
Perfect for when you want something light and easy on the stomach.

name: Masoor Dal
description: Quick-cooking red lentil dal with a tomato and onion base.
instructions: Wash masoor dal and pressure cook with turmeric and water for 2 whistles.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds, garlic, and chopped onion; sauté until light brown.
Add chopped tomato, red chili powder, and salt; cook until tomato softens.
Add the cooked masoor dal, simmer for 5 minutes.
Garnish with coriander and serve with rice or roti.
tips: Masoor dal cooks the fastest among all dals — do not overcook or it becomes too mushy.
This dal thickens as it cools, so keep it slightly thinner than desired.

name: Cabbage Sabzi
description: Shredded cabbage stir-fried with mustard seeds, turmeric, and green chilies.
instructions: Finely shred the cabbage and chop the onion.
Heat oil, add mustard seeds and curry leaves; let them splutter.
Add chopped onion and green chili; sauté for 2 minutes.
Add shredded cabbage, turmeric, and salt; stir-fry on medium-high heat for 5-7 minutes.
The cabbage should be cooked but still have a slight crunch. Squeeze lemon juice and serve.
tips: Do not cover the pan — cabbage should dry-fry, not steam.
Adding grated coconut at the end is a nice South Indian variation.

name: Aloo Matar
description: Potatoes and green peas cooked in a light tomato-based curry.
instructions: Peel and cube potatoes into bite-sized pieces.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add chopped onion and sauté until golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste, tomato, and spice powders; cook until masala is fragrant.
Add potatoes, peas, salt, and half a cup of water; cover and cook for 15 minutes.
Finish with garam masala, garnish with coriander, and serve with roti or puri.
tips: For a richer version, add a splash of cream at the end.
Frozen peas work perfectly and can be added in the last 5 minutes.

name: Paneer Tikka
description: Marinated paneer cubes and vegetables grilled until smoky and charred.
instructions: Cut paneer, capsicum, and onion into 1-inch cubes.
Mix yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, all spice powders, lemon juice, oil, and salt into a smooth marinade.
Coat paneer and vegetables in the marinade; refrigerate for 30 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill on high heat (or broil in oven) for 8-10 minutes, turning once.
Sprinkle chaat masala and serve with mint chutney and onion rings.
tips: Do not marinate for too long — the acid in yogurt can make paneer crumbly.
A hot grill or oven broiler gives the best char marks.

name: Tandoori Chicken
description: Chicken marinated in yogurt and spices, roasted until smoky with a vibrant red crust.
instructions: Make deep slashes in the chicken pieces for the marinade to penetrate.
Mix yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, tandoori masala, red chili powder, lemon juice, oil, and salt.
Coat chicken thoroughly in the marinade; refrigerate for at least 2 hours (overnight is best).
Bake at 220 degrees Celsius for 25-30 minutes, or grill until charred and juices run clear.
Sprinkle chaat masala, squeeze lemon, and serve with naan and onion rings.
tips: Deep slashes are essential for the marinade to reach the bone.
For smoky flavor at home, place a hot coal in a small bowl of oil inside the marinating container for 2 minutes.

name: Butter Chicken
description: Tender chicken pieces in a silky, buttery tomato-cream sauce with aromatic spices.
instructions: Marinate chicken in yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, red chili powder, and salt for 1 hour.
Grill or pan-sear marinated chicken until lightly charred; set aside.
For the sauce: melt butter, add tomato puree, soaked cashew paste, and spices; cook for 10 minutes.
Strain the sauce for smoothness, add cream, sugar, and the cooked chicken pieces; simmer 10 minutes.
Crush kasuri methi and sprinkle on top, finish with a swirl of cream, and serve with naan.
tips: Straining the sauce gives the characteristic silky-smooth texture.
Sugar is essential to balance the tomato acidity.

name: Chicken Curry
description: Home-style Indian chicken curry with onion-tomato gravy and warm spices.
instructions: Heat oil, add sliced onions, and cook until deep golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste, cook for a minute, then add chopped tomatoes and all spice powders.
Cook the masala until oil separates — this is the key to a rich gravy.
Add chicken pieces, coat with the masala, add half a cup of water, cover, and cook for 20-25 minutes.
Finish with garam masala and coriander; serve with rice or roti.
tips: Bone-in chicken gives far more flavor than boneless.
Browning the onions deeply is what makes or breaks this curry.

name: Fish Curry
description: Tender fish pieces simmered in a tangy coconut and tomato curry with curry leaves.
instructions: Rub fish pieces with turmeric and salt; let sit for 10 minutes.
Heat coconut oil, add mustard seeds and curry leaves; let them splutter.
Add chopped onion, cook until soft, then add ginger-garlic paste and tomatoes.
Add red chili powder, tamarind paste, coconut milk, and water; bring to a gentle simmer.
Gently slide in the fish pieces, cook uncovered for 8-10 minutes until fish is just cooked through.
tips: Do not stir the fish too much — it breaks apart easily.
Coconut oil is traditional and adds authentic coastal flavor.

name: Egg Curry
description: Boiled eggs simmered in a spiced onion-tomato gravy — a quick protein-rich dinner.
instructions: Hard boil eggs for 10 minutes, peel, and make shallow slits for the gravy to penetrate.
Heat oil, add sliced onions, and fry until golden brown.
Add ginger-garlic paste, tomatoes, and all spice powders; cook until oil separates.
Add a cup of water, bring to a simmer, then add the boiled eggs.
Simmer for 5-7 minutes, finish with garam masala and coriander; serve with rice or roti.
tips: Lightly frying the boiled eggs before adding to gravy adds color and texture.
The longer eggs sit in the gravy, the more flavorful they become.

name: Keema
description: Spiced minced meat curry cooked with peas, onions, and aromatic spices.
instructions: Heat oil, add cumin seeds, then add finely chopped onions and cook until deep golden.
Add ginger-garlic paste and sauté for a minute.
Add minced meat, break up any lumps, and cook on high heat until browned.
Add tomatoes, spice powders, salt, and peas; cover and cook for 15-20 minutes until meat is tender.
Finish with garam masala and coriander; serve with roti, naan, or pav.
tips: Brown the mince on high heat without stirring initially for better flavor.
Keema thickens as it rests — it is even better the next day.

name: Vegetable Biryani
description: Layered fragrant basmati rice and spiced mixed vegetables cooked in the dum style.

ingredients.name: whole spices (bay leaf, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon)

instructions: Soak rice for 30 minutes, then parboil in salted water with whole spices until 70% cooked; drain.
Slice onions thin and deep fry until crispy and golden (birista); set aside.
Cook vegetables with yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, biryani masala, and salt until half-done.
Layer: vegetable masala on the bottom, then rice, fried onions, mint, coriander, and saffron milk; repeat.
Seal the pot with dough or foil, cook on very low heat (dum) for 25 minutes; gently mix while serving.
tips: Parboiling rice to exactly 70% is critical — it finishes cooking during dum.
Sealing the pot tightly traps steam for the signature dum flavor.

name: Chicken Biryani
description: Aromatic layered rice and spiced chicken cooked slowly in sealed pot (dum style).

ingredients.name: whole spices (bay leaf, cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, star anise)

instructions: Marinate chicken with yogurt, ginger-garlic paste, biryani masala, and salt for at least 1 hour.
Soak rice for 30 minutes, parboil with whole spices until 70% done; drain.
Fry thinly sliced onions in ghee until deeply golden; remove half for garnish.
Cook marinated chicken with the remaining fried onions for 10 minutes until half-done.
Layer: chicken on the bottom, then rice, fried onions, mint, coriander, and saffron milk; seal and cook on very low heat for 25-30 minutes.
tips: Never skip marination — it tenderizes the chicken and builds flavor.
The sealed dum cooking is where the magic happens — patience is key.

name: Dal Khichdi
description: Comforting one-pot meal of rice and lentils cooked together with mild spices and ghee.
instructions: Wash rice and moong dal together until water runs clear.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds, chopped onion, green chili, and tomato; sauté for 2-3 minutes.
Add rice, dal, turmeric, salt, and water; bring to a boil.
Pressure cook for 3 whistles or simmer covered for 20 minutes until soft and porridge-like.
Serve with a generous dollop of ghee, papad, and pickle.
tips: Khichdi should be soft and mushy — add more water if needed.
This is the ultimate Indian comfort food, often served when unwell.

name: Cucumber Raita
description: Cool yogurt dip with grated cucumber, cumin, and fresh mint.
instructions: Grate cucumber and squeeze out excess water.
Whisk yogurt until smooth, add grated cucumber.
Mix in cumin powder, chopped mint, salt, and a pinch of red chili powder.
Refrigerate for 15 minutes before serving for best flavor.
tips: Squeezing out cucumber water prevents a watery raita.
Roasted cumin powder adds a deeper, nuttier flavor.

name: Boondi Raita
description: Creamy yogurt mixed with crispy gram flour droplets and spiced with cumin.
instructions: Soak boondi in water for 5 minutes until slightly soft, then squeeze out water.
Whisk yogurt until smooth and creamy.
Add softened boondi, cumin powder, salt, and chili powder; mix gently.
Garnish with coriander and serve chilled alongside biryani or pulao.
tips: Use plain boondi, not the masala variety, for raita.
Do not over-soak boondi or it becomes too mushy.

name: Jeera Rice
description: Fragrant basmati rice cooked with cumin seeds and ghee.
instructions: Wash and soak basmati rice for 20 minutes, then drain.
Heat ghee in a pan, add cumin seeds and bay leaf; let them splutter.
Add drained rice and sauté gently for 1-2 minutes.
Add water and salt, bring to a boil, then cover and cook on low heat for 12-14 minutes.
Fluff with a fork and serve with any dal or curry.
tips: Soaking rice ensures long, separate grains.
Do not stir the rice once water is added — it breaks the grains.

name: Plain Chapati
description: Thin whole wheat flatbread cooked on a hot griddle — the staple Indian bread.
instructions: Mix flour, salt, and water; knead into a soft, smooth dough for 5 minutes.
Rest the dough covered for 10 minutes.
Divide into small balls, dust with flour, and roll into thin circles.
Cook on a hot tawa until bubbles appear, flip, and cook until brown spots appear; it should puff up.
Apply a thin layer of ghee and serve immediately.
tips: Soft dough and thorough kneading give the softest chapatis.
Direct flame contact for the last few seconds helps chapati puff fully.

name: Naan
description: Soft, leavened Indian flatbread baked until pillowy with charred spots.
instructions: Mix warm milk, sugar, and yeast; let sit for 5 minutes until frothy.
Combine flour, baking powder, salt, yogurt, oil, and yeast mixture; knead into a soft dough.
Cover and let rest for 1 hour until doubled in size.
Divide into balls, roll into oval shapes, and cook on a very hot tawa or cast iron pan until charred and puffed.
Brush with garlic butter (minced garlic mixed with melted butter) and serve hot.
tips: A very hot surface is key — naan should char slightly for authentic flavor.
For tandoori effect at home, use a cast iron pan or place under a broiler.

name: Plain Steamed Rice
description: Simple steamed basmati rice — the foundation of an Indian meal.
instructions: Wash rice in several changes of water until water runs clear.
Soak for 20 minutes, then drain.
Add rice, fresh water, and salt to a pot; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat to lowest, cover tightly, and cook for 12 minutes.
Turn off heat, keep covered for 5 minutes, then fluff with a fork.
tips: The 1:2 rice-to-water ratio works for most basmati.
Never stir rice while cooking — it makes it gummy.

name: Vegetable Pulao
description: Fragrant basmati rice cooked with mixed vegetables and whole spices.
instructions: Wash and soak rice for 20 minutes; drain.
Heat ghee, add whole spices (bay leaf, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves), and sauté for a minute.
Add sliced onion and cook until translucent; add chopped vegetables and stir-fry for 2 minutes.
Add drained rice, salt, and water; bring to a boil, then cover and cook on low heat for 14 minutes.
Fluff gently with a fork and serve with raita.
tips: Handle the cooked pulao gently — vigorous stirring breaks the rice grains.
Toasting whole spices in ghee before adding rice adds tremendous fragrance.

name: Lemon Rice
description: South Indian tangy rice tempered with peanuts, mustard seeds, and lemon juice.
instructions: Heat oil, add mustard seeds, urad dal, chana dal, and peanuts; fry until dals are golden.
Add curry leaves, green chilies, and turmeric; sauté briefly.
Add cooked rice (preferably cooled), salt, and toss gently with the tempering.
Squeeze lemon juice over the rice, mix well, and serve.
Best served at room temperature — perfect for lunchboxes.
tips: Use cold or leftover rice — fresh hot rice can become mushy.
Add lemon juice after turning off heat to preserve its fresh flavor.

name: Plain Yogurt (Dahi)
description: Fresh homemade yogurt set overnight — a staple accompaniment to Indian meals.
instructions: Boil milk and let it cool to lukewarm (about 40 degrees Celsius — you should be able to hold your finger in it comfortably).
Mix in the yogurt starter thoroughly.
Pour into a container, cover, and keep in a warm place undisturbed for 6-8 hours.
Once set, refrigerate to stop fermentation.
Serve chilled as a side with any Indian meal.
tips: Temperature is critical — too hot kills the cultures, too cold prevents setting.
In cold weather, wrap the container in a towel or place in the oven with the light on.

name: Roasted Papad
description: Thin lentil wafer roasted until crispy — the simplest Indian side.
instructions: Hold a papad over a direct gas flame using tongs, rotating constantly.
The papad will puff up and develop charred spots within 15-20 seconds per side.
Alternatively, microwave for 30-40 seconds until crisp.
Serve immediately as a crunchy accompaniment to any meal.
tips: Keep the papad moving constantly over the flame to avoid burning.
Microwave method is easier but lacks the smoky flavor.

name: Aloo Paratha (Side)
description: Stuffed potato flatbread served as a hearty side with curd and pickle.
instructions: Knead flour, water, and salt into a soft dough; rest for 10 minutes.
Boil, peel, and mash potatoes; mix with green chili, cumin, amchur, and salt.
Roll a dough ball, stuff with potato filling, seal, and roll out carefully.
Cook on a hot tawa with ghee on both sides until golden.
Serve with a dollop of butter, yogurt, and pickle.
tips: Dust generously with flour while rolling to prevent sticking.
Press gently while cooking to ensure even browning.

name: Methi Thepla
description: Gujarati spiced fenugreek flatbread — perfect for travel and lunchboxes.
instructions: Wash and finely chop methi leaves; sprinkle with salt and let sit for 5 minutes.
Mix both flours, methi, yogurt, turmeric, chili powder, 1 tbsp oil, and salt; knead into a soft dough using water.
Divide into small balls and roll into thin circles.
Cook on a hot tawa with oil on both sides until golden spots appear.
Stack and wrap in foil — theplas stay fresh for days.
tips: Adding a bit of oil to the dough keeps theplas soft even when cold.
These are the quintessential Gujarati travel food.

name: Puri
description: Deep-fried puffed whole wheat bread — a festive Indian staple.
instructions: Mix flour, salt, and 1 tsp oil; knead with water into a stiff dough (stiffer than chapati dough).
Rest for 10 minutes, then divide into small balls.
Roll each ball into a small, thick circle (about 4 inches diameter).
Heat oil until very hot, slide in a puri, and press gently with a slotted spoon until it puffs up.
Flip once, drain on paper towels, and serve immediately with aloo sabzi or halwa.
tips: The dough must be stiff for puris to puff — soft dough absorbs too much oil.
Oil must be very hot — a small piece of dough should immediately rise to the surface.

name: Mixed Vegetable Raita
description: Yogurt dip with finely chopped mixed vegetables, seasoned with roasted cumin.
instructions: Finely dice cucumber, tomato, onion, and carrot.
Whisk yogurt until smooth.
Mix in all chopped vegetables, cumin powder, chaat masala, and salt.
Garnish with coriander and chill before serving.
tips: Deseed the tomato to prevent excess liquid.
This raita pairs especially well with biryani and pulao.

name: Mango Pickle (Aam ka Achaar)
description: Tangy and spicy raw mango pickle with mustard oil and traditional spices.
instructions: Wash and dry raw mangoes completely; cut into small pieces with the seed.
Mix mango pieces with salt, turmeric, and all spice powders; toss well.
Heat mustard oil until it smokes, let it cool completely.
Pour cooled oil over the spiced mango mixture and mix thoroughly.
Transfer to a clean glass jar, press down, keep in sunlight for 3-4 days, shaking daily.
tips: Mangoes and all utensils must be completely dry to prevent mold.
The pickle improves with age — best after a week of maturing.

name: Samosa
description: Crispy deep-fried pastry filled with spiced potatoes and peas.
instructions: Make a stiff dough with flour, oil, salt, and water; rest for 20 minutes.
Boil and coarsely mash potatoes; mix with peas, cumin, coriander, garam masala, amchur, green chili, and salt.
Divide dough into balls, roll into ovals, cut in half, and shape each half into a cone.
Fill cones with potato mixture, seal edges with water.
Deep fry on medium-low heat (not too hot) until golden and crispy; serve with tamarind and mint chutney.
tips: Fry on medium-low heat for crispy layers — high heat cooks the outside but leaves dough raw inside.
The dough must be stiff for flaky, layered samosas.

name: Pakora
description: Crispy gram flour fritters made with mixed vegetables like onion, spinach, and potato.
instructions: Thinly slice onion and potato; chop spinach and green chilies.
Mix besan, cumin, red chili powder, baking soda, and salt; add vegetables and minimal water to make a thick batter.
Heat oil to medium-hot. Drop spoonfuls of batter into the oil.
Fry in batches until deep golden and crispy, about 3-4 minutes per batch.
Drain on paper towels and serve hot with mint chutney or ketchup.
tips: Use minimal water — the moisture from vegetables should form most of the batter.
A pinch of baking soda makes pakoras extra crispy.

name: Dhokla
description: Steamed savory gram flour cake from Gujarat, spongy and tangy with a mustard seed tempering.
instructions: Mix besan, yogurt, water, turmeric, salt, and sugar into a smooth batter; let rest for 10 minutes.
Add eno fruit salt (or baking soda + lemon juice), mix quickly as it froths, and immediately pour into a greased steaming plate.
Steam for 12-15 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean.
For tempering: heat oil, add mustard seeds, curry leaves, green chilies, and a splash of water with sugar.
Pour tempering over the steamed dhokla, cut into squares, and serve with green chutney.
tips: Pour batter immediately after adding eno — the reaction is what makes dhokla spongy.
Do not open the steamer during cooking or dhokla will collapse.

name: Vada Pav
description: Mumbai's iconic street food — spiced potato fritter in a soft bun with chutneys.
instructions: Boil, peel, and mash potatoes. Make a paste of garlic and green chilies.
Heat 1 tbsp oil, add mustard seeds, curry leaves, and the garlic-chili paste; sauté briefly.
Add turmeric, mashed potatoes, and salt; mix well and shape into round patties.
Make a batter with besan, turmeric, salt, and water; dip each potato ball and deep fry until golden.
Slit pav, spread dry garlic chutney and green chutney, place the vada inside, and serve hot.
tips: The garlic chutney (dry coconut, garlic, red chili) is what makes vada pav iconic.
Press the pav onto a hot buttered tawa for a toasted version.

name: Pav Bhaji
description: Mashed mixed vegetable curry served with buttered, toasted bread rolls.
instructions: Boil potatoes, cauliflower, and peas until very soft; mash everything together.
Heat butter, add chopped onion and capsicum; sauté until soft.
Add chopped tomatoes, pav bhaji masala, and salt; cook until tomatoes break down.
Add the mashed vegetables, mix well, and cook on medium heat for 10 minutes, mashing continuously.
Slit and toast pav with butter, squeeze lemon over the bhaji, garnish with onion and coriander.
tips: Use a potato masher directly in the pan for the characteristic texture.
Generous butter is non-negotiable for authentic pav bhaji.

name: Aloo Tikki
description: Crispy spiced potato patties, shallow-fried until golden and crunchy.
instructions: Boil, peel, and mash potatoes; mix with mashed peas, grated ginger, green chili, cumin, garam masala, coriander, and salt.
Add breadcrumbs to bind, shape into flat round patties.
Heat oil in a flat pan, place tikkis, and shallow fry on medium heat for 3-4 minutes per side until crispy.
Serve with tamarind chutney, mint chutney, and yogurt.
tips: Chill the patties for 15 minutes before frying — they hold shape better.
Breadcrumbs help absorb moisture and make tikkis crispier.

name: Vegetable Spring Rolls
description: Crispy fried rolls filled with stir-fried cabbage, carrots, and bean sprouts.
instructions: Julienne all vegetables finely; mince garlic.
Heat 1 tbsp oil, sauté garlic, add all vegetables, soy sauce, vinegar, and salt; stir-fry on high heat for 2-3 minutes. Cool completely.
Place filling on a spring roll wrapper, fold sides in, and roll tightly; seal edge with cornstarch slurry.
Deep fry in medium-hot oil until golden and crispy, about 3-4 minutes.
Serve with sweet chili sauce or schezwan sauce.
tips: The filling must be completely cool before wrapping — hot filling makes wrappers soggy.
Roll tightly to prevent them from opening during frying.

name: Paneer Tikka (Dry)
description: Skewered paneer cubes and vegetables grilled dry with tandoori spices.
instructions: Cut paneer, capsicum, and onion into 1-inch cubes.
Mix yogurt, tandoori masala, lemon juice, oil, and salt into a marinade.
Coat paneer and vegetables, marinate for 20 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill, broil, or pan-fry on high heat until charred spots appear.
Sprinkle chaat masala and serve with lemon wedges and mint chutney.
tips: For smokier flavor, brush with a tiny bit of oil during grilling.
Cast iron pan works well as a substitute for a tandoor.

name: Corn Chaat
description: Boiled sweet corn tossed with onion, tomato, lime juice, and chaat spices.
instructions: Boil or steam corn kernels until tender; drain.
Toss hot corn with butter, then add finely chopped onion, tomato, and green chili.
Add lemon juice, chaat masala, and black salt; mix well.
Garnish with coriander and serve immediately while warm.
tips: For a smoky version, char the corn on an open flame before removing kernels.
Frozen corn works perfectly for a quick snack.

name: Bhel Puri
description: Crunchy puffed rice snack tossed with sev, chutneys, onions, and raw mango.
instructions: Finely chop onion, tomato, boiled potato, and coriander.
In a large bowl, combine puffed rice, chopped vegetables, and half the sev.
Add tamarind chutney, green chutney, lemon juice, and chaat masala; toss quickly.
Serve immediately, topped with remaining sev for crunch.
tips: Mix and serve immediately — bhel becomes soggy within minutes.
Adjust chutney quantities to taste — the balance of sweet, sour, and spicy is key.

name: Sev Puri
description: Flat crispy puris topped with potato, onion, chutneys, and sev.
instructions: Finely dice boiled potato, onion, and tomato.
