Arrange papdi on a plate, top each with diced potato and onion.
Drizzle tamarind chutney and green chutney over each puri.
Top generously with sev, sprinkle chaat masala and coriander, serve immediately.
tips: Assemble just before serving to keep papdi crispy.
Balance of sweet tamarind and spicy green chutney is what makes sev puri special.

name: Vegetable Cutlet
description: Mixed vegetable patty coated in breadcrumbs and shallow-fried until crispy.
instructions: Boil potato, carrot, peas, beans, and corn until tender; mash everything together.
Mix in garam masala, green chili, coriander, and salt.
Shape into oval patties, coat in breadcrumbs.
Shallow fry on medium heat until golden brown on both sides.
Serve with ketchup or mint chutney.
tips: Squeeze out any excess moisture from vegetables before mixing.
Chilling shaped cutlets for 15 minutes helps them hold together while frying.

name: Mathri
description: Crispy, flaky deep-fried crackers made with flour, ajwain, and ghee.
instructions: Mix flour, semolina, ajwain, pepper, and salt; rub in ghee until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs.
Knead into a stiff dough with water; rest for 15 minutes.
Roll out dough, cut into small circles or diamonds, and prick with a fork.
Deep fry on low-medium heat until golden and crispy, about 4-5 minutes per batch.
Cool completely and store in an airtight container — stays crispy for weeks.
tips: Fry on low heat — mathri needs slow cooking to be crispy through the center.
Pricking with a fork prevents puffing and ensures even crispness.

name: Murukku
description: Crunchy spiral South Indian snack made from rice flour and urad dal flour.
instructions: Mix rice flour, urad dal flour, butter, cumin, sesame seeds, asafoetida, and salt.
Add warm water gradually and knead into a smooth, pliable dough.
Load dough into a murukku press with a star-shaped disc.
Press spirals directly into medium-hot oil, fry until golden and crispy.
Drain on paper towels, cool completely, and store in airtight containers.
tips: The dough must be smooth and crack-free for even spirals.
Fry on medium heat — too hot and the outside burns while inside stays raw.

name: Banana Chips
description: Thin slices of raw banana deep-fried in coconut oil until crispy and golden.
instructions: Peel raw bananas and slice very thinly using a mandoline or sharp knife.
Dissolve turmeric and salt in water; briefly dip banana slices in this mixture.
Heat coconut oil in a deep pan until hot.
Fry banana slices in batches on medium heat until golden and crispy, about 3-4 minutes.
Drain on paper towels, cool completely, and store in airtight containers.
tips: Use a mandoline for uniformly thin slices — thickness determines crispness.
Coconut oil is traditional and gives the characteristic Kerala flavor.

name: Scrambled Eggs
description: Soft and creamy scrambled eggs cooked slowly with butter.
instructions: Crack eggs into a bowl, add milk, salt, and pepper; whisk until combined.
Melt butter in a non-stick pan over low heat.
Pour in the egg mixture and stir gently with a spatula, pushing eggs from edges to center.
Remove from heat while eggs are still slightly wet — they continue cooking from residual heat.
Garnish with chives and serve with toast.
tips: Low heat and constant stirring give the creamiest results.
Remove from heat just before eggs look done — they firm up off heat.

name: French Toast
description: Bread slices soaked in an egg and milk mixture, pan-fried until golden.
instructions: Whisk together eggs, milk, cinnamon, vanilla, and sugar in a shallow dish.
Dip each bread slice into the mixture, letting it soak for 15 seconds per side.
Melt butter in a pan over medium heat.
Cook each slice for 2-3 minutes per side until golden brown.
Serve with maple syrup, fresh berries, or powdered sugar.
tips: Day-old bread works best — it absorbs the custard without falling apart.
Thick-cut bread like brioche gives the most luxurious result.

name: Pancakes
description: Fluffy American-style pancakes served stacked with butter and maple syrup.
instructions: Mix flour, baking powder, sugar, and salt in a bowl.
In another bowl, whisk milk, egg, melted butter, and vanilla.
Combine wet and dry ingredients, stirring just until mixed — small lumps are fine.
Pour 1/4 cup batter onto a buttered griddle over medium heat; cook until bubbles form on surface, then flip.
Stack pancakes and serve with butter and maple syrup.
tips: Do not over-mix the batter — lumps are fine and over-mixing makes pancakes tough.
Wait for bubbles to appear and edges to set before flipping.

name: Granola Yogurt Bowl
description: Greek yogurt topped with crunchy granola, honey, and fresh fruits.
instructions: Spoon Greek yogurt into a bowl.
Top with granola, sliced banana, and blueberries.
Drizzle honey over the top.
Sprinkle chia seeds and serve immediately.
tips: Add granola just before eating to keep it crunchy.
Swap fruits based on what is in season.

name: Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in a spiced tomato and pepper sauce with cumin and paprika.
instructions: Heat olive oil, sauté diced onion and bell pepper until soft, about 5 minutes.
Add minced garlic, cumin, paprika, and chili flakes; cook for 1 minute.
Pour in canned tomatoes, break them up, season with salt, and simmer for 10 minutes until thickened.
Make 4 wells in the sauce, crack an egg into each well, cover, and cook for 5-7 minutes until whites are set.
Garnish with parsley and serve with crusty bread for dipping.
tips: Cover the pan for the eggs to cook evenly from top and bottom.
The yolks should still be runny — do not overcook.

name: Greek Salad
description: Classic Mediterranean salad with cucumber, tomato, olives, red onion, and feta cheese.
instructions: Cut cucumber and tomatoes into large chunks; slice red onion thinly.
Combine vegetables in a large bowl, add olives.
Whisk olive oil, vinegar, oregano, salt, and pepper together.
Pour dressing over vegetables and toss gently.
Top with a slab of feta cheese and serve — traditional Greek salad has the feta on top, not crumbled.
tips: Use ripe, in-season tomatoes for the best flavor.
Do not refrigerate too long — Greek salad is best at room temperature.

name: Caprese Salad
description: Simple Italian salad of fresh mozzarella, ripe tomatoes, and basil with olive oil.
instructions: Slice tomatoes and mozzarella into rounds of equal thickness.
Arrange alternating slices of tomato and mozzarella on a plate.
Tuck basil leaves between the slices.
Drizzle with olive oil and balsamic glaze, season with salt and pepper.
tips: Use the best quality mozzarella and tomatoes you can find — this dish depends entirely on ingredient quality.
Burrata can be substituted for mozzarella for a more luxurious version.

name: Minestrone Soup
description: Hearty Italian vegetable soup with beans, pasta, and seasonal vegetables.
instructions: Heat olive oil, sauté diced onion, carrot, and celery until softened, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic, Italian seasoning, and cook for 1 minute.
Add canned tomatoes, vegetable broth, and beans; bring to a boil.
Add diced zucchini and pasta; simmer for 12-15 minutes until pasta is tender.
Season with salt, serve topped with grated parmesan.
tips: Add pasta near the end so it does not overcook and become mushy.
This soup is even better the next day as flavors meld.

name: Ratatouille
description: Classic French Provencal stew of eggplant, zucchini, bell peppers, and tomatoes.
instructions: Dice all vegetables into roughly equal-sized cubes.
Heat olive oil, sauté onion and garlic until soft.
Add bell peppers and cook for 5 minutes, then add eggplant and zucchini.
Add canned tomatoes, thyme, salt, and pepper; cover and simmer for 25-30 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Finish with fresh basil and serve with crusty bread or as a side dish.
tips: Salting eggplant cubes for 15 minutes beforehand removes bitterness.
Each vegetable can also be cooked separately first for more distinct textures.

name: Quiche Lorraine
description: Classic French savory tart with bacon, cheese, and custard in a buttery crust.
instructions: Blind bake the pie crust at 190 degrees Celsius for 12 minutes with pie weights, then remove weights and bake 5 more minutes.
Cook bacon until crispy, chop, and scatter over the baked crust along with grated gruyere.
Whisk eggs, cream, milk, nutmeg, salt, and pepper until smooth.
Pour the custard over the bacon and cheese.
Bake at 175 degrees Celsius for 30-35 minutes until the custard is set but still slightly jiggly in the center.
tips: Blind baking prevents a soggy bottom crust.
The quiche continues to set as it cools — do not overbake.

name: Caesar Salad
description: Crisp romaine lettuce with creamy Caesar dressing, croutons, and parmesan.
instructions: For dressing: mince anchovy and garlic into a paste. Whisk with egg yolk, lemon juice, Dijon, and Worcestershire.
Slowly drizzle in olive oil while whisking to emulsify into a creamy dressing.
Tear or chop romaine into bite-sized pieces, wash and dry thoroughly.
Toss lettuce with dressing, croutons, and shaved parmesan.
Grind fresh black pepper on top and serve immediately.
tips: Dry the lettuce well — wet leaves dilute the dressing.
Homemade croutons from day-old bread are far superior to store-bought.

name: Gazpacho
description: Chilled Spanish tomato soup blended with cucumber, pepper, and sherry vinegar.
instructions: Roughly chop tomatoes, cucumber, and bell pepper.
Soak bread in water briefly, squeeze out excess.
Blend all vegetables with bread, garlic, olive oil, vinegar, and salt until smooth.
Strain through a sieve for silky texture (optional), then chill for at least 1 hour.
Serve cold, drizzled with olive oil and garnished with diced vegetables.
tips: Use the ripest, most flavorful tomatoes available.
Gazpacho tastes best when thoroughly chilled for several hours.

name: Nicoise Salad
description: French composed salad with tuna, green beans, potatoes, eggs, and olives.
instructions: Boil potatoes until tender, about 15 minutes; boil eggs for 7 minutes for jammy yolks; blanch green beans for 3 minutes.
Whisk olive oil, mustard, vinegar, and salt into a vinaigrette.
Arrange lettuce on a platter, then compose halved potatoes, green beans, halved tomatoes, olives, and flaked tuna in sections.
Halve the eggs and place on top.
Drizzle vinaigrette over the salad just before serving.
tips: A Nicoise is composed, not tossed — each ingredient is arranged separately.
Use good quality canned tuna packed in olive oil for best results.

name: Bruschetta
description: Toasted Italian bread topped with diced tomatoes, garlic, basil, and olive oil.
instructions: Dice tomatoes, mix with chopped basil, olive oil, balsamic vinegar, and salt; let sit 10 minutes.
Toast or grill bread slices until golden and crisp.
Rub each toast with a cut garlic clove.
Spoon the tomato mixture over the garlic-rubbed toast and serve immediately.
tips: Let the tomato mixture marinate briefly for the flavors to meld.
Serve immediately after topping — the bread goes soggy quickly.

name: Focaccia Sandwich
description: Thick, herbed Italian focaccia bread filled with mozzarella, roasted vegetables, and pesto.
instructions: Slice focaccia in half horizontally.
Spread pesto on the bottom half.
Layer sliced mozzarella, roasted red peppers, sun-dried tomatoes, and arugula.
Drizzle with olive oil, season with salt and pepper, place top half.
Press gently, slice, and serve.
tips: Lightly toast the focaccia for extra crunch.
Press the sandwich with a weight for a few minutes for a panini-style result.

name: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio
description: Simple Italian pasta with garlic, olive oil, chili flakes, and parsley.
instructions: Cook spaghetti in salted boiling water until al dente; reserve 1 cup pasta water before draining.
While pasta cooks, thinly slice garlic and gently fry in olive oil over low heat until golden (not brown).
Add chili flakes, then add the drained pasta directly to the pan.
Toss with a splash of pasta water to create a silky sauce that coats the noodles.
Finish with parsley and parmesan; serve immediately.
tips: Slice garlic thin and cook on low heat — burnt garlic tastes bitter.
Pasta water is the secret — its starch helps create the sauce.

name: Penne Arrabbiata
description: Penne pasta in a fiery tomato sauce with garlic and red chili.
instructions: Cook penne in salted boiling water until al dente; drain.
Heat olive oil, add sliced garlic and chili flakes; cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
Add canned tomatoes, break them up, season with salt, and simmer for 15 minutes.
Toss the cooked penne in the sauce until well coated.
Serve with torn basil and grated parmesan.
tips: Arrabbiata means angry — adjust chili to your heat preference.
The sauce should be slightly chunky, not smooth.

name: Margherita Pizza
description: Classic Italian pizza with tomato sauce, fresh mozzarella, and basil on a thin crust.
instructions: Crush tomatoes by hand with garlic, salt, and a drizzle of olive oil for the sauce (do not cook).
Stretch pizza dough into a thin round on a floured surface.
Spread tomato sauce evenly, leaving a border for the crust.
Tear mozzarella and distribute over the pizza.
Bake at the highest oven temperature (250+ degrees Celsius) for 8-12 minutes until crust is charred and cheese is bubbly; add fresh basil after baking.
tips: The hottest possible oven gives the best results — use a pizza stone if available.
Less is more — do not overload with toppings.

name: Mushroom Risotto
description: Creamy Italian rice dish slowly cooked with mushrooms, wine, and parmesan.
instructions: Keep broth warm in a saucepan on low heat.
Heat olive oil, sauté mushrooms until golden; set aside. Add butter, cook diced onion and garlic until soft.
Add rice and toast for 2 minutes until edges are translucent. Add wine and stir until absorbed.
Add warm broth one ladle at a time, stirring frequently, allowing each addition to absorb before adding more (about 18-20 minutes total).
Stir in mushrooms, parmesan, and remaining butter; the risotto should be creamy and flow like lava.
tips: Constant stirring releases starch from the rice, creating the signature creaminess.
Risotto should flow when plated — it thickens as it rests.

name: Lasagna
description: Layered Italian pasta bake with meat ragu, bechamel sauce, and mozzarella.
instructions: Make meat ragu: brown ground beef in olive oil, add onion, garlic, tomatoes, tomato paste, Italian seasoning, and salt; simmer 20 minutes.
Make bechamel: melt butter, add flour, cook 1 minute, then slowly whisk in milk until thick and smooth.
Layer in a baking dish: ragu, lasagna sheets, bechamel, mozzarella. Repeat 3 times.
Top with remaining bechamel, mozzarella, and parmesan.
Bake at 180 degrees Celsius for 30-35 minutes until bubbly and golden; rest 10 minutes before serving.
tips: Let lasagna rest for 10 minutes after baking — it holds together much better when sliced.
No-boil lasagna sheets simplify the process significantly.

name: Pasta Carbonara
description: Classic Roman pasta with egg, pecorino, black pepper, and guanciale.
instructions: Cook spaghetti in generously salted water until al dente; reserve 1 cup pasta water.
While pasta cooks, cut guanciale into small strips and render in a pan until crispy and golden.
Whisk egg yolks, whole egg, grated pecorino, and black pepper in a bowl.
Add drained pasta to the guanciale pan (off heat), then quickly toss with the egg-cheese mixture.
Add splashes of pasta water and toss vigorously to create a creamy sauce — the residual heat gently cooks the eggs without scrambling.
tips: Take the pan off the heat before adding the egg mixture — too much heat scrambles the eggs.
Authentic carbonara has no cream — creaminess comes from eggs and cheese.

name: Grilled Salmon
description: Salmon fillet seasoned simply and grilled until crispy skin and flaky flesh.
instructions: Pat salmon fillets dry, rub with olive oil, minced garlic, salt, and pepper.
Heat a grill or grill pan to high heat.
Place salmon skin-side down, cook for 4-5 minutes without moving.
Flip carefully and cook another 3-4 minutes until just opaque in the center.
Serve with lemon wedges and fresh dill.
tips: Do not move the salmon once placed — this allows the skin to crisp properly.
Salmon should be slightly translucent in the center — it continues cooking off heat.

name: Chicken Parmesan
description: Breaded chicken cutlet topped with marinara sauce and melted mozzarella.
instructions: Pound chicken breasts to even thickness, season with salt and pepper.
Set up breading station: flour, beaten egg, then breadcrumbs mixed with parmesan.
Coat each chicken piece in flour, dip in egg, then press into breadcrumb mixture.
Pan-fry in olive oil for 3-4 minutes per side until golden; transfer to a baking dish.
Spoon marinara over each cutlet, top with mozzarella, and bake at 200 degrees Celsius for 10-12 minutes until cheese is bubbly.
tips: Pounding the chicken to even thickness ensures uniform cooking.
Let the fried chicken drain briefly before adding sauce.

name: Beef Stew
description: Hearty slow-cooked beef with potatoes, carrots, and a rich gravy.
instructions: Cut beef into 2-inch cubes, toss with flour, salt, and pepper.
Brown beef in batches in olive oil over high heat; set aside.
Sauté onion, celery, and garlic in the same pot until soft.
Add tomato paste, red wine, broth, thyme, and bay leaf; return beef, bring to a simmer.
Add potatoes and carrots, cover, and cook on low heat for 1.5-2 hours until beef is fork-tender.
tips: Brown the beef in batches without crowding the pan — this builds deep flavor.
Low and slow is the only way — do not rush the cooking.

name: Mushroom Stroganoff
description: Creamy mushroom sauce with sour cream and paprika, served over egg noodles.
instructions: Cook egg noodles according to package directions; drain.
Melt butter, sauté sliced onion until soft, add garlic and mushrooms; cook until mushrooms are golden.
Add paprika and mustard, stir for a minute.
Add broth, simmer for 5 minutes until slightly reduced.
Remove from heat, stir in sour cream, season with salt, and serve over noodles with parsley.
tips: Add sour cream off the heat to prevent curdling.
A mix of mushroom varieties adds more depth of flavor.

name: Garlic Bread
description: Crusty bread slathered with garlic butter and herbs, baked until golden.
instructions: Mix softened butter with minced garlic, chopped parsley, and salt.
Slice bread in half lengthwise.
Spread garlic butter generously on both halves.
Bake at 190 degrees Celsius for 8-10 minutes until edges are golden and butter is bubbling.
Slice and serve warm alongside pasta or soup.
tips: Wrap in foil and bake for soft garlic bread; leave open for crispy.
Freshly minced garlic is far superior to garlic powder.

name: Mashed Potatoes
description: Creamy buttery mashed potatoes — the ultimate comfort side dish.
instructions: Peel and cut potatoes into equal-sized chunks.
Boil in salted water until fork-tender, about 15-18 minutes; drain well.
Return potatoes to the pot, add butter, and mash until smooth.
Gradually add warm milk while mashing until desired consistency is reached.
Season with salt and white pepper; serve immediately.
tips: Use warm milk, not cold — cold milk makes potatoes gluey.
Do not over-mash or use a food processor — it activates starch and makes them gummy.

name: Roasted Vegetables
description: Assorted vegetables roasted with olive oil and herbs until caramelized and tender.
instructions: Preheat oven to 220 degrees Celsius.
Cut all vegetables into similar-sized pieces for even cooking.
Toss with olive oil, minced garlic, Italian seasoning, salt, and pepper.
Spread in a single layer on a baking sheet — do not overcrowd.
Roast for 25-30 minutes, tossing halfway, until edges are caramelized.
tips: Do not overcrowd the pan — vegetables steam instead of roast when too close together.
High heat and a single layer are the secrets to caramelization.

name: Coleslaw
description: Crunchy shredded cabbage and carrot salad in a creamy mayonnaise dressing.
instructions: Finely shred cabbage and grate carrot.
Whisk together mayonnaise, vinegar, sugar, salt, and pepper.
Toss the vegetables with the dressing until evenly coated.
Refrigerate for at least 30 minutes before serving for flavors to meld.
tips: A mandoline makes shredding fast and uniform.
Coleslaw tastes better after resting in the fridge for an hour.

name: Hummus
description: Smooth chickpea dip blended with tahini, lemon, and garlic.
instructions: Drain chickpeas and rinse; reserve a few for garnish.
Blend tahini and lemon juice together first for 1 minute until lightened.
Add chickpeas, garlic, cumin, and salt; blend until smooth.
With blender running, add ice water to reach a creamy, light consistency.
Serve drizzled with olive oil and topped with reserved chickpeas and paprika.
tips: Blending tahini and lemon first creates an ultra-smooth base.
Ice water makes the hummus lighter and fluffier than room-temperature water.

name: Avocado Toast
description: Mashed avocado on toasted sourdough with lemon, chili flakes, and a poached egg.
instructions: Toast bread until golden and crispy.
Halve avocado, scoop flesh, and mash with lemon juice, salt, and pepper.
Spread mashed avocado thickly on toast.
Poach an egg in simmering water with a splash of vinegar for 3 minutes; place on top.
Sprinkle chili flakes and serve immediately.
tips: Use a ripe avocado — it should give slightly when pressed.
A splash of vinegar in the poaching water helps the egg white hold together.

name: Overnight Oats
description: Rolled oats soaked overnight in milk with chia seeds, topped with fruit and honey.
instructions: Combine oats, milk, chia seeds, honey, and vanilla in a jar.
Stir well, cover, and refrigerate overnight (at least 6 hours).
In the morning, stir and top with sliced banana and blueberries.
Add a splash of milk if too thick.
tips: Use a mason jar for easy grab-and-go breakfast.
Any milk works — dairy, almond, or oat milk.

name: Smoothie Bowl
description: Thick blended fruit smoothie served in a bowl with granola and fresh toppings.
instructions: Blend frozen banana, frozen berries, yogurt, and milk until thick and smooth.
Pour into a bowl — consistency should be much thicker than a drinkable smoothie.
Top with granola, sliced banana, chia seeds, and a drizzle of honey.
Serve immediately before it melts.
tips: Use frozen fruit for thickness — fresh fruit makes it too thin.
Add less liquid for a thicker, scoopable consistency.

name: Egg & Cheese Sandwich
description: Fried egg and melted cheese on a toasted English muffin.
instructions: Toast the English muffin halves until golden.
Melt butter in a pan, fry the egg to your preference (over-easy recommended).
Place cheese on the hot egg so it starts melting.
Place the egg and cheese on the muffin bottom, top with the other half.
Serve immediately while cheese is still melty.
tips: Cover the pan briefly after adding cheese to help it melt faster.
Add a slice of ham or bacon for a heartier version.

name: Banana Pancakes
description: Fluffy pancakes with mashed banana folded into the batter for natural sweetness.
instructions: Mash banana in a bowl, add egg and milk; whisk together.
Mix flour, baking powder, and cinnamon; fold into wet ingredients until just combined.
Melt butter on a griddle over medium heat, pour 1/4 cup batter per pancake.
Cook until bubbles form on surface, flip, cook another 1-2 minutes.
Stack and serve with maple syrup and sliced banana.
tips: Use a very ripe banana for the sweetest flavor.
Do not over-mix — a few lumps are fine.

name: Grilled Chicken Sandwich
description: Juicy grilled chicken breast with lettuce, tomato, and mayo on a toasted bun.
instructions: Pound chicken breast to even thickness, season with salt and pepper.
Brush with olive oil and grill for 5-6 minutes per side until cooked through.
Toast the bun lightly.
Spread mayo on the bun, layer lettuce, chicken, and tomato.
Serve with a side of fries or salad.
tips: Let chicken rest 3 minutes after grilling before placing on the bun.
Pounding to even thickness ensures uniform cooking.

name: BLT Sandwich
description: Classic bacon, lettuce, and tomato sandwich with mayo on toasted bread.
instructions: Cook bacon in a pan until crispy; drain on paper towels.
Toast bread until golden.
Spread mayo on both slices of toast.
Layer lettuce, tomato, and crispy bacon on one slice.
Season tomato with salt and pepper, top with second slice, cut diagonally, and serve.
tips: Cook bacon until very crispy for the best texture contrast.
Use ripe tomatoes in season for the best BLT.

name: Chicken Caesar Wrap
description: Grilled chicken with romaine, parmesan, and Caesar dressing in a flour tortilla.
instructions: Slice grilled chicken into strips.
Chop romaine lettuce and toss with Caesar dressing and croutons.
Place the dressed salad on the tortilla, add chicken strips and shaved parmesan.
Fold the bottom up, then roll tightly from one side.
Cut in half diagonally and serve.
tips: Warm the tortilla slightly for easier rolling.
Wrap tightly in foil for a packed lunch version.

name: Tuna Salad
description: Flaked tuna mixed with mayo, celery, and lemon, served on a bed of greens.
instructions: Drain tuna and flake with a fork.
Mix with mayo, diced celery, minced red onion, lemon juice, mustard, salt, and pepper.
Serve on a bed of mixed greens, in a sandwich, or with crackers.
tips: Use good quality canned tuna for best results.
Add a touch of Dijon mustard for a flavor boost.

name: Veggie Burger
description: Hearty black bean and sweet potato patty with avocado on a toasted bun.
instructions: Bake sweet potato at 200C for 25 min until soft; scoop flesh.
Mash black beans, mix with sweet potato, minced onion, garlic, breadcrumbs, cumin, paprika, and salt.
Shape into 2 patties, refrigerate 15 minutes.
Pan-fry in oil for 4 minutes per side until crispy crust forms.
Serve on toasted bun with sliced avocado, lettuce, and tomato.
tips: The patty mixture must be thick enough to hold shape — add more breadcrumbs if too wet.
Chilling before frying is essential for structure.

name: Pulled Pork Sandwich
description: Slow-cooked shredded pork in tangy BBQ sauce on a soft bun with coleslaw.
instructions: Rub pork shoulder with paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, brown sugar, and salt.
Place in slow cooker, add vinegar and half the BBQ sauce; cook on low for 8 hours or high for 4 hours.
Shred pork with two forks, mix with remaining BBQ sauce and cooking juices.
Pile onto soft buns and top with coleslaw.
tips: Low and slow is key — the collagen breaks down and makes the pork incredibly tender.
Let the meat rest 10 minutes before shredding.

name: Quesadilla
description: Crispy flour tortilla filled with melted cheese, peppers, and onions.
instructions: Sauté diced bell pepper, onion, and jalapeño until soft.
Place one tortilla in a dry pan, sprinkle half the cheese, add the cooked vegetables, then remaining cheese and second tortilla.
Cook on medium heat for 2-3 minutes until bottom is golden and crispy.
Flip carefully and cook the other side until golden.
Cut into wedges and serve with sour cream and salsa.
tips: Low-medium heat gives the cheese time to melt before the tortilla burns.
Press down gently while cooking for even crisping.

name: Burrito Bowl
description: Deconstructed burrito with rice, beans, corn, salsa, and toppings in a bowl.
instructions: Warm black beans with cumin and salt in a small pot.
Mix rice with lime juice.
Assemble bowls: rice base, then beans, corn, shredded lettuce.
Top with sliced avocado, salsa, sour cream, and shredded cheese.
Squeeze extra lime and serve.
tips: Warm all components for a hot bowl, or keep cold for a salad-style version.
Add grilled chicken or tofu for extra protein.

name: Cobb Salad
description: Composed salad with chicken, bacon, egg, avocado, blue cheese, and tomato.
instructions: Chop romaine lettuce and spread on a large platter.
Arrange rows of sliced chicken, crumbled bacon, halved eggs, diced avocado, halved tomatoes, and crumbled blue cheese.
Drizzle vinaigrette over the salad just before serving.
Toss at the table or serve composed.
tips: A Cobb salad is traditionally composed in rows, not tossed.
Use crispy bacon — it adds essential crunch.

name: Turkey Club Sandwich
description: Triple-decker sandwich with turkey, bacon, lettuce, tomato, and mayo.
instructions: Toast all three bread slices until golden.
Spread mayo on one side of each slice.
Layer first slice with turkey and lettuce.
Add second slice, layer with bacon and tomato.
Top with third slice, secure with toothpicks, cut into triangles.
tips: The toothpicks hold the tall sandwich together while cutting.
Use thick-cut bread for a sturdier club.

name: Mac and Cheese
description: Elbow pasta in a rich, creamy cheddar cheese sauce baked until golden on top.
instructions: Cook macaroni until al dente; drain.
Melt butter, add flour, cook for 1 minute; slowly whisk in milk until smooth and thick.
Remove from heat, stir in most of the grated cheddar, mustard, salt, and pepper until melted.
Stir in cooked macaroni, pour into a baking dish, top with remaining cheese and breadcrumbs.
Bake at 190 degrees Celsius for 15-20 minutes until golden and bubbly.
tips: A touch of mustard powder enhances the cheese flavor without tasting like mustard.
Use sharp cheddar for the most flavorful sauce.

name: Grilled Chicken Breast
description: Simply seasoned chicken breast grilled until juicy with a charred exterior.
instructions: Pound chicken breasts to even thickness.
Mix olive oil, garlic powder, paprika, Italian seasoning, salt, pepper, and lemon juice; coat chicken.
Let marinate for 15 minutes.
Grill over high heat for 5-6 minutes per side until internal temperature reaches 74 degrees Celsius.
Rest for 5 minutes before slicing and serving.
tips: Pounding to even thickness is the most important step for juicy chicken.
Always let chicken rest after cooking — cutting immediately releases all the juices.

name: Steak with Vegetables
description: Pan-seared steak cooked to medium-rare, served with roasted seasonal vegetables.
instructions: Bring steaks to room temperature, pat dry, season generously with salt and pepper.
Heat a cast iron pan until smoking hot, add oil, sear steak 3-4 minutes per side for medium-rare.
Add butter, garlic, and rosemary; baste the steak with melted butter for 1 minute.
Rest steak for 5 minutes while you roast asparagus and tomatoes at 220C for 10 minutes.
Slice steak against the grain and serve with vegetables.
tips: A smoking hot pan and dry surface are essential for a good sear.
Use a meat thermometer — 52C for medium-rare, 57C for medium.

name: BBQ Ribs
description: Slow-cooked pork ribs glazed with smoky BBQ sauce until fall-off-the-bone tender.
instructions: Mix brown sugar, paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, cumin, salt, and pepper into a dry rub.
Remove membrane from back of ribs, apply rub generously on all sides.
Wrap tightly in foil, bake at 150 degrees Celsius for 2.5 hours.
Unwrap, brush with BBQ sauce, and bake uncovered at 200C for 15-20 minutes until sticky and caramelized.
Let rest 10 minutes, cut between bones, and serve.
tips: Removing the membrane on the back is essential for tender ribs.
Low and slow in foil first, then high heat for the glaze.

name: Meatloaf
description: Classic American ground beef loaf baked with a sweet ketchup glaze.
instructions: Mix ground beef, breadcrumbs, diced onion, egg, milk, half the ketchup, Worcestershire, garlic powder, salt, and pepper.
Shape into a loaf on a lined baking sheet.
Spread remaining ketchup on top as a glaze.
Bake at 180 degrees Celsius for 45-55 minutes until internal temperature reaches 71C.
Rest for 10 minutes before slicing.
tips: Do not over-mix the meat or it becomes dense and tough.
A free-form loaf on a sheet pan browns better than one in a loaf pan.

name: Chicken Pot Pie
description: Creamy chicken and vegetable filling baked under a flaky golden pastry crust.
instructions: Poach chicken in broth until cooked, shred, and reserve broth.
Dice potato and carrot, boil until just tender.
Melt butter, add onion, cook until soft; add flour and stir 1 minute; slowly add reserved broth and milk.
Add chicken, potatoes, carrots, and peas to the sauce; pour into a baking dish.
Top with puff pastry, brush with egg wash, and bake at 200C for 25-30 minutes until golden.
tips: Vent the pastry with small cuts to let steam escape.
The filling should be thick enough to hold its shape when served.

name: Chili Con Carne
description: Spiced ground beef and bean stew with tomatoes, peppers, and warm spices.
instructions: Heat oil, add diced onion and bell pepper; sauté until soft.
Add garlic and ground beef; break up and brown.
Add chili powder, cumin, and paprika; cook 1 minute.
Add canned tomatoes and drained kidney beans; bring to a simmer.
Cook uncovered for 30 minutes until thick. Serve with rice, sour cream, and shredded cheese.
tips: Chili improves significantly after a day — make ahead when possible.
A small square of dark chocolate adds surprising depth to the chili.

name: Fish Tacos
description: Crispy battered fish in warm tortillas with cabbage slaw and chipotle mayo.
instructions: Mix flour, cornstarch, and salt; whisk in beer until smooth batter forms.
Cut fish into strips, dip in batter, and fry in hot oil until golden and crispy.
Shred cabbage finely, toss with lime juice and salt.
Mix mayo with chipotle sauce for the drizzle.
Warm tortillas, fill with fish, cabbage slaw, chipotle mayo, and a squeeze of lime.
tips: Use a firm white fish like cod or mahi-mahi that holds up to frying.
Double-layer the corn tortillas to prevent tearing.

name: Shrimp Scampi
description: Sautéed shrimp in a garlic butter and white wine sauce over linguine.
instructions: Cook linguine until al dente; reserve 1 cup pasta water.
Heat olive oil and half the butter; sauté minced garlic and chili flakes for 30 seconds.
Add shrimp in a single layer, cook 2 minutes per side until pink.
Add wine and lemon juice, simmer 2 minutes, then add remaining butter.
Toss with pasta and a splash of pasta water; garnish with parsley.
tips: Do not overcook shrimp — they turn rubbery in seconds past being done.
The wine sauce should be silky — pasta water helps emulsify it.

name: Pot Roast
description: Slow-braised beef chuck roast with root vegetables in a rich gravy.
instructions: Season beef with salt and pepper; sear on all sides in a hot Dutch oven with oil until deeply browned.
Remove beef, add onion and garlic; sauté until soft. Add tomato paste and cook 1 minute.
Return beef, add broth and thyme; bring to a simmer.
Cover and braise in oven at 160C for 2 hours.
Add potatoes and carrots, cover, and cook another 45-60 minutes until vegetables are tender and beef falls apart.
tips: Searing the meat deeply on all sides is what gives the gravy its rich flavor.
Do not open the oven frequently — trapped steam is doing the cooking.

name: Cornbread
description: Golden, slightly sweet cornbread baked in a cast iron skillet.
instructions: Preheat oven to 200C with a cast iron skillet inside.
Mix cornmeal, flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt.
Whisk buttermilk, egg, and most of the melted butter; combine with dry ingredients.
Brush hot skillet with remaining butter, pour batter in — it should sizzle.
Bake 18-22 minutes until golden and a toothpick comes out clean.
tips: Preheating the skillet gives the cornbread a crispy, golden crust.
Do not over-mix — stir just until combined.

name: Baked Beans
description: Slow-cooked navy beans in a sweet and smoky tomato-molasses sauce.
instructions: Preheat oven to 175C.
Mix drained beans with ketchup, molasses, mustard, Worcestershire, paprika, diced onion, and salt.
Pour into a baking dish, cover with foil.
Bake for 45 minutes, remove foil, bake 15 more minutes until bubbly and thickened.
Serve as a side with BBQ meats or sandwiches.
tips: The sauce thickens significantly as it bakes.
Add diced bacon for a traditional smoky variation.

name: Sweet Potato Fries
description: Crispy baked sweet potato fries seasoned with paprika and garlic.
instructions: Preheat oven to 220C.
Cut sweet potatoes into even sticks about 1cm thick.
Toss with olive oil, cornstarch, paprika, garlic powder, and salt.
Spread in a single layer on a parchment-lined baking sheet — do not overcrowd.
Bake 25-30 minutes, flipping halfway, until crispy and golden.
tips: Cornstarch is the secret to crispy baked fries.
A single layer without crowding is essential — use two trays if needed.

name: Garden Salad
description: Fresh mixed greens with cucumber, tomato, and a light vinaigrette.
instructions: Wash and dry mixed greens; place in a bowl.
Slice cucumber, halve tomatoes, thinly slice red onion; add to greens.
Whisk olive oil, vinegar, salt, and pepper into a quick vinaigrette.
Toss salad with dressing just before serving.
tips: Dry greens thoroughly — wet leaves dilute the dressing.
Dress the salad at the last moment to keep it crisp.

name: Buffalo Wings
description: Crispy oven-baked chicken wings tossed in a tangy hot sauce and butter glaze, served with celery and blue cheese dip.
instructions: Pat wings completely dry with paper towels — this is essential for crispiness.
Toss wings with baking powder, salt, pepper, and garlic powder.
Arrange in a single layer on a wire rack over a baking sheet.
Bake at 220 degrees Celsius for 40-45 minutes, flipping halfway, until skin is golden and crispy.
Melt butter, mix with hot sauce, and toss the baked wings in the sauce. Serve with celery sticks and blue cheese dressing.
tips: Baking powder draws moisture out of the skin, making oven-baked wings as crispy as fried ones.
Pat wings bone-dry before seasoning — any moisture prevents crisping.

name: Kung Pao Chicken
description: Spicy Sichuan stir-fry of diced chicken with peanuts, chillies, and a tangy soy-vinegar sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into 2 cm cubes, toss with 1 tbsp soy sauce and 1 tsp cornstarch; marinate 15 minutes.
Mix remaining soy sauce, dark soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, remaining cornstarch, and 2 tbsp water into a sauce.
Heat oil in a wok until smoking, stir-fry chicken until golden and cooked through; set aside.
In the same wok, toast Sichuan peppercorns and dried chillies until fragrant.
Add garlic and ginger, stir-fry 30 seconds, then return the chicken and pour in the sauce.
Toss until sauce thickens and coats the chicken, add peanuts and spring onions, drizzle sesame oil and serve.
tips: Toast the Sichuan peppercorns on low heat to avoid burning them.
Chinkiang vinegar is essential — regular rice vinegar lacks the deep flavour.

name: Mapo Tofu
description: Silken tofu braised in a fiery Sichuan sauce of doubanjiang, ground pork, and Sichuan peppercorns.
instructions: Cut tofu into 2 cm cubes and blanch in salted boiling water for 2 minutes; drain gently.
Heat oil in a wok, brown the ground pork until crispy.
Push pork aside, add doubanjiang and fermented black beans; fry until oil turns red.
Add garlic and ginger, stir-fry 30 seconds, then pour in stock and soy sauce.
Gently slide in the tofu, simmer for 5 minutes without stirring.
Mix cornstarch with 2 tbsp water, drizzle into the wok to thicken; sprinkle Sichuan peppercorn powder and spring onions.
tips: Blanching the tofu in salt water firms it up so it doesn't crumble.
Stir gently or just swirl the wok — silken tofu breaks easily.

name: Chinese Fried Rice
description: Wok-tossed day-old rice with egg, vegetables, and soy sauce for smoky wok hei flavour.
instructions: Beat eggs with a pinch of salt; scramble in a hot wok with 1 tbsp oil until just set, break into pieces and set aside.
Heat remaining oil until smoking, add diced carrot and peas; stir-fry 2 minutes.
Add garlic, stir 30 seconds, then add rice and press flat against the wok to sear.
Toss rice for 3-4 minutes until grains separate and start to crisp.
Return eggs, add soy sauce, white pepper, and sesame oil; toss well and finish with spring onions.
tips: Day-old refrigerated rice is essential — fresh rice turns mushy.
High heat and small batches give the best wok hei.

name: Dan Dan Noodles
description: Sichuan street-food noodles in a spicy, nutty sesame sauce topped with crispy pork mince.
instructions: Mix sesame paste, chilli oil, soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and Sichuan peppercorn powder with 3 tbsp hot water in each serving bowl.
Cook noodles according to packet instructions; drain, reserving some cooking water.
Heat oil in a wok, brown pork mince until crispy; add garlic and ya cai, fry 1 minute.
Divide noodles among bowls with the sauce, top with crispy pork mixture.
Garnish with crushed peanuts and sliced spring onions; toss before eating.
tips: The sauce should be made in the bowl before the noodles go in.
Use a splash of noodle water to loosen the sauce if it is too thick.

name: Sweet & Sour Pork
description: Crispy battered pork pieces tossed in a vibrant sweet and sour sauce with pineapple and bell peppers.
instructions: Cut pork into 3 cm cubes, season with salt, coat in beaten egg, then dredge in cornstarch.
Deep-fry pork in oil at 180C until golden and crispy; drain on paper towels.
Mix ketchup, vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, and 3 tbsp water in a small pan; heat until simmering.
In a clean wok, stir-fry bell peppers and pineapple for 2 minutes.
Add the sauce and fried pork, toss quickly to coat evenly, and serve immediately.
tips: Double-fry the pork for extra crunch — fry once, rest 5 minutes, fry again.
Toss the pork in the sauce at the last second to keep the coating crispy.

name: Steamed Dumplings
description: Juicy pork and cabbage dumplings wrapped in thin dough and steamed until translucent.
instructions: Mix flour with boiling water, knead into a smooth dough, cover, and rest 30 minutes.
Finely chop cabbage, salt it, let sit 10 minutes, then squeeze out excess water.
Mix pork, cabbage, ginger, garlic, soy sauce, sesame oil, spring onion, and white pepper.
Divide dough into 20 pieces, roll each into a thin round wrapper.
Place a spoonful of filling in each wrapper, pleat the edges to seal, and steam in a lined bamboo steamer for 12 minutes.
tips: Squeeze the cabbage very dry or the dumplings will be soggy inside.
Brush the steamer liner with oil to prevent sticking.

name: Hot and Sour Soup
description: Silky egg-drop soup with tofu, mushrooms, and a bold pepper-vinegar broth.
instructions: Soak dried mushrooms in hot water for 15 minutes; slice thinly.
Bring stock to a boil, add sliced mushrooms, julienned bamboo shoots, and tofu strips.
Add soy sauce, vinegar, and white pepper; simmer 5 minutes.
Mix cornstarch with 3 tbsp cold water, stir into the soup to thicken.
Beat the egg and drizzle slowly into the simmering soup while stirring; finish with chilli oil, sesame oil, and spring onion.
tips: Pour the egg in a thin stream while stirring gently for silky ribbons.
Adjust vinegar and white pepper to taste — the soup should be equally hot and sour.

name: Stir-Fry Vegetables
description: Crisp-tender mixed vegetables tossed in a savoury garlic-ginger sauce over high heat.
instructions: Cut all vegetables into similar-sized pieces for even cooking.
Mix soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and cornstarch with 2 tbsp water for the sauce.
Heat vegetable oil in a wok until smoking; add broccoli and carrot first, stir-fry 2 minutes.
Add bell pepper, snap peas, and baby corn; toss for 1 minute.
Add garlic and ginger, cook 30 seconds, pour in the sauce, and toss until everything is glossy and coated.
tips: The wok must be smoking hot — vegetables should sizzle loudly on contact.
Do not overcrowd the wok; cook in batches if needed.

name: Miso Soup
description: Light Japanese broth of dashi, white miso, tofu, and wakame seaweed.
instructions: Soak dried wakame in cold water for 5 minutes; drain.
Heat dashi stock until simmering — do not boil.
Cut tofu into 1 cm cubes, add to the stock along with wakame.
Remove from heat, dissolve miso paste in a ladle of warm dashi, then stir back into the pot.
Serve in bowls, garnished with sliced spring onion.
tips: Never boil miso — high heat kills the live cultures and makes it bitter.
Use a combination of white and red miso for more depth.

name: Teriyaki Salmon
description: Pan-seared salmon fillets glazed with a glossy homemade teriyaki sauce.
instructions: Mix soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar in a small bowl.
Pat salmon dry and season lightly with salt.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan, sear salmon skin-side down for 4 minutes until crispy.
Flip, pour teriyaki sauce into the pan, and cook 3 minutes, basting the fish with the sauce.
Serve over steamed rice, drizzle with the reduced sauce and sprinkle sesame seeds.
tips: Start with the skin side down on a cold pan for the crispiest skin.
Reduce the sauce until it coats the back of a spoon.

name: Chicken Katsu
description: Crispy panko-breaded chicken cutlet served with tonkatsu sauce and shredded cabbage.
instructions: Butterfly each chicken breast and pound to 1 cm thickness; season with salt and pepper.
Set up a breading station: flour, beaten eggs, and panko in separate dishes.
Coat chicken in flour, dip in egg, then press firmly into panko.
Shallow-fry in oil at 170C for 4-5 minutes per side until deep golden.
Rest on a wire rack for 2 minutes, slice into strips, and serve over rice with tonkatsu sauce and shredded cabbage.
tips: Press the panko onto the chicken firmly for an even, crispy crust.
Frying at too high a temperature burns the panko before the chicken cooks through.

name: Onigiri
description: Japanese rice balls shaped by hand and filled with salted salmon or umeboshi plum.
instructions: Cook sushi rice according to packet instructions; let cool until warm enough to handle.
Wet your hands and rub with salt to prevent sticking.
Take a handful of rice, flatten slightly, place a spoonful of salmon in the centre.
Mould into a triangular shape, pressing gently but firmly so the rice holds together.
Wrap the base with a strip of nori and sprinkle with sesame seeds.
tips: The rice must be warm but not hot — too cool and it won't stick, too hot and you'll burn yourself.
Press firmly enough to hold shape but not so hard the rice becomes dense.

name: Udon Soup
description: Thick, chewy udon noodles in a rich dashi and soy broth with soft-boiled egg and tempura bits.
instructions: Bring dashi stock to a simmer, add soy sauce, mirin, and salt; keep warm.
Cook udon noodles according to packet instructions; drain and rinse briefly under cold water.
Divide noodles into bowls, ladle the hot broth over them.
Top each bowl with a halved soft-boiled egg, tempura flakes, sliced spring onion, and a strip of nori.
tips: Rinsing the noodles removes excess starch and keeps them chewy.
For the best soft-boiled egg, cook for exactly 6.5 minutes, then ice bath immediately.

name: Gyoza
description: Pan-fried Japanese dumplings with a pork and cabbage filling, crispy on the bottom and steamed on top.
instructions: Finely chop cabbage, salt, and squeeze out all water; mix with pork, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, and spring onion.
Place a teaspoon of filling in the centre of each wrapper, wet the edges, and fold into a half-moon, crimping one side.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan, place gyoza flat-side down in a single layer.
Fry for 2 minutes until bottoms are golden, then add 60 ml water and immediately cover with a lid.
Steam for 5 minutes until water evaporates and the bottoms crisp up again; serve with a dip of rice vinegar, soy sauce, and chilli oil.
tips: The steam-then-fry technique is what gives gyoza their signature crispy bottom and tender top.
Make sure the pan is fully dry before the final crisp — the sizzling sound tells you.

name: Tempura Vegetables
description: Lightly battered seasonal vegetables deep-fried to a shatteringly crisp, golden coating.
instructions: Mix dashi, soy sauce, and mirin to make a tentsuyu dipping sauce; set aside with grated daikon.
Slice vegetables into thin, even pieces and pat completely dry.
Whisk flour, cornstarch, egg yolk, and ice-cold sparkling water very briefly — lumps are fine.
Heat oil to 180C, dip vegetables in batter, and fry in small batches for 2-3 minutes until pale gold.
Drain on a wire rack and serve immediately with tentsuyu dipping sauce.
tips: The batter must stay ice cold — place the bowl over ice while frying.
Under-mix the batter on purpose — lumps create a lighter, crispier coating.

name: Bibimbap
description: Korean mixed rice bowl topped with seasoned vegetables, gochujang sauce, a fried egg, and optional beef.
instructions: Marinate sliced beef in soy sauce, garlic, and 1 tsp sesame oil for 15 minutes.
Blanch spinach and bean sprouts separately; squeeze dry and season each with sesame oil and salt.
Julienne carrot and zucchini, sauté separately until just tender; slice and cook mushrooms.
Fry beef in a hot pan until browned; fry eggs sunny-side up.
Arrange rice in bowls, arrange each vegetable and the beef in neat sections on top, place the fried egg in the centre, and serve with gochujang and sesame seeds.
tips: The beauty of bibimbap is mixing it all together aggressively just before eating.
For dolsot bibimbap, serve in a searing-hot stone bowl for a crispy rice crust.

name: Kimchi Fried Rice
description: Spicy, tangy fried rice made with aged kimchi, gochujang, and topped with a runny fried egg.
instructions: Roughly chop the kimchi; reserve any kimchi juice.
Heat vegetable oil in a wok, stir-fry the kimchi for 2 minutes until slightly caramelised.
Add rice, gochujang, soy sauce, sugar, and a splash of kimchi juice; toss over high heat for 3 minutes.
Push the rice aside, fry eggs sunny-side up in the same pan.
Plate the rice, top with the fried egg, drizzle with sesame oil, and garnish with spring onion and sesame seeds.
tips: Aged, sour kimchi works far better than fresh kimchi for this dish.
Pressing the rice flat against the wok creates delicious crispy bits.

name: Bulgogi
description: Thinly sliced beef marinated in a sweet soy-pear sauce and grilled to caramelised perfection.
instructions: Blend pear, half the onion, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, and pepper into a smooth marinade.
Thinly slice beef against the grain, toss in the marinade, and refrigerate for at least 1 hour.
Slice remaining onion and spring onions.
Grill or pan-fry the marinated beef over very high heat for 2-3 minutes per side until caramelised.
Serve with steamed rice, lettuce wraps, and sesame seeds.
tips: Freeze the beef for 30 minutes before slicing — it makes thin cuts much easier.
The pear is a natural tenderiser and adds subtle sweetness.

name: Japchae
description: Korean glass noodles stir-fried with colourful vegetables, beef, and a sweet soy-sesame sauce.
instructions: Cook noodles in boiling water for 6 minutes; drain, rinse, and cut with scissors to manageable lengths.
Blanch spinach, squeeze dry, and season with sesame oil and salt.
Julienne carrot, bell pepper, and onion; slice mushrooms and beef thinly.
Stir-fry each vegetable and the beef separately with a little oil; season lightly.
Toss noodles with all the cooked ingredients, soy sauce, sugar, and sesame oil in a large bowl; garnish with sesame seeds.
tips: Cooking each component separately ensures every element is perfectly seasoned.
Japchae is traditionally served at room temperature and tastes great the next day.

name: Tteokbokki
description: Chewy Korean rice cakes simmered in a spicy-sweet gochujang sauce with fish cakes.
instructions: If using frozen rice cakes, soak in room-temperature water for 20 minutes.
Bring anchovy-kelp stock to a boil, whisk in gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, and garlic.
Add rice cakes, simmer for 8-10 minutes until the cakes are soft and the sauce is thick.
Add sliced fish cakes and hard-boiled eggs; simmer 3 more minutes.
Garnish with sliced spring onion and serve piping hot.
tips: Keep stirring occasionally or the rice cakes will stick to the bottom.
The sauce thickens significantly as it cools — serve immediately for the best texture.

name: Korean Fried Chicken
description: Double-fried chicken wings coated in a sticky, spicy gochujang glaze.
instructions: Pat wings dry, toss in a mix of cornstarch, rice flour, and a pinch of salt.
First fry: cook wings in 170C oil for 10 minutes until just cooked through; drain and rest 10 minutes.
Second fry: fry again at 190C for 3-4 minutes until deeply golden and shatteringly crispy.
While frying, simmer gochujang, soy sauce, honey, vinegar, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil until thick and glossy.
Toss the hot wings in the sauce; garnish with sesame seeds and spring onion.
tips: The double-fry technique is non-negotiable — it's what makes the coating stay crispy under the sauce.
Rest the wings at least 10 minutes between fries for the best result.

name: Pad Thai
description: Stir-fried rice noodles with shrimp, egg, bean sprouts, and a tangy tamarind sauce.
instructions: Soak rice noodles in room-temperature water for 30 minutes until pliable; drain.
Mix tamarind paste, fish sauce, and palm sugar with 2 tbsp water for the sauce.
Heat oil in a wok, sear shrimp and tofu cubes until golden; push to one side.
Crack eggs into the wok, scramble briefly, then add noodles and the sauce.
Toss for 2-3 minutes until noodles absorb the sauce; add bean sprouts and chives, toss once, and plate with crushed peanuts, lime wedge, and chilli flakes.
tips: Do not over-soak the noodles — they should be pliable but still firm, as they finish cooking in the wok.
Work in small batches and at high heat for the best flavour.

name: Thai Green Curry
description: Aromatic coconut milk curry with green curry paste, Thai basil, bamboo shoots, and chicken.
instructions: Heat oil in a pot, fry green curry paste for 1 minute until fragrant.
Add the thick cream from the top of the coconut milk; stir and cook until the oil separates.
Add sliced chicken, stir until coated and sealed on the outside.
Pour in the remaining coconut milk, bamboo shoots, eggplant, kaffir lime leaves, fish sauce, and sugar; simmer 15 minutes.
Stir in Thai basil leaves at the last moment and serve over jasmine rice.
tips: Frying the paste in the coconut cream before adding liquid unlocks the deepest flavour.
Tear the kaffir lime leaves to release their citrus aroma.

name: Tom Yum Soup
description: Hot and sour Thai soup with shrimp, lemongrass, galangal, and fresh lime juice.
instructions: Bruise lemongrass, slice galangal, tear kaffir lime leaves, and crush chillies.
Bring water to a boil with the aromatics; simmer 5 minutes.
Add halved mushrooms and tomatoes; cook 3 minutes.
Add shrimp and cook until just pink, about 2-3 minutes.
Remove from heat, stir in fish sauce, lime juice, nam prik pao, and coconut milk; garnish with coriander.
tips: Add lime juice off the heat — boiling it makes it bitter.
The aromatics are not eaten — push them aside when serving.

name: Massaman Curry
description: Rich, warming Thai curry with tender beef, potatoes, peanuts, and fragrant whole spices.
instructions: Cut beef into large cubes, peel and quarter potatoes, quarter the onion.
Heat oil, fry curry paste 1 minute, then add the thick coconut cream and stir until oil separates.
Add beef, seal on all sides, then pour in remaining coconut milk, cinnamon, cardamom, and bay leaves.
Simmer covered for 40 minutes, then add potatoes and onion; cook 20 more minutes until everything is tender.
Stir in tamarind, palm sugar, fish sauce, and peanuts; adjust seasoning and serve over rice.
tips: Massaman is a low-and-slow curry — do not rush it on high heat.
The balance between sweet tamarind and salty fish sauce is the key to getting the flavour right.

name: Thai Basil Stir Fry
description: Quick and fiery stir-fry of minced chicken with holy basil, chillies, and garlic over rice.
instructions: Pound garlic and chillies roughly in a mortar and pestle.
Heat oil in a wok until smoking, add garlic-chilli mixture and fry 30 seconds.
Add ground chicken, break apart and stir-fry on high heat until cooked and slightly crispy.
Add soy sauce, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar; toss well.
Remove from heat, fold in basil leaves until wilted, and serve over rice with a fried egg on top.
tips: Holy basil has a peppery flavour that is more authentic, but Thai basil is a fine substitute.
The basil must go in off the heat — it should wilt, not cook.

name: Pho
description: Vietnamese aromatic beef noodle soup with rice noodles, star anise-scented broth, and fresh herbs.
instructions: Char onion and ginger under a broiler until blackened on all sides.
Boil beef bones for 5 minutes, drain, rinse, and start fresh with clean water.
Add charred onion, ginger, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, fish sauce, and sugar; simmer for 1.5 hours, skimming regularly.
Cook rice noodles according to packet; divide into bowls.
Strain the broth, ladle piping hot over noodles and raw beef slices (the broth cooks the beef); serve with bean sprouts, basil, lime, chillies, hoisin, and Sriracha.
tips: The broth must be boiling hot when poured over the raw beef to cook it properly.
Charring the onion and ginger adds deep sweetness to the broth.

name: Banh Mi
description: Vietnamese crusty baguette filled with pate, cold cuts, pickled daikon and carrot, and fresh herbs.
instructions: Julienne daikon and carrot, toss with vinegar, sugar, and salt; pickle for at least 20 minutes.
Slice baguettes lengthwise, lightly toast until crisp outside and soft inside.
Spread pate on one side and mayonnaise mixed with soy sauce on the other.
Layer cold cuts, pickled vegetables, sliced cucumber, jalapeno, and fresh coriander.
Press closed gently and serve immediately.
tips: The bread must have a crispy crust and airy interior — this is what defines a great banh mi.
Quick-pickled vegetables need at least 20 minutes to develop the right tang.

name: Vietnamese Spring Rolls
description: Fresh rice paper rolls filled with shrimp, vermicelli, herbs, and lettuce, served with peanut dipping sauce.
instructions: Cook rice vermicelli according to packet; drain, rinse, and set aside.
Halve shrimp lengthwise, julienne cucumber, and prepare herbs.
Dip one rice paper in warm water for 5 seconds until just pliable — do not over-soak.
Lay lettuce, vermicelli, cucumber, herbs, and shrimp on the wrapper; fold the sides in and roll tightly.
Mix peanut butter, hoisin, Sriracha, lime juice, and warm water into a dipping sauce; top with crushed peanuts.
tips: Dip the rice paper for only a few seconds — it continues to soften after removal.
Roll as tightly as possible without tearing for the neatest presentation.

name: Bun Cha
description: Hanoi-style grilled pork patties and belly served over rice vermicelli with a tangy dipping broth.
instructions: Mix ground pork with 1 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp sugar, minced garlic, and minced shallot; shape into small patties.
Marinate pork belly slices in 1 tbsp fish sauce and 1 tbsp sugar for 15 minutes.
Grill patties and pork belly over charcoal or a grill pan until charred and cooked through.
Make the dipping broth: dissolve remaining sugar in warm water, add fish sauce, vinegar, lime juice, sliced chillies, and sliced shallot.
Cook vermicelli, drain, and serve alongside the grilled meats, dipping broth, lettuce, and fresh herbs.
tips: The dipping broth should have a perfect balance of sweet, sour, salty, and spicy.
Charcoal grilling gives the most authentic smoky flavour.

name: Falafel
description: Crispy deep-fried chickpea fritters seasoned with herbs, cumin, and coriander.
instructions: Drain soaked chickpeas (do not use canned) and pulse in a food processor with onion, garlic, and herbs until coarse but holds together.
Mix in cumin, coriander, salt, pepper, and baking powder.
Refrigerate mixture for 30 minutes, then shape into small patties or balls.
Deep fry in 180C oil for 3-4 minutes until deep golden and crispy.
Serve in pita with tahini sauce, pickles, and fresh salad.
tips: Never use canned chickpeas — they are too wet and falafel will fall apart.
Soaking dried chickpeas overnight is essential for the right texture.

name: Chicken Shawarma
description: Warmly spiced chicken thighs roasted with cumin, paprika, and turmeric, sliced thin.
instructions: Combine yogurt, all spices, garlic, lemon juice, olive oil, and salt into a marinade.
Coat chicken thighs thoroughly, marinate for at least 1 hour (overnight is best).
Bake at 220C for 20-25 minutes, or grill until charred on edges and cooked through.
Let rest 5 minutes, then slice thin against the grain.
Serve in warm pita with pickled turnips, tahini sauce, and fresh salad.
tips: Chicken thighs stay juicier than breast for shawarma.
Broiling for the last 2-3 minutes gives the signature charred edges.

name: Tabbouleh
description: Fresh Lebanese salad of finely chopped parsley, bulgur wheat, tomato, and lemon.
instructions: Soak bulgur in hot water for 15 minutes until soft; drain and squeeze out excess water.
Finely chop parsley, mint, tomatoes, and spring onion.
Combine bulgur with the chopped herbs and vegetables.
Dress with lemon juice, olive oil, salt, and pepper; toss well.
Serve at room temperature — tabbouleh should be predominantly green with parsley.
tips: Parsley is the star, not the bulgur — use far more parsley than grain.
Squeeze excess water from the bulgur to prevent a soggy salad.

name: Baba Ganoush
description: Smoky roasted eggplant dip blended with tahini, lemon, and garlic.
instructions: Roast whole eggplants directly over a gas flame or under a broiler until skin is charred and flesh is collapsed.
Peel off charred skin, drain the flesh for 10 minutes to remove excess liquid.
Mash or pulse eggplant flesh with tahini, lemon juice, garlic, and salt.
Drizzle with olive oil, sprinkle paprika and parsley.
Serve with warm pita bread or raw vegetables.
tips: Charring over an open flame gives the signature smoky flavor.
Draining the eggplant prevents a watery dip.

name: Middle Eastern Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in a rich tomato and bell pepper sauce seasoned with cumin and harissa.
instructions: Heat olive oil, sauté diced onion and bell pepper until soft.
Add garlic, cumin, paprika, and harissa; cook for 1 minute.
Add canned tomatoes, season with salt, and simmer for 10 minutes until thick.
Make wells, crack eggs into the sauce, cover, and cook 5-7 minutes until whites are set.
Crumble feta over top, garnish with parsley, serve with crusty bread.
tips: The sauce should be thick before adding eggs — too thin and eggs sink to the bottom.
Cover the pan to cook egg whites from the top as well.

name: Lamb Kofta
description: Spiced ground lamb shaped onto skewers and grilled until charred and juicy.
instructions: Grate onion, squeeze out excess moisture.
Mix lamb with grated onion, garlic, parsley, all spices, salt, and pepper; knead for 2 minutes.
Shape mixture onto flat skewers in long oval shapes.
Grill over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side until charred but still juicy inside.
Serve with pita, hummus, tahini sauce, and fresh salad.
tips: Kneading the meat mixture well binds it so it holds on the skewer.
Squeezing moisture from the onion prevents the kofta from falling apart.

name: Fattoush
description: Levantine bread salad with crispy pita chips, vegetables, and a tangy sumac dressing.
instructions: Tear or cut pita into pieces, toast or fry until crispy and golden.
Chop lettuce, cucumber, tomatoes, radishes, spring onion, mint, and parsley.
Whisk olive oil, lemon juice, sumac, pomegranate molasses, and salt into a dressing.
Toss vegetables with dressing, then add crispy pita just before serving.
Serve immediately — the pita should be crunchy, not soggy.
tips: Add pita at the very last moment to maintain crunch.
Sumac provides the signature tangy, lemony flavor.

name: Mujaddara
description: Middle Eastern comfort dish of lentils and rice topped with crispy caramelized onions.
instructions: Cook lentils in water for 15 minutes until half-tender.
Add washed rice, cumin, cinnamon, and salt to the lentils; cook on low heat for 15 minutes until both are tender.
While rice cooks, thinly slice all onions and fry in olive oil on medium-low heat for 20-25 minutes until deeply caramelized and crispy.
Fluff the rice and lentils with a fork.
Serve topped generously with crispy fried onions and a side of yogurt.
tips: The crispy onions are the soul of this dish — do not rush them.
Cook onions on medium-low heat for deep caramelization, not burning.

name: Labneh
description: Thick strained yogurt served as a creamy dip with olive oil and za'atar.
instructions: Mix yogurt with salt.
Place in a cheesecloth-lined strainer over a bowl; refrigerate for 12-24 hours to drain whey.
Transfer thick labneh to a plate, spread into a swirl.
Drizzle generously with olive oil, sprinkle za'atar and mint.
Serve with warm pita bread or fresh vegetables.
tips: The longer you strain, the thicker the labneh — 24 hours gives a cream cheese consistency.
Full-fat yogurt is essential; low-fat versions produce a grainy texture.

name: Lamb Shawarma Wrap
description: Thin-sliced spiced lamb wrapped in flatbread with pickles, tahini, and fresh vegetables.
instructions: Slice lamb thin, marinate in yogurt, all spices, garlic, and salt for at least 2 hours.
Cook lamb in a hot pan or grill in batches until charred and cooked through.
Warm flatbreads on a hot pan for 30 seconds each side.
Layer lamb, lettuce, sliced tomato, and pickled turnips on the flatbread.
Drizzle with tahini, roll tightly, and serve.
tips: Marinating overnight yields the most flavorful shawarma.
Cook lamb in small batches on very high heat for proper charring.

name: Muhammara
description: Syrian roasted red pepper and walnut dip with pomegranate molasses and cumin.
instructions: Toast walnuts in a dry pan for 3-4 minutes until fragrant.
Pulse roasted red peppers, walnuts, and breadcrumbs in a food processor until coarse.
Add pomegranate molasses, cumin, chili flakes, lemon juice, and salt; pulse to combine.
Drizzle in olive oil while pulsing until desired consistency.
Serve drizzled with more olive oil and a sprinkle of walnuts.
tips: The texture should be coarse, not smooth like hummus.
Pomegranate molasses gives the distinctive sweet-tart flavor.

name: Chicken Kabob
description: Juicy marinated chicken pieces threaded onto skewers and grilled over charcoal.
instructions: Bloom saffron in 1 tbsp warm water for 10 minutes.
Mix yogurt, lemon juice, olive oil, garlic, grated onion, saffron water, and all spices.
Cut chicken into 2-inch cubes, coat in marinade, refrigerate for at least 2 hours.
Thread onto skewers and grill over high heat for 4-5 minutes per side.
Serve with saffron rice, grilled tomatoes, and warm flatbread.
tips: Grated onion in the marinade tenderizes the chicken and adds moisture.
Do not overcook — chicken thighs should be juicy, not dry.

name: Koshari
description: Egyptian national dish of rice, lentils, and pasta topped with spiced tomato sauce and crispy onions.
instructions: Cook lentils in water for 15 minutes until almost tender, then add rice and cook together until done.
Cook pasta separately until al dente; drain.
Thinly slice onions and fry in oil until deeply caramelized and crispy; set aside.
For tomato sauce: sauté garlic, add canned tomatoes, vinegar, cumin, chili, and salt; simmer 10 minutes.
Layer rice-lentils, then pasta, top with tomato sauce and crispy fried onions.
tips: The crispy fried onions are essential — they provide crunch and sweetness.
Each component is simple, but layered together they create a satisfying dish.

name: Stuffed Grape Leaves (Dolma)
description: Tender grape leaves filled with herbed rice, pine nuts, and lemon.
instructions: Rinse grape leaves and pat dry; if very salty, blanch briefly.
Mix raw rice with finely chopped onion, herbs, pine nuts, 1 tbsp olive oil, tomato paste, allspice, and salt.
Place a spoonful of filling on each grape leaf, fold sides in, and roll tightly.
Arrange rolls tightly in a pot, seam-side down, drizzle with remaining olive oil and lemon juice.
Add water, weigh down with a plate, cover, and simmer on low heat for 45-50 minutes until rice is cooked.
tips: Pack the rolls tightly in the pot so they hold their shape during cooking.
Serve at room temperature for the best flavor.

name: Manakish
description: Lebanese flatbread topped with za'atar and olive oil, baked until golden.
instructions: Divide dough into 4 pieces, roll each into a thin round.
Mix za'atar with olive oil and a pinch of salt into a paste.
Spread the za'atar mixture evenly over each dough round.
Bake at 230C for 8-10 minutes until dough is golden and crispy on edges.
Serve warm, folded with labneh, tomatoes, and fresh mint.
tips: The dough should be thin for a crispy result.
Za'atar and olive oil should be generously applied.

name: Moussaka
description: Layered casserole of spiced lamb, eggplant, and creamy bechamel sauce baked golden.
instructions: Slice eggplant into 1cm rounds, salt and let drain for 30 minutes; pat dry and brush with olive oil, then grill or bake at 200C until golden.
Brown ground lamb with diced onion and garlic; add tomatoes, cinnamon, allspice, and salt; simmer 15 minutes.
Make bechamel: melt butter, stir in flour, slowly whisk in milk until thick; remove from heat and stir in egg yolk and nutmeg.
Layer eggplant slices, then meat sauce, then more eggplant in a baking dish; pour bechamel over the top.
Bake at 180C for 40 minutes until golden and bubbly; let rest 15 minutes before cutting.
tips: Salting and draining the eggplant removes bitterness and excess moisture.
Resting after baking lets the layers set so slices hold together.

name: Musakhan
description: Palestinian sumac chicken baked on caramelized onion flatbread with pine nuts.
instructions: Season chicken with 1 tbsp sumac, allspice, cinnamon, salt, and 2 tbsp olive oil; roast at 200C for 30 minutes until golden.
Meanwhile, slice onions thinly and cook in remaining olive oil over medium-low heat for 20 minutes until deeply caramelized.
Stir remaining sumac into the caramelized onions and cook 2 more minutes.
Spread the sumac onions over the flatbread, place roasted chicken on top, and return to oven for 10 minutes.
Toast pine nuts in a dry pan until golden and scatter over the dish before serving.
tips: Low and slow caramelization of the onions is the heart of this dish — do not rush it.
Sumac should be fresh and vibrant purple-red for the best flavor.

name: Kabsa
description: Saudi Arabian spiced rice with tender chicken, tomatoes, and aromatic whole spices.
instructions: Brown chicken pieces in olive oil on all sides; remove and set aside.
In the same pot, sauté diced onion until golden, add garlic, whole spices, and ground spices; cook 1 minute.
Add chopped tomatoes and tomato paste; cook 5 minutes until reduced.
Wash and drain rice, add to the pot with water and salt; nestle chicken pieces on top.
Bring to a boil, reduce to low, cover tightly, and cook for 20-25 minutes until rice is fluffy and chicken is cooked through.
tips: Do not stir the rice once it is layered — let it steam undisturbed for the best texture.
Soaking the rice for 20 minutes before cooking shortens the cook time and gives fluffier grains.

name: Kibbeh
description: Crispy bulgur shells stuffed with spiced ground lamb, onions, and toasted pine nuts.
instructions: Soak bulgur in water for 30 minutes; drain and squeeze out excess moisture.
Make the shell: blend soaked bulgur with lean ground lamb, half the grated onion, allspice, and salt until a smooth paste forms.
Make the filling: sauté remaining diced onion, add ground lamb, pine nuts, cinnamon, cumin, and pepper; cook until browned.
Shape kibbeh: take a golf-ball-sized portion of the shell mixture, hollow it out, fill with a spoonful of filling, and seal into a torpedo shape.
Deep-fry at 175C for 4-5 minutes until deep golden and crispy; drain on paper towels.
tips: Keep your hands wet while shaping to prevent the bulgur mixture from sticking.
The shell mixture must be very smooth — blend it twice if needed.

name: Omelette
description: Classic folded omelette with cheese, mushrooms, and fresh herbs cooked in butter.
instructions: Whisk eggs with milk, salt, and pepper until well combined.
Sauté sliced mushrooms in half the butter until golden; set aside.
Melt remaining butter in a non-stick pan over medium heat, pour in eggs, and swirl to coat the pan.
When edges set, gently push cooked egg toward center and tilt pan to let raw egg flow to the edges.
Add cheese and mushrooms to one half, fold over, and slide onto a plate; garnish with chives.
tips: A non-stick pan and medium heat are essential for a smooth, unbroken omelette.
Fill the omelette while the center is still slightly wet — it finishes cooking from the residual heat.

name: Grilled Cheese Sandwich
description: Golden crispy buttered bread with melted gooey cheese inside.
instructions: Butter the outside of both bread slices.
Spread a thin layer of Dijon mustard on the inside of one slice.
Layer cheese between the slices with buttered sides facing out.
Cook in a pan over medium-low heat for 3-4 minutes per side until bread is deeply golden and cheese is fully melted.
Cut diagonally and serve hot.
tips: Medium-low heat gives the cheese time to melt before the bread burns.
A mix of cheddar and gruyere gives the best stretch and flavor.

name: Tomato Soup
description: Velvety smooth roasted tomato soup finished with cream and fresh basil.
instructions: Sauté diced onion and garlic in olive oil until soft and translucent.
Add canned tomatoes with their juice, vegetable broth, sugar, salt, and pepper; bring to a simmer.
Cook for 20 minutes to concentrate the flavors.
Blend until completely smooth using an immersion blender or regular blender.
Stir in cream, taste and adjust seasoning, and serve topped with torn basil leaves.
tips: A pinch of sugar balances the acidity of the tomatoes without making it sweet.
For a dairy-free version, substitute coconut cream for the heavy cream.

name: Pasta Aglio e Olio
description: Spaghetti tossed in golden garlic olive oil with chili flakes and fresh parsley.
instructions: Cook spaghetti in generously salted boiling water until al dente; reserve 1 cup pasta water before draining.
While pasta cooks, slowly heat olive oil with thinly sliced garlic over medium-low heat until garlic is golden — not brown.
Add chili flakes and cook 30 seconds more.
Toss drained pasta into the garlic oil with a splash of pasta water; toss vigorously to emulsify.
Serve topped with chopped parsley and grated parmesan.
tips: Slice garlic thin and cook low and slow — burnt garlic is bitter and ruins the dish.
Pasta water is essential for creating a silky sauce that clings to the noodles.

name: Banana Smoothie
description: Creamy blended banana smoothie with yogurt, honey, and a hint of cinnamon.
instructions: Peel bananas and break into chunks.
Add banana, yogurt, milk, honey, cinnamon, and ice cubes to a blender.
Blend on high for 30-60 seconds until completely smooth and frothy.
Pour into glasses and serve immediately.
tips: Frozen banana slices make a thicker, colder smoothie without diluting with too much ice.
Use ripe bananas with brown spots for the sweetest, most flavorful smoothie.

name: Fruit Salad
description: Fresh mixed seasonal fruits tossed in a light honey-lime dressing with mint.
instructions: Wash all fruit; hull and halve strawberries, peel and dice mango and kiwi, halve grapes, slice banana.
Combine all prepared fruit in a large bowl.
Whisk honey and lime juice together until dissolved.
Drizzle the honey-lime dressing over the fruit and toss gently.
Garnish with torn mint leaves and serve chilled.
tips: Add banana just before serving to prevent browning.
Use whatever seasonal fruits are at their best — the recipe is flexible.

name: Oatmeal
description: Warm, creamy cooked oats topped with banana, cinnamon, and honey.
instructions: Bring milk or water to a simmer with a pinch of salt.
Add oats and cook on low heat for 5-7 minutes, stirring occasionally.
The oatmeal is done when creamy and the oats are tender.
Top with sliced banana, a drizzle of honey, and a sprinkle of cinnamon.
tips: A pinch of salt brings out the natural sweetness of oats.
For creamier oatmeal, use a mix of milk and water.

name: Hard Boiled Eggs
description: Perfectly cooked hard-boiled eggs with a fully set but not chalky yolk.
instructions: Place eggs in a single layer in a pot, cover with cold water by 1 inch.
Add salt, bring to a rolling boil over high heat.
Once boiling, remove from heat, cover, and let sit for exactly 10 minutes.
Transfer eggs to an ice bath for 5 minutes to stop cooking.
Peel under running water and serve whole, halved, or sliced.
tips: Starting in cold water and using residual heat prevents rubbery whites.
Older eggs peel more easily than fresh ones.

name: Pasta with Pesto
description: Al dente pasta tossed with fresh homemade basil pesto and parmesan.
instructions: Cook pasta in salted boiling water until al dente; reserve 1 cup pasta water.
Blend basil, pine nuts, garlic, and parmesan in a food processor; stream in olive oil.
Toss drained pasta with pesto and a splash of pasta water until coated.
Serve with extra parmesan and a drizzle of olive oil.
tips: Do not cook the pesto — it should stay raw and vibrant green.
Pasta water helps emulsify the sauce and makes it cling to the pasta.

name: Oven Roasted Chicken
description: Whole chicken roasted with herbs and garlic until skin is crispy and meat is juicy.
instructions: Pat chicken completely dry with paper towels — this is the most important step for crispy skin.
Rub softened butter mixed with minced garlic, salt, and pepper under and over the skin.
Stuff the cavity with lemon halves, thyme, and rosemary.
Truss the legs, drizzle with olive oil, and place in a roasting pan.
Roast at 200C for 1 hour 15 minutes until skin is golden and internal temperature reaches 74C; rest 15 minutes before carving.
tips: A dry surface is the number one secret to crispy skin.
Resting for 15 minutes lets juices redistribute — do not skip this.

name: Vegetable Stir-Fry
description: Quick mixed vegetable stir-fry in a savory soy-garlic sauce, served over rice.
instructions: Cut all vegetables into bite-sized pieces of similar thickness.
Mix soy sauce, sesame oil, and cornstarch with 2 tbsp water for the sauce.
Heat oil in a wok or large pan over high heat.
Add garlic and ginger, then vegetables starting with hardest (broccoli, carrot) first.
Add sauce, toss for 1-2 minutes until vegetables are crisp-tender and glazed; serve over rice.
tips: Prep everything before you start cooking — stir-frying moves fast.
Do not overcook — vegetables should be bright and still have a slight crunch.

name: Banana Bread
description: Moist, tender quick bread made with overripe bananas and a hint of cinnamon.
instructions: Mash bananas in a bowl until smooth with some lumps remaining.
Mix in melted butter, sugar, egg, and vanilla.
Fold in flour, baking soda, cinnamon, and salt until just combined.
Fold in walnuts if using, pour into a greased loaf pan.
Bake at 175C for 55-60 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean; cool in pan for 10 minutes.
tips: The riper and blacker the bananas, the better the bread — overripe is ideal.
Do not over-mix the batter or the bread becomes tough.

name: Chicken Noodle Soup
description: Comforting clear broth soup with tender chicken, egg noodles, and vegetables.
instructions: Heat olive oil, sauté diced onion, carrot, and celery until softened.
Add garlic, cook 30 seconds, then pour in chicken broth with bay leaf.
