Add whole chicken breast, bring to a simmer, cook for 15 minutes until chicken is cooked through.
Remove chicken, shred with two forks, return to pot.
Add egg noodles, cook for 6-8 minutes until tender; season and garnish with parsley.
tips: Cooking chicken in the broth adds flavor to both.
Add noodles near the end — they absorb broth and can become mushy if overcooked.

name: Fruit Smoothie
description: Quick blended fruit drink with yogurt, banana, and berries.
instructions: Add banana, frozen berries, yogurt, milk, and honey to a blender.
Blend on high for 30-60 seconds until smooth and creamy.
Adjust thickness with more milk if needed.
Pour into a glass and serve immediately.
tips: Using frozen fruit gives a thick, cold smoothie without needing ice.
Any combination of fruits works — experiment freely.

name: Lentil Soup
description: Hearty, warming red lentil soup with cumin, garlic, and a squeeze of lemon.
instructions: Heat olive oil, sauté diced onion and carrot until soft.
Add garlic, cumin, and paprika; cook 1 minute until fragrant.
Add washed lentils and broth; bring to a boil, then simmer for 20 minutes until lentils are soft.
Blend partially for a creamy-chunky texture, or fully for smooth soup.
Season with salt and lemon juice; serve with a drizzle of olive oil and crusty bread.
tips: Red lentils cook quickly and do not need soaking.
Lemon juice added at the end brightens the entire soup.

name: Mushroom Omelette
description: Fluffy folded omelette filled with sauteed mushrooms and melted cheese.
instructions: Sauté sliced mushrooms in half the butter until golden; set aside.
Beat eggs with salt and pepper until uniform.
Melt remaining butter in a non-stick pan over medium heat, pour in eggs.
Gently push cooked edges toward center, tilting pan to let raw egg flow to edges.
When almost set but still slightly wet on top, add mushrooms and cheese to one half; fold and slide onto plate.
tips: A French omelette should be pale yellow with no browning.
Slightly wet on top when folding — residual heat finishes cooking.

name: Spaghetti Bolognese
description: Rich Italian-style meat sauce with ground beef, tomatoes, and herbs over spaghetti.
instructions: Finely dice onion, carrot, and celery (soffritto).
Heat olive oil, sauté soffritto until soft, add garlic.
Add ground beef, break up and brown thoroughly.
Add wine, cook until reduced, then add tomatoes, tomato paste, and Italian seasoning.
Simmer on low for 30 minutes; serve over al dente spaghetti with parmesan.
tips: The soffritto (onion, carrot, celery) is the flavor base — cook it low and slow.
Simmer the sauce longer for deeper flavor — 30 minutes is the minimum.

name: Fried Egg
description: A perfectly fried egg with crispy edges and a runny yolk.
instructions: Heat butter or oil in a non-stick pan over medium heat.
Crack egg into the pan gently.
Cook for 2-3 minutes until white is set and edges are slightly crispy.
Season with salt and pepper; serve immediately.
tips: Crack the egg into a small bowl first to avoid shell fragments.
For over-easy, flip gently and cook 30 seconds more.

name: Toast with Butter and Jam
description: Simple toasted bread with butter and fruit preserves.
instructions: Toast bread until golden and crispy.
Spread butter on warm toast so it melts.
Top with fruit jam of your choice.
Serve immediately with tea or coffee.
tips: Spread butter while the toast is hot for even melting.
Thick-cut artisan bread makes a more satisfying breakfast.

name: Kung Pao Chicken
description: Sichuan stir-fried chicken with peanuts, dried chilies, and Sichuan peppercorns in a savory-sweet sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into 2cm cubes, marinate with 1 tbsp soy sauce and 1 tsp cornstarch for 15 minutes.
Mix remaining soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, remaining cornstarch, and 2 tbsp water into a sauce.
Heat oil in a wok until smoking, stir-fry chicken until golden; set aside.
Toast Sichuan peppercorns and dried chilies in the wok until fragrant.
Add garlic and ginger, stir-fry 30 seconds, return chicken, pour in sauce.
Toss until sauce thickens, add peanuts and spring onions, drizzle sesame oil.
tips: Toast Sichuan peppercorns on low heat to avoid burning.
Chinkiang vinegar is essential for authentic flavor.

name: Mapo Tofu
description: Soft tofu in a fiery Sichuan sauce with ground pork, doubanjiang, and Sichuan peppercorns.
instructions: Cut tofu into 2cm cubes, blanch in salted water for 2 minutes; drain gently.
Heat oil in wok, brown ground pork until crispy.
Add doubanjiang and fermented black beans, stir-fry 1 minute until oil turns red.
Add garlic and ginger, stir 30 seconds, then pour in chicken stock and soy sauce.
Slide in tofu gently, simmer 5 minutes. Mix cornstarch with water, stir in to thicken.
Sprinkle Sichuan peppercorn powder and spring onions, serve over steamed rice.
tips: Blanching tofu removes excess water and firms it up.
Add the doubanjiang to oil first to release its red color and flavor.

name: General Tso's Chicken
description: Crispy battered chicken in a sweet, tangy, and mildly spicy sauce with steamed broccoli.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces, coat with egg and cornstarch.
Deep fry in batches at 180C until golden and crispy, about 4 minutes; drain.
Mix soy sauce, rice vinegar, sugar, hoisin, and 3 tbsp water for the sauce.
In a clean wok, stir-fry garlic, ginger, and dried chilies for 30 seconds.
Pour in sauce, bring to a simmer, add fried chicken and toss to coat.
Drizzle sesame oil, serve with steamed broccoli and rice.
tips: Double-frying makes the coating extra crispy — fry once, rest 5 min, fry again.
The sauce should coat the chicken in a thin, glossy layer, not pool at the bottom.

name: Sweet and Sour Pork
description: Crispy battered pork pieces with bell peppers and pineapple in a tangy sweet and sour sauce.
instructions: Cut pork into 3cm cubes, coat with beaten egg then cornstarch.
Deep fry at 180C until golden and crispy, about 4-5 minutes; drain.
Mix ketchup, rice vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, and 3 tbsp water for the sauce.
Stir-fry bell pepper, onion, and pineapple for 2 minutes in 1 tbsp oil.
Pour in sauce, bring to a boil, add fried pork and toss until coated.
Serve immediately over steamed rice.
tips: Fry pork in small batches to keep oil temperature consistent.
Add pineapple last to keep its texture firm.

name: Chow Mein
description: Stir-fried egg noodles with vegetables and a savory soy-oyster sauce.
instructions: Cook noodles until just al dente, drain and toss with sesame oil to prevent sticking.
Heat vegetable oil in a wok over high heat, add garlic and stir 15 seconds.
Add shredded cabbage and julienned carrot, stir-fry 2 minutes.
Add noodles, soy sauce, and oyster sauce; toss vigorously for 2 minutes.
Add bean sprouts and spring onions, toss for 30 seconds and serve.
tips: High heat and quick tossing give chow mein its signature smoky wok flavor.
Do not overcook the noodles initially — they finish cooking in the wok.

name: Egg Fried Rice
description: Classic Chinese fried rice with egg, spring onions, and a touch of soy sauce.
instructions: Beat eggs with a pinch of salt. Heat oil in a wok until smoking.
Scramble eggs quickly, breaking into small pieces; set aside.
Add more oil, stir-fry garlic for 15 seconds, add rice and toss vigorously.
Add soy sauce, white pepper, and peas; stir-fry 2-3 minutes until rice is slightly crispy.
Return eggs, add spring onions and sesame oil, toss and serve.
tips: Day-old refrigerated rice is essential — fresh rice becomes mushy.
Keep the wok screaming hot for the best fried rice texture.

name: Hot and Sour Soup
description: Spicy and tangy soup with tofu, mushrooms, bamboo shoots, and a silky egg ribbon.
instructions: Bring chicken stock to a boil. Add sliced mushrooms and bamboo shoots, simmer 3 minutes.
Add soy sauce, vinegar, and white pepper.
Add diced tofu, simmer 2 minutes.
Mix cornstarch with 3 tbsp water, stir into soup to thicken.
Beat egg, slowly drizzle into simmering soup while stirring gently to form ribbons.
Finish with sesame oil and chili oil, serve hot.
tips: Add vinegar after turning off heat for a sharper tang.
Stir in one direction when adding egg for clean ribbons.

name: Wonton Soup
description: Delicate pork and shrimp wontons in a light ginger-scallion broth.
instructions: Mince shrimp, mix with pork, 1 tbsp soy sauce, ginger, sesame oil, white pepper, and chopped spring onion.
Place 1 tsp filling in center of each wrapper, wet edges, fold into triangle and seal.
Bring chicken stock to a boil, season with remaining soy sauce.
Cook wontons in boiling stock for 3-4 minutes until they float.
Add halved bok choy, cook 1 minute. Serve in bowls with broth and garnish with spring onion.
tips: Do not overfill wontons or they will burst while cooking.
A splash of sesame oil in the broth adds depth.

name: Dan Dan Noodles
description: Spicy Sichuan noodles with ground pork, sesame paste, and chili oil in a numbing sauce.
instructions: Cook noodles according to package directions; drain.
Brown ground pork in oil with minced garlic and preserved mustard greens until crispy.
Mix sesame paste, soy sauce, chili oil, vinegar, sugar, Sichuan peppercorn powder, and 3 tbsp hot water in each bowl.
Divide noodles into bowls, top with pork mixture.
Garnish with chopped spring onions and extra chili oil to taste.
tips: Thin out the sesame paste with hot water for a smooth sauce.
Ya cai (preserved mustard greens) adds authentic flavor — substitute with pickled vegetables if unavailable.

name: Char Siu Pork
description: Cantonese BBQ pork glazed with a sweet honey-hoisin marinade, roasted until caramelized.
instructions: Cut pork into thick strips about 3cm wide.
Mix hoisin, soy sauce, oyster sauce, honey, five-spice, minced garlic, fermented tofu, and wine.
Marinate pork for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight.
Roast on a wire rack at 200C for 15 minutes, then reduce to 180C.
Baste with marinade every 10 minutes for another 20-25 minutes until caramelized and charred at edges.
Rest 5 minutes, slice and serve with steamed rice.
tips: The red color traditionally comes from fermented red tofu, not food coloring.
Broil for the last 2 minutes for extra charring.

name: Peking Duck Wraps
description: Crispy roasted duck slices with hoisin sauce, cucumber, and spring onion in thin pancakes.
instructions: Pat duck dry, rub inside with five-spice and salt. Pour boiling water over skin twice to tighten it.
Brush skin with honey and soy sauce mixture, hang or refrigerate uncovered overnight to dry.
Roast on a rack at 180C for 1.5-2 hours, basting every 30 minutes.
Carve skin and meat into thin slices.
Serve with warm Mandarin pancakes, hoisin sauce, cucumber batons, and spring onion shreds.
Wrap a few slices of duck, sauce, and vegetables in each pancake.
tips: Drying the skin overnight is the secret to crackly crisp skin.
Serve the skin separately from the meat for the best texture experience.

name: Chinese Braised Eggplant
description: Silky eggplant braised in a savory garlic sauce with soy, vinegar, and a hint of sugar.
instructions: Cut eggplant into thick batons. Heat oil in a wok, fry eggplant until golden and soft; set aside.
In the same wok, stir-fry garlic, ginger, and chili flakes for 30 seconds.
Add soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and 3 tbsp water; bring to a simmer.
Return eggplant, braise for 3-4 minutes until sauce is absorbed.
Mix cornstarch with 1 tbsp water, stir in to thicken. Finish with sesame oil and spring onion.
tips: Chinese eggplants work best — they are thinner and creamier than globe eggplants.
Do not skip frying — it gives the eggplant a silky, melt-in-mouth texture.

name: Cashew Chicken
description: Tender chicken stir-fried with roasted cashews, vegetables, and a light soy-oyster sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces, toss with 1 tbsp soy sauce and cornstarch.
Heat oil in a wok, stir-fry chicken until golden; set aside.
Stir-fry garlic, onion, bell pepper, and celery for 2 minutes.
Return chicken, add remaining soy sauce and oyster sauce, toss 1 minute.
Add cashews, drizzle sesame oil, toss and serve over rice.
tips: Add cashews at the very end to keep them crunchy.
Toast raw cashews in a dry pan first if not using pre-roasted.

name: Black Pepper Beef
description: Tender beef strips stir-fried with onions and bell peppers in a bold black pepper sauce.
instructions: Slice beef thinly against the grain, marinate with 1 tbsp soy sauce and cornstarch for 15 minutes.
Heat oil in a wok until smoking, sear beef in batches until browned; set aside.
Add butter, stir-fry sliced onion and bell pepper for 2 minutes.
Add garlic and freshly cracked black pepper, stir 30 seconds.
Return beef, add remaining soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar; toss for 1 minute.
Serve immediately over steamed rice.
tips: Slice beef against the grain for tenderness.
Coarsely crack black pepper fresh — pre-ground lacks the punch.

name: Ma La Xiang Guo
description: Sichuan dry pot with assorted vegetables and protein in a numbing-spicy chili sauce.
instructions: Slice all vegetables and protein into bite-sized pieces. Blanch hard vegetables (lotus root, potato, broccoli) briefly.
Heat oil in a wok, toast Sichuan peppercorns and dried chilies until fragrant.
Add doubanjiang, stir-fry 1 minute until oil turns red.
Add garlic and cumin seeds, stir 30 seconds.
Add protein first, cook 3 minutes, then add all vegetables and stir-fry 3-4 minutes.
Add soy sauce, toss vigorously until everything is coated. Serve immediately.
tips: Blanch harder vegetables first so everything finishes cooking at the same time.
Adjust chili and peppercorn quantities to your spice tolerance.

name: Twice Cooked Pork
description: Sichuan classic of boiled then stir-fried pork belly with leeks, doubanjiang, and fermented black beans.
instructions: Boil pork belly with ginger slices for 20 minutes until just cooked. Cool and slice thinly.
Heat wok with oil, add pork slices and stir-fry until edges curl and fat renders.
Push pork aside, add doubanjiang and fermented black beans; stir-fry 1 minute.
Add shaoxing wine and soy sauce, toss pork to coat.
Add sliced leek and bell pepper, stir-fry 2 minutes until just wilted.
Add sugar, toss, and serve with steamed rice.
tips: Slice the pork as thin as possible for the best texture.
The pork slices should curl at the edges — that is the sign of proper cooking.

name: Steamed Fish with Ginger and Scallion
description: Delicate whole steamed fish draped with ginger, scallion, and sizzling hot soy-sesame oil.

ingredients.name: whole white fish (sea bass or snapper)

instructions: Score fish on both sides, rub with wine and salt. Place on a plate with ginger slices underneath.
Steam over high heat for 8-10 minutes until fish flakes easily.
Discard any liquid on the plate and the steaming ginger.
Top fish with fresh julienned ginger and spring onion shreds.
Heat vegetable oil and sesame oil until smoking, pour sizzling oil over the fish.
Drizzle soy sauce mixed with sugar, garnish with cilantro, and serve immediately.
tips: Do not overcook — the fish should just flake. Start checking at 8 minutes.
The sizzling oil is essential — it cooks the aromatics and releases their fragrance.

name: Mongolian Beef
description: Crispy beef strips in a sweet and savory soy-brown sugar sauce with scallions.
instructions: Slice flank steak thinly against the grain, toss with cornstarch until fully coated.
Deep fry beef in batches at 180C until crispy, about 2 minutes; drain.
In a clean wok, sauté garlic and ginger in 1 tbsp oil for 30 seconds.
Add soy sauce, brown sugar, hoisin, and water; bring to a simmer.
Add fried beef, toss to coat in sauce. Add spring onion cut into 5cm lengths.
Drizzle sesame oil and serve over steamed rice.
tips: Slice against the grain for tender beef.
The beef should be coated in a sticky, glossy sauce — not swimming in liquid.

name: Shrimp Lo Mein
description: Egg noodles tossed with plump shrimp, vegetables, and a savory soy-sesame sauce.
instructions: Cook noodles until al dente, drain and toss with sesame oil.
Heat oil in a wok, stir-fry shrimp until just pink, about 2 minutes; set aside.
Stir-fry garlic, ginger, sliced mushrooms, and julienned carrot for 2 minutes.
Add bok choy, stir-fry 1 minute until wilted.
Add noodles, soy sauce, and oyster sauce; toss everything together.
Return shrimp, toss to combine, and serve.
tips: Cook shrimp separately and add back at the end to avoid overcooking.
Lo mein noodles should be saucy but not soupy.

name: Chinese Broccoli with Oyster Sauce
description: Blanched gai lan drizzled with a glossy oyster sauce and garlic oil.
instructions: Trim tough ends of gai lan, cut into 10cm lengths.
Blanch in boiling salted water for 2-3 minutes until bright green and tender-crisp; drain.
Arrange on a serving plate.
Mix oyster sauce, soy sauce, sugar, and 1 tbsp water.
Heat oil, fry sliced garlic until golden, pour garlic oil and sauce over the broccoli.
Drizzle sesame oil and serve.
tips: Do not overcook — gai lan should be bright green and slightly crunchy.
Fried garlic chips add crunch and flavor.

name: Congee
description: Silky Chinese rice porridge slow-cooked until creamy, served with savory toppings.
instructions: Rinse rice and add to a large pot with chicken stock and sliced ginger.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to a gentle simmer.
Cook uncovered for 45-60 minutes, stirring occasionally, until rice breaks down into a creamy porridge.
Season with soy sauce, white pepper, and sesame oil.
Ladle into bowls, top with sliced spring onion, fried shallots, and optional century egg.
tips: Stir occasionally to prevent sticking, especially near the end.
For a silkier texture, soak rice for 30 minutes before cooking.

name: Lion's Head Meatballs
description: Large, tender pork meatballs braised in a napa cabbage and soy broth.
instructions: Mix pork with egg, ginger, 1 tbsp soy sauce, wine, cornstarch, white pepper, and minced spring onion.
Shape into 4 large meatballs (about the size of a tennis ball).
Brown meatballs in oil on all sides; do not cook through.
Line a clay pot or Dutch oven with napa cabbage leaves.
Nestle meatballs in the cabbage, pour in stock and remaining soy sauce.
Cover and braise on low heat for 25-30 minutes until meatballs are cooked through and cabbage is silky.
tips: The meatballs should be large — lion's head size, not regular meatballs.
Braising in cabbage keeps the meatballs moist and flavors the broth.

name: Cumin Lamb
description: Xinjiang-style stir-fried lamb with cumin seeds, dried chilies, and onions.
instructions: Slice lamb thinly, marinate with soy sauce and cornstarch for 15 minutes.
Toast cumin seeds in a dry wok until fragrant; set aside half.
Heat oil in wok until smoking, stir-fry lamb in batches until browned.
Add sliced onion, garlic, dried chilies, and chili flakes; stir-fry 2 minutes.
Add remaining toasted cumin seeds and salt, toss 1 minute.
Garnish with cilantro and serve with flatbread or rice.
tips: Toast cumin seeds until fragrant but not burnt — they go from perfect to bitter quickly.
Use boneless lamb leg for the best texture.

name: Egg Drop Soup
description: Light chicken broth thickened with cornstarch and finished with silky egg ribbons.
instructions: Bring chicken stock to a boil, season with soy sauce, salt, and white pepper.
Mix cornstarch with 2 tbsp cold water, stir into the boiling broth to thicken slightly.
Beat eggs. Reduce heat so broth is barely simmering.
Slowly drizzle beaten egg into the broth in a thin stream while stirring gently.
Turn off heat, drizzle sesame oil, and garnish with sliced spring onion.
tips: Pour egg in a very thin stream for delicate ribbons, not clumps.
The broth should be just simmering, not boiling vigorously, when adding eggs.

name: Scallion Pancakes
description: Flaky, crispy Chinese flatbread layered with sesame oil and chopped scallions.
instructions: Mix flour with boiling water, knead until smooth, rest 30 minutes covered.
Divide into 4 portions. Roll each into a thin circle.
Brush with sesame oil, sprinkle salt and chopped spring onion.
Roll up into a log, then coil into a spiral. Flatten and roll into a circle again.
Pan-fry in oil over medium heat for 2-3 minutes per side until golden and flaky.
Serve with a dipping sauce of soy sauce, vinegar, and chili oil.
tips: Boiling water makes the dough softer and more pliable than cold water dough.
The rolling-coiling-rolling technique creates the flaky layers.

name: Pad Thai
description: Stir-fried rice noodles with shrimp, tofu, bean sprouts, egg, and tamarind sauce topped with peanuts.
instructions: Soak rice noodles in warm water for 20 minutes until pliable; drain.
Mix tamarind paste, fish sauce, and palm sugar into a sauce.
Heat oil in a wok, fry diced tofu until golden; push aside. Add shrimp, cook until pink.
Crack eggs into the wok, scramble roughly.
Add noodles and tamarind sauce, toss for 2-3 minutes until noodles are tender and coated.
Add bean sprouts and garlic chives, toss briefly. Serve with crushed peanuts, lime wedges, and chili flakes.
tips: Do not over-soak the noodles or they become mushy.
High heat and quick tossing are essential for proper pad Thai.

name: Thai Green Curry
description: Creamy coconut curry with chicken, Thai eggplant, bamboo shoots, and fresh basil.
instructions: Heat oil, scoop the thick cream from top of coconut milk and fry with curry paste until fragrant and oil separates.
Add chicken pieces, stir to coat in paste, cook 3 minutes.
Pour in remaining coconut milk, add kaffir lime leaves, bring to a simmer.
Add eggplant and bamboo shoots, cook 10 minutes until chicken is done.
Season with fish sauce and palm sugar. Stir in Thai basil and sliced chili.
Serve over jasmine rice.
tips: Fry the paste in coconut cream first to bloom the spices.
Add basil at the very end — it wilts and loses flavor if overcooked.

name: Tom Yum Goong
description: Hot and sour Thai soup with shrimp, mushrooms, lemongrass, galangal, and lime.
instructions: Bring stock to a boil with bruised lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, and crushed chilies.
Simmer 5 minutes to infuse the aromatics.
Add mushrooms and shrimp, cook 3 minutes until shrimp are pink.
Turn off heat. Add fish sauce, lime juice, and nam prik pao. Stir.
Taste and adjust — it should be equally sour, spicy, and savory.
Garnish with cilantro leaves and serve.
tips: Do not boil after adding lime juice — it becomes bitter.
The aromatics are for flavor, not eating — leave them in but do not eat them.

name: Massaman Curry
description: Rich Thai curry with tender beef, potatoes, peanuts, and warm spices in coconut milk.
instructions: Fry curry paste in coconut cream until oil separates and paste is fragrant.
Add beef cubes, stir to coat, cook 5 minutes.
Pour in remaining coconut milk, add cinnamon, cardamom, and bay leaves.
Simmer 30 minutes until beef is nearly tender.
Add cubed potatoes and quartered onion, cook 15 minutes more.
Season with fish sauce, palm sugar, and tamarind. Add peanuts and serve over rice.
tips: Massaman is a mild, warm-spiced curry — it should not be fiery hot.
Low and slow braising makes the beef meltingly tender.

name: Pad See Ew
description: Stir-fried wide rice noodles with chicken, Chinese broccoli, egg, and sweet soy sauce.
instructions: Slice chicken thinly, marinate with 1 tbsp soy sauce. Cut broccoli into pieces.
Heat oil in a wok until smoking, stir-fry chicken until cooked; set aside.
Crack egg into the wok, scramble briefly, then add noodles immediately.
Add dark soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar; toss noodles for 2 minutes.
Add broccoli and return chicken, toss 1 minute until broccoli is tender-crisp.
Finish with white pepper and serve.
tips: The wok must be scorching hot for proper char on the noodles.
Fresh wide rice noodles work best — dried ones need soaking first.

name: Thai Basil Chicken (Pad Kra Pao)
description: Stir-fried ground chicken with holy basil, chilies, and garlic, served over rice with a fried egg.
instructions: Pound garlic and chilies in a mortar into a rough paste.
Heat oil in a wok until smoking, add garlic-chili paste and stir-fry 15 seconds.
Add ground chicken, break apart and stir-fry on high heat until cooked, about 3 minutes.
Add soy sauce, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar; toss 1 minute.
Turn off heat, add basil leaves and stir until just wilted.
Serve over rice, topped with a crispy fried egg.
tips: Holy basil is traditional and has a peppery flavor — Thai basil is a good substitute.
The basil should wilt from residual heat, not cook in the wok.

name: Larb Gai
description: Thai minced chicken salad with roasted rice powder, lime, chilies, mint, and fish sauce.
instructions: Dry-toast rice in a pan until golden, grind to a powder.
Cook chicken in a dry wok with 2 tbsp water, breaking it apart, until just cooked.
Remove from heat. Add lime juice, fish sauce, sliced shallots, chili flakes, and roasted rice powder.
Toss with torn mint and cilantro leaves, sliced spring onion.
Taste and adjust — should be sour, salty, and spicy in equal measure.
Serve in lettuce cups with sticky rice on the side.
tips: Roasted rice powder adds nutty crunch and is essential to authentic larb.
Cook the chicken just until done — overcooked ground meat is dry and bland.

name: Som Tum (Green Papaya Salad)
description: Spicy Thai salad of shredded green papaya with tomatoes, peanuts, dried shrimp, and lime dressing.
instructions: Peel and shred green papaya into thin strips using a grater or knife.
Pound garlic and chilies in a mortar to a rough paste.
Add long beans cut into 2cm pieces, bruise lightly with pestle.
Add dried shrimp and peanuts, bruise gently.
Add tomatoes, papaya, palm sugar, fish sauce, and lime juice; toss and pound lightly to combine.
Taste and adjust — it should be sour, salty, sweet, and spicy.
tips: Pound and toss gently — do not mash everything to a paste.
Green papaya should be firm and crunchy, not soft.

name: Tom Kha Gai
description: Creamy Thai coconut soup with chicken, galangal, lemongrass, mushrooms, and lime.
instructions: Bring coconut milk and stock to a gentle simmer with galangal, bruised lemongrass, and lime leaves.
Simmer 5 minutes to infuse aromatics.
Add sliced chicken and mushrooms, cook 8 minutes until chicken is done.
Season with fish sauce, sugar, and crushed chilies.
Turn off heat, add lime juice and stir.
Serve in bowls garnished with cilantro and a drizzle of coconut cream.
tips: Tom kha should be creamy, not watery — use full-fat coconut milk.
Do not boil vigorously or the coconut milk may split.

name: Panang Curry
description: Thick, rich Thai curry with beef, peanuts, kaffir lime, and red curry paste in coconut cream.
instructions: Scoop thick cream from coconut milk, fry with curry paste until oil separates.
Add sliced beef, stir-fry 3 minutes to coat in paste.
Pour in remaining coconut milk, add torn kaffir lime leaves, simmer 15 minutes.
Season with fish sauce and palm sugar.
Stir in crushed peanuts. Garnish with sliced red chili, julienned lime leaves, and basil.
Serve over jasmine rice.
tips: Panang is thicker than other Thai curries — use less liquid for a richer result.
Julienne a kaffir lime leaf for garnish — it adds fragrance.

name: Thai Pineapple Fried Rice
description: Fried rice with pineapple, cashews, shrimp, and curry powder served in a pineapple shell.
instructions: Heat oil in a wok, scramble egg and break into pieces; set aside.
Stir-fry shrimp until pink, set aside.
Add rice to the wok, stir-fry 2 minutes on high heat.
Add curry powder, soy sauce, fish sauce, and sugar; toss well.
Add pineapple, cashews, raisins, return shrimp and egg; toss 1 minute.
Garnish with spring onion. Serve in a hollowed pineapple half for presentation.
tips: Day-old rice works best for fried rice.
The combination of sweet pineapple and savory curry powder is key.

name: Pad Kra Pao (Spicy Basil Pork)
description: Fiery stir-fried ground pork with holy basil, Thai chilies, and garlic over rice.
instructions: Pound garlic and chilies in a mortar to a rough paste.
Heat oil in a wok until smoking, add paste and stir-fry 15 seconds.
Add ground pork, stir-fry on high heat for 3 minutes, breaking it apart.
Add soy sauce, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar; toss 1 minute.
Turn off heat, add basil leaves and stir until wilted from residual heat.
Serve over steamed rice with a fried egg on top.
tips: The wok must be extremely hot for this dish.
Use holy basil if available — it has a peppery kick that Thai basil lacks.

name: Thai Red Curry
description: Classic red curry with chicken, bell peppers, and bamboo shoots in coconut milk.
instructions: Fry curry paste in coconut cream until fragrant and oil separates.
Add chicken pieces, stir to coat, cook 3 minutes.
Pour in remaining coconut milk, add kaffir lime leaves, bring to a simmer.
Add sliced bell pepper and bamboo shoots, cook 10 minutes.
Season with fish sauce and palm sugar.
Stir in Thai basil, serve over jasmine rice.
tips: Splitting the coconut cream and milk gives a richer base.
Adjust curry paste quantity to your spice preference.

name: Khao Soi
description: Northern Thai coconut curry noodle soup with chicken, crispy noodles, and pickled mustard greens.
instructions: Fry curry paste with curry powder in coconut cream until fragrant.
Add chicken, coat in paste, add remaining coconut milk and 300ml water; simmer 20 minutes.
Cook egg noodles. Reserve a handful and deep-fry until crispy for topping.
Season soup with fish sauce and palm sugar.
Serve noodles in bowls with curry, topped with crispy noodles.
Garnish with sliced shallot, pickled mustard greens, and lime wedges.
tips: Crispy fried noodle topping is essential for authentic khao soi.
The curry should be rich and thick, not thin and soupy.

name: Thai Fish Cakes (Tod Mun Pla)
description: Bouncy pan-fried fish cakes with red curry paste, long beans, and kaffir lime leaves.
instructions: Blend fish fillets to a paste in a food processor with curry paste, fish sauce, egg, and sugar.
Finely slice kaffir lime leaves and chop long beans into small pieces; fold into fish paste.
Shape into flat patties about 6cm across.
Pan-fry in oil over medium heat for 3 minutes per side until golden and firm.
Serve with sweet chili sauce and a quick cucumber relish.
tips: Pound or process the fish until it becomes bouncy and springy, not crumbly.
The kaffir lime leaves add an irreplaceable citrus aroma.

name: Mango Sticky Rice
description: Sweet Thai dessert of ripe mango with coconut sticky rice and a drizzle of coconut cream.
instructions: Soak glutinous rice in water for at least 4 hours or overnight. Drain.
Steam rice for 20-25 minutes until tender and translucent.
Heat coconut milk with sugar and salt until dissolved; do not boil.
Pour coconut sauce over hot rice, stir gently, cover and rest 10 minutes to absorb.
Slice ripe mango. Serve sticky rice alongside mango slices.
Drizzle coconut cream on top and sprinkle sesame seeds.
tips: The rice must be soaked — skipping this step results in hard, crunchy rice.
Use the sweetest, most fragrant mangoes you can find.

name: Thai Coconut Soup with Vegetables
description: Mild and creamy coconut soup with mixed vegetables, tofu, lemongrass, and lime.
instructions: Bring coconut milk and stock to a simmer with lemongrass, galangal, and lime leaves.
Infuse for 5 minutes.
Add sliced mushrooms, diced carrot, and cubed tofu; simmer 8 minutes.
Season with soy sauce, sugar, and lime juice.
Garnish with cilantro and serve.
tips: This is a milder alternative to tom kha — great for those who prefer less heat.
Add chili oil or fresh chilies at the table for those who want spice.

name: Thai Yellow Curry
description: Mild Thai curry with chicken, potatoes, and onion in a turmeric-coconut sauce.
instructions: Fry curry paste in oil with turmeric until fragrant.
Add chicken pieces, stir to coat, cook 3 minutes.
Pour in coconut milk, add cubed potatoes and quartered onion.
Simmer 20 minutes until potatoes are tender and chicken is cooked.
Season with fish sauce and palm sugar.
Serve over steamed jasmine rice.
tips: Yellow curry is the mildest of Thai curries — perfect for spice-sensitive eaters.
Do not cut potatoes too small — they should be chunky.

name: Thai Chicken Satay
description: Grilled marinated chicken skewers with a rich peanut dipping sauce and cucumber relish.
instructions: Slice chicken into thin strips, marinate with coconut milk, turmeric, curry powder, minced lemongrass, and garlic for 1 hour.
Thread onto skewers.
Make peanut sauce: warm peanut butter with 3 tbsp coconut milk, soy sauce, lime juice, sugar, and chili flakes.
Grill skewers over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side until charred.
Serve with peanut sauce and a quick cucumber-shallot-vinegar relish.
tips: Soak wooden skewers in water for 30 minutes to prevent burning.
Flatten the chicken strips for even cooking.

name: Crying Tiger (Suea Rong Hai)
description: Grilled marinated beef served sliced with a spicy, sour roasted chili dipping sauce.
instructions: Marinate steak with oyster sauce and soy sauce for 30 minutes.
Grill over high heat to medium-rare, about 4-5 minutes per side. Rest 5 minutes.
Make dipping sauce: mix fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, chili flakes, roasted rice powder, and sliced shallots.
Slice steak against the grain into thin strips.
Arrange on a plate, serve with dipping sauce, cilantro, and steamed rice.
tips: The name comes from the beef being so spicy it makes a tiger cry.
Rest the meat before slicing for juicy results.

name: Chicken Enchiladas
description: Corn tortillas filled with shredded chicken and cheese, baked in red enchilada sauce.
instructions: Poach chicken in salted water until cooked; shred with two forks.
Sauté diced onion and garlic in oil, add shredded chicken, cumin, and salt.
Spread a thin layer of enchilada sauce on the bottom of a baking dish.
Fill each tortilla with chicken mixture and cheese, roll up and place seam-down.
Pour remaining sauce over the enchiladas, top with remaining cheese.
Bake at 190C for 20 minutes until bubbly. Top with sour cream and cilantro.
tips: Briefly warm tortillas in oil before rolling to prevent cracking.
Use a mix of cheddar and Monterey Jack for the best melt.

name: Carnitas
description: Slow-braised pork shoulder shredded and crisped, served in tacos with onion and cilantro.
instructions: Cut pork into large chunks, season with salt, pepper, cumin, and oregano.
Place in Dutch oven with orange juice, lime juice, garlic, and quartered onion.
Cover and braise at 160C for 2.5 hours until fork-tender.
Shred pork, spread on a baking sheet, and broil 5 minutes until edges are crispy.
Warm tortillas and fill with carnitas.
Top with diced white onion, cilantro, and a squeeze of lime.
tips: The orange juice tenderizes the pork and adds subtle sweetness.
Broiling the shredded meat at the end creates the signature crispy edges.

name: Tacos al Pastor
description: Marinated pork tacos with pineapple, onion, and cilantro on corn tortillas.
instructions: Soak dried chilies in hot water 15 minutes. Blend with achiote, garlic, vinegar, cumin, and oregano.
Slice pork thinly, marinate in chili sauce for at least 2 hours.
Grill pork slices on high heat until charred, about 3 minutes per side.
Grill pineapple slices until caramelized.
Chop pork and pineapple into small pieces.
Serve in warm corn tortillas with diced onion, cilantro, and lime.
tips: The chili-achiote marinade is what gives al pastor its signature red color.
Charring the pineapple brings out its sweetness.

name: Chiles Rellenos
description: Roasted poblano peppers stuffed with cheese, battered, and fried in a tomato sauce.
instructions: Roast poblanos over an open flame or under broiler until charred. Place in a bag to steam 10 minutes, then peel.
Make a small slit in each pepper and remove seeds. Stuff with cheese.
Separate eggs. Beat whites to stiff peaks, fold in yolks and flour.
Dip stuffed peppers in batter, fry in oil until golden on all sides.
Blend tomatoes with onion, garlic, and cumin for the sauce. Simmer 10 minutes.
Serve peppers in warm tomato sauce.
tips: The egg batter should be fluffy like a cloud — stiff egg whites are essential.
Do not remove the stem — it helps keep the chile intact during frying.

name: Pozole Rojo
description: Traditional Mexican hominy soup with pork in a red chili broth, garnished with cabbage, radish, and lime.
instructions: Simmer pork shoulder in salted water with quartered onion and garlic for 1.5 hours until tender. Shred.
Toast dried chilies in a dry pan, soak in hot water 15 minutes, then blend with garlic and cumin.
Strain chili sauce into the pork broth. Add drained hominy and oregano.
Simmer 20 minutes for flavors to meld.
Serve in deep bowls with shredded cabbage, sliced radishes, lime, and oregano.
Accompany with tostadas on the side.
tips: Toast the chilies just until fragrant — burnt chilies make a bitter sauce.
Pozole always improves the next day.

name: Chicken Mole
description: Chicken braised in a complex, rich mole sauce with chocolate, dried chilies, and spices.
instructions: Toast dried chilies in a dry pan, soak in hot water 20 minutes.
Toast sesame seeds, almonds, and spices in a dry pan until fragrant.
Blend soaked chilies, toasted nuts and seeds, onion, garlic, tomatoes, raisins, cumin, and cinnamon into a smooth paste.
Fry the paste in oil for 5 minutes, stirring constantly.
Add chicken stock and chocolate, stir until melted and combined. Simmer 10 minutes.
Add chicken pieces, cover and braise 40 minutes until chicken is fall-off-the-bone tender. Serve with rice.
tips: Mole has 20+ ingredients — the complexity is the point. Do not simplify.
The chocolate adds richness, not sweetness — use dark, bitter Mexican chocolate.

name: Quesadillas
description: Crispy flour tortillas filled with melted cheese, peppers, onions, and chicken.
instructions: Sauté diced bell pepper, onion, and jalapeño until soft.
Place a tortilla in a dry pan, layer cheese, chicken, and sautéed vegetables.
Top with more cheese and a second tortilla.
Cook on medium heat 2-3 minutes per side until golden and crispy.
Cut into wedges and serve with sour cream and salsa.
tips: Medium heat gives cheese time to melt before the tortilla burns.
Press down gently while cooking for even crisping.

name: Burrito Bowl
description: Deconstructed burrito with cilantro lime rice, black beans, corn, salsa, guacamole, and choice of protein.
instructions: Season chicken with cumin, chili powder, and salt; grill or pan-fry until cooked. Slice.
Mix rice with lime juice and chopped cilantro.
Warm black beans with a pinch of cumin.
Assemble bowls: cilantro-lime rice, black beans, corn, sliced chicken.
Top with sliced avocado, salsa, sour cream, and shredded lettuce.
Squeeze lime over everything and serve.
tips: Cilantro-lime rice is what makes this restaurant quality.
For meal prep, keep wet toppings separate until serving.

name: Mexican Street Corn (Elote)
description: Grilled corn on the cob slathered with mayo, cotija cheese, chili powder, and lime.
instructions: Grill corn over high heat, turning occasionally, until charred in spots (about 10 minutes).
Mix mayo with garlic powder.
Brush grilled corn generously with mayo mixture.
Sprinkle with crumbled cotija cheese and chili powder.
Squeeze lime juice over the corn and garnish with cilantro.
tips: Cotija cheese is salty — go easy on salt elsewhere.
If no grill, broil the corn in the oven for similar char.

name: Huevos Rancheros
description: Fried eggs on corn tortillas with ranchero sauce, black beans, and avocado.
instructions: Make ranchero sauce: sauté diced onion, garlic, and jalapeño in oil. Add tomatoes and cumin, simmer 10 minutes.
Warm black beans in a small pot with a pinch of cumin.
Fry eggs sunny-side-up in a separate pan.
Lightly fry tortillas in oil until just crispy at edges.
Place tortillas on plates, spread with beans, ladle ranchero sauce, top with fried eggs.
Garnish with sliced avocado and cilantro.
tips: The yolks should be runny — they mix with the sauce beautifully.
Cotija cheese crumbled on top is an excellent addition.

name: Chilaquiles
description: Crispy tortilla chips simmered in salsa verde, topped with crema, cheese, and a fried egg.
instructions: Heat salsa verde in a large skillet until simmering.
Add tortilla chips, toss to coat, cook 2-3 minutes until chips soften slightly but still have some crunch.
Fry eggs sunny-side-up in a separate pan.
Plate the chilaquiles, drizzle with crema.
Top with crumbled queso fresco, sliced avocado, sliced onion, and cilantro.
Crown with the fried eggs and serve immediately.
tips: Use thick, sturdy tortilla chips — thin ones disintegrate instantly.
Serve immediately — chilaquiles go from perfect to soggy fast.

name: Tamales
description: Corn masa dough filled with pork in red chili sauce, steamed in corn husks.
instructions: Soak corn husks in warm water for 1 hour. Simmer pork until tender, shred.
Toast and soak chilies, blend with garlic, cumin, and some pork broth. Simmer shredded pork in this sauce.
Beat lard until fluffy, gradually mix in masa harina, stock, baking powder, and salt until a spreadable dough forms.
Spread masa on corn husks, add a spoonful of pork filling, fold and tie.
Stand tamales upright in a steamer, steam for 1-1.5 hours until masa pulls away from husk.
Rest 10 minutes before unwrapping and serving with salsa.
tips: The masa is ready when a small ball floats in water.
Do not overfill — the masa needs room to expand during steaming.

name: Sopes
description: Thick corn masa bases with pinched edges, topped with beans, meat, lettuce, and crema.
instructions: Mix masa harina with water and salt to form a soft dough. Divide into 8 balls.
Flatten each into a thick disc (about 8cm across, 1cm thick).
Cook on a dry griddle for 2 minutes per side until cooked but not crispy.
While still warm, pinch edges up to form a shallow rim.
Lightly fry sopes in oil until golden on the bottom.
Top with warm refried beans, shredded chicken, lettuce, crema, queso fresco, and salsa.
tips: Pinch the edges while the masa is still warm — it hardens as it cools.
The base should be crispy outside but soft inside.

name: Tostadas
description: Crispy flat corn tortillas topped with refried beans, chicken, lettuce, avocado, and salsa.
instructions: Fry tortillas in oil until flat and crispy on both sides; drain on paper towels.
Spread warm refried beans on each tostada.
Layer with shredded chicken and shredded lettuce.
Top with sliced avocado, crumbled queso fresco, a drizzle of crema, and salsa.
Squeeze lime over the top and serve immediately.
tips: Eat tostadas from the edges inward — biting from the center makes everything fall off.
The base must be completely flat and crispy to support the toppings.

name: Carne Asada
description: Marinated grilled skirt steak sliced thin, served with warm tortillas, guacamole, and salsa.
instructions: Blend lime juice, orange juice, garlic, jalapeño, cilantro, cumin, oil, and salt into a marinade.
Marinate steak for at least 1 hour (up to overnight).
Grill over very high heat for 3-4 minutes per side for medium-rare.
Rest 5 minutes, then slice thinly against the grain.
Grill onion slices alongside the steak.
Serve with warm tortillas, grilled onions, guacamole, and salsa.
tips: Skirt steak must be cooked over very high heat for a charred crust.
Always slice against the grain for tender results.

name: Chicken Fajitas
description: Sizzling marinated chicken strips with peppers and onions, served with warm tortillas.
instructions: Slice chicken into strips, marinate with lime juice, cumin, chili powder, paprika, garlic, oil, and salt for 30 minutes.
Heat a cast iron pan until smoking. Cook chicken strips until charred, about 3 minutes per side; set aside.
In the same pan, stir-fry sliced peppers and onions until slightly charred but still crunchy.
Return chicken to the pan, toss together.
Serve sizzling on a hot plate with warm tortillas, sour cream, and guacamole.
tips: A screaming hot pan is essential for the signature fajita char.
Do not crowd the pan — cook in batches for proper searing.

name: Arroz con Pollo
description: One-pot Mexican chicken and rice cooked with tomatoes, peppers, and spices.
instructions: Season chicken with salt and cumin. Brown skin-side down in oil until golden; set aside.
Sauté diced onion, garlic, and bell pepper in the same pot for 3 minutes.
Add rice, stir 1 minute to toast. Add tomatoes, stock, and oregano.
Nestle chicken on top, bring to a boil, then cover and reduce to low.
Cook 20 minutes until rice is tender and chicken is cooked through.
Add peas in the last 5 minutes. Fluff rice and serve.
tips: Do not stir the rice after adding liquid — let it cook undisturbed.
The chicken drippings flavor the rice as it cooks.

name: Mexican Black Beans
description: Slow-simmered black beans with onion, garlic, cumin, and epazote.
instructions: Soak beans overnight in water. Drain and rinse.
Place beans in a pot with quartered onion, whole garlic cloves, jalapeño, and epazote.
Cover with water by 5cm, bring to a boil, then simmer 1-1.5 hours until creamy.
Add salt, cumin, and olive oil in the last 15 minutes of cooking.
Mash some beans against the side of the pot for a creamier texture.
Serve garnished with cilantro and a squeeze of lime.
tips: Do not add salt until the beans are nearly done — salt toughens the skins early on.
Epazote is the traditional Mexican herb for beans — oregano is a good substitute.

name: Guacamole with Chips
description: Chunky fresh guacamole with ripe avocados, lime, cilantro, onion, and jalapeño, served with tortilla chips.
instructions: Halve avocados and scoop flesh into a bowl. Mash with a fork to desired chunkiness.
Fold in finely diced onion, jalapeño (seeds removed for less heat), and diced tomato.
Add lime juice, chopped cilantro, and salt. Stir gently.
Taste and adjust salt and lime.
Serve immediately with tortilla chips.
tips: Leave some chunks — guacamole should not be a smooth paste.
Press plastic wrap directly onto the surface to prevent browning if storing.

name: Churros
description: Crispy fried dough sticks coated in cinnamon sugar, served with chocolate dipping sauce.
instructions: Bring water, butter, 1 tbsp sugar, and salt to a boil. Remove from heat, stir in flour until a smooth dough forms.
Cool slightly, beat in egg and vanilla until smooth and pipeable.
Heat oil to 180C. Pipe dough through a star-tipped piping bag directly into oil (cut with scissors).
Fry 3-4 minutes, turning, until deep golden and crispy. Drain on paper towels.
Roll hot churros in cinnamon-sugar mixture (remaining sugar + cinnamon).
Make chocolate sauce: melt chocolate with cream. Serve churros with chocolate for dipping.
tips: The star tip is essential — it creates ridges that crisp up beautifully.
Fry at 180C exactly — too low and they absorb oil, too high and they burn outside before cooking inside.

name: Bibimbap
description: Korean mixed rice bowl with assorted vegetables, beef, gochujang sauce, and a fried egg.
instructions: Marinate sliced beef in soy sauce, sesame oil, and garlic for 15 minutes.
Blanch spinach and bean sprouts separately, squeeze dry, season with sesame oil and salt.
Sauté julienned carrot, zucchini, and mushrooms separately with a touch of oil and salt.
Cook marinated beef in a hot pan until browned.
Assemble: hot rice in a bowl, arrange vegetables and beef in sections on top.
Fry eggs sunny-side-up, place on top. Serve with gochujang and sesame seeds. Mix before eating.
tips: Each vegetable is cooked separately to preserve its individual flavor and texture.
For dolsot bibimbap, use a hot stone bowl to get crispy rice at the bottom.

name: Bulgogi
description: Korean marinated beef grilled or pan-fried with pear, soy, garlic, and sesame.
instructions: Slice beef very thinly against the grain (partially freeze for easier slicing).
Blend pear, soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and onion into a marinade.
Marinate beef for at least 1 hour (up to overnight).
Cook in a very hot pan or on a grill in batches until caramelized, about 2 minutes per side.
Garnish with sliced spring onions and sesame seeds.
Serve with steamed rice and lettuce wraps.
tips: The pear is a natural meat tenderizer — do not skip it.
Do not crowd the pan — caramelization requires high heat and space.

name: Kimchi Jjigae
description: Spicy Korean kimchi stew with pork belly, tofu, and vegetables simmered in a rich broth.
instructions: Cut pork belly into bite-sized pieces. Sauté in a pot with sesame oil until fat renders.
Add chopped kimchi and its juice, stir-fry 3 minutes.
Add gochugaru, gochujang, soy sauce, and garlic; stir 1 minute.
Pour in water or stock, add sliced onion, bring to a boil.
Reduce heat, simmer 15 minutes. Add cubed tofu, simmer 5 more minutes.
Garnish with sliced spring onion. Serve bubbling hot with rice.
tips: Use well-aged, sour kimchi — fresh kimchi is too mild for jjigae.
The stew should be served still bubbling in the pot.

name: Japchae
description: Korean glass noodles stir-fried with vegetables, beef, soy sauce, and sesame oil.
instructions: Cook glass noodles according to package, drain, cut into manageable lengths, toss with 1 tbsp sesame oil and 1 tbsp soy sauce.
Sauté marinated beef (soy sauce, sugar, garlic) until browned; set aside.
Sauté each vegetable separately with a pinch of salt: carrot, onion, mushrooms, bell pepper, spinach.
Make a thin egg crepe, slice into strips.
Combine everything in a large bowl, add remaining soy sauce, sugar, and sesame oil.
Toss gently, garnish with sesame seeds and egg strips. Serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Cooking each vegetable separately is key to authentic japchae.
Glass noodles are best cut with scissors after cooking.

name: Tteokbokki
description: Chewy Korean rice cakes in a sweet and spicy gochujang sauce with fish cakes.
instructions: If rice cakes are frozen, soak in water 15 minutes to soften.
Bring anchovy stock to a boil with gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, and garlic.
Add rice cakes, cook 8-10 minutes, stirring frequently, until cakes are soft and sauce thickens.
Add sliced fish cakes and halved hard-boiled eggs, cook 3 more minutes.
The sauce should be thick and coat the rice cakes.
Garnish with sliced spring onion and serve.
tips: Stir frequently to prevent the rice cakes from sticking to the bottom.
The sauce thickens as the rice cake starch releases — add water if it gets too thick.

name: Korean Fried Chicken
description: Double-fried crispy chicken coated in a sweet-spicy gochujang glaze.
instructions: Pat wings dry. Coat with a mix of cornstarch, flour, salt, and pepper.
Fry at 160C for 8 minutes until cooked but pale. Drain and rest 10 minutes.
Fry again at 180C for 5 minutes until deeply golden and extra crispy.
Make sauce: cook gochujang, soy sauce, honey, garlic, ginger, and rice vinegar until thick.
Toss fried chicken in the sauce, drizzle sesame oil.
Garnish with sesame seeds and serve immediately.
tips: Double frying is the secret to Korean fried chicken — the rest between fries lets moisture escape from the crust.
Toss in sauce just before serving — sitting in sauce makes the coating soggy.

name: Sundubu Jjigae
description: Spicy Korean soft tofu stew with seafood, vegetables, and a cracked egg.
instructions: Heat sesame oil in a small pot, sauté diced onion and garlic.
Add gochugaru and gochujang, stir 30 seconds.
Pour in anchovy stock, bring to a boil. Add sliced zucchini.
Add clams and shrimp, cook 3 minutes.
Gently slide in silken tofu in large spoonfuls (do not stir too much).
Simmer 5 minutes, crack an egg on top, cover for 1 minute. Serve bubbling with rice.
tips: Silken tofu is extremely delicate — add it gently and do not stir vigorously.
The stew should be served still bubbling in its pot.

name: Dakgalbi
description: Spicy Korean stir-fried chicken with vegetables, sweet potato, and rice cakes in a gochujang sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces. Mix gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, garlic, and ginger into a marinade.
Marinate chicken for 30 minutes.
Slice sweet potato, cabbage, and onion. Soak rice cakes if using dried.
Stir-fry chicken in sesame oil for 5 minutes.
Add sweet potato and rice cakes, cook 5 minutes with a splash of water.
Add cabbage and onion, stir-fry 5 minutes until everything is cooked. Garnish with perilla leaves.
tips: Dakgalbi is traditionally cooked on a large flat pan at the table.
Add mozzarella cheese in the center for a popular modern variation.

name: Samgyeopsal (Grilled Pork Belly)
description: Thick-cut pork belly grilled at the table, wrapped in lettuce with garlic, ssamjang, and kimchi.
instructions: Slice pork belly into 1cm thick pieces.
Grill on a hot pan or grill plate until crispy and golden on both sides, about 4 minutes per side.
Slice raw garlic cloves. Set up lettuce, perilla leaves, ssamjang, kimchi, and garlic at the table.
Cut cooked pork into bite-sized pieces with scissors.
To eat: place pork in a lettuce leaf, add ssamjang, garlic, and kimchi, wrap and eat in one bite.
Dip in sesame oil mixed with salt and pepper for an alternative.
tips: Cut the pork thick — thin slices dry out on the grill.
The lettuce wrap should be eaten in one bite — Korean ssam tradition.

name: Kimchi Fried Rice
description: Stir-fried rice with aged kimchi, pork, gochujang, and a fried egg on top.
instructions: Dice pork belly, fry in a wok until fat renders and meat is crispy.
Add chopped kimchi and its juice, stir-fry 2 minutes.
Add rice, gochujang, soy sauce, and sesame oil; stir-fry on high heat 3 minutes.
Make a well in the center, crack eggs in and scramble, then mix into rice.
Alternatively, fry eggs separately and place on top.
Garnish with spring onion, sesame seeds, and seaweed flakes.
tips: The kimchi must be well-fermented and sour for the best flavor.
Day-old rice is essential — fresh rice becomes mushy.

name: Doenjang Jjigae
description: Korean fermented soybean paste stew with tofu, zucchini, and vegetables.
instructions: Bring anchovy stock to a boil. Add doenjang paste and stir to dissolve.
Add diced potato, cook 5 minutes until half-tender.
Add diced zucchini, onion, and minced garlic; simmer 5 minutes.
Add cubed tofu and sliced green chili, simmer 5 more minutes.
Sprinkle gochugaru and sliced spring onion.
Serve hot with steamed rice.
tips: Doenjang is intensely savory — a little goes a long way.
This is Korean comfort food at its best — simple but deeply flavorful.

name: Gimbap
description: Korean seaweed rice rolls filled with vegetables, egg, and pickled radish.
instructions: Season warm rice with sesame oil, salt, and sesame seeds.
Blanch spinach, squeeze dry, season with sesame oil. Julienne carrot and sauté briefly.
Make thin egg crepes, roll and slice into strips.
Place seaweed on a bamboo mat, spread rice thinly leaving 2cm at the top edge.
Arrange fillings in a line: spinach, carrot, egg, cucumber, pickled radish.
Roll tightly using the mat, brush outside with sesame oil, slice into 2cm rounds.
tips: Do not overfill — a tight roll is better than a fat, sloppy one.
Wetting the knife between cuts gives clean slices.

name: Galbi (Korean BBQ Short Ribs)
description: Sweet soy-marinated beef short ribs grilled until caramelized and tender.
instructions: Score the meat between bones if using flanken cut.
Blend pear, onion, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, sugar, mirin, sesame oil, and pepper into a marinade.
Marinate ribs for at least 4 hours, preferably overnight.
Grill over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side until caramelized.
Cut between bones into individual pieces.
Garnish with sesame seeds and spring onion, serve with rice and ssamjang.
tips: The pear tenderizes the meat naturally — do not skip it.
High heat and a quick cook give the best result — do not overcook.

name: Jajangmyeon
description: Korean-Chinese noodles in a thick black bean sauce with pork and vegetables.
instructions: Dice all vegetables and pork into 1cm cubes.
Heat oil, fry chunjang paste for 2 minutes to remove raw taste.
Add diced pork, cook until browned.
Add onion, potato, zucchini, and cabbage; stir-fry 3 minutes.
Add 300ml water and sugar, simmer 10 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Thicken with cornstarch slurry. Cook noodles, drain, top with sauce and julienned cucumber.
tips: Frying the black bean paste first removes its raw, bitter edge.
Jajangmyeon should be mixed thoroughly before eating.

name: Haemul Pajeon (Korean Seafood Pancake)
description: Crispy Korean pancake loaded with spring onions, shrimp, squid, and a soy dipping sauce.
instructions: Mix flour, rice flour, egg, ice water, and salt into a thin batter.
Cut spring onions to the length of the pan. Chop shrimp and squid.
Heat oil in a large flat pan. Pour in batter, spread thinly.
Arrange spring onions and seafood on top, press gently into batter.
Cook on medium-high for 3-4 minutes until bottom is golden and crispy.
Flip carefully, cook 3 minutes more. Make dipping sauce: soy sauce, vinegar, sesame oil. Serve hot.
tips: Ice water in the batter makes it extra crispy — similar to tempura technique.
Press the pancake down firmly while cooking for maximum crunch.

name: Greek Moussaka
description: Layered casserole of eggplant, spiced lamb mince, and creamy bechamel sauce baked until golden.
instructions: Slice eggplant into 1cm rounds, salt and rest 30 minutes, pat dry. Brush with oil and grill until golden.
Brown lamb with diced onion and garlic. Add tomatoes, cinnamon, oregano, salt; simmer 20 minutes.
Make bechamel: melt butter, add flour, cook 1 minute. Slowly whisk in milk until thick. Off heat, stir in egg yolk, nutmeg, and parmesan.
Layer a baking dish: eggplant, meat sauce, eggplant, meat sauce.
Pour bechamel over the top, smooth evenly.
Bake at 180C for 40 minutes until golden and bubbling. Rest 15 minutes before cutting.
tips: Resting the moussaka after baking allows it to set and slice cleanly.
Salting the eggplant removes bitterness and excess moisture.

name: Grilled Halloumi Salad
description: Warm grilled halloumi cheese on a bed of mixed greens with roasted vegetables and pomegranate.
instructions: Slice halloumi into 1cm thick slices. Pat dry.
Grill or pan-fry halloumi in a dry or lightly oiled pan until golden, about 2 minutes per side.
Arrange mixed greens on a platter with diced cucumber, halved tomatoes, sliced onion, and pepper.
Place warm halloumi on top.
Dress with olive oil, lemon juice, oregano, and salt.
Scatter pomegranate seeds and serve immediately while halloumi is warm.
tips: Halloumi must be served warm — it becomes rubbery as it cools.
A high-heat, dry pan gives the best golden crust.

name: Spanakopita
description: Flaky Greek phyllo pastry filled with spinach, feta, dill, and onion.
instructions: Wilt spinach in a pan, squeeze out all water, and chop finely.
Sauté diced onion until soft. Mix with spinach, crumbled feta, eggs, chopped dill, spring onion, nutmeg, salt, and pepper.
Melt butter with olive oil. Layer 6 phyllo sheets in a baking dish, brushing each with butter-oil mix.
Spread spinach filling evenly. Top with 6 more phyllo sheets, each brushed with butter-oil.
Score the top layers into squares or diamonds.
Bake at 180C for 35-40 minutes until deeply golden and flaky.
tips: Squeeze every drop of water from the spinach — wet filling makes soggy pastry.
Score only the top layers before baking so it is easy to cut after.

name: Lemon Herb Salmon
description: Baked salmon fillets with lemon, garlic, dill, and olive oil served with roasted vegetables.
instructions: Place salmon on a parchment-lined baking sheet with asparagus and tomatoes around it.
Drizzle olive oil, squeeze lemon juice, scatter minced garlic and dill over everything.
Season with salt and pepper. Lay lemon slices on top of salmon.
Bake at 200C for 12-15 minutes until salmon is just cooked (slightly translucent in the center).
The vegetables should be tender and slightly charred.
Serve immediately with the pan juices spooned over.
tips: Do not overcook salmon — it should be just cooked through, slightly pink in the center.
Skin-on salmon holds together better during baking.

name: Chicken Souvlaki
description: Greek marinated chicken skewers grilled and served in pita with tzatziki, tomato, and onion.
instructions: Cut chicken into 3cm cubes. Marinate in olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, oregano, salt, and pepper for 1 hour.
Thread onto skewers.
Make tzatziki: grate cucumber, squeeze dry, mix with yogurt, garlic, lemon juice, and salt.
Grill skewers over high heat, turning, until charred and cooked through, about 10 minutes.
Warm pita bread on the grill.
Serve chicken in pita with tzatziki, sliced tomato, and red onion.
tips: Marinate at least 1 hour — overnight is even better.
Do not over-pack the skewers — leave small gaps for even cooking.

name: Chicken Katsu
description: Crispy panko-breaded chicken cutlet served with tonkatsu sauce, shredded cabbage, and rice.
instructions: Pound chicken breasts to 1.5cm even thickness. Season with salt and pepper.
Set up breading station: flour, beaten eggs, panko breadcrumbs.
Coat chicken in flour, dip in egg, press into panko, ensuring full coverage.
Shallow fry in oil at 170C for 4-5 minutes per side until deep golden.
Drain on a wire rack. Rest 3 minutes, then slice into 2cm strips.
Serve with shredded cabbage, tonkatsu sauce, lemon wedge, and steamed rice.
tips: Press panko firmly onto the chicken for a thick, crunchy crust.
Shallow frying works perfectly — no need to deep fry.

name: Gyudon (Beef Bowl)
description: Thinly sliced beef simmered with onions in a sweet soy-dashi broth, served over rice.
instructions: Slice onion into thin half-moons.
Combine dashi, soy sauce, mirin, sugar, sake, and ginger in a pan; bring to a simmer.
Add onion, cook 5 minutes until soft and translucent.
Add beef slices, spread out in a single layer, cook 3-4 minutes.
Optionally crack eggs over the top, cover 2 minutes for soft-set eggs.
Serve over hot rice, garnished with pickled ginger and spring onion.
tips: Slice beef as thin as possible — partially freeze it for easier slicing.
The broth should just coat the rice, not make it soupy.

name: Yakitori
description: Japanese grilled chicken skewers glazed with a sweet soy tare sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into 3cm pieces and spring onion into 3cm lengths.
Make tare sauce: simmer soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar until reduced by half and syrupy.
Thread chicken and spring onion alternately onto skewers.
Grill over high heat, turning frequently, for 8-10 minutes.
Brush with tare sauce in the last 2 minutes, allowing it to caramelize.
Sprinkle shichimi togarashi and serve immediately.
tips: Baste with tare at the end only — too early and the sugar burns.
Chicken thigh is essential — breast dries out over high heat.

name: Okonomiyaki
description: Japanese savory cabbage pancake with pork, topped with okonomiyaki sauce, mayo, and bonito flakes.
instructions: Shred cabbage finely. Mix flour, eggs, and dashi into a batter.
Fold in cabbage, tenkasu, and pickled ginger gently — do not overmix.
Heat oil in a large pan. Pour batter into a round shape about 2cm thick.
Lay pork belly slices on top. Cook on medium 5 minutes until bottom is golden.
Flip carefully (use a plate to help). Cook pork side down 5 more minutes.
Top with okonomiyaki sauce, mayo in zigzag lines, bonito flakes, and aonori.
tips: Do not press down on the pancake while cooking — it should be thick and fluffy.
The bonito flakes dance in the heat — that is normal and part of the presentation.

name: Tonkotsu Ramen
description: Rich, creamy pork bone broth ramen with chashu pork, soft-boiled egg, and nori.
instructions: Heat tonkotsu broth with soy sauce, mirin, minced garlic, and ginger. Simmer 10 minutes.
Cook ramen noodles according to package directions. Drain.
Slice chashu pork, warm briefly in the simmering broth.
Halve the soft-boiled eggs (yolks should be jammy, not hard).
Divide noodles into bowls, ladle hot broth over.
Top with chashu, egg halves, corn, sliced spring onion, nori, and sesame seeds.
tips: The broth should be milky white and rich — this comes from long boiling of pork bones.
A jammy egg (6.5 minutes boiled, then ice bath) is the ramen standard.

name: Tempura
description: Light, crispy battered shrimp and vegetables fried to golden perfection, served with tentsuyu dipping sauce.
instructions: Make tentsuyu: combine dashi, soy sauce, and mirin; warm gently.
Mix flour and cornstarch. Beat egg with ice water. Combine with flour — mix briefly, leaving lumps.
Heat oil to 180C. Peel and devein shrimp, score belly to prevent curling.
Slice vegetables into thin pieces for even cooking.
Dip in batter lightly, fry in batches: vegetables 2-3 minutes, shrimp 2 minutes until pale golden.
Serve immediately with tentsuyu and grated daikon.
tips: Ice-cold batter and lumps are essential — overmixed batter becomes heavy and doughy.
Fry in small batches to maintain oil temperature.

name: Udon Noodle Soup
description: Thick wheat noodles in a hot dashi-soy broth topped with tempura bits, spring onion, and kamaboko.
instructions: Bring dashi stock to a simmer with soy sauce and mirin.
Cook udon noodles according to package (boil for frozen, rinse for fresh). Drain.
Rehydrate wakame in warm water for 5 minutes if using dried.
Divide noodles into bowls, ladle hot broth over.
Top with kamaboko slices, tenkasu, wakame, and sliced spring onion.
Sprinkle shichimi togarashi to taste and serve hot.
tips: Do not overcook udon — they should be chewy, not mushy.
The broth should be light and clear, not heavy.

name: Miso Glazed Salmon
description: Salmon fillets marinated in sweet white miso glaze, broiled until caramelized.
instructions: Mix miso, mirin, sake, sugar, and soy sauce into a smooth marinade.
Coat salmon fillets with marinade, cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours (up to 24 hours).
Wipe off excess marinade (it will burn under the broiler).
Broil salmon on high for 6-8 minutes until the top is deeply caramelized and fish is cooked through.
Drizzle sesame oil, garnish with spring onion and sesame seeds.
Serve with steamed rice and pickled vegetables.
tips: Wipe off excess marinade before broiling to prevent burning.
The longer you marinate, the deeper the flavor penetrates.

name: Chirashi Bowl
description: Scattered sushi bowl with assorted sashimi, vegetables, and seasoned rice.
instructions: Cook sushi rice. While hot, fold in rice vinegar mixed with sugar and salt. Fan to cool.
Slice sashimi into uniform pieces.
Slice cucumber, avocado, and tamagoyaki.
Spread seasoned rice in a wide bowl.
Arrange all toppings artfully on top of the rice — scatter, do not stack.
Garnish with nori strips. Serve with soy sauce, wasabi, and pickled ginger.
tips: Use the freshest sashimi-grade fish available.
The rice must be at room temperature, not hot or cold.

name: Japanese Curry Rice
description: Thick, sweet Japanese curry with chicken, potatoes, carrots, and onion served over rice.
instructions: Cut chicken, potatoes, carrots, and onions into large chunks.
Heat oil, sauté onions until golden (10 minutes). Add chicken, cook until sealed.
Add carrots and potatoes, stir 2 minutes.
Add water, bring to a boil, simmer 15 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Turn off heat, add curry roux blocks, stir until dissolved.
Simmer on low for 5 more minutes until thick. Serve over rice with fukujinzuke.
tips: Add the roux off the heat so it dissolves evenly without lumping.
Japanese curry improves dramatically the next day.

name: Takoyaki
description: Crispy-outside, creamy-inside octopus balls with takoyaki sauce, mayo, and bonito flakes.
instructions: Mix flour, egg, and dashi into a thin, smooth batter.
Heat takoyaki pan, brush with oil. Fill each hole 3/4 full with batter.
Place a piece of octopus, tenkasu, pickled ginger, and spring onion in each.
Top off with more batter. Cook 2 minutes until edges set.
Use a pick to rotate each ball 90 degrees. Continue rotating until round and golden all over.
Drizzle with takoyaki sauce and mayo, top with bonito flakes and aonori.
tips: A proper takoyaki pan is essential — a regular pan will not work.
The batter should be very thin and liquid — thicker than crepe batter is too thick.

name: Edamame
description: Steamed young soybeans in their pods, tossed with flaky sea salt.
instructions: Bring a pot of salted water to a boil.
Add frozen edamame, cook 3-4 minutes until tender.
Drain well, toss with sesame oil and flaky sea salt.
Serve warm as a snack or appetizer.
Eat by squeezing pods to pop out the beans.
tips: Do not overcook — edamame should be bright green and slightly firm.
A touch of chili flakes or garlic makes an excellent variation.

name: Onigiri
description: Japanese rice balls filled with salmon, umeboshi, or tuna mayo, wrapped in nori.
instructions: Season cooked rice with a pinch of salt. Let it cool until handleable but still warm.
Prepare fillings: flake cooked salmon, pit umeboshi.
Wet hands with salted water. Take a handful of rice, make an indent, add filling.
Shape into a triangle by cupping with both hands, pressing gently but firmly.
Wrap a strip of nori around the base.
Sprinkle with sesame seeds and serve.
tips: Wet hands with salted water to prevent sticking.
The rice should be warm, not hot or cold — warm rice molds best.

name: Cold Soba Noodles
description: Chilled buckwheat noodles served on a bamboo mat with tsuyu dipping sauce.
instructions: Cook soba noodles according to package. Drain and rinse under cold running water until completely cold.
Make tsuyu: combine dashi, soy sauce, and mirin; chill.
Place cold soba on a bamboo mat or plate.
Garnish with nori strips and sesame seeds.
Serve tsuyu in a small cup alongside with sliced spring onion, wasabi, and grated daikon.
Dip a few noodles at a time into the sauce and eat.
tips: Rinse soba thoroughly under cold water to remove starch for the best texture.
Slurping is encouraged — it aerates the noodles and enhances flavor.

name: Teriyaki Salmon
description: Pan-seared salmon glazed with a sweet and savory homemade teriyaki sauce.
instructions: Mix soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar into teriyaki sauce.
Pat salmon dry, season with salt. Heat oil in a pan over medium-high heat.
Place salmon skin-side down, cook 4 minutes until skin is crispy.
Flip, pour teriyaki sauce into the pan. Cook 3 minutes, spooning sauce over salmon.
The sauce should reduce to a thick, glossy glaze.
Serve over rice, garnished with spring onion and sesame seeds.
tips: Start skin-side down for crispy skin.
Let the sauce reduce and caramelize — do not add too much at once.

name: Pho Bo (Vietnamese Beef Pho)
description: Aromatic beef broth with rice noodles, thinly sliced beef, bean sprouts, basil, and lime.
instructions: Char onions and ginger over an open flame until blackened. Toast star anise, cinnamon, and cloves in a dry pan.
Blanch bones in boiling water 5 minutes, drain, and rinse.
Simmer bones with charred aromatics and toasted spices in 2L water for 1 hour, skimming froth.
Season broth with fish sauce and rock sugar. Strain.
Cook rice noodles according to package; drain.
Slice beef paper-thin. Place noodles in bowls, top with raw beef, ladle boiling broth over (it cooks the beef). Serve with bean sprouts, basil, lime, hoisin, and sriracha.
tips: Charring the onion and ginger gives pho its distinctive smoky sweetness.
The broth must be boiling when ladled to cook the raw beef.

name: Banh Mi
description: Vietnamese crusty baguette sandwich with pork, pickled daikon and carrot, jalapeño, and cilantro.
instructions: Quick-pickle daikon and carrot: julienne, toss with vinegar, sugar, and salt, rest 20 minutes.
Season pork with soy sauce and fish sauce. Pan-fry or grill until cooked, slice thinly.
Split baguettes lengthwise, toast until crispy outside but soft inside.
Spread pate on one side and mayo on the other.
Layer sliced pork, pickled vegetables, cucumber slices, jalapeño, and cilantro.
Press lightly and serve.
tips: The bread should be crispy and light — a proper Vietnamese baguette, not a dense French one.
The contrast of rich meat and tangy pickles is what makes banh mi special.

name: Bun Cha
description: Hanoi-style grilled pork patties and belly with rice noodles, herbs, and a tangy dipping broth.
instructions: Mix ground pork with 1 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp sugar, and minced garlic. Form into small patties.
Slice pork belly thin, marinate with 1 tbsp fish sauce and sugar.
Grill patties and belly slices over high heat until charred.
Make dipping broth: mix remaining fish sauce, sugar, vinegar, lime juice, sliced chili, and 100ml warm water. Add julienned carrot.
Cook rice vermicelli, drain and rinse cold.
Serve noodles and herbs on a plate, grilled pork on the side, and dipping broth in a bowl.
tips: The charring on the pork is essential — it should be slightly caramelized, not just cooked.
Dip the noodles and pork into the broth as you eat.

name: Goi Cuon (Fresh Spring Rolls)
description: Vietnamese fresh rice paper rolls with shrimp, pork, vermicelli, herbs, and peanut dipping sauce.
instructions: Cook vermicelli, drain and cool. Slice pork thinly. Halve shrimp lengthwise.
Make peanut sauce: warm hoisin with peanut butter, garlic, chili, and enough water to thin. Add lime juice.
Dip one rice paper in warm water until just pliable (5 seconds, no more).
Place lettuce, herbs, vermicelli, and pork in the lower third. Fold bottom up, fold sides in.
Place shrimp along the fold line (pink side against the wrapper so they show through).
Roll tightly. Serve with peanut dipping sauce.
tips: Do not over-soak the rice paper — it becomes too soft and tears. Just 5 seconds.
Place shrimp facing out so the pink shows through the translucent wrapper.

name: Cao Lau
description: Hoi An specialty of thick chewy noodles with pork, greens, crispy croutons, and a light broth.

ingredients.name: cao lau noodles or thick udon

instructions: Marinate pork in soy sauce, five-spice, sugar, and garlic for 30 minutes. Roast at 200C for 20 minutes. Slice thinly.
Fry wonton wrapper strips in oil until crispy for croutons.
Cook noodles according to package; drain.
Mix a small amount of soy sauce with a splash of the pork cooking juices for a light sauce.
Assemble bowls: noodles, a few spoonfuls of sauce (not soupy), sliced pork, bean sprouts, and lettuce.
Top with crispy croutons and fresh herbs. Squeeze lime over.
tips: Cao lau is served with very little liquid — it is not a soup.
The crispy croutons add essential texture contrast.

name: Com Tam (Broken Rice)
description: Vietnamese broken rice with grilled pork chop, fried egg, pickled vegetables, and fish sauce dressing.
instructions: Blend lemongrass, garlic, shallot, 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp sugar, and oil. Marinate pork chops 1 hour.
Cook broken rice (cook like regular rice with slightly less water).
Grill pork chops over high heat until charred and cooked through, about 5 minutes per side.
Fry eggs sunny-side-up.
Make nuoc cham: mix remaining fish sauce, sugar, lime juice, chili, and 3 tbsp water.
Plate: rice, grilled pork, fried egg, cucumber, tomato, pickled vegetables. Drizzle nuoc cham.
tips: Broken rice has a unique texture — regular rice is not a substitute.
The nuoc cham dressing ties everything together — it should be sweet, salty, sour, and spicy.

name: Bun Bo Hue
description: Spicy Vietnamese beef noodle soup from Hue with lemongrass, shrimp paste, and thick rice noodles.
instructions: Blanch beef and pork bones, drain and rinse. Cover with fresh water and simmer 1.5 hours.
Pound lemongrass, add to broth along with charred onion. Simmer.
Mix shrimp paste with chili oil, add to broth. Season with fish sauce and sugar.
Remove beef shank, slice thinly. Strain broth.
Cook noodles according to package; drain into bowls.
Ladle broth over noodles, top with beef slices. Serve with bean sprouts, basil, banana blossom, lime, and chili.
tips: Bun bo Hue should be noticeably spicy — the chili oil is not optional.
Shrimp paste gives the broth its unique funky depth.

name: Canh Chua (Vietnamese Sour Soup)
description: Tangy tamarind soup with fish, tomatoes, pineapple, okra, and bean sprouts.
instructions: Dissolve tamarind paste in 1L of warm water, strain out solids.
Bring tamarind broth to a simmer, add tomato wedges and pineapple chunks.
Add sliced taro stem or celery, and okra. Simmer 5 minutes.
Add fish pieces (cut into chunks), cook 3-4 minutes until just done.
Season with fish sauce and sugar. Add bean sprouts.
Garnish with fried garlic, Thai basil, and sliced chili. Serve with steamed rice.
tips: Add fish last — it breaks apart if cooked too long.
The soup should be notably sour — adjust tamarind to taste.

name: Banh Xeo
description: Crispy Vietnamese crepe filled with shrimp, pork, bean sprouts, and herbs, wrapped in lettuce.
instructions: Mix rice flour, coconut milk, turmeric, a pinch of salt, and enough water for a thin batter. Rest 15 minutes.
Make dipping sauce (nuoc cham): fish sauce, sugar, lime juice, water, and sliced chili.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan. Add pork and shrimp, cook 2 minutes.
Pour a thin layer of batter, swirl to coat. Add bean sprouts and spring onion.
Cover and cook 3-4 minutes until bottom is crispy and golden.
Fold in half. Wrap pieces of crepe with lettuce and herbs, dip in nuoc cham.
tips: The crepe should be paper-thin and shatteringly crispy.
Wrapping in lettuce and herbs before dipping is the traditional way to eat it.

name: Vietnamese Lemongrass Chicken
description: Caramelized chicken stir-fried with lemongrass, garlic, and chili in a sweet fish sauce glaze.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces. Mince lemongrass, garlic, shallot, and chili.
Marinate chicken with fish sauce, sugar, lemongrass, garlic, and pepper for 30 minutes.
Heat oil in a wok until very hot. Add chicken in a single layer, cook without stirring 3 minutes for caramelization.
Stir-fry 3-4 minutes more until chicken is cooked and sauce is sticky.
Add sliced chili and shallot, toss 1 minute.
Garnish with spring onion, serve with steamed rice.
tips: Do not stir immediately — let the chicken caramelize for a few minutes first.
Use the tender inner part of the lemongrass stalk only.

name: Beef Bourguignon
description: Classic French beef stew braised in red wine with mushrooms, pearl onions, bacon, and herbs.
instructions: Cut beef into 5cm cubes, pat dry. Brown deeply in oil in batches; set aside.
Cook diced bacon until crispy. Sauté diced onion, carrot, and garlic in bacon fat.
Add tomato paste, cook 1 minute. Sprinkle flour, stir 1 minute.
Add wine and stock, scraping up browned bits. Add thyme, bay leaves, and beef.
Cover and braise in oven at 160C for 2 hours.
Meanwhile, sauté mushrooms and pearl onions in butter. Add to stew in the last 30 minutes. Serve with mashed potatoes.
tips: Use a decent drinking wine — if you would not drink it, do not cook with it.
Deep browning of the beef is the foundation of all flavor in this dish.

name: Coq au Vin
description: French braised chicken in red wine with mushrooms, bacon lardons, and pearl onions.
instructions: Brown chicken pieces skin-side down until deeply golden. Set aside.
Cook lardons until crispy. Add carrot, garlic, and onions, sauté 5 minutes.
Add tomato paste and flour, stir 1 minute. Deglaze with wine.
Add stock, thyme, bay leaf, and chicken. Bring to a simmer.
Cover and braise at 160C for 1 hour until chicken is falling off the bone.
Sauté mushrooms in butter, add to stew. Adjust seasoning, garnish with parsley.
tips: Marinate chicken in wine overnight for even deeper flavor.
The sauce should be rich and glossy — reduce uncovered if too thin.

name: Chicken Schnitzel
