description: Thin, crispy breaded chicken cutlet pounded flat and pan-fried until golden.
instructions: Butterfly chicken breasts, open flat. Pound between plastic wrap to 5mm thickness.
Season with salt and pepper. Set up breading: flour, beaten eggs, breadcrumbs.
Coat chicken in flour, dip in egg, press into breadcrumbs.
Heat oil and butter in a large pan. Fry schnitzels 3 minutes per side until deep golden.
Drain on paper towels.
Serve immediately with lemon wedges, parsley, and a side salad or potato salad.
tips: Pounding thin is the most important step — uneven thickness means uneven cooking.
Shake the pan gently while frying to create a wavy, extra-crispy crust.

name: Hungarian Goulash
description: Rich paprika-spiced beef stew with potatoes, onions, and caraway seeds.
instructions: Dice onions finely. Cook slowly in oil until deeply golden, about 15 minutes.
Remove from heat, stir in paprika quickly (it burns fast).
Add cubed beef, stir to coat, cook 5 minutes.
Add garlic, tomato paste, caraway, and bell pepper. Pour in stock.
Simmer 1.5 hours until beef is tender. Add cubed potatoes and carrots in the last 30 minutes.
Serve with a dollop of sour cream and crusty bread.
tips: Cook onions low and slow — they are the base of all flavor.
Add paprika off the heat to prevent burning — burnt paprika turns bitter.

name: Fish and Chips
description: Beer-battered cod fried until crispy, served with thick-cut chips, mushy peas, and tartar sauce.
instructions: Cut potatoes into thick chips, rinse starch, pat dry. Fry at 150C for 5 minutes, drain, rest 10 minutes. Fry again at 190C until golden.
Mix flour, cornstarch, baking powder, and salt. Whisk in cold beer until smooth.
Season fish, dust with flour, dip in batter.
Deep fry at 180C for 5-6 minutes until golden and crispy. Drain on a rack.
Make mushy peas: cook peas, mash with butter, season.
Serve fish and chips with mushy peas, lemon wedges, and malt vinegar.
tips: Double frying chips is essential — once to cook through, once to crisp.
Ice-cold beer in the batter makes it light and crispy.

name: Shepherd's Pie
description: Savory lamb mince in gravy topped with creamy mashed potatoes, baked until golden.
instructions: Boil potatoes until tender, mash with butter, milk, salt, and pepper.
Brown lamb mince, breaking apart. Add diced onion, carrot, celery, and garlic; cook 5 minutes.
Add tomato paste, Worcestershire, thyme, and stock. Simmer 15 minutes until thick.
Stir in peas. Pour into a baking dish.
Spoon mashed potato on top, spread evenly, fork the surface for texture.
Bake at 200C for 25 minutes until top is golden and filling is bubbling.
tips: Fork marks on the mashed potato top create crispy peaks.
Use lamb for shepherd's pie, beef for cottage pie — they are different dishes.

name: Beef Stroganoff
description: Tender beef strips in a creamy mushroom and sour cream sauce, served over egg noodles.
instructions: Slice beef into thin strips. Season with salt, pepper, and paprika.
Sear beef in butter over very high heat in batches; set aside (do not cook through).
Sauté mushrooms until golden, add onion and garlic, cook 3 minutes.
Sprinkle flour, stir 1 minute. Add stock and mustard, simmer 5 minutes.
Return beef, cook 2 minutes. Remove from heat, stir in sour cream.
Serve immediately over cooked egg noodles, garnished with parsley.
tips: Add sour cream off the heat to prevent curdling.
Sear beef in small batches on very high heat for browning, not steaming.

name: Swedish Meatballs
description: Tender pork and beef meatballs in a creamy gravy, served with mashed potatoes and lingonberry jam.
instructions: Soak breadcrumbs in milk. Mix with beef, pork, grated onion, egg, allspice, nutmeg, and salt.
Roll into small balls (about 3cm). Brown in butter in batches; set aside.
Make gravy: add flour to pan drippings, cook 1 minute. Gradually whisk in stock.
Add cream and soy sauce, simmer until thickened. Season to taste.
Return meatballs to gravy, simmer 10 minutes until cooked through.
Serve with mashed potatoes, lingonberry jam, and pickled cucumber.
tips: The allspice and nutmeg are what make these distinctly Swedish.
Small, uniform meatballs cook evenly — use a small scoop.

name: Pierogi
description: Polish dumplings filled with potato and cheese, boiled then pan-fried in butter with onions.
instructions: Make dough: mix flour, egg, sour cream, water, and salt. Knead until smooth, rest 30 minutes.
Boil potatoes until tender, mash with cheese, salt, pepper, and 1 tbsp butter.
Roll dough thin (2mm). Cut circles with a glass. Place 1 tbsp filling in center, fold, seal edges firmly.
Boil pierogi in salted water for 3-4 minutes (they float when done).
Pan-fry in butter until golden on both sides.
Serve with caramelized onions (sliced onion cooked slowly in butter until deep golden) and sour cream.
tips: Seal edges firmly by pressing with a fork — any gap and they burst while boiling.
The pan-fry after boiling adds essential crispy texture.

name: Paella
description: Spanish saffron rice with shrimp, mussels, chicken, chorizo, and vegetables.
instructions: Bloom saffron in warm stock. Season and brown chicken pieces in oil; set aside.
Sauté diced chorizo, onion, garlic, and bell pepper. Add grated tomato and paprika.
Add rice, stir 1 minute to toast. Pour in saffron stock.
Nestle chicken into rice. Bring to a boil, then simmer undisturbed 15 minutes.
Add shrimp and mussels, pushing into rice. Add peas. Cook 5-8 minutes until mussels open.
Turn up heat for 2 minutes for socarrat (crispy bottom). Rest 5 minutes. Serve with lemon wedges.
tips: Never stir paella once the liquid is added — stirring prevents socarrat.
Socarrat (the crispy rice crust at the bottom) is the prize — turn up heat at the end.

name: Croque Monsieur
description: French grilled ham and cheese sandwich with bechamel sauce and melted Gruyere.
instructions: Make bechamel: melt butter, add flour, cook 1 minute. Slowly whisk in milk until thick. Season with nutmeg and salt.
Spread mustard on two slices of bread. Layer ham and grated Gruyere.
Top with second slice. Spread bechamel on top and sides, top with more Gruyere.
Place on a baking sheet under the broiler.
Broil 3-5 minutes until cheese is bubbly and golden.
Serve immediately while cheese is still melting.
tips: The bechamel is what distinguishes this from a regular ham and cheese sandwich.
For a Croque Madame, add a fried egg on top.

name: Wiener Schnitzel
description: Traditional Austrian veal cutlet pounded thin, breaded, and fried until golden and puffy.
instructions: Pound veal cutlets between plastic wrap to 4mm thickness.
Season with salt. Set up breading: flour, beaten eggs, fine breadcrumbs.
Coat cutlets: flour, egg, breadcrumbs. Do not press crumbs on — keep the coating loose.
Heat oil and butter in a large pan. Fry cutlets, spooning hot fat over the top to puff the coating.
Fry 2-3 minutes per side until golden. Drain on a rack.
Serve immediately with lemon wedges, parsley, and lingonberry jam.
tips: A loose breadcrumb coating that puffs away from the meat is the hallmark of great schnitzel.
Spooning hot fat over the top while frying creates the signature puffy crust.

name: Sauerbraten
description: German pot roast marinated in vinegar and spices for days, braised until tender with a gingersnap gravy.
instructions: Combine vinegar, wine, sliced onion, carrot, celery, spices, sugar, and 200ml water. Marinate beef 3-5 days in the fridge, turning daily.
Remove beef, pat dry, strain marinade and reserve vegetables and liquid separately.
Brown beef deeply on all sides. Add reserved vegetables, cook 5 minutes.
Add strained marinade. Cover and braise at 160C for 2.5-3 hours until fork-tender.
Remove beef. Crumble gingersnap cookies into the sauce, simmer until thick.
Slice beef, serve with gravy. Traditional sides: red cabbage and potato dumplings.
tips: The 3-5 day marinade is not optional — it transforms the beef.
Gingersnap cookies are the traditional thickener for the gravy.

name: Bangers and Mash
description: British pork sausages with creamy mashed potatoes and rich onion gravy.
instructions: Brown sausages in oil on all sides, then bake at 200C for 15 minutes until cooked through.
Boil potatoes until tender, mash with butter, milk, salt, and pepper.
For onion gravy: slowly cook sliced onions in butter until deep golden, about 15 minutes.
Add flour, stir 1 minute. Add stock and Worcestershire sauce, simmer until thick.
Pile mashed potatoes on plates, top with sausages.
Ladle onion gravy generously over everything. Serve with mustard.
tips: Low and slow onion cooking is what makes the gravy rich and sweet.
Use good-quality pork sausages with high meat content.

name: Chicken Tikka Masala
description: British-Indian classic of tandoori-spiced chicken pieces in a creamy tomato-fenugreek sauce.
instructions: Marinate chicken cubes in yogurt, half the garam masala, turmeric, chili, ginger, and garlic for 1 hour.
Grill or broil chicken until charred. Can also pan-fry.
Melt butter, sauté diced onion until golden. Add remaining spices, cook 30 seconds.
Add canned tomatoes, simmer 15 minutes, then blend until smooth.
Return sauce to pan, add cream, sugar, fenugreek, and salt. Simmer 5 minutes.
Add chicken pieces, simmer 5 more minutes. Garnish with cilantro, serve with naan or rice.
tips: Charring the marinated chicken is essential for authentic flavor.
Dried fenugreek leaves (kasoori methi) add the signature British-Indian flavor.

name: BBQ Pulled Pork
description: Slow-smoked pork shoulder pulled apart and tossed in tangy BBQ sauce, served on brioche buns.
instructions: Mix paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, cumin, cayenne, mustard powder, brown sugar, and salt into a rub.
Apply rub generously all over pork shoulder.
Place in a slow cooker with apple cider vinegar. Cook on low for 8-10 hours or high for 4-5 hours.
Shred pork with two forks. Discard excess liquid but keep some for moisture.
Mix shredded pork with BBQ sauce.
Pile onto brioche buns and top with coleslaw.
tips: The pork is done when it shreds effortlessly with a fork.
Reserve some unsauced pork — let people add their own level of sauce.

name: New England Clam Chowder
description: Creamy soup loaded with clams, potatoes, celery, and bacon in a thick cream base.
instructions: Cook diced bacon until crispy; set aside, keep the fat.
In bacon fat and butter, sauté diced onion, celery, and garlic until soft.
Add flour, stir 2 minutes. Gradually add clam juice and milk, stirring constantly.
Add cubed potatoes, bay leaf, and thyme. Simmer 15 minutes until potatoes are tender.
Add clams, cream, and cooked bacon. Heat through (do not boil).
Season with salt and white pepper. Serve in bread bowls with oyster crackers.
tips: Do not boil after adding cream — it can curdle.
The clam juice is where most of the flavor comes from — do not discard it.

name: Chicken Pot Pie
description: Creamy chicken and vegetable filling baked under a golden puff pastry crust.
instructions: Poach chicken in stock until cooked; shred. Reserve stock.
Dice potato, carrot, celery, and onion. Sauté in butter until softened.
Add flour, stir 1 minute. Gradually add reserved stock and milk until thick.
Add shredded chicken, peas, and thyme. Season with salt and pepper. Pour into a baking dish.
Top with puff pastry, tuck edges, brush with egg wash. Cut steam vents.
Bake at 200C for 25-30 minutes until pastry is puffed and deep golden.
tips: Cut vents in the pastry to let steam escape and keep it flaky.
The filling should be thick enough to hold its shape when served.

name: Baked Mac and Cheese
description: Creamy three-cheese macaroni baked with a crunchy breadcrumb topping.
instructions: Cook macaroni until just al dente; drain.
Melt butter, add flour, cook 1 minute. Gradually whisk in milk until smooth and thick.
Off heat, stir in cheddar, Gruyere, cream cheese, mustard powder, and salt until melted.
Fold in cooked macaroni. Pour into a buttered baking dish.
Top with panko mixed with melted butter and paprika.
Bake at 190C for 20 minutes until golden and bubbly.
tips: Three cheeses gives depth — sharp cheddar for flavor, Gruyere for nuttiness, cream cheese for creaminess.
Cook pasta slightly under al dente — it finishes cooking in the oven.

name: Buffalo Wings
description: Crispy baked chicken wings tossed in Frank's hot sauce and butter glaze with blue cheese dip.
instructions: Pat wings completely dry. Toss with baking powder, salt, pepper, and garlic powder.
Arrange in a single layer on a wire rack over a baking sheet.
Bake at 220C for 45 minutes, flipping halfway, until crispy.
Melt butter, mix with hot sauce.
Toss baked wings in the buffalo sauce until coated.
Serve with celery, carrot sticks, and blue cheese dressing.
tips: Baking powder on dry wings creates crispiness rivaling deep-fried.
Do not sauce the wings until just before serving — the crust stays crispiest.

name: Jambalaya
description: Cajun one-pot rice dish with andouille sausage, chicken, shrimp, and the holy trinity of vegetables.
instructions: Brown sliced sausage and seasoned chicken in oil; set aside.
Sauté the holy trinity: diced onion, celery, and bell pepper with garlic.
Add Cajun seasoning, tomatoes, and bay leaf. Stir 2 minutes.
Add rice and stock. Return sausage and chicken. Bring to a boil.
Cover, reduce to low, cook 20 minutes without stirring.
Add shrimp in the last 5 minutes. Fluff with a fork, garnish with spring onion.
tips: Do not stir once the rice is in — let it develop a crust on the bottom.
Add shrimp at the end so they do not overcook.

name: Cobb Salad
description: Composed American salad with grilled chicken, bacon, avocado, hard-boiled egg, blue cheese, and tomato.
instructions: Chop romaine lettuce, spread on a large platter.
Cook bacon until crispy; crumble.
Slice chicken, halve eggs, dice avocado, halve tomatoes.
Arrange ingredients in neat rows across the lettuce: chicken, bacon, eggs, avocado, tomatoes, blue cheese.
Drizzle vinaigrette over the salad.
Garnish with chopped chives, serve immediately.
tips: A Cobb salad is traditionally composed in rows, not tossed.
The vinaigrette can be served on the side for a lighter option.

name: Po' Boy Sandwich
description: New Orleans sandwich of crispy fried shrimp on French bread with lettuce, tomato, and remoulade.
instructions: Peel and devein shrimp. Soak in buttermilk for 15 minutes.
Mix cornmeal, flour, and Cajun seasoning. Dredge shrimp in the mixture.
Deep fry at 180C for 2-3 minutes until golden and crispy; drain.
Make remoulade: mix mayo, hot sauce, pickle relish, and lemon juice.
Split bread, spread remoulade on both sides.
Layer with lettuce, tomato, and fried shrimp. Serve immediately.
tips: Cornmeal gives the shrimp that signature crispy, crunchy coating.
The bread should be crusty outside but soft and fluffy inside.

name: Cornbread
description: Southern-style golden cornbread baked in a cast iron skillet with buttermilk and honey.
instructions: Preheat oven to 220C with a cast iron skillet inside.
Mix cornmeal, flour, baking powder, baking soda, and salt.
Whisk buttermilk, egg, honey, and melted butter. Combine with dry ingredients until just mixed.
Remove hot skillet, add remaining butter to sizzle, pour in batter.
Bake 18-22 minutes until golden and a toothpick comes out clean.
Cool 5 minutes in skillet, then slice and serve with butter and honey.
tips: Preheating the skillet with butter gives cornbread its signature crispy crust.
Do not over-mix — lumpy batter is fine.

name: Shrimp and Grits
description: Creamy stone-ground grits topped with sauteed shrimp, bacon, and a lemony butter sauce.
instructions: Cook grits in stock and milk, stirring frequently, for 20 minutes until thick and creamy. Stir in cheese and butter.
Cook diced bacon until crispy; set aside, keep the fat.
Season shrimp with Cajun seasoning. Sauté in bacon fat 2 minutes per side until pink.
Add garlic, cook 30 seconds. Add lemon juice and 2 tbsp butter; swirl to make a sauce.
Spoon creamy grits into bowls.
Top with shrimp and sauce, crumbled bacon, and sliced spring onion.
tips: Use stone-ground grits, not instant — the texture difference is dramatic.
The lemony butter sauce ties everything together.

name: Xiaolongbao
description: Shanghai-style soup dumplings with pork filling and rich broth inside
instructions: Make aspic by chilling pork stock with gelatin, chop into cubes.
Mix pork with ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, scallions, and aspic cubes.
Knead dough, rest 30 min, roll thin wrappers and pleat around filling.
Steam dumplings for 8-10 minutes until skin is translucent.
tips: Serve on cabbage leaves to prevent sticking.
Dip in black vinegar with ginger shreds.

name: Sichuan Boiled Fish (Shui Zhu Yu)
description: Tender white fish poached in fiery chili oil broth with bean sprouts
instructions: Blanch bean sprouts and place in a deep bowl.
Poach fish slices in seasoned broth until just cooked, lay over sprouts.
Fry dried chilies and Sichuan peppercorns in oil until fragrant.
Pour sizzling oil over the fish and serve immediately.
tips: Use firm white fish like bass or tilapia so it holds together.
Adjust chili quantity to your heat tolerance.

name: Cantonese Roast Duck
description: Crispy-skinned roast duck glazed with maltose and five-spice
instructions: Rub duck cavity with five-spice, ginger, and star anise.
Blanch duck in boiling water, then coat skin with maltose-vinegar glaze.
Hang dry for 4 hours or use a fan to dry the skin.
Roast at 200C for 90 minutes, basting occasionally until skin is crisp.
tips: Air-drying the skin is the key to crispiness.
Serve with steamed buns and hoisin sauce.

name: Har Gow (Crystal Shrimp Dumplings)
description: Translucent steamed shrimp dumplings, a classic dim sum staple
instructions: Chop shrimp coarsely, mix with diced bamboo shoots, sesame oil, and pepper.
Make dough with wheat starch and boiling water, knead until smooth.
Roll thin wrappers, fill with shrimp mixture, pleat edges shut.
Steam for 6-8 minutes until wrappers turn translucent.
tips: Use boiling water for the dough to activate the starch.
Work quickly as the dough dries out fast.

name: Char Siu Bao (BBQ Pork Buns)
description: Fluffy steamed buns filled with sweet Cantonese BBQ pork
instructions: Make dough with flour, yeast, sugar, and warm water; let rise 1 hour.
Dice char siu pork, cook with oyster sauce, hoisin, sugar, and cornstarch slurry.
Divide dough into portions, flatten, fill with pork, and pinch shut.
Steam buns for 12 minutes until puffy and cooked through.
tips: Let buns rest 5 minutes before steaming so they puff evenly.
Use cake flour for a softer, whiter bun.

name: Sichuan Dry Pot Chicken (Gan Guo Ji)
description: Spicy dry-fried chicken with vegetables and numbing Sichuan peppers
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-size pieces, marinate with soy sauce 15 minutes.
Fry potato slices until golden, set aside.
Stir-fry chicken with ginger, chilies, and peppercorns until crispy.
Add celery and potato back, toss together and serve in a hot pot.
tips: Keep the heat high for a dry, slightly charred texture.
Add lotus root or cauliflower for variety.

name: Zhajiangmian (Beijing Fried Sauce Noodles)
description: Thick wheat noodles topped with savory fermented bean paste and pork sauce
instructions: Cook noodles according to package, drain and rinse with cold water.
Fry ground pork until browned, add bean pastes with a splash of water.
Simmer sauce until thick and glossy, about 5 minutes.
Top noodles with sauce, julienned cucumber, and scallions.
tips: Tian mian jiang (sweet bean paste) is the authentic sauce base.
Shredded radish and edamame are traditional garnishes.

name: Kung Pao Shrimp
description: Spicy stir-fried shrimp with peanuts, dried chilies, and Sichuan pepper
instructions: Marinate shrimp with cornstarch and a splash of soy sauce.
Stir-fry dried chilies and peppercorns in oil until fragrant.
Add shrimp, cook until pink, then add sauce of soy, vinegar, and sugar.
Toss in peanuts and scallions, serve immediately.
tips: Devein shrimp but keep tails on for presentation.
Toast peanuts first for extra crunch.

name: Hunan Orange Chicken
description: Crispy fried chicken in tangy orange and chili glaze, Hunan-style
instructions: Cut chicken into pieces, coat in cornstarch.
Deep fry chicken until golden and crispy, drain on paper towels.
Stir-fry garlic and chilies, add orange juice, zest, and soy sauce.
Toss crispy chicken in the glaze and serve hot.
tips: Use fresh orange juice for the best flavor.
Double-fry the chicken for extra crispiness.

name: Mapo Eggplant
description: Silky eggplant braised in spicy doubanjiang and Sichuan pepper sauce
instructions: Cut eggplant into chunks, salt and drain for 10 minutes.
Fry eggplant until softened and lightly browned.
Stir-fry garlic, ginger, and doubanjiang until fragrant.
Add eggplant back with soy sauce and a splash of water, braise 5 minutes.
tips: Salting draws out moisture for better texture.
Garnish with Sichuan pepper oil for extra numbing heat.

name: Tomato and Egg Stir-Fry
description: Silky scrambled eggs with juicy tomatoes in sweet-savory sauce
instructions: Beat eggs with a pinch of salt, scramble in hot oil until just set, remove.
Cook diced tomatoes in the same pan until saucy and soft.
Return eggs to the pan, add sugar, toss gently to combine.
Garnish with scallions and serve with rice.
tips: Use ripe, juicy tomatoes for the best sauce.
Don't overcook the eggs; they should be soft and custardy.

name: Sichuan Wontons in Chili Oil (Hong You Chao Shou)
description: Pork wontons drenched in spicy, numbing chili oil sauce
instructions: Mix pork with soy sauce, sesame oil, and minced ginger for filling.
Wrap small amounts of filling in wonton skins, sealing edges with water.
Boil wontons until they float, about 3-4 minutes.
Toss in sauce of chili oil, soy sauce, garlic, sesame paste, and peppercorn.
tips: Make extra and freeze uncooked wontons for quick meals.
Adjust chili oil to your spice preference.

name: Salt and Pepper Tofu
description: Crispy fried tofu cubes tossed with chili, garlic, and Sichuan salt
instructions: Press tofu, cut into cubes, coat generously in cornstarch.
Deep fry until golden and crispy on all sides.
Stir-fry garlic, chilies, and scallions briefly.
Toss crispy tofu with the aromatics and peppercorn salt.
tips: Press tofu well for maximum crispiness.
Sichuan peppercorn salt is made by toasting peppercorns with salt and grinding.

name: Cantonese Steamed Spare Ribs with Black Beans
description: Tender pork ribs steamed with fermented black beans and garlic
instructions: Chop ribs into bite-size pieces, rinse and pat dry.
Marinate with mashed black beans, garlic, soy sauce, sugar, and cornstarch.
Arrange on a plate, top with sliced chili.
Steam over high heat for 20 minutes until tender.
tips: Rinse fermented black beans briefly to reduce saltiness.
This is a classic dim sum dish.

name: Bang Bang Chicken (Bon Bon Ji)
description: Cold shredded chicken in creamy sesame-chili sauce, Sichuan appetizer
instructions: Poach chicken breast in gently simmering water for 15 minutes, cool.
Shred chicken by hand into thin strips.
Mix sesame paste, chili oil, soy sauce, vinegar, and sugar into a smooth sauce.
Arrange chicken over sliced cucumber, drizzle with sauce.
tips: Poach gently to keep the chicken tender and juicy.
Let chicken cool in the poaching liquid for extra moisture.

name: Drunken Chicken (Zui Ji)
description: Cold poached chicken marinated in Shaoxing wine, a Shanghai classic
instructions: Poach chicken legs with ginger and scallions until cooked through.
Plunge into ice water to stop cooking and tighten the skin.
Submerge chicken in mixture of Shaoxing wine, poaching broth, salt, and sugar.
Refrigerate overnight, chop and serve cold.
tips: Must marinate at least 8 hours for the wine flavor to penetrate.
Use the best quality Shaoxing wine you can find.

name: Braised Sea Cucumber with Scallions (Cong Shao Hai Shen)
description: Shandong banquet dish of sea cucumber braised with caramelized scallions
instructions: Blanch sea cucumber in ginger water to remove any odor.
Fry scallion whites in oil until deeply caramelized and fragrant.
Add sea cucumber, soy sauce, wine, sugar, and stock; braise 20 minutes.
Reduce sauce until glossy, garnish with scallion greens.
tips: Sea cucumber must be fully rehydrated over several days before cooking.
This is considered a prestige dish in Shandong cuisine.

name: La Zi Ji (Chongqing Chicken with Chilies)
description: Crispy fried chicken pieces buried in a mountain of dried chilies
instructions: Cut chicken into small cubes, marinate with soy sauce and cornstarch.
Deep fry chicken until golden and very crispy.
In a wok, fry dried chilies and peppercorns until darkened and fragrant.
Add chicken back, toss vigorously so flavors coat every piece.
tips: The chilies are for flavor, not eating — pick around them.
Sichuan peppercorns give the characteristic numbing sensation.

name: Three Cup Chicken (San Bei Ji)
description: Taiwanese-Chinese braised chicken with soy sauce, sesame oil, and rice wine
instructions: Cut chicken into bone-in pieces, pat dry.
Fry chicken in sesame oil until golden on all sides.
Add garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and rice wine; braise covered for 20 minutes.
Uncover, reduce sauce until thick, toss in basil leaves and serve.
tips: Equal parts soy, sesame oil, and wine is the classic 'three cups' ratio.
Thai basil is essential — do not substitute with Italian basil.

name: Squirrel-Shaped Mandarin Fish (Song Shu Gui Yu)
description: Jiangsu classic: scored fish deep-fried into a squirrel shape with sweet-sour sauce
instructions: Score fish flesh in a crosshatch pattern without cutting the skin.
Coat fish heavily in cornstarch, shaking off excess.
Deep fry at high heat until flesh puffs out like a squirrel tail.
Pour hot sweet-sour sauce with peas and shrimp over the fish.
tips: The crosshatch scoring must be deep and precise for the puff effect.
Pour the sauce tableside while sizzling for dramatic presentation.

name: Ants Climbing a Tree (Ma Yi Shang Shu)
description: Glass noodles stir-fried with seasoned pork mince in spicy bean sauce
instructions: Soak glass noodles in warm water until pliable, drain.
Stir-fry pork mince until browned, add doubanjiang and ginger.
Add noodles and soy sauce, toss until noodles absorb all the sauce.
Garnish with scallions and serve.
tips: The 'ants' are the pork bits clinging to the noodle 'trees'.
Don't oversoak the noodles or they'll become mushy.

name: Yu Xiang Shredded Pork (Fish-Fragrant Pork)
description: Sichuan sweet-sour-spicy shredded pork with wood ear mushrooms and bamboo
instructions: Shred pork, marinate with soy sauce and cornstarch.
Soak wood ear mushrooms, shred along with bamboo shoots.
Stir-fry pork until just cooked, add doubanjiang and garlic.
Add vegetables, pour in sweet-sour sauce, toss until glossy.
tips: Despite the name, there is no fish — the flavor profile mimics fish seasoning.
Balance of sweet, sour, salty, and spicy is key.

name: Beggar's Chicken (Jiao Hua Ji)
description: Whole chicken stuffed and wrapped in lotus leaf and clay, then baked
instructions: Season chicken inside and out with wine, soy sauce, and five-spice.
Stuff cavity with ginger and scallions.
Wrap tightly in lotus leaves, then encase in a thick layer of clay or salt dough.
Bake at 180C for 2 hours, crack open the clay at the table.
tips: If no clay, use a thick salt dough crust as a substitute.
The lotus leaf imparts a unique smoky-sweet fragrance.

name: Dong Po Pork (Dongpo Rou)
description: Hangzhou braised pork belly slow-cooked until melt-in-mouth tender
instructions: Cut pork belly into large squares, blanch in boiling water.
Line pot with scallions and ginger, place pork skin-side down.
Add soy sauce, wine, and sugar; bring to a boil, then simmer on low for 2 hours.
Flip pork skin-side up, serve with the reduced braising liquid.
tips: Low and slow is essential — do not rush the braise.
Named after the Song Dynasty poet Su Dongpo.

name: Tea-Smoked Duck (Zhang Cha Ya)
description: Sichuan duck smoked with tea leaves, camphor, and rice for deep aroma
instructions: Rub duck with salt, five-spice, and peppercorns; cure overnight in fridge.
Steam duck for 40 minutes until cooked through.
Line a wok with foil, add tea, rice, and sugar; smoke duck over medium heat 15 minutes.
Optionally deep-fry briefly for crispy skin before serving.
tips: Smoke in a well-ventilated area or use a range hood.
The three smoking ingredients give the characteristic complex flavor.

name: Clay Pot Rice (Bo Zai Fan)
description: Cantonese rice cooked in a clay pot with Chinese sausage and crispy rice crust
instructions: Soak rice 30 minutes, place in oiled clay pot with water and ginger.
Cook on medium heat until water is absorbed, lay sausage slices on top.
Cover and cook on low 10 minutes to form the crispy rice crust (guo ba).
Drizzle soy sauce and sesame oil, add blanched bok choy and fried egg.
tips: The prized part is the crispy scorched rice on the bottom.
Can substitute with a heavy-bottomed pot if no clay pot available.

name: Hainanese Chicken Rice
description: Poached chicken served with fragrant rice cooked in chicken fat and broth
instructions: Poach whole chicken in ginger-scallion water until just cooked, ice bath immediately.
Cook rice in chicken fat with garlic, using the poaching broth as liquid.
Chop chicken into pieces, arrange over cucumber slices.
Serve with chili sauce, ginger paste, and dark soy on the side.
tips: Ice bath after poaching gives the chicken silky, gelatinous skin.
The rice should be fragrant and slightly oily from the chicken fat.

name: Cold Sesame Noodles (Liang Mian)
description: Chilled wheat noodles tossed in creamy sesame paste and chili oil dressing
instructions: Cook noodles, rinse under cold water and drain well.
Whisk sesame paste with soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and chili oil until smooth.
Toss noodles with the sauce until evenly coated.
Top with shredded cucumber and serve cold.
tips: Thin the sauce with a splash of noodle cooking water if too thick.
Add crushed peanuts for extra texture.

name: Beef Chow Fun (Gan Chao Niu He)
description: Cantonese stir-fried flat rice noodles with beef and bean sprouts
instructions: Slice beef thinly, marinate with soy sauce and cornstarch.
Stir-fry beef on highest heat until just seared, set aside.
Add noodles to the wok with soy sauces, toss vigorously for wok hei.
Return beef, add bean sprouts and scallions, toss 30 seconds and serve.
tips: Maximum heat (wok hei) is essential for this dish.
Separate stuck noodles gently before cooking.

name: Smashed Cucumber Salad (Pai Huang Gua)
description: Crunchy smashed cucumbers in garlic, chili oil, and black vinegar dressing
instructions: Smash cucumbers with the flat side of a knife, break into rough pieces.
Salt cucumbers and let drain for 10 minutes, squeeze out water.
Mix garlic, chili oil, vinegar, sesame oil, and sugar into a dressing.
Toss cucumbers with the dressing and serve immediately.
tips: Smashing creates irregular surfaces that grab more sauce.
Serve within 30 minutes or cucumbers become watery.

name: Singapore Noodles (Xing Zhou Mi Fen)
description: Curry-spiced rice vermicelli stir-fried with shrimp, pork, and peppers
instructions: Soak rice vermicelli in warm water until pliable, drain.
Scramble eggs, set aside. Stir-fry shrimp and sliced pork until cooked.
Add vermicelli and curry powder, toss on high heat.
Mix in bell pepper, bean sprouts, and egg, serve immediately.
tips: Despite the name, this dish was invented in Hong Kong.
Use Madras curry powder for the most authentic flavor.

name: Chinese Steamed Egg Custard (Zheng Dan)
description: Silky smooth steamed egg custard with soy sauce and sesame oil
instructions: Beat eggs gently, mix with warm water (1:1.5 ratio), strain through a sieve.
Pour into a heat-proof bowl, cover with plastic wrap.
Steam on medium-low heat for 12-15 minutes until just set.
Drizzle with soy sauce, sesame oil, and scallions.
tips: Straining the mixture removes air bubbles for a silky texture.
Steam on low heat — high heat causes bubbles and a rough surface.

name: Xinjiang Cumin Lamb Ribs
description: Grilled lamb ribs seasoned with cumin, chili flakes, and Sichuan pepper
instructions: Cut ribs into individual pieces, pat dry.
Toast cumin, chili, and peppercorns, grind coarsely.
Rub ribs with oil, garlic, salt, and spice mix.
Grill or pan-fry over high heat until charred and cooked through.
tips: These are a popular Xinjiang street food sold at night markets.
Lamb shoulder chops work as a substitute for ribs.

name: Wood Ear Mushroom Salad (Liang Ban Mu Er)
description: Cold wood ear mushrooms dressed with vinegar, chili, and cilantro
instructions: Soak wood ear mushrooms in warm water 30 minutes, trim tough stems.
Blanch mushrooms in boiling water 1 minute, drain and cool.
Toss with garlic, chili oil, vinegar, soy sauce, and cilantro.
Serve cold as an appetizer.
tips: Wood ears should be crunchy — do not overcook.
A classic Northern Chinese cold dish served before the main meal.

name: Suan La Fen (Hot and Sour Sweet Potato Noodles)
description: Chewy sweet potato noodles in tangy, spicy broth with peanuts and pickles
instructions: Soak sweet potato noodles in hot water until chewy-soft.
Mix chili oil, vinegar, soy sauce in a bowl to make the broth base.
Add noodles to the bowl, ladle hot water or stock over them.
Top with crushed peanuts, preserved greens, and scallions.
tips: Chongqing-style calls for extra chili oil and Sichuan pepper.
Adjust vinegar-to-chili ratio to your preference.

name: Gua Bao (Braised Pork Belly Buns)
description: Steamed folded buns stuffed with braised pork belly, pickled greens, and peanuts
instructions: Braise pork belly with soy sauce, five-spice, sugar, and water for 45 minutes until tender.
Steam bao buns until fluffy and warm.
Slice pork belly, tuck into buns with pickled greens, peanuts, and cilantro.
Serve immediately while buns are warm.
tips: Gua bao is popular street food across Taiwan and China.
The contrast of rich pork with tangy pickles is essential.

name: Fish-Fragrant Eggplant (Yu Xiang Qie Zi)
description: Sichuan braised eggplant in sweet, sour, and spicy sauce with no fish
instructions: Cut eggplant into strips, fry in oil until softened and golden.
Stir-fry doubanjiang, garlic, and ginger until fragrant.
Add eggplant back with vinegar, sugar, soy sauce, and a splash of water.
Simmer until sauce is thick, garnish with scallions.
tips: Yu xiang (fish-fragrant) is a Sichuan flavor profile, not a fish dish.
The balance of sweet, sour, salty, and spicy should be equal.

name: Char Kway Teow
description: Smoky stir-fried flat rice noodles with shrimp, Chinese sausage, and egg
instructions: Heat wok until smoking, add oil and fry sausage slices.
Push aside, crack eggs and scramble roughly.
Add noodles and soy sauces, toss on highest heat for wok hei.
Add shrimp, bean sprouts, and chives, toss 1 minute and serve.
tips: Wok hei (breath of the wok) is the signature smoky flavor.
Use the widest, hottest burner you have.

name: Chicken Lettuce Wraps (Sang Choy Bao)
description: Cantonese minced chicken in crispy lettuce cups with water chestnuts
instructions: Stir-fry ground chicken with garlic until cooked through.
Add diced water chestnuts, hoisin sauce, soy sauce, and sesame oil.
Cook until sauce is absorbed and mixture is slightly dry.
Spoon into crisp lettuce cups and serve immediately.
tips: Water chestnuts add essential crunch to the filling.
Use butter lettuce if iceberg is unavailable.

name: Turnip Cake (Lo Bak Go)
description: Pan-fried Cantonese radish cake with Chinese sausage and dried shrimp
instructions: Grate daikon, cook in a pot until soft, reserving the liquid.
Mix rice flour with daikon liquid into a batter, stir in diced sausage and shrimp.
Pour into a greased pan, steam for 45 minutes until firm.
Cool, slice, and pan-fry until golden and crispy on both sides.
tips: A must-have at dim sum and during Chinese New Year.
Better the next day after refrigerating — firms up for crispier frying.

name: Dan Dan Chicken Noodles
description: Spicy Sichuan noodles with ground chicken in sesame-chili sauce
instructions: Cook noodles, drain and divide into bowls.
Brown ground chicken with preserved vegetables until crispy.
Mix sesame paste, chili oil, peppercorn, and soy sauce in each bowl.
Top noodles with chicken crumbles, toss and eat.
tips: Ya cai (preserved mustard stems) is the traditional vegetable used.
Adjust the ratio of sesame to chili for your preference.

name: Century Egg Tofu
description: Cold silken tofu topped with century egg, soy sauce, and sesame oil
instructions: Gently unmold silken tofu onto a plate.
Dice century eggs and arrange on top.
Drizzle with soy sauce, sesame oil, and chili oil.
Garnish with scallions and serve cold.
tips: A classic Chinese appetizer that requires zero cooking.
Use Japanese silken tofu for the smoothest texture.

name: Twice-Cooked Tofu (Hui Guo Doufu)
description: Vegetarian take on the Sichuan classic with pan-fried tofu and leeks
instructions: Slice tofu thickly, pan-fry until golden on both sides.
Stir-fry leek slices and garlic until softened.
Add doubanjiang and sweet bean paste, fry until oil turns red.
Add tofu back, toss gently with soy sauce until well coated.
tips: The original uses pork belly — tofu is the vegetarian adaptation.
Doubanjiang from Pixian county is considered the best.

name: Pineapple Bun (Bo Lo Bao)
description: Hong Kong sweet bun with a crumbly cookie crust, no actual pineapple
instructions: Make bread dough with flour, yeast, sugar, egg, and milk; let rise 1 hour.
Make cookie topping by creaming butter, sugar, egg, flour, and custard powder.
Shape dough into buns, press a disc of cookie topping on each.
Score topping in crosshatch, bake at 180C for 15 minutes until golden.
tips: Named for the crackled top that resembles pineapple skin.
For the classic Hong Kong style, slice open and add a cold butter slab.

name: Hong Kong Egg Tart (Dan Tat)
description: Flaky pastry shells filled with silky smooth egg custard
instructions: Make pastry by rubbing butter into flour, bind with cold water, chill 30 minutes.
Whisk eggs, sugar, warm milk, and vanilla, strain for smoothness.
Press pastry into tart molds, fill with custard to 80% full.
Bake at 200C for 18-20 minutes until custard is just set with slight wobble.
tips: Strain the custard twice for the smoothest texture.
A dim sum classic — must be served warm.

name: Red Bean Soup (Hong Dou Tang)
description: Cantonese sweet red bean soup with dried tangerine peel, served warm or cold
instructions: Soak red beans overnight, drain and rinse.
Simmer beans with tangerine peel in water for 45 minutes until soft.
Add rock sugar, stir until dissolved.
Serve warm with a drizzle of coconut milk.
tips: A traditional Cantonese dessert soup (tong sui).
Can be served chilled in summer.

name: Jianbing (Chinese Breakfast Crepe)
description: Savory street food crepe with egg, crispy wonton, and hoisin-chili sauce
instructions: Mix flours with water into a thin batter, spread on a hot flat griddle.
Crack an egg on top, spread it thin, sprinkle scallions.
Flip, spread hoisin and chili sauce, place crispy wonton in the center.
Fold crepe over the filling and serve immediately.
tips: The most popular street breakfast in Northern China.
Mung bean flour gives the characteristic flavor.

name: Soy Milk with Youtiao
description: Fresh warm soy milk paired with golden fried dough sticks
instructions: Soak soybeans overnight, blend with water and strain for fresh soy milk.
Simmer soy milk, sweeten lightly with sugar.
Make dough with flour, baking powder, salt; rest 2 hours.
Stretch dough into strips, deep-fry in pairs until golden and puffy.
tips: A classic Chinese breakfast combination.
Dip youtiao into soy milk — sweet or savory soy milk both work.

name: Sheng Jian Bao (Shanghai Pan-Fried Buns)
description: Pan-fried pork buns with crispy bottoms and juicy soup inside
instructions: Make yeast dough, let rise 1 hour until doubled.
Mix pork with ginger, soy sauce, sesame oil, and chilled stock for filling.
Fill and pleat buns, rest 15 minutes.
Pan-fry in oiled skillet with lid and water until bottoms are golden crisp.
tips: The combination of frying and steaming gives the signature crispy-fluffy texture.
Sprinkle sesame seeds and scallions on top before serving.

name: Malatang (Sichuan Spicy Hot Pot Soup)
description: Build-your-own spicy soup with skewered vegetables, tofu, and meats
instructions: Dissolve ma la paste in boiling water or stock to make spicy broth.
Prepare ingredients: slice tofu, clean vegetables, soak noodles.
Cook ingredients in the broth by type — hard vegetables first, leafy greens last.
Ladle into bowls, drizzle with sesame paste.
tips: Malatang is the street food version of Sichuan hot pot.
Customize spice level by adjusting the amount of ma la paste.

name: Stir-Fried Water Spinach with Garlic (Chao Kong Xin Cai)
description: Wok-fried water spinach with garlic, a staple Cantonese vegetable dish
instructions: Cut water spinach into 3-inch pieces, separating stems from leaves.
Heat wok until smoking, add oil and garlic, fry 10 seconds.
Add stems first, stir-fry 1 minute, then add leaves.
Season with soy sauce, salt, and chili, toss 30 seconds and serve.
tips: Cook on the highest heat possible for the best wok hei.
Hollow stems cook slower than leaves — stagger the timing.

name: Kung Pao Tofu
description: Sichuan stir-fried tofu with peanuts, chilies, and sweet-savory sauce
instructions: Press and cube tofu, coat lightly in cornstarch and pan-fry until golden.
Stir-fry chilies and peppercorns in oil until fragrant.
Add sauce of soy, vinegar, and sugar, then the tofu.
Toss in peanuts and scallions, serve immediately.
tips: Press tofu thoroughly for the best crispy texture.
A satisfying vegetarian version of the Sichuan classic.

name: Sichuan Hot Pot
description: Communal spicy broth pot for cooking meats, seafood, and vegetables at the table
instructions: Dissolve hot pot base in boiling water in a communal pot.
Prepare platters of thinly sliced meats, tofu, vegetables, and noodles.
Bring broth to a simmer at the table, cook ingredients by dipping.
Make dipping sauce with sesame oil, garlic, cilantro, and chili.
tips: Thinly sliced meats cook in seconds — don't overcook.
Split pot (yuan yang) lets you have spicy and mild sides.

name: White Cut Chicken (Bai Qie Ji)
description: Cantonese poached chicken with silky skin, served with ginger-scallion oil
instructions: Bring a large pot of water to boil with ginger slices.
Submerge chicken, return to a gentle simmer for 30 minutes.
Plunge into ice water immediately for 15 minutes.
Chop chicken, serve with ginger-scallion oil (hot oil poured over minced ginger and scallions).
tips: Ice bath is critical for the characteristic jelly-like skin.
The chicken should be just barely cooked — slightly pink near bones is traditional.

name: Tanghulu (Candied Fruit Skewers)
description: Beijing street snack of hawthorn berries coated in crackly sugar glaze
instructions: Thread hawthorn berries onto bamboo skewers.
Boil sugar and water until it reaches hard crack stage (150C).
Dip skewered fruit into the hot sugar syrup, rotating to coat.
Place on oiled parchment to cool and harden.
tips: Use a candy thermometer for perfect sugar stage.
Strawberries and grapes are popular modern substitutes for hawthorn.

name: Mango Pudding
description: Silky Cantonese dim sum dessert of chilled mango with coconut cream
instructions: Blend mango flesh until smooth.
Dissolve gelatin in warm water, stir in sugar until dissolved.
Mix gelatin liquid with mango puree and coconut cream.
Pour into molds, refrigerate 4 hours until set.
tips: Use the ripest mangoes for the sweetest, most fragrant result.
A staple dessert at Hong Kong dim sum restaurants.

name: Chinese Tea Eggs (Cha Ye Dan)
description: Hard-boiled eggs marinated in soy sauce, star anise, and black tea
instructions: Hard boil eggs, cool, then gently crack shells all over without peeling.
Simmer soy sauce, tea, spices, sugar, and salt in water.
Add cracked eggs to the liquid, simmer on low for 1 hour.
Let eggs steep in liquid overnight in the fridge for best pattern and flavor.
tips: The marble pattern comes from the cracked shell — crack evenly for best results.
A popular convenience store snack across China and Taiwan.

name: Chinese Zucchini Pancakes (Xi Hu Lu Bing)
description: Savory shredded zucchini pancakes with egg, a Northern Chinese breakfast
instructions: Grate zucchini, salt and squeeze out excess water.
Mix with eggs, flour, scallions, and salt into a batter.
Pan-fry spoonfuls in oil until golden on both sides.
Serve hot with black vinegar dipping sauce.
tips: Squeeze the zucchini very well or pancakes will be soggy.
Common breakfast food in Northern China.

name: Mantou (Chinese Steamed Buns)
description: Plain steamed bread buns, soft and pillowy, a Northern Chinese staple
instructions: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, and water; knead until smooth.
Let dough rise for 1 hour until doubled.
Divide into 10 pieces, shape into smooth rounds.
Steam on high for 15 minutes until puffed and cooked through.
tips: Mantou is the bread of Northern China — served with every meal.
Can be pan-fried the next day for crispy mantou.

name: Home-Style Tofu (Jia Chang Doufu)
description: Pan-fried tofu braised with pork, peppers, and doubanjiang in a homey sauce
instructions: Pan-fry tofu slices until golden on both sides, set aside.
Stir-fry pork slices, add doubanjiang and garlic until fragrant.
Add peppers and tofu back, pour in soy sauce and a splash of water.
Braise 3 minutes until sauce thickens, garnish with scallions.
tips: Jia chang means 'home-style' — this is everyday Sichuan comfort food.
Can omit pork for a vegetarian version.

name: Lo Mai Gai (Sticky Rice in Lotus Leaf)
description: Glutinous rice with chicken, Chinese sausage, and mushroom steamed in lotus leaf
instructions: Soak glutinous rice 4 hours, soak mushrooms and lotus leaves.
Marinate diced chicken with soy and oyster sauce.
Mix rice with sliced sausage, mushrooms, and chicken.
Wrap portions in lotus leaves, steam for 30 minutes.
tips: A classic dim sum dish — the lotus leaf imparts a unique fragrance.
Can be made ahead and resteamed to serve.

name: Sichuan Chili Oil Wonton (Suanla Chaoshou)
description: Folded pork wontons drenched in mouth-numbing chili oil and black vinegar
instructions: Mix pork with a splash of soy sauce, ginger, and sesame oil for filling.
Fold wontons in the chaoshou style (overlapping two corners).
Boil until they float, about 3 minutes.
Serve in a bowl of chili oil, black vinegar, garlic, soy, and peppercorn oil.
tips: Chaoshou means 'crossed hands' — the folding style unique to Sichuan.
Make the sauce while wontons boil.

name: Honey Walnut Shrimp
description: Crispy battered shrimp with candied walnuts in creamy mayo-honey sauce
instructions: Candy walnuts by boiling in sugar water then frying until crispy.
Coat shrimp in cornstarch, deep fry until golden.
Mix mayonnaise, honey, and condensed milk into a creamy sauce.
Toss fried shrimp in the sauce, top with candied walnuts.
tips: A Chinese-American banquet classic.
Make sure shrimp are very dry before coating for maximum crispiness.

name: Braised Tofu Pockets (You Doufu)
description: Fried tofu puffs braised in soy sauce and star anise until deeply savory
instructions: Blanch tofu puffs in hot water to remove excess oil.
Simmer with soy sauce, star anise, sugar, ginger, and stock.
Braise on low heat for 15 minutes until tofu absorbs all the flavors.
Garnish with scallions and serve.
tips: Tofu puffs soak up sauce like sponges — very satisfying.
Great as a side dish or stuffed with minced pork.

name: Chinese Sausage Rice (Lap Cheong Fan)
description: Fragrant rice cooked with sweet Chinese sausage and soy sauce
instructions: Wash rice and place in rice cooker with water and ginger.
Slice sausages diagonally, lay on top of rice before cooking.
Cook rice as normal — the sausage renders fat into the rice.
Blanch bok choy, serve over rice with soy sauce and sesame oil drizzle.
tips: The rendered sausage fat makes the rice incredibly fragrant.
A classic lazy dinner in Cantonese households.

name: Black Sesame Tang Yuan
description: Glutinous rice balls filled with sweet black sesame paste in ginger syrup
instructions: Mix sesame paste with sugar and lard, chill until firm, roll into small balls.
Knead glutinous rice flour with warm water into a smooth dough.
Flatten dough pieces, wrap around sesame filling, roll into smooth balls.
Boil in ginger syrup until they float, serve in the syrup.
tips: Traditionally eaten during Lantern Festival and Winter Solstice.
The filling should ooze out when you bite in.

name: Xinjiang Big Plate Chicken (Da Pan Ji)
description: Xinjiang braised chicken with potatoes and peppers over hand-pulled noodles
instructions: Cut chicken into pieces, sear until golden.
Add dried chilies, cumin, ginger, garlic, soy sauce, and water.
Add chunked potatoes, braise 25 minutes until tender.
Add peppers, cook 5 minutes more, serve over hand-pulled noodles.
tips: The noodles soak up the spicy braising sauce — the best part.
A signature dish of Xinjiang's Muslim Uyghur cuisine.

name: Siu Mai (Pork and Shrimp Dumplings)
description: Open-topped dim sum dumplings filled with pork, shrimp, and mushroom
instructions: Chop shrimp coarsely, mix with pork, diced mushrooms, soy sauce, and sesame oil.
Place filling in the center of wonton wrappers, gather edges around but leave top open.
Garnish each with a piece of shrimp or fish roe on top.
Steam for 10 minutes until cooked through.
tips: Siu mai should be open at the top showing the filling.
A must-order dish at any dim sum restaurant.

name: Spicy Garlic Eggplant (Suan Xiang Qie Zi)
description: Charred eggplant in garlic-heavy spicy sauce, a Sichuan home-cooking classic
instructions: Cut eggplant into batons, fry in oil until charred and soft.
In the same wok, fry garlic and chili flakes until fragrant.
Add soy sauce, vinegar, sugar, and a splash of water.
Return eggplant, toss to coat, garnish with scallions.
tips: Use the long thin Asian eggplant variety for best results.
The heavy garlic is what distinguishes this from other eggplant dishes.

name: Cantonese Lemon Chicken
description: Crispy battered chicken cutlets glazed with tangy sweet lemon sauce
instructions: Butterfly and flatten chicken breasts, coat in egg then flour-cornstarch mix.
Deep fry until golden and crispy, drain and slice.
Make sauce by simmering lemon juice, sugar, and cornstarch slurry.
Pour hot lemon sauce over sliced chicken and serve.
tips: Slice chicken after frying so sauce coats every piece.
Use fresh lemons, not bottled juice.

name: Tomato Beef Noodle Soup
description: Comforting Chinese noodle soup with beef brisket in tangy tomato broth
instructions: Sear beef brisket chunks until browned on all sides.
Add quartered tomatoes, ginger, star anise, soy sauce, and water.
Simmer for 1.5 hours until beef is fall-apart tender.
Cook noodles, ladle soup and beef over, garnish with scallions.
tips: The tomato-based broth is lighter than Taiwanese beef noodle soup.
Use ripe tomatoes for a naturally sweet broth.

name: Buddha's Delight (Luohan Zhai)
description: Mixed vegetarian stir-fry with tofu, mushrooms, and glass noodles for Lunar New Year
instructions: Soak mushrooms and glass noodles until softened.
Stir-fry tofu until golden, set aside.
Stir-fry all vegetables with mushrooms, add soy sauce and stock.
Add glass noodles and tofu, simmer until noodles absorb the sauce.
tips: Traditionally made with 10-18 ingredients for Buddhist vegetarian celebrations.
Each ingredient has symbolic meaning for the new year.

name: Da Bao (Large Steamed Pork Buns)
description: Big fluffy steamed buns stuffed with seasoned pork and vegetables
instructions: Make yeast dough, let rise until doubled.
Salt-wilt cabbage, squeeze dry, mix with pork, soy sauce, ginger, and sesame oil.
Divide dough, flatten pieces, fill with pork mixture, pleat shut.
Steam for 18 minutes until puffed and cooked through.
tips: These are larger than char siu bao — a full meal in a bun.
A Beijing breakfast street food staple.

name: Rou Jia Mo (Chinese Hamburger)
description: Xi'an-style flatbread stuffed with slow-braised spiced pork
instructions: Braise pork with all spices, soy sauce, and wine for 1.5 hours until falling apart.
Make flatbread dough, rest 30 minutes, then form into discs.
Bake or pan-fry flatbreads until golden and slightly crispy.
Shred braised pork, stuff generously into split flatbreads.
tips: Considered one of the oldest 'sandwiches' in the world from Xi'an.
The pork should be so tender it shreds with a spoon.

name: Biang Biang Noodles
description: Xi'an hand-pulled wide belt noodles with chili oil and garlic
instructions: Make dough with flour, water, and salt; rest 1 hour.
Pull and slap dough into wide, flat belt-like noodles.
Boil noodles 2-3 minutes, place in bowls with garlic, chili, and soy.
Heat oil until smoking, pour over the chili and garlic to sizzle and bloom.
tips: The noodles should be wide as a belt — that's the signature.
The sizzling oil hitting the chili is what creates the deep flavor.

name: Moo Shu Pork
description: Stir-fried shredded pork with wood ear, eggs, and cabbage in thin pancakes
instructions: Shred pork, marinate with soy sauce and cornstarch.
Scramble eggs, set aside. Stir-fry pork until cooked.
Add soaked wood ears and shredded cabbage, cook until wilted.
Return eggs, toss together. Serve with warm pancakes, hoisin, and scallions.
tips: Spread hoisin on the pancake first, then add the filling.
Mandarin pancakes can be steamed or dry-fried.

name: Mei Cai Kou Rou (Steamed Pork Belly with Preserved Mustard Greens)
description: Hakka dish of layered pork belly and mei cai steamed until meltingly tender
instructions: Boil whole pork belly until just cooked, rub skin with soy sauce.
Deep fry pork belly skin-side down until blistered, cool and slice.
Layer sliced pork skin-side down in a bowl, top with seasoned mei cai.
Steam for 2 hours, invert onto a plate to reveal the skin.
tips: The long steaming makes pork belly incredibly soft.
A Hakka Chinese celebration dish served at banquets.

name: Jian Dui (Sesame Balls)
description: Deep-fried glutinous rice balls coated in sesame seeds with red bean filling
instructions: Mix glutinous rice flour with sugar and warm water into a dough.
Flatten pieces, fill with red bean paste, roll into smooth balls.
Roll balls in sesame seeds, pressing gently to adhere.
Deep fry on low heat, pressing gently to expand, until golden all over.
tips: Fry on low heat — high heat will burst them.
Press gently with a skimmer while frying to help them puff up.

name: Douhua (Silken Tofu Pudding)
description: Warm silky soy pudding in sweet ginger syrup, a Cantonese breakfast classic
instructions: Heat fresh soy milk to 85C.
Dissolve gypsum powder in water, pour hot soy milk over it quickly.
Cover and let set undisturbed for 15 minutes.
Make ginger syrup by simmering ginger with brown sugar. Spoon tofu pudding into bowls, drizzle with syrup.
tips: Do not stir after pouring — it sets from the heat like a custard.
Popular as breakfast in Guangdong and Hong Kong.

name: Sichuan Pepper Chicken (Jiao Ma Ji)
description: Cold chicken drenched in intensely numbing Sichuan peppercorn and chili sauce
instructions: Poach chicken thighs until just cooked, ice bath, then shred or chop.
Mix peppercorn oil, chili oil, soy sauce, sugar, and minced garlic.
Arrange chicken on a plate, pour the numbing sauce over.
Garnish with scallion slivers and serve cold.
tips: The peppercorn oil is what gives the extreme numbing sensation.
Best served chilled on a hot day.

name: Yangzhou Fried Rice
description: Classic Jiangsu fried rice with shrimp, char siu, peas, and egg
instructions: Beat eggs, scramble in hot wok, break into small pieces.
Stir-fry shrimp and diced char siu until cooked.
Add cold rice, toss on high heat to separate grains.
Add peas, scallions, soy sauce, and return egg. Toss until smoky.
tips: Day-old rice is essential — fresh rice will be mushy.
Yangzhou fried rice is the 'original' Chinese fried rice recipe.

name: Suan Cai Yu (Pickled Vegetable Fish)
description: Northeast Chinese sour pickled cabbage soup with tender fish fillets
instructions: Slice fish, marinate with salt, white pepper, and cornstarch.
Stir-fry pickled cabbage with ginger and garlic until fragrant.
Add stock, bring to a boil, simmer 10 minutes for sour broth.
Gently poach fish slices in the broth until just cooked through.
tips: The sour flavor comes entirely from the fermented cabbage.
A comfort food staple in Dongbei (Northeastern China).

name: Salt Baked Chicken (Yan Ju Ji)
description: Hakka whole chicken baked in a mound of hot salt for incredibly moist meat
instructions: Rub chicken with wine, five-spice, and sand ginger; air-dry 1 hour.
Wrap chicken tightly in parchment paper.
Heat salt in a large pot until very hot, bury the wrapped chicken in salt.
Cover and cook on low heat for 1 hour. Unwrap and chop to serve.
tips: Sand ginger powder (sha jiang) is the signature Hakka spice.
The salt is reusable — store it for next time.

name: Stir-Fried Lotus Root with Vinegar
description: Crispy lotus root slices tossed in black vinegar and chili, a refreshing side
instructions: Peel and slice lotus root thinly, soak in vinegar water to prevent browning.
Stir-fry dried chilies and peppercorns in oil until fragrant.
Add lotus root, stir-fry on high heat for 2 minutes, keeping it crunchy.
Add vinegar and sugar, toss and serve immediately.
tips: Keep the lotus root crunchy — do not overcook.
The vinegar water soak keeps the slices white and crisp.

name: Pasta all'Amatriciana
description: Roman pasta with guanciale, tomato, pecorino, and chili flakes
instructions: Crisp guanciale in olive oil until golden, remove and set aside.
Add tomatoes and chili flakes to the rendered fat, simmer 15 minutes.
Cook bucatini until al dente, toss with sauce and guanciale.
Serve topped with grated pecorino romano.
tips: Use guanciale, not pancetta, for authentic flavor.
Pecorino romano is traditional; Parmesan changes the dish.

name: Pasta Puttanesca
description: Bold Neapolitan pasta with olives, capers, anchovies, and tomatoes
instructions: Saute garlic and anchovies in olive oil until anchovies dissolve.
Add tomatoes, olives, capers, and chili flakes; simmer 15 minutes.
Cook spaghetti al dente and toss with sauce.
Serve immediately with fresh parsley.
tips: Anchovies melt into the sauce and add umami, not fishiness.
Do not add cheese — it's not traditional for this dish.

name: Cacio e Pepe
description: Classic Roman pasta with pecorino cheese and black pepper
instructions: Toast black pepper in a dry pan until fragrant.
Cook pasta in salted water, reserving 2 cups pasta water.
Combine finely grated pecorino with a ladle of pasta water to form a cream.
Toss hot pasta with pecorino cream and pepper, adding pasta water as needed.
tips: Add pasta water gradually to avoid clumping the cheese.
Use freshly cracked pepper for the best flavor.

name: Pasta alla Norma
description: Sicilian pasta with fried eggplant, tomato sauce, and ricotta salata
instructions: Slice and salt eggplant, let drain 20 minutes, then fry until golden.
Saute garlic in olive oil, add tomatoes and basil, simmer 15 minutes.
Cook rigatoni al dente and toss with tomato sauce.
Top with fried eggplant and crumbled ricotta salata.
tips: Salting the eggplant removes bitterness and excess moisture.
Ricotta salata can be substituted with feta in a pinch.

name: Fettuccine Alfredo
description: Creamy Roman-style fettuccine with butter and Parmigiano-Reggiano
instructions: Cook fettuccine in well-salted water until al dente.
Melt butter in a wide pan, add a ladle of pasta water.
Toss hot fettuccine with butter, adding Parmigiano gradually.
Serve immediately with fresh cracked pepper.
tips: The original Roman version uses only butter and cheese, no cream.
Toss vigorously to emulsify the cheese into a silky sauce.

name: Pasta e Fagioli
description: Hearty Italian soup with small pasta and cannellini beans
instructions: Saute onion, celery, and garlic in olive oil until soft.
Add tomatoes, beans, and broth; simmer 20 minutes.
Mash half the beans for a thicker texture, add pasta.
Cook until pasta is tender, serve with olive oil drizzle.
tips: Use small pasta shapes that fit on a spoon.
This soup thickens as it sits; add broth when reheating.

name: Ribollita
description: Tuscan bread soup with cannellini beans, kale, and vegetables
instructions: Saute onion, carrot, and celery in olive oil until soft.
Add tomatoes, beans, and kale; cook until kale wilts.
Tear bread into pieces and stir into soup; simmer 20 minutes.
Let rest 10 minutes before serving drizzled with olive oil.
tips: Use day-old Tuscan bread for the best texture.
Ribollita means 'reboiled' — it's even better the next day.

name: Osso Buco alla Milanese
description: Braised veal shanks in white wine with gremolata
instructions: Season and flour veal shanks, brown in olive oil on all sides.
Saute onion, carrot, and celery; add white wine and reduce by half.
Add broth, cover, and braise in oven at 160C for 2 hours.
Serve topped with gremolata of lemon zest, garlic, and parsley.
tips: Don't skip the gremolata — it brightens the rich braise.
Traditionally served with risotto alla milanese (saffron risotto).

name: Saltimbocca alla Romana
description: Veal escalopes wrapped with prosciutto and sage in white wine sauce
instructions: Place sage leaf and prosciutto on each veal piece, secure with toothpick.
Dust lightly with flour, pan-fry prosciutto-side down in butter 2 minutes.
Flip and cook 2 more minutes, remove from pan.
Deglaze pan with white wine, reduce to a sauce and pour over meat.
tips: Saltimbocca means 'jumps in the mouth' in Italian.
Pound the veal thin for quick, even cooking.

name: Risotto alla Milanese
description: Golden saffron risotto from Milan with Parmigiano and butter
instructions: Saute onion in butter, add rice and toast 2 minutes.
Add white wine and stir until absorbed.
Ladle in warm broth gradually, stirring, with saffron dissolved in first ladle.
Finish with cold butter and Parmigiano, rest 2 minutes before serving.
tips: Use real saffron threads, not powder — toast them first for more flavor.
The final butter stir (mantecatura) creates the creamy texture.

name: Risotto ai Frutti di Mare
description: Seafood risotto with shrimp, mussels, and calamari
instructions: Saute garlic in olive oil, add seafood and cook 3 minutes; remove.
Toast rice in the same pan, add wine and reduce.
Add fish stock gradually, stirring, with halved cherry tomatoes.
Return seafood in final 2 minutes, finish with parsley.
tips: Never add cheese to seafood risotto — it's an Italian rule.
Use a mix of shrimp, mussels, and calamari rings.

name: Eggplant Parmigiana
description: Baked layers of fried eggplant with tomato sauce and mozzarella
instructions: Slice and salt eggplant, fry in olive oil until golden.
Make tomato sauce with garlic, canned tomatoes, and basil.
Layer eggplant, sauce, mozzarella, and Parmigiano in a baking dish.
Bake at 180C for 30 minutes until bubbly and golden.
tips: Salting eggplant draws out moisture for crispier frying.
Let it rest 10 minutes after baking for cleaner slices.

name: Tiramisu
description: Classic Italian dessert with espresso-soaked ladyfingers and mascarpone cream
instructions: Separate eggs; whisk yolks with sugar until pale and thick.
Fold mascarpone into yolk mixture until smooth.
Whip egg whites to stiff peaks and fold into mascarpone mixture.
Dip ladyfingers in espresso, layer with cream, refrigerate 6 hours, dust with cocoa.
tips: Strong, cooled espresso works best — not instant coffee.
Refrigerate at least 6 hours for the best set texture.

name: Panna Cotta
description: Silky Piedmontese cream dessert with vanilla and berry coulis
instructions: Bloom gelatin in cold water for 5 minutes.
Heat cream with sugar and vanilla until just simmering; stir in gelatin.
Pour into ramekins and refrigerate at least 4 hours.
Make berry coulis by simmering berries with lemon juice, serve alongside.
tips: Do not boil the cream or the gelatin will lose its setting power.
The wobble test: it should jiggle like jelly when set.

name: Cannoli
description: Sicilian fried pastry tubes filled with sweet ricotta cream
instructions: Make dough with flour, Marsala, lard, and cinnamon; rest 30 minutes.
Roll thin, wrap around cannoli tubes, fry at 180C until golden.
Drain ricotta, mix with powdered sugar, chocolate chips, and cinnamon.
Fill shells just before serving to keep them crisp.
tips: Fill cannoli right before eating — they go soggy quickly.
Drain ricotta overnight in cheesecloth for the smoothest filling.

name: Pizza Quattro Formaggi
description: Four-cheese pizza with mozzarella, gorgonzola, fontina, and Parmigiano
instructions: Stretch dough into a round on a floured surface.
Tear mozzarella and distribute with crumbled gorgonzola and cubed fontina.
Grate Parmigiano over top and drizzle with olive oil.
Bake at 250C for 8-10 minutes until crust is charred and cheese is bubbling.
tips: Preheat your oven as hot as it goes with a pizza stone inside.
Add a drizzle of honey after baking for a sweet-savory twist.

name: Pizza Diavola
description: Spicy Italian pizza with tomato, mozzarella, and spicy salami
instructions: Stretch dough into a round and spread tomato sauce evenly.
Top with torn mozzarella and sliced spicy salami.
Sprinkle chili flakes and drizzle olive oil.
Bake at 250C for 8-10 minutes until bubbly and charred.
tips: Use nduja (spicy spreadable salami) for extra heat.
Let the pizza rest 2 minutes before slicing.

name: Focaccia
description: Ligurian olive oil flatbread with rosemary and flaky sea salt
instructions: Mix flour, yeast, water, and 2 tbsp oil; knead until smooth.
Let rise 1 hour, then stretch into an oiled sheet pan.
Dimple with fingers, drizzle remaining oil, top with rosemary and salt.
Bake at 220C for 20 minutes until golden and crispy.
tips: Generous olive oil in the pan creates the crispy bottom.
Let dough rest 10 minutes if it springs back when stretching.

name: Arancini
description: Sicilian fried risotto balls stuffed with mozzarella and peas
instructions: Mix cold risotto with one beaten egg and cooked peas.
Shape into balls with a cube of mozzarella in the center.
Dip in beaten egg, coat in breadcrumbs.
Deep fry at 180C for 3-4 minutes until golden and crisp.
tips: Use day-old risotto — it's firmer and easier to shape.
Freeze for 30 minutes before frying for a better hold.

name: Vitello Tonnato
description: Piedmontese cold sliced veal with creamy tuna-caper sauce
instructions: Poach veal in salted water with aromatics for 40 minutes, let cool.
Blend tuna, mayonnaise, capers, anchovies, and lemon juice until smooth.
Slice veal thinly and arrange on a platter.
Drape tuna sauce over veal, garnish with capers and lemon.
tips: Chill the veal completely before slicing for clean cuts.
This dish is best made a day ahead and refrigerated.

name: Carpaccio di Manzo
description: Paper-thin raw beef slices with arugula, Parmigiano, and lemon dressing
instructions: Freeze beef for 30 minutes, then slice paper-thin with a sharp knife.
Arrange slices on a cold plate, covering the surface.
Top with arugula, shaved Parmigiano, and capers.
Drizzle with lemon juice and olive oil, season with salt and pepper.
tips: Semi-frozen beef is much easier to slice thin.
Use the highest quality beef you can find.

name: Gnocchi al Pomodoro
description: Pillowy potato gnocchi in fresh tomato basil sauce
instructions: Boil potatoes whole, peel, rice while hot, mix with flour and egg.
Roll into ropes, cut into pieces, press with a fork for ridges.
Make sauce: saute garlic, add tomatoes and basil, simmer 15 minutes.
Boil gnocchi until they float, toss with sauce and serve.
tips: Use starchy potatoes and work the dough as little as possible.
Over-mixing makes gnocchi tough — stop as soon as dough comes together.

name: Gnocchi al Gorgonzola
description: Potato gnocchi in a creamy gorgonzola and walnut sauce
instructions: Melt butter with gorgonzola and cream over low heat, stirring.
Toast walnuts in a dry pan and roughly chop.
Boil gnocchi until they float, drain and add to sauce.
Toss gnocchi in sauce, top with walnuts and pepper.
tips: Use dolce gorgonzola for a milder, creamier sauce.
Don't boil the sauce or the cheese will separate.

name: Penne alla Vodka
description: Penne in a creamy tomato-vodka sauce with Parmigiano
instructions: Saute garlic and chili flakes in olive oil.
Add tomatoes, simmer 10 minutes, then add vodka and cook 3 minutes.
Stir in cream and cook until sauce thickens slightly.
Toss with cooked penne and Parmigiano.
tips: The vodka enhances tomato flavor — the alcohol cooks off.
Add pasta water to adjust sauce consistency.

name: Orecchiette con Cime di Rapa
description: Puglian ear-shaped pasta with broccoli rabe, garlic, and anchovies
instructions: Blanch broccoli rabe in boiling salted water 2 minutes, remove with a slotted spoon.
Cook orecchiette in the same water until al dente.
Saute garlic, anchovies, and chili in olive oil; add broccoli rabe.
Toss with drained pasta and a splash of pasta water.
tips: Cook pasta and broccoli rabe in the same water for better flavor.
Anchovies dissolve into the oil and add savory depth.

name: Insalata Tricolore
description: Classic Italian salad with radicchio, arugula, and endive
instructions: Wash and tear radicchio, separate endive leaves, toss with arugula.
Whisk olive oil and balsamic vinegar with salt.
Drizzle dressing over salad and toss gently.
Serve immediately as a side or starter.
tips: The three colors (red, white, green) represent the Italian flag.
Add shaved Parmigiano for a richer version.

name: Chicken Cacciatore
description: Hunter-style braised chicken with tomatoes, olives, and herbs
instructions: Brown chicken thighs in olive oil, remove and set aside.
Saute onion, pepper, and garlic; deglaze with white wine.
Add tomatoes and olives, return chicken, cover and simmer 40 minutes.
Serve with crusty bread or polenta.
tips: Bone-in, skin-on thighs give the best flavor and texture.
This improves overnight as flavors meld.

name: Fritto Misto
description: Mixed fried seafood and vegetables with lemon
instructions: Clean seafood, slice zucchini into thin rounds.
Make light batter with flour and ice-cold sparkling water.
Deep fry seafood and vegetables in batches at 180C until golden.
Drain on paper towels, season with salt, serve with lemon wedges.
tips: Keep batter ice cold for maximum crispiness.
Don't overcrowd the fryer — fry in small batches.

name: Caponata
description: Sicilian sweet and sour eggplant relish with tomatoes and capers
instructions: Cube and fry eggplant in olive oil until golden, drain on paper towels.
Saute celery until soft, add tomatoes, olives, and capers.
Stir in vinegar and sugar, simmer 10 minutes.
Add fried eggplant, stir gently, cool to room temperature before serving.
tips: Caponata tastes best at room temperature or slightly cool.
Make a day ahead — the flavors develop overnight.

name: Polenta with Mushroom Ragu
description: Creamy polenta topped with rich mushroom and tomato ragu
instructions: Bring broth to a boil, whisk in polenta gradually, stir for 30 minutes.
Saute onion and garlic, add sliced mushrooms, cook until browned.
Add tomatoes to mushrooms and simmer 15 minutes.
Stir butter and Parmigiano into polenta, serve topped with ragu.
tips: Constant stirring prevents lumps in polenta.
Use a mix of porcini, cremini, and shiitake for depth.

name: Cotoletta alla Milanese
description: Bone-in veal cutlet breaded and fried in butter, Milanese style
instructions: Pound veal cutlets to even thickness, keeping the bone.
Dredge in flour, dip in beaten eggs, coat in breadcrumbs.
Fry in clarified butter over medium heat, 4 minutes per side.
Serve immediately with lemon wedges and arugula.
tips: Use clarified butter — whole butter will burn at frying temperatures.
Press breadcrumbs firmly for an even, crispy coating.

name: Panzanella
description: Tuscan bread salad with tomatoes, cucumber, red onion, and basil
instructions: Tear bread into chunks and soak briefly in water, squeeze dry.
Chop tomatoes, cucumber, and onion.
Toss bread with vegetables, basil, oil, and vinegar.
Let sit 15 minutes before serving for flavors to meld.
tips: Use overripe, juicy summer tomatoes for the best result.
Stale bread is essential — fresh bread will turn to mush.

name: Linguine alle Vongole
description: Classic Neapolitan pasta with clams in white wine and garlic
instructions: Saute garlic and chili in olive oil, add clams and white wine, cover.
Cook 5-7 minutes until all clams open; discard any that don't.
Cook linguine al dente, toss with clam sauce and fresh parsley.
Serve immediately with the clams in their shells.
tips: Soak clams in salted water 30 minutes to purge sand.
Never add cheese to clam pasta.

name: Italian Wedding Soup
description: Light broth with mini meatballs, escarole, and tiny pasta
instructions: Mix ground pork with egg, breadcrumbs, Parmigiano, and garlic; form tiny meatballs.
Bring chicken broth to a simmer, add meatballs and cook 10 minutes.
Add chopped escarole and pasta, cook until pasta is tender.
Serve with extra Parmigiano and crusty bread.
tips: Make meatballs marble-sized for the best proportion with the broth.
The 'wedding' refers to the marriage of meat and greens, not an actual wedding.

name: Tortellini in Brodo
description: Bolognese stuffed pasta served in clear rich meat broth
instructions: Make filling by finely chopping prosciutto and mortadella, mixing with Parmigiano, egg, and nutmeg.
Roll pasta thin, cut into squares, place filling and fold into tortellini shape.
Bring rich beef broth to a gentle simmer.
Cook tortellini in broth for 3-4 minutes, serve in bowls with broth.
tips: The traditional tortellini shape mimics Venus's navel, according to legend.
A rich, homemade broth is what makes this dish special.

name: Involtini di Melanzane
description: Eggplant rolls stuffed with ricotta and baked in tomato sauce
instructions: Slice eggplant lengthwise, grill or bake until pliable.
Mix ricotta with Parmigiano and basil, spread on eggplant slices.
Roll up and place seam-side down in a tomato sauce-lined baking dish.
Top with mozzarella and bake at 180C for 25 minutes.
tips: Grilling the eggplant slices adds a smoky flavor.
Don't overfill the rolls or they'll burst open.

name: Spaghetti alle Cozze
description: Spaghetti with mussels in a white wine and garlic sauce
instructions: Saute garlic in olive oil, add mussels and white wine, cover.
Cook until mussels open (5-7 minutes), discard any closed ones.
Add halved cherry tomatoes to the pan, cook 2 minutes.
Toss with cooked spaghetti and fresh parsley.
tips: Scrub and debeard mussels before cooking.
Reserve some mussels in shells for a beautiful presentation.

name: Supplì al Telefono
description: Roman fried rice croquettes with a stretchy mozzarella center
instructions: Take spoonfuls of cold tomato risotto and shape around a mozzarella cube.
Dip in beaten egg and coat in breadcrumbs.
Deep fry at 180C for 3 minutes until golden brown.
Break open to see the mozzarella stretch like a telephone cord.
tips: The name 'al telefono' comes from the stretching mozzarella resembling a phone cord.
Use day-old risotto for the easiest shaping.

name: Pesce all'Acqua Pazza
description: Neapolitan fish fillets poached in crazy water with tomatoes and olives
instructions: Saute garlic in olive oil, add halved cherry tomatoes and cook 3 minutes.
Add white wine, olives, capers, and a cup of water; bring to a simmer.
Nestle fish fillets in the liquid, cover and poach 10 minutes.
Serve fish with the broth spooned over, with crusty bread for dipping.
tips: Acqua pazza means 'crazy water' — named for the lively bubbling broth.
Sea bass, branzino, or red snapper work best.

name: Spaghetti alla Gricia
description: The original Roman pasta with guanciale and pecorino, no tomato
instructions: Render guanciale in a cold pan over medium heat until crisp.
Cook spaghetti al dente, reserving pasta water.
Remove pan from heat, add pasta and toss with guanciale fat.
Add pecorino gradually with pasta water, tossing to create a creamy sauce.
tips: Gricia is the ancestor of both carbonara and amatriciana.
Remove pan from heat before adding cheese to prevent clumping.

name: Cioppino
description: Italian-American seafood stew with tomatoes, wine, and mixed shellfish
instructions: Saute onion, fennel, and garlic in olive oil until soft.
Add white wine and reduce by half, then add tomatoes and simmer 15 minutes.
Add fish first, then shellfish, cover and cook 8-10 minutes.
Serve in deep bowls with grilled bread.
tips: Add shellfish in order of cooking time — fish first, then shrimp, then clams/mussels.
This stew originated in San Francisco's Italian fishing community.

name: Bruschetta al Pomodoro e Basilico
description: Toasted bread topped with fresh diced tomatoes, garlic, and basil
instructions: Dice tomatoes, mix with torn basil, olive oil, and salt; let sit 10 minutes.
Toast or grill bread slices until golden and crisp.
Rub each slice with a cut garlic clove.
Spoon tomato mixture over bread and serve immediately.
tips: Use the ripest, most flavorful tomatoes you can find.
Serve immediately so the bread stays crispy.

name: Zabaglione
description: Warm Italian egg custard whipped with Marsala wine and sugar
instructions: Whisk egg yolks and sugar in a heatproof bowl over simmering water.
Gradually add Marsala, whisking constantly for 8-10 minutes.
Continue until mixture is thick, foamy, and tripled in volume.
Spoon into glasses over fresh berries and serve warm.
tips: Keep whisking constantly or the eggs will scramble.
The bowl should never touch the water — only steam heats it.

name: Pappa al Pomodoro
description: Tuscan tomato and bread soup, thick and comforting
instructions: Saute garlic in olive oil, add chopped tomatoes and cook 15 minutes.
Tear bread into pieces, add to pot with broth and basil.
Simmer 15 minutes, stirring until bread breaks down into a thick porridge.
Serve drizzled with olive oil and fresh basil.
tips: The soup should be thick enough that a spoon stands up in it.
Use summer tomatoes for the best flavor, or San Marzano canned.

name: Chicken Piccata
description: Pan-fried chicken cutlets in a bright lemon-caper butter sauce
instructions: Pound chicken thin, season and dredge in flour.
Pan-fry in olive oil until golden, 3 minutes per side; remove.
Add white wine and lemon juice to pan, scraping up fond.
Swirl in butter and capers, return chicken to pan and spoon sauce over.
tips: Pounding the chicken ensures even, quick cooking.
Add a handful of parsley at the end for color.

name: Affogato
description: Vanilla gelato drowned in a shot of hot espresso
instructions: Place a scoop of gelato in each serving glass.
Pull a fresh shot of hot espresso.
Pour espresso directly over gelato and serve immediately.
Optionally grate dark chocolate on top.
tips: Serve immediately — the magic is in the contrast of hot and cold.
Use high-quality gelato and freshly pulled espresso.

name: Baked Ziti
description: Italian-American baked pasta with ricotta, mozzarella, and meat sauce
instructions: Brown ground beef with garlic, add tomatoes and basil, simmer 20 minutes.
Cook ziti until slightly underdone, mix with ricotta.
