Layer pasta, meat sauce, and mozzarella in a baking dish.
Top with Parmigiano and bake at 190C for 25 minutes until bubbly.
tips: Undercook the pasta slightly — it finishes in the oven.
Let rest 10 minutes after baking for neater slices.

name: Parmigiana di Zucchine
description: Baked layers of zucchini with tomato sauce and mozzarella
instructions: Slice zucchini lengthwise, grill or pan-fry until lightly golden.
Make tomato sauce with garlic, canned tomatoes, and basil.
Layer zucchini, sauce, mozzarella, and Parmigiano in a baking dish.
Bake at 180C for 25 minutes until golden and bubbling.
tips: Grilling the zucchini instead of frying makes this lighter.
Salt the zucchini first to remove excess water.

name: Pasta alla Norcina
description: Umbrian pasta with sausage, cream, and black truffle
instructions: Remove sausage from casings and brown in a pan, breaking into crumbles.
Deglaze with white wine and cook until evaporated.
Add cream and simmer until slightly thickened.
Toss with cooked rigatoni and pecorino, finish with black pepper.
tips: Use fennel-spiced Italian sausage for authentic Umbrian flavor.
A few drops of truffle oil at the end elevates this dish.

name: Insalata di Mare
description: Cold Italian seafood salad with lemon, parsley, and olive oil
instructions: Poach seafood separately until just cooked, drain and cool.
Slice celery thinly and mince garlic.
Toss cooled seafood with celery, garlic, parsley, lemon juice, and olive oil.
Chill for at least 1 hour before serving.
tips: Don't overcook the seafood — it should be tender, not rubbery.
Best served at cool room temperature, not ice cold.

name: Ravioli Burro e Salvia
description: Ricotta and spinach ravioli in brown butter and sage sauce
instructions: Wilt spinach, squeeze dry, and mix with ricotta and Parmigiano for filling.
Roll pasta thin, fill and seal ravioli.
Boil ravioli until they float, about 3-4 minutes.
Brown butter with sage leaves and pour over ravioli.
tips: Squeeze every drop of moisture from the spinach.
Browned butter has a nutty aroma — don't let it burn.

name: Farinata di Ceci
description: Ligurian crispy chickpea flour flatbread baked in olive oil
instructions: Whisk chickpea flour with water, salt, and 2 tbsp olive oil; rest 30 minutes.
Pour remaining olive oil into a hot cast iron pan.
Pour in batter, top with rosemary.
Bake at 230C for 15-20 minutes until golden and crispy at edges.
tips: Let the batter rest — this makes it lighter and crispier.
The top should be golden and the edges should be dark and crispy.

name: Stracciatella alla Romana
description: Roman egg drop soup with Parmigiano in clear chicken broth
instructions: Bring chicken broth to a gentle simmer.
Beat eggs with Parmigiano, semolina, parsley, and nutmeg.
Pour egg mixture in a thin stream into simmering broth while stirring.
Cook 2 minutes until egg forms 'little rags' in the broth.
tips: Don't boil vigorously or the egg will break up too finely.
Stracciatella means 'little rags' — the egg shreds in the soup.

name: Peperonata
description: Slow-cooked sweet peppers with tomatoes and onions
instructions: Slice peppers and onion into strips.
Saute onion and garlic in olive oil until soft.
Add peppers and tomatoes, cover and cook on low 30 minutes.
Stir in basil and serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Use a mix of red, yellow, and orange peppers for color.
Low and slow cooking makes the peppers silky and sweet.

name: Pollo alla Diavola
description: Spatchcocked chicken grilled with chili, lemon, and herbs
instructions: Spatchcock chicken by removing the backbone and pressing flat.
Marinate in lemon juice, olive oil, garlic, chili, and rosemary for 1 hour.
Grill or roast at 220C for 40-45 minutes until skin is crispy.
Rest 10 minutes before carving, serve with pan juices.
tips: Pressing with a heavy pan while grilling ensures even crisping.
The chicken is done when thigh juices run clear (75C internal).

name: Pasta al Limone
description: Light and creamy lemon pasta from the Amalfi Coast
instructions: Cook spaghetti al dente, reserve pasta water.
Melt butter with lemon zest and cream, simmer 2 minutes.
Toss pasta with sauce, adding lemon juice and Parmigiano.
Add pasta water as needed, finish with fresh basil.
tips: Use the zest of all lemons but juice of only two — adjust to taste.
This dish should taste bright, not heavy despite the cream.

name: Porchetta
description: Slow-roasted pork belly rolled with fennel, garlic, and herbs
instructions: Score pork belly skin, rub flesh with fennel, garlic, herbs, and lemon zest.
Roll tightly and tie with kitchen twine, rub skin with salt and oil.
Roast at 160C for 2.5 hours, then increase to 230C for 20 minutes for crackling.
Rest 20 minutes before slicing into thick rounds.
tips: Dry the skin thoroughly before roasting for the crispiest crackling.
Let the rolled porchetta rest overnight in the fridge for best flavor.

name: Braciole
description: Thin beef rolls stuffed with breadcrumbs and cheese, braised in tomato sauce
instructions: Pound beef thin, spread filling of breadcrumbs, Parmigiano, garlic, and parsley.
Roll up tightly and secure with toothpicks or kitchen twine.
Brown rolls in olive oil, deglaze with red wine.
Add tomatoes, cover and braise on low for 2 hours until fork-tender.
tips: The braising sauce becomes a rich pasta sauce — serve it over rigatoni.
Low and slow is the key to tender braciole.

name: Zuppa Toscana
description: Tuscan soup with Italian sausage, potatoes, and kale in creamy broth
instructions: Brown sausage in a pot, breaking into crumbles, remove.
Saute onion and garlic, add sliced potatoes and broth.
Simmer until potatoes are tender, add kale and sausage.
Stir in cream, cook 5 minutes more, serve hot.
tips: Use spicy Italian sausage for extra kick.
The kale should be just wilted, not overcooked.

name: Limoncello Semifreddo
description: Frozen Italian dessert with limoncello, cream, and meringue
instructions: Whip cream to soft peaks, fold in limoncello and lemon zest.
Whisk egg whites with sugar to stiff, glossy peaks.
Gently fold meringue into cream mixture.
Pour into a loaf pan, freeze at least 6 hours, slice to serve.
tips: Line the pan with plastic wrap for easy unmolding.
Remove from freezer 5 minutes before slicing for cleaner cuts.

name: Melanzane alla Griglia
description: Grilled eggplant with garlic, mint, and vinegar dressing
instructions: Slice eggplant into 1cm rounds, brush with olive oil.
Grill on high heat 3-4 minutes per side until charred.
Layer grilled slices with sliced garlic and mint leaves.
Drizzle with vinegar and olive oil, marinate 30 minutes before serving.
tips: This antipasto improves after a few hours of marinating.
Works equally well on a grill pan or outdoor grill.

name: Bucatini alla Carbonara
description: Thick hollow pasta with egg, guanciale, and pecorino in the Roman style
instructions: Render guanciale until crispy, reserve the fat.
Whisk egg yolks with pecorino and generous black pepper.
Cook bucatini al dente, add to guanciale pan off heat.
Pour egg mixture over pasta, toss quickly with pasta water to create silky sauce.
tips: Take the pan off heat before adding eggs to prevent scrambling.
The residual heat and pasta water create the creamy sauce.

name: Birria Tacos
description: Crispy corn tortillas dipped in consomé and filled with tender braised beef birria and melted cheese.
instructions: Toast and rehydrate chiles, blend with onion, garlic, and spices into a smooth sauce.
Braise beef in the chile sauce with broth for 2 hours until fork-tender, then shred.
Dip tortillas in consomé, fill with shredded beef and cheese, and pan-fry until crispy.
Serve with consomé for dipping, topped with cilantro and diced onion.
tips: Use a mix of guajillo and ancho chiles for depth.
The consomé is essential — serve it in small bowls alongside.

name: Cochinita Pibil
description: Yucatan-style slow-roasted pork marinated in achiote paste and sour orange juice, wrapped in banana leaves.
instructions: Blend achiote paste with orange juice, lime juice, and garlic to make the marinade.
Coat pork in marinade, wrap in banana leaves, and refrigerate overnight.
Slow-roast wrapped pork at 150°C for 3 hours until falling apart.
Shred and serve with pickled red onion and habanero salsa.
tips: Seville orange is traditional — substitute with orange plus lime juice.
Pickle red onions in lime juice and salt for at least 30 minutes.

name: Mole Negro
description: Oaxacan black mole with charred chiles, chocolate, and spices served over chicken.
instructions: Char and rehydrate chiles, then blend with toasted sesame seeds, fried plantain, onion, and garlic.
Cook the sauce in oil until darkened, add chocolate and broth, simmer 45 minutes.
Poach chicken thighs in the mole sauce until cooked through.
Serve with rice, garnished with sesame seeds.
tips: Charring the chiles until almost black gives the mole its signature dark color.
This sauce freezes beautifully for months.

name: Mole Verde
description: Bright green mole made with pumpkin seeds, tomatillos, and herbs, served with pork.
instructions: Boil tomatillos and jalapeños until soft, then blend with toasted pumpkin seeds, cilantro, lettuce, and garlic.
Cook the green sauce in oil for 10 minutes until thickened.
Simmer sliced pork in the verde sauce until tender and cooked through.
Serve with warm tortillas and rice.
tips: Toast pumpkin seeds in a dry skillet until they pop for the best flavor.
Lettuce adds body and a mild sweetness to the sauce.

name: Pozole Verde
description: Green pozole with hominy and chicken in a tangy tomatillo-herb broth.
instructions: Simmer chicken in salted water until cooked, shred and reserve the broth.
Roast tomatillos and poblanos, blend with cilantro, onion, and garlic.
Add green sauce and drained hominy to the broth, simmer 30 minutes.
Serve with shredded chicken, radishes, cabbage, and tostadas.
tips: Roasting the tomatillos adds a smoky depth to the green broth.
Garnishes are essential — offer shredded cabbage, radish, oregano, and lime.

name: Tlacoyos
description: Thick oval masa cakes stuffed with refried beans, topped with nopales, salsa, and cheese.
instructions: Knead masa harina with warm water and salt to form a smooth dough.
Flatten into oval shapes, stuff with refried beans, seal and press flat.
Cook on a hot griddle with a little oil until golden on both sides.
Top with diced nopales, salsa verde, crumbled queso fresco, and cilantro.
tips: Keep dough covered with a damp towel to prevent drying.
Nopales can be grilled or boiled — grilling adds better flavor.

name: Enchiladas Suizas
description: Chicken enchiladas smothered in creamy green tomatillo sauce and melted cheese.
instructions: Poach and shred chicken, set aside.
Boil tomatillos and jalapeño, blend with onion, garlic, and sour cream.
Fill tortillas with chicken, roll and place in a baking dish, cover with sauce and cheese.
Bake at 190°C for 20 minutes until bubbly and golden.
tips: Quickly fry tortillas in oil before rolling to prevent them from breaking.
Oaxaca cheese melts beautifully — substitute mozzarella if needed.

name: Enchiladas Rojas
description: Corn tortillas rolled around cheese and onion, bathed in a rich red chile sauce.
instructions: Toast and rehydrate chiles, blend with garlic, onion, oregano, and some soaking liquid.
Strain sauce, fry in oil until darkened and thickened.
Dip tortillas in warm sauce, fill with crumbled cheese and onion, roll up.
Arrange in dish, pour remaining sauce over, top with cheese and sour cream.
tips: Removing chile seeds reduces heat — keep some for spicier enchiladas.
Frying the sauce in oil deepens the flavor significantly.

name: Mexican Ceviche
description: Fresh raw fish cured in lime juice with tomato, onion, cilantro, and serrano chiles.
instructions: Dice fish into small cubes and marinate in lime juice for 25 minutes until opaque.
Dice tomato, onion, cucumber, and serrano chile finely.
Drain excess lime juice from fish, toss with vegetables and cilantro.
Serve with tostadas, topped with sliced avocado.
tips: Use the freshest fish possible — snapper or sea bass work best.
Do not over-cure — fish becomes rubbery after 40 minutes.

name: Aguachile
description: Raw shrimp marinated in a fiery lime-chile sauce with cucumber and red onion.
instructions: Butterfly shrimp and arrange flat on a plate.
Blend lime juice with serrano chiles, cilantro, and salt until smooth.
Pour green chile sauce over shrimp, add sliced cucumber and red onion.
Refrigerate 15 minutes and serve cold with tostadas.
tips: Shrimp should be extremely fresh since they are barely cooked by the acid.
Adjust serrano quantity to your heat tolerance.

name: Gorditas
description: Thick fried masa pockets stuffed with chicharrón prensado, salsa, and cream.
instructions: Mix masa harina with warm water and salt, form thick round patties.
Deep-fry in hot oil until golden and puffed, about 4 minutes per side.
Slice open to create a pocket, stuff with warm chicharrón prensado.
Top with salsa, sour cream, shredded lettuce, and crumbled cheese.
tips: Make the masa patties thick enough to split open after frying.
Can be cooked on a griddle instead of fried for a lighter version.

name: Flautas
description: Tightly rolled corn tortillas filled with shredded chicken, fried until crispy and golden.
instructions: Poach and shred chicken, season with salt and a squeeze of lime.
Place chicken on tortillas, roll very tightly and secure with toothpicks.
Deep-fry in batches until golden and crispy, about 3 minutes.
Serve topped with sour cream, salsa verde, shredded lettuce, and cheese.
tips: Warm tortillas before rolling so they don't crack.
Remove toothpicks before serving.

name: Carnitas Tacos
description: Slow-braised pork carnitas in small corn tortillas with onion, cilantro, and salsa.
instructions: Cut pork into chunks, simmer in water with orange, garlic, and salt until liquid evaporates.
Fry pork in its own rendered fat until edges are crispy and golden.
Warm corn tortillas on a hot griddle.
Assemble tacos with carnitas, diced onion, cilantro, and salsa verde.
tips: The key is letting the liquid evaporate so the pork fries in its own fat.
Use small double-stacked tortillas for authentic street-style tacos.

name: Tacos de Barbacoa
description: Slow-cooked beef cheeks in a rich chile sauce, served in soft corn tortillas.
instructions: Blend rehydrated chiles with garlic, onion, cumin, and vinegar.
Coat beef cheeks in sauce, wrap in foil, and slow-cook at 150°C for 4 hours.
Shred beef and toss with some cooking juices.
Serve in warm tortillas with chopped onion, cilantro, and lime.
tips: Beef cheeks give the most tender result — substitute chuck if unavailable.
Save the cooking juices for a rich consomé.

name: Tacos de Lengua
description: Tender braised beef tongue sliced and served in corn tortillas with salsa verde.
instructions: Simmer beef tongue with onion, garlic, and bay leaves for 3 hours until tender.
Peel the skin off the tongue and dice the meat into small cubes.
Sear diced tongue on a hot griddle until slightly crispy on edges.
Serve in warm tortillas with salsa verde, cilantro, and lime.
tips: The tongue must be fully cooked to peel easily.
Dicing and searing after braising gives the best texture.

name: Tacos de Suadero
description: Mexico City street tacos with thin-cut beef brisket slow-fried until crispy.
instructions: Simmer brisket in salted water until tender, about 1.5 hours, then slice thin.
Fry thin slices in lard on a hot griddle until edges crisp up.
Warm tortillas on the same griddle to absorb some of the meat juices.
Serve with salsa roja, chopped onion, cilantro, and lime.
tips: The meat should be fork-tender before frying.
Use the rendered fat to warm the tortillas for extra flavor.

name: Tacos Dorados
description: Crispy fried rolled tacos filled with potato, topped with lettuce, cream, and salsa.
instructions: Boil and mash potatoes with salt and a pinch of cumin.
Fill tortillas with mashed potato, roll tightly, and secure with toothpicks.
Fry in hot oil until golden and crispy on all sides.
Top with shredded lettuce, sour cream, salsa verde, and crumbled cheese.
tips: Season the potato filling well — it's the star of the dish.
These are also excellent with shredded chicken filling.

name: Enfrijoladas
description: Tortillas dipped in creamy black bean sauce, filled with cheese and topped with cream.
instructions: Blend cooked black beans with onion, garlic, and epazote until smooth.
Cook bean sauce in oil until thickened, adjust consistency with water.
Dip tortillas in warm bean sauce, fold around crumbled cheese.
Serve topped with more sauce, sour cream, sliced onion, and cheese.
tips: Epazote is traditional — substitute cilantro if unavailable.
The sauce should coat the tortillas like a thick blanket.

name: Entomatadas
description: Tortillas bathed in a simple fresh tomato sauce, filled with cheese.
instructions: Roast tomatoes, chile, onion, and garlic, then blend into a smooth sauce.
Fry sauce in oil for 5 minutes until slightly darkened.
Dip tortillas in warm sauce, fold with crumbled cheese inside.
Serve topped with remaining sauce, sour cream, and extra cheese.
tips: Roasting the tomatoes first gives the sauce a deeper, sweeter flavor.
Can add shredded chicken for a heartier version.

name: Papadzules
description: Yucatecan dish of tortillas filled with hard-boiled eggs in a pumpkin seed sauce.
instructions: Toast pumpkin seeds and blend with epazote and warm water into a smooth green sauce.
Hard-boil eggs, peel, and chop.
Dip tortillas in pumpkin seed sauce, fill with chopped egg, and roll.
Top with more sauce and a drizzle of tomato-habanero salsa.
tips: Do not over-blend pumpkin seeds or the oil will separate too early.
The contrast between the green and red sauces makes this dish stunning.

name: Chiles en Nogada
description: Poblano chiles stuffed with picadillo and topped with walnut cream sauce and pomegranate.
instructions: Roast and peel poblanos, make a slit for stuffing.
Cook picadillo with ground pork, diced fruits, and spices.
Blend walnuts with cream cheese and milk for the nogada sauce.
Stuff chiles, cover with walnut sauce, and garnish with pomegranate and parsley.
tips: This is traditionally a room-temperature dish, not served hot.
Best in August-September when pomegranates and fresh walnuts are in season.

name: Rajas con Crema
description: Roasted poblano strips cooked in a creamy sauce with corn and onions.
instructions: Roast and peel poblanos, cut into strips (rajas).
Sauté onion and garlic, add rajas and corn, cook 5 minutes.
Pour in cream, add cheese, and simmer until melted and thickened.
Serve with warm tortillas or as a side dish.
tips: Charring the poblanos well makes peeling easier and adds smokiness.
This is a perfect vegetarian taco filling.

name: Calabacitas con Queso
description: Mexican sautéed squash with corn, tomatoes, and melted cheese.
instructions: Dice squash, tomatoes, onion, and poblano.
Sauté onion and poblano, add squash and corn, cook 10 minutes.
Add diced tomato, season with salt and pepper, cook 5 more minutes.
Top with shredded cheese, cover to melt, and serve.
tips: Mexican gray squash (calabacita) is ideal but zucchini works too.
Don't overcook — the squash should have a slight bite.

name: Ensalada de Nopales
description: Chilled cactus paddle salad with tomato, onion, cilantro, and queso fresco.
instructions: Boil nopales until tender, drain and rinse away the slime, then dice.
Dice tomato and red onion, chop cilantro.
Toss everything with lime juice, oregano, and salt.
Top with crumbled queso fresco and serve at room temperature.
tips: Rinsing cooked nopales well removes the mucilaginous texture.
This salad pairs perfectly with grilled meats.

name: Esquites
description: Mexican corn in a cup with mayo, lime, chile powder, and cotija cheese.
instructions: Sauté corn kernels in butter with epazote until charred and tender.
Toss with mayonnaise and lime juice.
Serve in cups topped with crumbled cotija cheese and chile powder.
Add extra lime wedges on the side.
tips: Use fresh corn cut off the cob for the best flavor and texture.
Epazote is traditional but optional.

name: Picadillo
description: Mexican ground beef hash with potatoes, tomatoes, and warm spices.
instructions: Brown ground beef with onion and garlic, drain excess fat.
Add diced potatoes, chopped tomatoes, cumin, and chipotle.
Simmer covered for 20 minutes until potatoes are tender.
Serve with rice and warm tortillas, garnished with cilantro.
tips: Some regions add raisins and olives for a sweet-savory twist.
This is a perfect filling for empanadas or chiles rellenos.

name: Albóndigas Soup
description: Mexican meatball soup in a light tomato-chipotle broth with vegetables.
instructions: Mix ground beef with cooked rice, egg, and seasonings, form into small meatballs.
Blend tomatoes with chipotle and onion, cook in a pot with broth.
Add meatballs, diced carrots, and zucchini to the broth, simmer 25 minutes.
Serve hot with chopped cilantro and lime wedges.
tips: Adding rice to meatballs keeps them tender and moist.
Do not stir vigorously or meatballs may break apart.

name: Tinga de Pollo
description: Shredded chicken braised in a smoky chipotle-tomato sauce with onions.
instructions: Poach chicken breasts, then shred with two forks.
Blend tomatoes with chipotle, garlic, and oregano.
Sauté sliced onion, add the sauce, simmer 10 minutes.
Add shredded chicken, cook 10 more minutes until flavors meld.
tips: Tinga makes an excellent filling for tostadas, tacos, or tortas.
Adjust chipotle quantity to control the heat and smokiness.

name: Menudo Rojo
description: Traditional Mexican tripe soup in a red chile broth with hominy.
instructions: Clean and boil tripe for 2 hours until tender, cut into small pieces.
Toast and rehydrate chiles, blend with garlic, onion, and oregano.
Add chile sauce and drained hominy to the tripe broth, simmer 30 minutes.
Serve with oregano, diced onion, crushed chile flakes, and lime.
tips: Menudo is traditionally a weekend morning dish, said to cure hangovers.
Thorough cleaning of the tripe is essential.

name: Pambazos
description: Bread rolls dipped in guajillo sauce and fried, stuffed with potato and chorizo.
instructions: Cook crumbled chorizo with diced boiled potatoes until crispy.
Blend rehydrated guajillo chiles with garlic into a smooth sauce.
Dip bread rolls in guajillo sauce, fry on a griddle until crispy outside.
Split rolls, fill with potato-chorizo mixture, top with lettuce, cream, and cheese.
tips: The bread should be completely coated in red sauce before frying.
Pambazo bread is soft and absorbent — use telera or bolillo as substitutes.

name: Torta Ahogada
description: Guadalajara-style sandwich drowned in a spicy chile de árbol tomato sauce.
instructions: Blend roasted tomatoes, chiles de árbol, garlic, and vinegar into a thin spicy sauce.
Split birote rolls, spread with refried beans, fill with warm carnitas.
Place sandwiches in a dish and pour the hot sauce over until drowned.
Serve with sliced onion and lime wedges.
tips: Birote bread is hard-crusted and doesn't dissolve in sauce — use crusty rolls.
This dish is meant to be messy — eat with a fork and knife.

name: Molletes
description: Open-faced bolillo rolls topped with refried beans, melted cheese, and pico de gallo.
instructions: Split bolillos in half, spread generously with refried beans.
Top with shredded cheese and broil until cheese is melted and bubbly.
Make pico de gallo by dicing tomato, onion, jalapeño, and cilantro.
Top molletes with pico de gallo and serve immediately.
tips: Molletes are a classic Mexican breakfast or snack.
Use day-old bread for the best texture when toasted.

name: Sopa de Tortilla
description: Classic Mexican tortilla soup with fried tortilla strips in a tomato-chile broth.
instructions: Cut tortillas into strips and fry until crispy, set aside.
Blend roasted tomatoes, garlic, and onion, fry in oil, add chicken broth.
Toast pasilla chiles and add to simmering soup for 15 minutes.
Ladle into bowls, top with tortilla strips, avocado, cheese, and cream.
tips: The pasilla chile gives a mild, fruity heat — do not substitute with ancho.
Garnishes make this soup — don't skip the avocado and cheese.

name: Sopa de Lima
description: Yucatecan lime soup with shredded chicken and crispy tortilla strips.
instructions: Simmer chicken in broth with onion, garlic, and whole habanero until cooked, then shred.
Roast and dice tomatoes, add to broth with lime juice.
Cut tortillas into strips and fry until crispy.
Serve soup with shredded chicken, tortilla strips, and lime slices.
tips: Use Yucatan limes (lima agria) if available — they have a unique floral flavor.
Keep the habanero whole so it flavors the broth without making it too spicy.

name: Chiles Toreados
description: Blistered serrano chiles pan-fried with soy sauce and lime, served as a fiery side.
instructions: Blister whole serrano chiles in hot oil until charred on all sides.
Add sliced onion rings and cook until softened.
Splash with soy sauce and lime juice, toss well.
Serve immediately as a side dish with any meal.
tips: The soy-lime combination is surprisingly traditional in Mexican taco stands.
Handle with care — the steam from blistering chiles is very potent.

name: Arroz Rojo
description: Mexican red rice cooked in tomato broth with peas and carrots.
instructions: Rinse and soak rice 10 minutes, drain thoroughly.
Fry rice in oil until golden, add blended tomato, onion, and garlic.
Add broth, diced carrot, and peas, bring to a boil.
Cover, reduce to low heat, cook 18 minutes without lifting the lid.
tips: Do not stir the rice once the broth is added or it becomes sticky.
Soaking the rice before frying gives each grain a better texture.

name: Frijoles Charros
description: Cowboy-style pinto beans stewed with bacon, chorizo, tomato, and chile.
instructions: Cook pinto beans until tender (or use canned), reserve the broth.
Fry diced bacon and crumbled chorizo until crispy.
Add diced onion, tomato, jalapeño, and garlic, cook 5 minutes.
Add beans with broth, simmer 20 minutes, finish with cilantro.
tips: These beans are meant to be soupy, not thick.
Add a beer for extra depth of flavor.

name: Frijoles Negros Refritos
description: Creamy refried black beans with onion and epazote, a staple Mexican side.
instructions: Cook black beans until soft, or use canned, reserving liquid.
Fry diced onion and garlic in lard until golden.
Add beans and mash to desired consistency, adding bean liquid as needed.
Cook until thick and creamy, top with crumbled cheese.
tips: Lard gives the most authentic flavor — substitute oil for a lighter version.
Reserve some whole beans to stir in at the end for texture.

name: Empanadas de Pollo
description: Crispy fried turnovers filled with seasoned shredded chicken and potato.
instructions: Make dough from masa harina, water, and salt. Divide into balls and press flat.
Shred poached chicken, mix with diced cooked potato, onion, tomato, and chipotle.
Place filling on dough rounds, fold in half, seal edges with a fork.
Deep-fry until golden and crispy, about 4 minutes per side.
tips: Press dough thin enough so they cook through but thick enough to hold filling.
These can also be baked at 200°C for a lighter version.

name: Mole de Olla
description: Rustic Mexican beef and vegetable stew in a light ancho-pasilla chile broth.
instructions: Simmer beef shank in water until tender, about 1.5 hours, skim broth.
Toast and blend chiles with some broth, strain into the pot.
Add chayote, corn cut in rounds, zucchini, and green beans.
Simmer 20 minutes, add epazote, and serve with lime and tortillas.
tips: This is not the same as thick mole sauces — it should be a light broth.
The vegetables should be cut in large rustic chunks.

name: Camarones al Ajillo
description: Mexican garlic shrimp sautéed in butter with guajillo chile and lime.
instructions: Sauté sliced garlic and crumbled guajillo chile in butter until fragrant.
Add shrimp in a single layer, cook 2 minutes per side until pink.
Deglaze with white wine and lime juice, cook 1 minute more.
Serve immediately with crusty bread, garnished with parsley.
tips: Do not overcook the shrimp — they should be just pink and curled.
Use the biggest shrimp you can find for the best presentation.

name: Camarones a la Diabla
description: Devilishly spicy shrimp in a fiery red chile sauce.
instructions: Toast and rehydrate all chiles, blend with tomato, garlic, and onion.
Fry sauce in oil until darkened and thick, about 8 minutes.
Add cleaned shrimp to the sauce, cook 4 minutes until pink.
Serve with rice and a squeeze of lime.
tips: Remove seeds from chiles de árbol if you want less heat.
This sauce also works beautifully with chicken or fish.

name: Huaraches
description: Large sandal-shaped masa flatbreads topped with beans, meat, salsa, and cheese.
instructions: Mix masa with water and salt, form into large oval sandal shapes.
Press a layer of refried beans inside the masa, seal and flatten.
Cook on a hot griddle until cooked through and slightly crispy.
Top with sliced carne asada, salsa verde, cheese, lettuce, and cream.
tips: The beans inside the masa add flavor and help the huarache hold its shape.
Make sure the griddle is very hot for the best crust.

name: Quesabirria
description: Birria-style quesadillas with melted cheese and consomé for dipping.
instructions: Braise beef in a chile sauce (guajillo, garlic, onion, oregano) for 2 hours until tender.
Shred beef, strain the consomé and reserve.
Dip tortillas in consomé, fill with shredded beef and cheese, fold and griddle until crispy.
Serve with a bowl of hot consomé for dipping, topped with cilantro and onion.
tips: The consomé dip is what makes this dish addictive.
Press the quesadillas firmly on the griddle for maximum crispiness.

name: Tamales de Rajas con Queso
description: Steamed masa tamales filled with roasted poblano strips and melted cheese.
instructions: Soak corn husks in warm water. Roast and peel poblanos, cut into strips.
Beat lard until fluffy, mix in masa, broth, baking powder, and salt until spongy.
Spread masa on husks, add poblano strips and cheese, fold and tie.
Steam tamales upright for 1 hour until masa pulls away from husk.
tips: Test masa readiness: a small ball should float in water.
Let tamales rest 15 minutes after steaming for the masa to set.

name: Tamales de Mole
description: Traditional tamales filled with shredded chicken and rich mole sauce.
instructions: Prepare masa by beating lard, mixing with masa harina, broth, and salt.
Heat mole paste with broth until smooth, toss with shredded chicken.
Spread masa on soaked corn husks, add chicken mole filling, fold.
Steam for 1 hour, garnish with sesame seeds when serving.
tips: Use store-bought mole paste for convenience — Doña María is a good brand.
These freeze well for up to 3 months.

name: Torta de Milanesa
description: Mexican sandwich with breaded fried cutlet, avocado, beans, and jalapeños.
instructions: Pound chicken thin, dredge in flour, egg, and breadcrumbs.
Fry breaded cutlets in hot oil until golden and cooked through.
Split rolls, spread with refried beans, layer with milanesa.
Top with avocado slices, pickled jalapeños, and lettuce.
tips: Pound the chicken very thin and even for quick, crispy frying.
Toasting the bolillo adds a great crunch contrast.

name: Pollo al Pastor
description: Achiote-marinated chicken grilled with pineapple, pastor-style.
instructions: Blend achiote paste with orange juice, garlic, and vinegar to make marinade.
Marinate chicken thighs for at least 1 hour.
Grill chicken and pineapple slices until charred, then dice.
Serve in tortillas with diced pineapple, onion, and cilantro.
tips: Achiote paste gives the signature red-orange color and earthy flavor.
Grilling over charcoal adds the most authentic smoky flavor.

name: Chalupas
description: Small fried masa boats topped with shredded pork, salsa verde, and onion from Puebla.
instructions: Mix masa with water and salt, form small thick rounds and press a rim around edges.
Fry masa boats in hot oil until crispy on the outside.
Top with shredded pork and salsa verde.
Garnish with finely diced raw onion.
tips: Chalupas from Puebla are small — about 8cm across.
The raised rim holds the toppings in place.

name: Tres Leches Cake
description: Sponge cake soaked in three milks: evaporated, condensed, and heavy cream.
instructions: Beat eggs and sugar until pale and fluffy, fold in flour gently.
Bake at 175°C for 25 minutes until golden and springy.
Mix three milks together, poke holes in cake, pour milk mixture over.
Refrigerate overnight, serve with whipped cream and cinnamon.
tips: The cake must be completely cool before adding the milk mixture.
Overnight soaking gives the best results — the cake should be moist but not falling apart.

name: Arroz con Leche
description: Mexican cinnamon rice pudding made with whole milk, raisins, and condensed milk.
instructions: Boil rice in water with cinnamon sticks until tender.
Add whole milk and condensed milk, stir frequently.
Cook on low heat until thick and creamy, about 20 minutes.
Stir in raisins and vanilla, serve warm or chilled.
tips: Stir constantly toward the end to prevent sticking.
Mexican-style is usually creamier than Spanish versions.

name: Flan Napolitano
description: Creamy Mexican custard with cream cheese on a caramel base.
instructions: Caramelize sugar in a flan mold until dark amber.
Blend eggs, condensed milk, evaporated milk, cream cheese, and vanilla.
Pour through a strainer into the caramel-lined mold.
Bake in a water bath at 175°C for 50 minutes, chill overnight and invert.
tips: The water bath prevents the custard from cracking.
Chill at least 8 hours for the caramel to liquefy into a sauce.

name: Horchata
description: Refreshing Mexican rice water drink with cinnamon and vanilla.
instructions: Soak rice and cinnamon sticks in water for at least 4 hours or overnight.
Blend soaked rice and cinnamon with the soaking water until smooth.
Strain through a fine mesh sieve or cheesecloth twice.
Add sugar and vanilla, stir to dissolve, serve over ice.
tips: Double-straining is essential for a smooth, non-gritty drink.
Some vendors add a splash of evaporated milk for richness.

name: Agua de Jamaica
description: Tart and refreshing hibiscus flower iced tea sweetened with sugar.
instructions: Bring water to a boil, add dried hibiscus flowers, remove from heat.
Steep for 15 minutes until deeply red, then strain.
Stir in sugar until dissolved, add lime juice.
Chill and serve over ice.
tips: Adjust sugar to taste — Jamaica is naturally very tart.
The spent flowers can be candied or used in salads.

name: Mexican Hot Chocolate
description: Rich hot chocolate made with Mexican chocolate, cinnamon, and a hint of chile.
instructions: Heat milk with cinnamon stick until simmering.
Add chopped Mexican chocolate and stir until fully melted.
Add vanilla and a pinch of cayenne pepper.
Froth with a molinillo or whisk and serve hot.
tips: Ibarra or Abuelita are the most common Mexican chocolate brands.
Traditional molinillo frothing creates the signature foam.

name: Mango con Chile
description: Fresh mango slices dusted with chile-lime seasoning, a classic Mexican street snack.
instructions: Peel and slice mangoes into spears or cubes.
Squeeze lime juice over mango.
Drizzle with chamoy and dust generously with Tajín.
Serve immediately as a refreshing snack.
tips: Use firm but ripe mangoes — too soft and they fall apart.
Tajín can be substituted with any chile-lime powder.

name: Conchas
description: Mexican sweet bread rolls topped with a crunchy sugar shell in a seashell pattern.
instructions: Mix flour, yeast, sugar, eggs, butter, and milk into a soft enriched dough.
Knead 10 minutes until smooth, let rise 1 hour until doubled.
Make topping: cream butter, sugar, and flour, press shell pattern on top of rolls.
Bake at 180°C for 18 minutes until golden. Cool on rack.
tips: Use a concha cutter for the pattern or score with a knife.
These are best eaten the same day — warm with coffee.

name: Chile Relleno de Queso
description: Egg-battered poblano chiles stuffed with melted cheese in tomato sauce.
instructions: Roast and peel poblanos, make a slit and remove seeds, stuff with cheese.
Separate eggs, beat whites to stiff peaks, fold in yolks and flour.
Coat stuffed chiles in batter, fry until golden on all sides.
Serve in roasted tomato sauce with rice.
tips: The egg batter must be very fluffy — beat whites until stiff peaks form.
Dredge chiles in flour before battering for better adhesion.

name: Coctel de Camarones
description: Mexican shrimp cocktail in a tomato-citrus broth with avocado and hot sauce.
instructions: Mix ketchup, lime juice, orange juice, and hot sauce to make the cocktail base.
Dice cucumber, avocado, and red onion.
Toss shrimp and vegetables with the cocktail sauce.
Serve in tall glasses with saltine crackers and extra hot sauce.
tips: This is nothing like American shrimp cocktail — it is more of a cold soup.
Use the biggest shrimp you can find for the best result.

name: Chamoyada
description: Frozen mango slush with chamoy sauce and Tajín, a sweet-sour-spicy treat.
instructions: Blend frozen mango with water and lime juice until slushy.
Drizzle chamoy inside the cup in a spiral pattern.
Pour mango slush into the cup.
Top with more chamoy and a generous dusting of Tajín.
tips: Use frozen mango straight from the freezer for the best slushy texture.
Rim the cup with chamoy and Tajín for extra flair.

name: Poc Chuc
description: Yucatecan grilled pork marinated in sour orange and served with pickled onion.
instructions: Marinate thin pork slices in orange juice, lime juice, and salt for 30 minutes.
Make xni-pec salsa: dice tomato, habanero, and onion, mix with lime and cilantro.
Grill pork over high heat until charred, about 3 minutes per side.
Serve with xni-pec salsa, pickled red onion, and black beans.
tips: Pound pork very thin for quick, even grilling.
Xni-pec (dog's nose salsa) should be very spicy — the habanero is essential.

name: Chiles Anchos Rellenos
description: Dried ancho chiles stuffed with a sweet-savory picadillo and topped with walnut cream.
instructions: Rehydrate ancho chiles in warm water, carefully remove seeds.
Cook picadillo: brown pork, add diced apple, raisins, piloncillo, and cinnamon.
Stuff chiles with the filling.
Blend walnuts with cream cheese and milk, drizzle over stuffed chiles.
tips: Handle rehydrated chiles gently — they tear easily.
This is a holiday dish, often served for Christmas celebrations.

name: Sincronizadas
description: Flour tortilla sandwich with ham, cheese, and beans pressed on a griddle.
instructions: Spread refried beans on one tortilla, layer ham and shredded cheese.
Top with second tortilla, press together.
Cook on a hot griddle until golden and cheese melts, flip once.
Cut into wedges and serve with avocado slices and salsa.
tips: Press down firmly with a spatula for maximum crispiness.
These are a popular late-night snack in Mexico City.

name: Gaeng Keow Wan Tofu (Green Curry Tofu)
description: Creamy Thai green curry with tofu, bamboo shoots, and Thai basil in coconut milk.
instructions: Press tofu and cut into cubes. Simmer green curry paste in a splash of coconut milk for 2 minutes.
Add remaining coconut milk, bamboo shoots, and tofu. Simmer 15 minutes.
Season with fish sauce, stir in Thai basil, and serve over jasmine rice.
tips: Use extra-firm tofu for better texture.
Add Thai eggplant for authenticity.

name: Thai Peanut Noodles
description: Rice noodles tossed in a savory-sweet peanut sauce with crunchy vegetables and lime.
instructions: Cook rice noodles per package directions, drain and rinse with cold water.
Whisk peanut butter, soy sauce, lime juice, and a splash of warm water into a smooth sauce.
Toss noodles with sauce, julienned carrot, sliced cucumber, and top with crushed peanuts.
tips: Add sriracha for heat.
Chill before serving for a refreshing cold noodle dish.

name: Thai Coconut Chicken Soup (Tom Kha Variation)
description: Silky coconut broth with chicken, mushrooms, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves.
instructions: Simmer lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves in coconut milk for 10 minutes.
Add sliced chicken and mushrooms, cook until chicken is done, about 12 minutes.
Season with fish sauce and lime juice, garnish with cilantro.
tips: Bruise lemongrass stalks to release more flavor.
Remove aromatics before serving if preferred.

name: Thai Basil Fried Rice (Khao Pad Kra Pao)
description: Smoky wok-fried rice with Thai holy basil, chili, garlic, and a fried egg on top.
instructions: Heat oil in wok over high heat. Fry garlic and chilies until fragrant.
Add rice, toss vigorously with soy sauce until heated through and slightly charred.
Stir in Thai basil until wilted. Serve topped with a fried egg.
tips: Use day-old rice for best results.
Holy basil is preferred but sweet basil works too.

name: Thai Red Curry with Shrimp
description: Plump shrimp simmered in a rich red curry coconut sauce with bell peppers and Thai basil.
instructions: Fry red curry paste in a splash of coconut milk until fragrant, about 2 minutes.
Add remaining coconut milk, sliced bell pepper, and shrimp. Simmer 8 minutes until shrimp are pink.
Season with fish sauce, stir in Thai basil, and serve with rice.
tips: Devein shrimp for cleaner presentation.
Add bamboo shoots or pea eggplants for variety.

name: Nam Tok Moo (Thai Grilled Pork Salad)
description: Chargrilled pork slices tossed with toasted rice powder, herbs, lime, and chili flakes.
instructions: Grill pork over high heat until charred and cooked through, about 12 minutes. Rest and slice thinly.
Toss sliced pork with lime juice, sliced shallots, chili flakes, and toasted rice powder.
Add cilantro and mint, toss gently, serve immediately with sticky rice.
tips: Make toasted rice powder by dry-roasting jasmine rice and grinding.
Pork neck is the traditional cut for this dish.

name: Pad Woon Sen (Stir-Fried Glass Noodles)
description: Bouncy glass noodles stir-fried with egg, vegetables, and a savory soy-oyster sauce.
instructions: Soak glass noodles in warm water 10 minutes, drain. Scramble eggs in oiled wok, set aside.
Stir-fry garlic, shredded carrot, and cabbage for 2 minutes on high heat.
Add noodles, soy sauce, oyster sauce, scrambled egg, toss until well combined and heated through.
tips: Don't oversoak noodles or they'll become mushy.
Add shrimp or chicken for protein.

name: Thai Crab Fried Rice (Khao Pad Poo)
description: Wok-fried jasmine rice with lump crab meat, egg, and a squeeze of lime.
instructions: Heat oil in wok, scramble eggs, push to side. Fry garlic until golden.
Add rice and crab meat, toss on high heat with soy sauce for 3 minutes.
Squeeze lime over top, garnish with sliced green onions, serve with cucumber slices.
tips: Use day-old rice for best wok hei.
Fresh crab is ideal but canned works in a pinch.

name: Yam Woon Sen (Thai Glass Noodle Salad)
description: Tangy glass noodle salad with shrimp, pork, lime dressing, and fresh herbs.
instructions: Soak glass noodles, drain. Poach shrimp and pork separately until cooked. Let cool.
Make dressing with lime juice, fish sauce, and minced chili.
Toss noodles, shrimp, pork with dressing, add cilantro and sliced shallots, serve at room temperature.
tips: Serve immediately as noodles absorb dressing quickly.
Add celery for crunch.

name: Gai Yang (Thai Grilled Chicken)
description: Juicy Thai-style grilled chicken marinated in lemongrass, garlic, cilantro root, and pepper.
instructions: Blend lemongrass, garlic, cilantro root, pepper, soy sauce, and palm sugar into a paste. Marinate chicken for 30 minutes.
Grill over medium heat, turning occasionally, until charred and cooked through, about 25 minutes.
Rest 5 minutes, chop, and serve with sticky rice and sweet chili sauce.
tips: Marinate overnight for deeper flavor.
Flatten chicken pieces for even cooking.

name: Pad Pak Boong (Stir-Fried Morning Glory)
description: Fiery wok-tossed water spinach with garlic, chili, and fermented soybean paste.
instructions: Heat oil in wok until smoking. Add crushed garlic and sliced chili, fry 10 seconds.
Add morning glory, toss rapidly on high heat for 1 minute.
Season with soy sauce and oyster sauce, toss once more, and serve immediately.
tips: The wok must be extremely hot for the signature smoky flavor.
Substitute with regular spinach if morning glory is unavailable.

name: Khai Jiao (Thai Omelette)
description: Puffy, crispy-edged Thai omelette fried in plenty of oil, served over rice with chili sauce.
instructions: Beat eggs vigorously with fish sauce, soy sauce, and white pepper until frothy.
Heat generous oil in wok until very hot. Pour in egg mixture, let puff and crisp for 1 minute.
Fold omelette, drain briefly, serve over jasmine rice with sriracha.
tips: High heat and plenty of oil is the secret to the signature puffiness.
Add minced pork for Khai Jiao Moo Sap.

name: Kuay Teow Reua (Thai Boat Noodle Soup)
description: Intensely flavored dark broth with rice noodles, beef, herbs, and a splash of vinegar.
instructions: Simmer beef with star anise, cinnamon, and dark soy sauce in water for 30 minutes. Slice beef thinly.
Cook rice noodles, divide into bowls. Ladle hot broth over noodles and sliced beef.
Top with bean sprouts, cilantro, Thai basil, and a splash of white vinegar.
tips: Traditionally served in small bowls for concentrated flavor.
Add pork liver or meatballs for authenticity.

name: Tod Man Khao Pod (Thai Corn Fritters)
description: Crispy golden corn fritters seasoned with red curry paste and kaffir lime leaves.
instructions: Mix corn, curry paste, finely sliced kaffir lime leaves, rice flour, and egg into a thick batter.
Drop spoonfuls into hot oil, fry until golden and crispy, about 3 minutes per side.
Drain on paper towels and serve with sweet chili sauce.
tips: Fresh corn gives the best sweetness and crunch.
Flatten fritters slightly for even cooking.

name: Pad Kee Mao (Drunken Noodles)
description: Spicy, smoky wide rice noodles stir-fried with Thai basil, chili, and garlic.
instructions: Soak noodles in warm water until pliable. Stir-fry sliced chicken in hot wok until cooked, set aside.
In same wok, fry garlic and chili, add noodles, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. Toss on high heat.
Return chicken, add Thai basil, toss until wilted and serve immediately.
tips: The key is extremely high heat for wok hei.
Use flat sen yai noodles for authenticity.

name: Gaeng Liang (Thai Pumpkin Curry Soup)
description: Fragrant shrimp paste-based curry soup with pumpkin, corn, and Thai basil.
instructions: Pound shallots, white pepper, and shrimp paste into a rough paste.
Boil water, add paste, cubed pumpkin, and corn. Simmer until pumpkin is tender, about 15 minutes.
Add shrimp, cook 3 minutes, stir in Thai basil and serve.
tips: This is one of Thailand's healthiest traditional soups.
Use butternut squash as a pumpkin substitute.

name: Pla Rad Prik (Thai Fried Fish with Chili Sauce)
description: Crispy deep-fried whole fish topped with a tangy, sweet, and spicy tamarind-chili sauce.
instructions: Score fish on both sides, pat dry, dust with rice flour. Deep fry until golden and crispy.
In a separate pan, fry garlic and chili, add tamarind, palm sugar, fish sauce, simmer until thick.
Pour sauce over crispy fish, garnish with crispy Thai basil leaves.
tips: Dry the fish thoroughly for maximum crispiness.
Sea bass or tilapia work well for this dish.

name: Gai Pad Med Mamuang (Thai Cashew Chicken)
description: Tender chicken stir-fried with roasted cashews, dried chilies, and a sweet soy glaze.
instructions: Stir-fry diced chicken in hot oil until golden. Add garlic and dried chilies, fry 30 seconds.
Add onion wedges, soy sauce, and oyster sauce. Toss for 2 minutes.
Stir in cashews, toss to combine, and serve with steamed rice.
tips: Toast cashews separately first for extra crunch.
Don't burn the dried chilies.

name: Som Tum Poo (Green Papaya Salad with Crab)
description: Punchy shredded papaya salad with salted crab, tomato, long beans, and peanuts.
instructions: Shred green papaya. In a mortar, pound garlic, chili, and long beans lightly.
Add salted crab, tomato, palm sugar, lime juice, and fish sauce. Pound gently to mix.
Add shredded papaya, toss and pound lightly. Top with crushed peanuts.
tips: This is the Isaan-style version with crab.
Adjust chili to taste — it should be spicy, sour, and sweet.

name: Pla Neung Manao (Steamed Fish with Lime)
description: Delicate steamed fish fillet topped with a fiery lime, garlic, and chili dressing.
instructions: Place fish on a plate, steam over boiling water for 12 minutes until flaky.
Pound garlic and chili in mortar, mix with lime juice, fish sauce, and palm sugar.
Pour dressing over steamed fish, garnish with cilantro and serve.
tips: Use sea bass for the most authentic version.
The dressing should be intensely sour, salty, and spicy.

name: Moo Ping (Thai Grilled Pork Skewers)
description: Sweet and savory grilled pork skewers marinated in coconut milk, garlic, and palm sugar.
instructions: Slice pork thinly, marinate in coconut milk, garlic, palm sugar, fish sauce, and pepper for 30 minutes.
Thread onto bamboo skewers, grill over medium-high heat for 3-4 minutes per side.
Serve with sticky rice and jaew dipping sauce.
tips: Soak bamboo skewers in water first to prevent burning.
Pork shoulder gives the best fat-to-meat ratio.

name: Kanom Jeen Nam Ya (Thai Rice Noodles with Fish Curry)
description: Fermented rice noodles served with a rich, herbal fish curry sauce and fresh vegetables.
instructions: Boil and flake fish. Blend fish, red curry paste, krachai, and a splash of coconut milk into a paste.
Simmer paste with remaining coconut milk for 15 minutes until thickened and fragrant.
Serve curry over rice noodles with raw bean sprouts, long beans, and Thai basil on the side.
tips: Krachai (fingerroot) gives the authentic flavor but galangal can substitute.
This is a beloved Thai street food lunch.

name: Larb Moo (Thai Minced Pork Salad)
description: Zesty minced pork salad with toasted rice powder, fresh mint, cilantro, and fierce chili.
instructions: Cook ground pork in a dry pan until just done, breaking into small pieces. Let cool slightly.
Toss with lime juice, fish sauce, sliced shallots, chili flakes, and toasted rice powder.
Fold in mint and cilantro, serve at room temperature with sticky rice and cabbage leaves.
tips: Don't overcook the pork — it should be juicy.
Toasted rice powder adds the essential nutty crunch.

name: Gaeng Som (Thai Sour Curry)
description: Tangy, spicy southern Thai sour curry with shrimp, vegetables, and tamarind broth.
instructions: Bring water to boil, dissolve sour curry paste. Add tamarind, palm sugar, and fish sauce.
Add chopped green beans and cabbage, simmer 8 minutes until tender.
Add shrimp, cook 3 minutes, taste and adjust sour-sweet balance.
tips: This curry has NO coconut milk — it's meant to be light and tangy.
Thai sour curry paste is different from red curry paste.

name: Moo Grob (Thai Crispy Pork Belly)
description: Shatteringly crispy pork belly with crackling skin, often served over rice with chili vinegar.
instructions: Score pork skin, rub flesh side with five-spice and pepper. Rub skin with vinegar, salt, and baking soda.
Roast at 200°C skin-side up for 60 minutes, then blast at 240°C for 15 minutes until skin puffs and crackles.
Rest 10 minutes, slice into pieces, serve over rice with sweet chili or chili vinegar.
tips: Pat the skin completely dry before seasoning for maximum crackle.
Poke holes in the skin with a fork for even puffing.

name: Peek Gai Tod (Thai Fried Chicken Wings)
description: Double-fried crispy chicken wings tossed in a sticky sweet chili fish sauce glaze.
instructions: Marinate wings in fish sauce and garlic for 15 minutes. Coat in rice flour.
Deep fry at 160°C for 8 minutes, rest 5 minutes, then fry again at 190°C for 3 minutes until super crispy.
Toss in sweet chili sauce and serve immediately.
tips: Double frying is the key to lasting crispiness.
Rice flour gives a lighter, crunchier crust than wheat flour.

name: Khao Man Gai (Thai Chicken Rice)
description: Silky poached chicken served over fragrant garlic-ginger rice with a tangy soybean dipping sauce.
instructions: Poach chicken in gently simmering salted water with ginger slices for 25 minutes. Reserve broth.
Fry garlic in chicken fat, add rice, toast 2 minutes. Cook rice with chicken broth.
Slice chicken, serve over rice with cucumber slices and soybean-chili-ginger dipping sauce.
tips: Ice bath the chicken after poaching for silky, jiggly skin.
This is Thailand's version of Hainanese chicken rice.

name: Phat Phak Bung Fai Daeng (Fire-Roasted Morning Glory)
description: Wok-tossed morning glory with garlic and chili in a dramatic flame technique.
instructions: Heat oil in wok until smoking. Flash-fry garlic, chili, and soybean paste for 10 seconds.
Throw in morning glory, toss vigorously for 30 seconds on highest heat.
Season with soy sauce, toss once more, serve immediately.
tips: This dish must be cooked extremely fast on very high heat.
Known as 'flying vegetable' when tossed from wok to plate.

name: Massaman Beef Stew
description: Slow-braised beef in a rich massaman curry with potatoes, peanuts, and warm spices.
instructions: Sear beef chunks, set aside. Fry curry paste in coconut cream until oil separates.
Add beef, coconut milk, potatoes, tamarind, and palm sugar. Simmer gently for 90 minutes.
Add peanuts in the last 10 minutes. Serve with jasmine rice.
tips: Low and slow is the key — don't rush the simmer.
Massaman curry paste includes cinnamon, cardamom, and cloves.

name: Khanom Tuay (Thai Coconut Pudding)
description: Silky two-layered coconut pudding with a sweet bottom and salty coconut cream top.
instructions: Mix rice flour, sugar, and half the coconut milk with pandan water to make the sweet layer. Steam in small cups for 10 minutes.
Mix remaining coconut milk with a pinch of salt and a little rice flour for the savory top layer.
Pour salty layer on top of set sweet layer, steam 5 more minutes until set.
tips: The contrast of sweet bottom and salty top is the signature.
Use fresh pandan for the best color and aroma.

name: Gluay Buat Chi (Bananas in Coconut Milk)
description: Warm poached bananas in sweet, salted coconut milk — a classic Thai comfort dessert.
instructions: Bring coconut milk, sugar, salt, and pandan leaves to a gentle simmer.
Add halved bananas, poach gently for 5 minutes until just tender.
Serve warm in bowls with the coconut milk broth.
tips: Use slightly underripe bananas so they hold their shape.
Nam Wa bananas are traditional but any short variety works.

name: Gai Hor Bai Toey (Pandan Chicken)
description: Marinated chicken wrapped in pandan leaves and deep-fried until fragrant and crispy.
instructions: Marinate diced chicken in soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and garlic for 20 minutes.
Wrap each piece in a pandan leaf, securing with a toothpick.
Deep fry parcels for 5-6 minutes until golden. Serve with sweet chili sauce.
tips: The pandan leaf infuses an incredible aroma into the chicken.
Don't unwrap before eating — pull the chicken out of the leaf.

name: Gaeng Hung Lay (Northern Thai Pork Curry)
description: Rich Burmese-influenced pork curry with ginger, tamarind, and warm spices from Chiang Mai.
instructions: Simmer cubed pork belly in hung lay paste with ginger, tamarind, and palm sugar for 45 minutes.
Add peanuts and pickled garlic in the last 10 minutes.
Serve with steamed jasmine rice — the sauce should be thick and glossy.
tips: This is Chiang Mai's signature curry, distinct from central Thai curries.
The flavor should be sweet-sour-savory without coconut milk.

name: Pla Meuk Yang (Thai Grilled Squid)
description: Chargrilled whole squid basted with a sweet soy glaze, served with seafood dipping sauce.
instructions: Clean squid, score the body in a crosshatch pattern. Mix soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar, and garlic into a glaze.
Grill squid over high heat, basting with glaze, about 2-3 minutes per side.
Slice and serve with nam jim seafood dipping sauce and lime wedges.
tips: Don't overcook — squid goes from tender to rubbery in seconds.
A common Thai street food, best from charcoal grills.

name: Khao Kluk Kapi (Shrimp Paste Fried Rice)
description: Aromatic fried rice with shrimp paste served with sweet pork, dried shrimp, and mango.
instructions: Fry shrimp paste in oil until fragrant. Add rice and toss on high heat for 3 minutes.
Push rice aside, scramble egg in the wok, then mix together.
Serve rice mound surrounded by sliced green mango, fried shallots, dried shrimp, and sweet pork.
tips: This is a composed dish — each element is placed around the rice.
The shrimp paste gives an intense umami base.

name: Nam Tok Neua (Thai Beef Waterfall Salad)
description: Grilled beef salad with charred edges, tossed in lime, fish sauce, and toasted rice powder.
instructions: Grill steak over high heat to medium-rare, about 4 minutes per side. Rest and slice thinly against the grain.
Toss sliced beef with lime juice, fish sauce, sliced shallots, chili flakes, and toasted rice powder.
Fold in fresh mint, serve immediately with sticky rice.
tips: The name 'waterfall' refers to the juices dripping during grilling.
Medium-rare is essential for tender slices.

name: Pad King Tofu (Stir-Fried Tofu with Ginger)
description: Crispy tofu stir-fried with julienned ginger, mushrooms, and a light soy-oyster sauce.
instructions: Pan-fry cubed tofu until golden on all sides. Set aside.
Stir-fry julienned ginger and sliced mushrooms for 2 minutes. Add bell pepper.
Return tofu, add soy sauce and oyster sauce, toss. Garnish with green onions.
tips: Fresh young ginger is milder and works best here.
Press tofu well for crispier results.

name: Gaeng Pa (Thai Jungle Curry)
description: Fiery coconut-free curry packed with vegetables, herbs, and green peppercorns.
instructions: Fry jungle curry paste in oil until fragrant. Add sliced chicken, stir-fry 3 minutes.
Add water, bamboo shoots, long beans, and green peppercorns. Simmer 12 minutes.
Stir in Thai basil, season with fish sauce. Serve with rice.
tips: This is a NO-coconut-milk curry — thin, spicy broth.
Green peppercorns give the distinctive tongue-numbing heat.

name: Goong Makham (Thai Tamarind Shrimp)
description: Large shrimp glazed in a caramelized sweet-sour tamarind sauce with crispy shallots.
instructions: Fry shallots until crispy, set aside. In same pan, sear shrimp until pink, set aside.
Make sauce: simmer tamarind, palm sugar, fish sauce, and garlic until thick and glossy.
Toss shrimp in sauce, plate and top with crispy shallots.
tips: The sauce should coat the back of a spoon — thick and sticky.
Use head-on shrimp for the most dramatic presentation.

name: Pad Kra Pao Goong (Thai Basil Shrimp)
description: Wok-fired shrimp with holy basil, chili, and garlic in a savory sauce served over rice.
instructions: Heat oil in wok until smoking. Stir-fry garlic and chili for 10 seconds.
Add shrimp, toss on high heat until pink, about 2 minutes.
Add soy sauce, oyster sauce, and holy basil. Toss until basil wilts, serve over rice.
tips: Holy basil gives the authentic peppery flavor.
Cook fast on high heat — shrimp overcook quickly.

name: Ice Cream Kati (Thai Coconut Ice Cream)
description: Rich coconut milk ice cream served in a coconut shell with peanuts and sweet corn.
instructions: Heat coconut milk with sugar and salt until dissolved. Chill completely.
Churn in an ice cream maker until thick and creamy, about 20 minutes.
Serve scoops topped with sweet corn, crushed peanuts, and a spoonful of sticky rice.
tips: No ice cream maker? Freeze in a pan, stirring every 30 minutes.
The peanut-corn-sticky rice combo is the traditional topping.

name: Gaeng Phed Ped Yang (Red Curry Roast Duck)
description: Succulent roast duck in red curry with pineapple, tomatoes, and Thai basil.
instructions: Fry red curry paste in coconut cream until oil separates. Add remaining coconut milk.
Add sliced roast duck, pineapple chunks, and halved tomatoes. Simmer 10 minutes.
Season with fish sauce, stir in Thai basil, serve with steamed rice.
tips: Buy ready-roasted duck from a Chinese BBQ shop.
The pineapple adds essential sweetness to balance the curry.

name: Yam Som O (Thai Pomelo Salad)
description: Refreshing pomelo segments tossed with toasted coconut, dried shrimp, peanuts, and chili.
instructions: Peel pomelo and break into segments, then shred into small pieces.
Make dressing with lime juice, fish sauce, palm sugar, and chili.
Toss pomelo with dressing, toasted coconut, dried shrimp, peanuts, and sliced shallots.
tips: Use pink pomelo for the prettiest presentation.
This is a common festive Thai dish.

name: Yam Pla Dook Foo (Crispy Catfish Salad)
description: Fluffy shredded deep-fried catfish served with a spicy green mango salad.
instructions: Grill or steam catfish, flake meat finely. Deep fry flaked fish until golden and puffy.
Shred green mango, slice shallots and chili. Mix with lime juice and fish sauce.
Mound crispy fish on plate, top with green mango salad, serve immediately.
tips: The catfish should be extremely fluffy after frying.
Eat quickly — the crispiness fades fast.

name: Khao Niao Durian (Sticky Rice with Durian)
description: Warm sticky rice with rich coconut cream and fresh durian — a polarizing Thai delicacy.
instructions: Soak sticky rice 4 hours, then steam until tender, about 20 minutes.
Heat coconut milk with sugar and salt. Mix half into the hot sticky rice.
Serve sticky rice with durian flesh and drizzle remaining coconut sauce on top.
tips: Use fresh durian for the most authentic experience.
This is as beloved in Thailand as mango sticky rice.

name: Moo Tod Kratiem (Thai Garlic Pepper Pork)
description: Golden fried pork marinated with garlic, black pepper, and cilantro root, served over rice.
instructions: Pound garlic, pepper, and cilantro root into a paste. Marinate sliced pork with paste and soy sauce for 15 minutes.
Pan-fry pork slices in hot oil until golden and cooked through, about 4 minutes per side.
Serve over rice with cucumber slices and a fried egg if desired.
tips: Cilantro root is essential for the authentic Thai flavor.
Don't skip the black pepper — it's a key element.

name: Massaman Gai (Massaman Chicken Curry)
description: Tender chicken simmered in a mild, aromatic massaman curry with potato and onion.
instructions: Fry massaman paste in coconut cream until fragrant. Add chicken, sear 3 minutes.
Pour in coconut milk, add chunked potatoes and quartered onion. Simmer 30 minutes.
Add peanuts and palm sugar, cook 5 more minutes. Serve with rice.
tips: Massaman is the mildest Thai curry — perfect for spice-sensitive eaters.
Originally Muslim-influenced, it uses warm spices like cinnamon and star anise.

name: Tom Yum Goong Nam Khon (Creamy Tom Yum Shrimp)
description: The creamy version of tom yum with shrimp, mushrooms, and a rich chili-coconut broth.
instructions: Simmer lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves in water for 10 minutes.
Add mushrooms, shrimp, and nam prik pao (roasted chili paste). Cook until shrimp are pink.
Stir in coconut milk, squeeze in lime juice. Serve garnished with cilantro.
tips: Nam prik pao is the key to the creamy version — it adds richness and color.
Don't boil after adding coconut milk.

name: Gaeng Keow Wan Gai (Green Curry Chicken)
description: Classic Thai green curry with tender chicken, bamboo shoots, and Thai eggplant in coconut milk.
instructions: Fry green curry paste in coconut cream until fragrant. Add chicken, cook 5 minutes.
Pour in remaining coconut milk, add eggplant and bamboo shoots. Simmer 15 minutes.
Season with fish sauce and palm sugar, stir in Thai basil, serve with rice.
tips: Thai eggplant are small and round — use regular eggplant if unavailable.
The curry should be rich and creamy, not watery.

name: Yam Mamuang (Thai Green Mango Salad)
description: Crunchy shredded green mango tossed with dried shrimp, peanuts, and a tangy lime dressing.
instructions: Shred green mango into thin strips. Slice shallots thinly.
Make dressing with lime juice, fish sauce, and palm sugar.
Toss mango, shallots, dried shrimp, and peanuts with dressing. Serve immediately.
tips: The mango must be unripe — sour and crunchy.
Add fresh chili for heat.

name: Gaeng Garee Gai (Yellow Curry Chicken)
description: Mild, turmeric-golden chicken curry with potatoes and onion in a creamy coconut base.
instructions: Fry yellow curry paste and turmeric in coconut cream until fragrant. Add chicken, sear 3 minutes.
Pour in coconut milk, add cubed potatoes and sliced onion. Simmer 25 minutes.
Season with fish sauce. Serve with rice or roti canai.
tips: This is the mildest Thai curry — great for beginners.
Traditionally served with roti bread for dipping.

name: Tom Zab (Thai Northeastern Pork Rib Soup)
description: Clear, intensely herbal Isaan pork rib soup with lemongrass, galangal, and dill.
instructions: Simmer pork ribs in water with lemongrass and galangal for 30 minutes until tender.
Season with fish sauce and lime juice. Add crushed chili.
Stir in fresh dill just before serving. Serve with sticky rice.
tips: Dill is the signature herb of Isaan soups — don't substitute.
The broth should be clear, sour, and intensely herbal.

name: Khao Soi Gai (Chiang Mai Curry Noodles)
description: Northern Thai coconut curry soup with egg noodles, chicken, and crispy noodle topping.
instructions: Fry curry paste with turmeric in coconut cream. Add chicken, coat well, cook 5 minutes.
Pour in coconut milk and water, simmer 25 minutes until chicken is tender.
Serve over egg noodles, topped with crispy fried noodles, sliced shallots, and pickled mustard greens.
tips: Reserve some noodles, fry until crispy for the essential topping.
This is Chiang Mai's most famous noodle dish.

name: Sangkaya Fak Thong (Pumpkin Custard)
description: Silky coconut custard steamed inside a whole pumpkin — a beloved Thai dessert.
instructions: Cut top off pumpkin, scoop out seeds. Whisk eggs with coconut milk, palm sugar, salt, and pandan extract.
Pour custard into pumpkin, replace lid, steam for 45 minutes until custard is set.
Cool, slice pumpkin into wedges so each piece has custard attached.
tips: The pumpkin must be small enough to fit in your steamer.
The custard should be just set — not over-steamed.

name: Khao Niao Sang Kaya (Sticky Rice with Egg Custard)
description: Sweet coconut sticky rice paired with a rich, firm pandan-scented egg custard.
instructions: Soak sticky rice, steam until tender. Mix with half the coconut milk, sugar, and salt.
Whisk eggs with remaining coconut milk, palm sugar, and pandan extract. Steam custard for 20 minutes.
Serve warm sticky rice with sliced custard on the side.
tips: The custard should be firm enough to slice.
A Thai market staple, often sold in banana leaf wraps.

name: Kor Moo Yang (Thai Grilled Pork Neck)
description: Juicy chargrilled pork neck marinated in garlic, lemongrass, and fish sauce.
instructions: Blend garlic, lemongrass, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and palm sugar. Marinate pork neck for 20 minutes.
Grill over medium heat, turning every 5 minutes, until charred and cooked through, about 20 minutes.
Rest 5 minutes, slice across the grain, serve with sticky rice and jaew dipping sauce.
tips: Pork neck has the perfect fat content for grilling.
This is Thailand's most popular street food grilled meat.

name: Pad Kra Pao Gai Sap (Thai Basil Minced Chicken)
description: Lightning-fast stir-fry of minced chicken with holy basil, chili, and garlic over rice.
instructions: Heat oil in wok until smoking. Fry garlic and crushed chili 10 seconds.
Add ground chicken, break apart, stir-fry on high heat until cooked, about 3 minutes.
Add soy sauce, oyster sauce, and holy basil. Toss until basil wilts, serve over rice with a fried egg.
tips: This is Thailand's most-eaten weekday lunch.
Holy basil is non-negotiable for the authentic version.

name: Yam Tofu Tod (Crispy Tofu Salad)
description: Golden fried tofu cubes tossed in a spicy lime dressing with fresh herbs and roasted chili.
instructions: Cut tofu into cubes, deep fry until golden and crispy on all sides.
Make dressing with lime juice, fish sauce, and roasted chili flakes.
Toss crispy tofu with dressing, sliced shallots, and cilantro. Serve immediately.
tips: Serve quickly before tofu loses crispiness.
Add minced pork for a heartier version.

name: Khai Pad Kra Pao (Stir-Fried Egg with Basil)
description: Simple scrambled eggs stir-fried with holy basil, chili, and oyster sauce over rice.
instructions: Heat oil in wok. Fry garlic and crushed chili for 10 seconds.
Crack in eggs, let set slightly, then scramble on high heat for 1 minute.
Add oyster sauce and Thai basil, toss until basil wilts, serve over rice.
tips: Don't over-scramble — eggs should be slightly runny.
A quick, cheap Thai street food meal.

name: Chicken Katsu Curry
description: Crispy panko-breaded chicken cutlet served over rice with a rich Japanese curry sauce.
instructions: Dice onion, carrot, and potato into bite-sized pieces and simmer in 600 ml water until tender, about 15 minutes.
Turn off heat, add curry roux blocks, and stir until dissolved; simmer on low 5 minutes until thickened.
Season chicken breasts with salt, dredge in flour, dip in beaten egg, then coat generously with panko.
Heat oil in a deep pan to 170 C and fry chicken 5-6 minutes per side until golden and cooked through.
Rest chicken on a wire rack for 2 minutes, then slice into strips.
Serve sliced katsu over steamed rice with curry sauce ladled alongside.
tips: Press panko firmly onto chicken for a crunchier crust.
Japanese curry roux brands like Golden Curry or Vermont Curry are widely available.

name: Beef Gyudon (Rice Bowl)
description: Thinly sliced beef and onions simmered in a sweet soy-dashi broth, served over steamed rice.
instructions: Slice onion into thin half-moons and separate the layers.
Combine dashi, soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and sake in a shallow pan and bring to a simmer.
Add onion slices and cook for 5 minutes until softened.
Spread beef slices over the onions and simmer 3-4 minutes until just cooked through.
Skim any foam, then spoon beef and sauce over bowls of steamed rice.
Top with sliced spring onion and pickled ginger.
tips: Use beef sliced paper-thin (shabu-shabu cut) for the most authentic texture.
A raw egg yolk dropped on top is a popular Yoshinoya-style addition.

name: Tonkatsu (Pork Cutlet)
description: Deep-fried panko-crusted pork loin served with shredded cabbage and tonkatsu sauce.
instructions: Score pork edges to prevent curling, season with salt and pepper.
Set up breading station: flour, beaten egg, and panko in separate shallow dishes.
Dredge pork in flour, dip in egg, then press firmly into panko on both sides.
Heat oil to 170 C in a deep pot and fry pork for 6-7 minutes, flipping once, until deep golden.
Rest on a wire rack for 3 minutes, then slice into 2 cm strips.
Serve over rice with finely shredded cabbage, tonkatsu sauce, and a lemon wedge.
tips: Double-fry for extra crunch: fry 4 minutes, rest 2, then fry again for 2 minutes.
Make tonkatsu sauce by mixing Worcestershire sauce, ketchup, and a pinch of sugar.

name: Yakitori (Grilled Chicken Skewers)
description: Bite-sized chicken pieces grilled on bamboo skewers with a sweet soy tare glaze.
instructions: Cut chicken thighs into 2.5 cm cubes and cut spring onion whites into 3 cm pieces.
Soak bamboo skewers in water for 20 minutes to prevent burning.
Make tare sauce by simmering soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar until slightly thickened, about 5 minutes.
Thread chicken and spring onion pieces alternately onto skewers.
Grill skewers over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side, brushing with tare sauce twice during cooking.
Serve with a final brush of tare and a sprinkle of shichimi togarashi.
tips: Chicken thighs stay juicier than breast on the grill.
For authentic yakitori, grill over binchotan charcoal if available.

name: Saba Shioyaki (Grilled Mackerel)
description: Salt-grilled mackerel fillet with crispy skin, a staple of Japanese home cooking.
instructions: Pat mackerel fillets dry and sprinkle both sides generously with sea salt.
Let salted fish rest for 20 minutes, then pat dry again to remove moisture.
Grill skin-side up under a broiler on high for 5-6 minutes until skin is blistered and crispy.
Flip and grill flesh-side for 2-3 minutes until just cooked through.
Grate daikon radish finely for a fresh condiment.
Serve mackerel with grated daikon, lemon wedge, steamed rice, and miso soup.
tips: The salt-rest step draws out moisture and concentrates flavor — do not skip it.
Score the skin lightly if fillets are thick to ensure even cooking.

name: Nikujaga (Meat and Potato Stew)
description: Hearty Japanese comfort stew of thinly sliced beef, potatoes, and onions in a sweet soy broth.
instructions: Cut potatoes into large chunks, slice onion into wedges, and cut carrot into rolling cuts.
Heat oil in a pot, stir-fry beef until just browned, then add onion and cook 2 minutes.
Add potatoes, carrot, and drained shirataki noodles; stir briefly.
Pour in dashi, sake, sugar, soy sauce, and mirin; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat, cover with a drop lid (otoshibuta), and simmer 20-25 minutes until potatoes are tender.
Let rest 10 minutes off heat for flavors to absorb before serving.
tips: A drop lid (or circle of parchment paper) keeps ingredients submerged for even flavor.
Nikujaga tastes even better reheated the next day.

name: Oyakodon (Chicken and Egg Rice Bowl)
description: Chicken and onion simmered in sweet dashi broth, finished with softly set eggs over rice.
instructions: Cut chicken thigh into bite-sized pieces and slice onion into thin half-moons.
Combine dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar in a small lidded skillet and bring to a simmer.
Add onion and simmer 3 minutes until softened, then add chicken pieces.
Cook chicken for 5 minutes until just done, keeping the broth at a gentle simmer.
Beat eggs lightly (don't over-mix), pour over chicken in a spiral, cover, and cook 30 seconds.
Slide the egg-chicken mixture over bowls of hot rice and garnish with mitsuba.
tips: For silky eggs, pour in two stages: first half to set, then the second half for a runny finish.
Use a small pan that fits over one rice bowl for the best shape.

name: Teriyaki Salmon
description: Pan-seared salmon glazed with a homemade teriyaki sauce of soy, mirin, and sake.
instructions: Pat salmon fillets dry and season lightly with salt.
Mix soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar to make teriyaki sauce.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan over medium-high and sear salmon skin-side down for 4 minutes.
Flip salmon and cook 2 more minutes, then pour teriyaki sauce into the pan.
Reduce sauce for 2 minutes, spooning it over the salmon until glossy and thickened.
Serve over rice, drizzle remaining sauce, and top with sesame seeds and sliced spring onion.
tips: Start skin-side down for crispy skin that holds the glaze beautifully.
Do not overcook salmon — it should be slightly translucent in the center.

name: Miso Glazed Eggplant (Nasu Dengaku)
description: Roasted eggplant halves topped with a sweet white miso glaze, a classic Japanese vegetarian dish.
instructions: Halve eggplants lengthwise and score the flesh in a crosshatch pattern.
Brush cut sides with vegetable oil and roast face-down at 200 C for 15 minutes until soft.
Meanwhile, whisk together miso paste, mirin, sugar, sake, and sesame oil in a small saucepan.
Heat miso glaze over low heat, stirring until smooth and slightly thickened, about 3 minutes.
Flip roasted eggplants, spread miso glaze generously over the cut sides.
Broil for 3-4 minutes until glaze is bubbly and lightly caramelized; top with sesame seeds and spring onion.
tips: Japanese eggplants are ideal due to their thin skin and creamy flesh.
The miso glaze can be made ahead and stored refrigerated for a week.

name: Vegetable Tempura
description: Lightly battered and fried seasonal vegetables with a crispy, airy coating served with tentsuyu dipping sauce.
instructions: Cut sweet potato into 5 mm rounds, eggplant into fan shapes, and trim green beans.
Make tentsuyu by simmering dashi, soy sauce, and mirin together; set aside.
Prepare batter by lightly mixing egg, ice water, flour, and cornstarch — leave lumps for crispiness.
Heat oil to 170 C; dip vegetables in batter and fry in small batches for 2-3 minutes until pale golden.
Drain on a wire rack and serve immediately while still crispy.
Serve with warm tentsuyu dipping sauce and grated daikon.
tips: Keep batter ice cold and lumpy — overmixing creates a heavy, doughy coating.
Fry in small batches to maintain oil temperature.

name: Cold Soba Noodles (Zaru Soba)
description: Chilled buckwheat noodles served on a bamboo mat with concentrated tsuyu dipping sauce.
instructions: Boil soba noodles in plenty of unsalted water according to package directions, about 4-5 minutes.
Drain and rinse thoroughly under cold running water, rubbing gently to remove starch.
Make tsuyu by combining dashi, soy sauce, and mirin; chill in the refrigerator.
Arrange cold soba on a zaru (bamboo mat) or plate.
Finely slice spring onion, cut nori into thin strips, and prepare a small mound of wasabi.
Serve noodles with chilled tsuyu in a small cup, condiments on the side for mixing in.
tips: Rinse soba thoroughly — the starch makes them gummy if left on.
For 100% buckwheat soba (juwari), cook more gently as they break easily.

name: Udon Noodle Soup
description: Thick wheat noodles in a clear dashi-soy broth topped with kamaboko, spring onion, and tempura flakes.
instructions: Bring dashi stock to a simmer and season with soy sauce, mirin, and salt.
Cook udon noodles according to package directions, then drain and rinse briefly.
Divide hot noodles between two deep bowls.
Ladle the hot broth over the noodles until just covered.
Top with sliced kamaboko, chopped spring onion, and tempura flakes.
Serve immediately with shichimi togarashi on the side.
tips: Fresh or frozen udon has a much better chewy texture than dried.
For kitsune udon, top with sweet simmered aburaage (fried tofu pouch).

name: Japanese Curry Rice
description: Mild, thick, and sweet Japanese-style curry with chunks of potato, carrot, and onion over rice.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces, dice potatoes and carrots, and slice onions.
Heat oil in a large pot, cook onions until translucent, then add chicken and brown lightly.
Add carrots and potatoes, stir briefly, then pour in water and bring to a boil.
Skim foam, reduce heat, and simmer covered for 15 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Turn off heat, add curry roux blocks, and stir until completely dissolved.
Simmer on low for 5-10 minutes until thick, then serve over steamed rice with fukujinzuke.
tips: Mixing two different curry roux brands creates a more complex flavor.
Japanese curry thickens as it cools — reheat with a splash of water.

name: Agedashi Tofu
description: Lightly fried silken tofu cubes in a warm dashi-soy broth with grated ginger and daikon.
instructions: Drain tofu and cut into 3 cm cubes; pat thoroughly dry with paper towels.
Dust tofu cubes lightly with cornstarch, shaking off excess.
Heat oil to 180 C and fry tofu cubes for 2-3 minutes until golden and crisp outside.
Drain on a wire rack and place 4-5 pieces in each serving bowl.
Heat dashi, soy sauce, and mirin together for the sauce; pour over fried tofu.
Top with grated daikon, grated ginger, sliced spring onion, and bonito flakes.
