tips: Pressing tofu removes moisture and prevents oil splatter during frying.
Serve immediately — the crispy coating softens quickly in the broth.

name: Chawanmushi (Steamed Egg Custard)
description: Silky savory egg custard steamed with shrimp, chicken, and mushroom hidden inside.
instructions: Beat eggs gently (avoid bubbles), mix with cooled dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and salt.
Strain egg mixture through a fine sieve for a silky texture.
Place shrimp, diced chicken, sliced shiitake, and kamaboko into chawanmushi cups.
Pour egg mixture gently into cups, filling to 80% capacity.
Steam on low heat for 12-15 minutes until custard is set but still jiggly.
Top with mitsuba sprigs and serve warm.
tips: Low, gentle steam is key — high heat creates bubbles and a pockmarked texture.
Cover cups with foil to prevent condensation dripping into the custard.

name: Takikomi Gohan (Mixed Rice)
description: Japanese seasoned rice cooked with mushrooms, vegetables, and chicken in dashi, soy, and sake.
instructions: Wash rice and soak for 30 minutes, then drain.
Cut chicken into small pieces; slice shiitake, carrot into matchsticks, and shave gobo thinly.
Slice aburaage into thin strips and briefly blanch to remove excess oil.
Place rice in a rice cooker, add dashi, soy sauce, sake, mirin, and salt.
Arrange all toppings over the rice without stirring.
Cook using the regular rice cooker setting; once done, fold toppings gently into the rice.
tips: Do not stir before cooking — toppings sit on top so the rice cooks evenly underneath.
Gobo adds an earthy depth; soak shavings in vinegar water to prevent browning.

name: Niku Udon (Meat Udon)
description: Chewy udon noodles in dashi broth topped with sweet soy-simmered sliced beef and spring onion.
instructions: Simmer soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and sake in a small pan; add sliced onion and cook until soft.
Add beef slices to the sweet soy mixture and cook until just done, about 3 minutes; set aside.
Bring dashi stock to a simmer and season with 1 tbsp soy sauce.
Cook udon noodles per package, drain, and divide into bowls.
Ladle hot broth over noodles, then top with the simmered beef and onion.
Garnish with sliced spring onion and shichimi togarashi.
tips: Keep beef slightly rare when simmering — it will continue cooking in the hot broth.
Frozen udon noodles give the best chewy texture.

name: Karaage (Japanese Fried Chicken)
description: Double-fried bite-sized chicken thigh marinated in soy, ginger, and garlic with a light potato starch crust.
instructions: Cut chicken thighs into 3 cm pieces; combine with soy sauce, sake, grated ginger, and minced garlic.
Marinate for 30 minutes (or up to overnight in the fridge).
Coat marinated chicken pieces in potato starch, shaking off excess.
Heat oil to 160 C and fry chicken for 3 minutes; remove and rest for 3 minutes.
Increase oil to 180 C and fry again for 2 minutes until dark golden and extra crispy.
Serve with lemon wedges and Japanese mayonnaise for dipping.
tips: The double-fry method is essential for the signature extra-crispy karaage texture.
Potato starch gives a lighter, crispier crust than regular flour.

name: Hayashi Rice (Hashed Beef Rice)
description: Tender sliced beef and mushrooms in a rich demi-glace sauce served over steamed rice.
instructions: Slice onions thinly and mushrooms into quarters.
Melt butter in a large pan, cook onions on medium-low for 15 minutes until deeply caramelized.
Add beef slices and mushrooms, cook until beef is browned, about 3 minutes.
Pour in red wine and simmer until reduced by half.
Add demi-glace, tomato paste, ketchup, and Worcestershire sauce; simmer for 15 minutes.
Serve over steamed rice and garnish with fresh parsley.
tips: Patiently caramelizing the onions is the secret to the sauce's depth.
Hayashi roux cubes are a shortcut available at Japanese grocery stores.

name: Chirashi Sushi
description: Seasoned sushi rice topped with an assortment of sashimi-grade fish, egg, and vegetables.
instructions: Cook sushi rice, then fold in a mixture of rice vinegar, sugar, and salt while still hot; fan to cool.
Make thin egg crepe (kinshi tamago): beat eggs, cook as a thin omelet, and slice into thin strips.
Boil shrimp for 2 minutes, peel, devein, and butterfly.
Slice salmon and tuna into thin pieces at an angle.
Spread seasoned rice in a wide bowl and arrange fish, shrimp, egg strips, and sliced cucumber on top.
Garnish with shredded nori, pickled ginger, and serve with soy sauce on the side.
tips: Fan the rice as you fold in vinegar for a glossy, non-sticky finish.
Use the freshest fish possible — quality of sashimi makes or breaks this dish.

name: Tofu Steak with Mushroom Sauce
description: Pan-seared firm tofu steaks with a savory mushroom and butter soy sauce.
instructions: Drain tofu, press for 15 minutes, then cut into 2 cm thick steaks.
Dust tofu steaks with cornstarch on all sides.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan and sear tofu for 3-4 minutes per side until golden; set aside.
In the same pan, melt butter and saute minced garlic and shimeji mushrooms for 3 minutes.
Add soy sauce, mirin, and pepper; cook until sauce is slightly reduced.
Pour mushroom sauce over tofu steaks and garnish with sliced spring onion.
tips: Press tofu well for a firm texture that sears properly without crumbling.
Shimeji, enoki, or shiitake all work well for the sauce.

name: Kinpira Gobo (Braised Burdock Root)
description: Matchstick-cut burdock root and carrot stir-fried in a sweet soy glaze with sesame and chili.
instructions: Scrub gobo and shave into thin matchsticks; soak in vinegar water for 5 minutes to prevent browning.
Cut carrot into similar-sized matchsticks.
Heat sesame oil in a pan over medium heat and stir-fry gobo for 3 minutes.
Add carrot and red chili flakes, stir-fry another 2 minutes.
Add soy sauce, mirin, and sugar; cook stirring until liquid is absorbed, about 3 minutes.
Sprinkle with sesame seeds and serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Kinpira keeps well for 4-5 days refrigerated — perfect make-ahead side.
The shaving technique for gobo is called sasagaki — scrape like sharpening a pencil.

name: Hijiki Seaweed Salad
description: Reconstituted hijiki seaweed simmered with soy sauce, carrots, and aburaage in a sweet dashi broth.
instructions: Soak dried hijiki in water for 30 minutes until fully reconstituted, then drain.
Cut carrot into thin matchsticks and slice aburaage into thin strips.
Heat sesame oil in a pan and stir-fry hijiki and carrot for 2 minutes.
Add aburaage strips and stir briefly.
Pour in dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar; simmer on low for 10 minutes until liquid is mostly absorbed.
Toss in cooked edamame and serve warm or chilled.
tips: Hijiki expands dramatically when soaked — 30 g dry yields about 150 g reconstituted.
This dish is a staple in Japanese bento boxes and keeps well for 3-4 days.

name: Japanese Hamburg Steak
description: Juicy Japanese-style hamburger patties with a tangy demi-glace and ketchup sauce.
instructions: Finely dice onion, saute in butter until translucent, and cool completely.
Soak panko in milk; combine with ground meat, cooled onion, egg, salt, and nutmeg.
Knead mixture well, then form into 2 oval patties, tossing between hands to remove air pockets.
Heat oil in a skillet, sear patties on high for 2 minutes per side until browned.
Add 50 ml water, cover, and steam-cook on medium-low for 8 minutes until cooked through.
Remove patties; add ketchup and Worcestershire to the pan drippings, simmer into a sauce, and pour over.
tips: The toss-between-hands technique removes air and prevents cracking during cooking.
A mix of beef and pork gives the best juicy texture.

name: Kakuni (Braised Pork Belly)
description: Melt-in-your-mouth pork belly slowly braised in soy sauce, sake, and ginger until tender.
instructions: Cut pork belly into 4 cm cubes.
Boil pork cubes with spring onion greens and ginger slices for 20 minutes to remove excess fat; drain and discard water.
Place par-boiled pork in a clean pot with fresh water, sake, soy sauce, mirin, sugar, and fresh ginger slices.
Bring to a boil, then reduce to the lowest simmer, cover with a drop lid, and cook for 90 minutes.
Check tenderness — pork should break apart easily; if not, simmer another 20 minutes.
Serve pork with reduced braising liquid and a dab of karashi mustard.
tips: The initial boil removes impurities and excess fat for a cleaner final braise.
Low and slow is key — rushing the braise results in tough pork.

name: Shogayaki (Ginger Pork)
description: Thin pork slices pan-fried with a bold ginger-soy sauce, a beloved Japanese lunch set staple.
instructions: Grate ginger finely and mix with soy sauce, mirin, and sake to make the sauce.
Lightly dust pork slices with flour.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium-high and cook pork for 2 minutes per side until golden.
Pour ginger sauce over the pork and cook for 1-2 minutes until sauce is glossy and caramelized.
Pile shredded raw cabbage on serving plates.
Arrange ginger pork alongside cabbage with steamed rice.
tips: Fresh grated ginger is essential — jarred ginger lacks the punch.
Shogayaki is the most popular teishoku (lunch set) dish in Japan.

name: Miso Soup with Tofu
description: Classic Japanese miso soup with silken tofu, wakame seaweed, and spring onion in dashi broth.
instructions: Bring dashi stock to a gentle simmer in a pot.
Rehydrate dried wakame in water for 5 minutes, then drain.
Cut silken tofu into 1.5 cm cubes.
Place miso paste in a ladle, submerge in the broth, and dissolve by stirring with chopsticks.
Add tofu cubes and wakame to the soup; heat through for 1 minute without boiling.
Serve in bowls topped with finely sliced spring onion.
tips: Never boil miso soup after adding the paste — boiling destroys the flavor and probiotics.
A combination of white and red miso gives a more complex taste.

name: Mapo Tofu (Japanese-style)
description: Mild Japanese adaptation of mapo tofu with silken tofu in a savory miso-soy meat sauce.
instructions: Cut tofu into 2 cm cubes and gently warm in salted water for 3 minutes; drain.
Heat sesame oil in a wok, stir-fry minced garlic and ginger for 30 seconds.
Add ground pork and cook until browned, breaking into fine crumbles.
Stir in doubanjiang, miso paste, soy sauce, and mirin with 150 ml water; simmer 3 minutes.
Gently slide in tofu cubes, stir carefully, and simmer 3 minutes.
Thicken with cornstarch slurry, garnish with spring onion, and serve over rice.
tips: Japanese-style uses less chili than Sichuan — adjust doubanjiang to taste.
Warming tofu in salted water firms it slightly so it doesn't break apart.

name: Yaki Onigiri (Grilled Rice Balls)
description: Crispy-surfaced grilled rice balls brushed with soy sauce, a popular Japanese snack or side.
instructions: Cook rice and let it cool slightly until handleable but still warm.
Wet hands with salted water and shape rice firmly into triangular onigiri.
Mix soy sauce and mirin to make the glaze.
Heat a non-stick pan or grill over medium heat and brush lightly with sesame oil.
Place onigiri on the pan and grill for 3-4 minutes per side until a golden crust forms.
Brush soy-mirin glaze on each side during the last minute of grilling; sprinkle with sesame seeds.
tips: Pack the rice tightly — loose onigiri will fall apart on the grill.
A cast iron pan gives the best crust if you don't have a charcoal grill.

name: Bibimbap
description: Korean mixed rice bowl with seasoned vegetables, gochujang sauce, and a fried egg on top.
instructions: Blanch spinach and bean sprouts separately; squeeze dry and season each with sesame oil, salt, and garlic.
Julienne carrot and zucchini; saute each separately with a pinch of salt until just tender.
Slice shiitake mushrooms and saute with soy sauce until golden.
Fry eggs sunny-side up with runny yolks.
Arrange rice in bowls, place each vegetable in separate sections around the bowl.
Top with fried egg, a generous dollop of gochujang, and sesame seeds; mix before eating.
tips: For dolsot bibimbap, heat a stone bowl until smoking and press rice in for a crispy crust.
Each vegetable should be seasoned and cooked separately for distinct flavors.

name: Bulgogi (Korean BBQ Beef)
description: Thinly sliced beef marinated in a sweet soy-pear sauce, grilled or pan-fried until caramelized.
instructions: Slice beef thinly against the grain (freezing for 30 minutes makes slicing easier).
Blend Asian pear, garlic, and ginger into a puree for the marinade base.
Combine puree with soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, and pepper; marinate beef for at least 1 hour.
Slice onion into rings and cut spring onion into 5 cm pieces.
Grill or pan-fry beef and onion over high heat for 2-3 minutes per side until caramelized.
Garnish with spring onion and sesame seeds; serve with rice and lettuce wraps.
tips: Asian pear is a natural meat tenderizer — do not skip it.
Do not overcrowd the pan or the beef will steam rather than caramelize.

name: Japchae (Glass Noodle Stir-Fry)
description: Sweet potato glass noodles stir-fried with colorful vegetables, beef, and a sweet soy-sesame sauce.
instructions: Cook dangmyeon noodles per package, drain, and cut into shorter lengths with scissors.
Slice beef thinly, marinate in 1 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tsp sugar, and minced garlic.
Blanch spinach, squeeze dry, and season with sesame oil and salt.
Julienne carrot, bell pepper, and onion; slice mushrooms; saute each separately.
Stir-fry marinated beef until cooked through.
Combine everything in a large bowl with remaining soy sauce, sugar, and sesame oil; toss well and top with sesame seeds.
tips: Cooking each vegetable separately preserves its color and texture — essential for authentic japchae.
Japchae is served at room temperature and tastes great the next day.

name: Kimchi Jjigae (Kimchi Stew)
description: Spicy, sour stew of fermented kimchi with pork belly and tofu simmered until deeply flavored.
instructions: Cut pork belly into bite-sized slices and kimchi into large pieces.
Heat sesame oil in a pot and saute pork belly until fat renders, about 3 minutes.
Add kimchi and stir-fry together for 2-3 minutes until fragrant.
Add water, gochugaru, soy sauce, and minced garlic; bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer for 15 minutes to develop deep flavor.
Add cubed tofu and sliced onion, simmer 5 more minutes, and garnish with spring onion.
tips: Use aged (sour) kimchi for the best jjigae — fresh kimchi lacks the depth.
Kimchi jjigae tastes better each time it is reheated.

name: Sundubu Jjigae (Soft Tofu Stew)
description: Bubbling-hot spicy stew with uncurdled soft tofu, seafood, and a raw egg cracked on top.
instructions: Heat sesame oil in a stone pot (ttukbaegi) and saute minced garlic and gochugaru for 30 seconds.
Add diced onion and stir briefly, then pour in anchovy-kelp stock.
Add clams and shrimp, bring to a boil, and cook 3 minutes until clams open.
Squeeze in soft tofu in large spoonfuls, add gochujang, and stir gently.
Simmer vigorously for 5 minutes until bubbling fiercely.
Crack a raw egg on top, garnish with spring onion, and serve immediately while still bubbling.
tips: A stone pot (ttukbaegi) retains heat and keeps the stew bubbling at the table.
The egg cooks from the residual heat — serve immediately for the best texture.

name: Dakgalbi (Spicy Chicken Stir-Fry)
description: Chunky chicken thighs stir-fried with cabbage, sweet potato, and rice cakes in a gochujang sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces and chop cabbage into large squares.
Slice sweet potato into thin half-moons and soak rice cakes in warm water.
Make sauce by mixing gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, minced garlic, ginger, and sesame oil.
Toss chicken in sauce and marinate for 15 minutes.
Heat a large flat pan, add marinated chicken, sweet potato, cabbage, and rice cakes.
Stir-fry over high heat for 10-12 minutes, turning frequently, until chicken is cooked and vegetables are tender.
tips: Add mozzarella cheese in the center for the popular cheese dakgalbi variation.
Dakgalbi is traditionally cooked at the table on a large iron plate.

name: Galbi Jjim (Braised Short Ribs)
description: Soy-braised beef short ribs with vegetables, slowly cooked until fall-off-the-bone tender.
instructions: Soak short ribs in cold water for 2 hours to draw out blood, then drain and blanch in boiling water for 5 minutes.
Blend Asian pear, garlic, and ginger into a puree.
Combine puree with soy sauce, sugar, and sesame oil for the braising sauce.
Place blanched ribs in a pot, pour sauce over, add 500 ml water, and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and braise covered for 90 minutes until meat is very tender.
Add large-cut carrot, daikon, jujubes, and chestnuts; cook 20 more minutes until vegetables are done.
tips: Soaking ribs overnight removes excess blood for a cleaner-tasting braise.
Galbi jjim is a celebratory dish served at Korean holidays and special occasions.

name: Korean Fried Chicken
description: Double-fried ultra-crispy chicken coated in a sticky sweet-spicy gochujang glaze.
instructions: Pat chicken wings dry, season with salt and pepper, then toss in a mix of cornstarch and flour.
Heat oil to 160 C, fry wings in batches for 8 minutes until cooked through; drain and rest 5 minutes.
Increase oil to 180 C and double-fry wings for 3-4 minutes until shatteringly crisp.
Make sauce: combine gochujang, honey, soy sauce, rice vinegar, minced garlic, and ginger in a pan; heat until bubbling.
Toss crispy fried chicken in the sauce until evenly coated.
Sprinkle with sesame seeds and serve immediately.
tips: Double frying is the secret to Korean fried chicken's signature crunch that stays crispy even with sauce.
For yangnyeom chicken, use this spicy glaze; for dakgangjeong, use a sweeter soy garlic version.

name: Tteokbokki (Spicy Rice Cakes)
description: Chewy rice cakes simmered in a fiery gochujang sauce with fish cakes, a beloved Korean street food.
instructions: Soak rice cakes in warm water for 10 minutes if using dried; separate any stuck pieces.
Cut fish cake sheets into triangles or bite-sized pieces.
Bring anchovy-kelp stock to a boil and stir in gochujang, gochugaru, sugar, and soy sauce.
Add rice cakes and simmer for 8-10 minutes, stirring often, until the sauce thickens and rice cakes are chewy.
Add fish cake pieces and halved boiled eggs in the last 3 minutes.
Garnish with sliced spring onion and serve piping hot.
tips: Stir frequently — rice cakes stick easily and the sauce thickens fast.
Add ramen noodles for the popular rabokki variation.

name: Jeyuk Bokkeum (Spicy Pork Stir-Fry)
description: Thinly sliced pork stir-fried in a fiery gochujang and gochugaru sauce with onions and cabbage.
instructions: Slice pork thinly and combine with gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, garlic, and ginger; marinate 15 minutes.
Slice onion into half-moons, cut cabbage into squares, and chop spring onion.
Heat sesame oil in a large pan or wok over high heat.
Add marinated pork and stir-fry for 4-5 minutes until pork is cooked and edges are caramelized.
Add onion and cabbage, stir-fry 3 more minutes until vegetables are wilted but still crunchy.
Finish with spring onion and sesame seeds; serve with steamed rice.
tips: Pork shoulder has ideal fat content for this dish — too lean and it dries out.
Jeyuk bokkeum is a staple at Korean university cafeterias and home kitchens alike.

name: Samgyeopsal (Grilled Pork Belly)
description: Thick-cut pork belly slices grilled at the table, wrapped in lettuce with garlic, ssamjang, and kimchi.
instructions: Slice pork belly into 1 cm thick pieces.
Heat a grill or cast iron pan over high heat until very hot.
Grill pork belly slices for 3-4 minutes per side until cooked through and edges are crispy.
Slice raw garlic and cut green chilies into rings.
Cut grilled pork into bite-sized pieces with kitchen scissors at the table.
Wrap pork in lettuce and perilla leaves with a dab of ssamjang, slice of garlic, and kimchi.
tips: A meat-fat ratio of 3:7 to 4:6 is ideal for samgyeopsal.
Dip grilled pork in sesame oil mixed with salt for a simple classic accompaniment.

name: Doenjang Jjigae (Soybean Paste Stew)
description: Earthy, savory stew of fermented soybean paste with tofu, zucchini, and mushrooms.
instructions: Bring anchovy-kelp stock to a boil in a stone pot or heavy saucepan.
Dissolve doenjang paste into the stock, stirring well.
Add diced potato and sliced onion; simmer 5 minutes until potato softens.
Add cubed tofu, sliced zucchini, mushrooms, and minced garlic.
Simmer for 10 minutes on medium heat until all vegetables are tender.
Add sliced green chili and spring onion; serve bubbling hot.
tips: A small spoon of gochugaru adds a gentle heat if desired.
Doenjang jjigae is the most common everyday stew in Korean households.

name: Gamjatang (Pork Bone Soup)
description: Hearty spicy soup with pork neck bones, potatoes, and perilla seeds in a rich bone broth.
instructions: Soak pork neck bones in cold water for 2 hours to remove blood, then blanch in boiling water for 10 minutes; drain.
Place bones in a large pot with fresh water, ginger, and spring onion stems; boil for 60 minutes until meat is tender.
Remove bones temporarily; stir doenjang, gochugaru, garlic, and perilla seed powder into the broth.
Halve potatoes and add to the pot; simmer 15 minutes until nearly tender.
Return pork bones and add torn napa cabbage leaves; cook 5 more minutes.
Serve piping hot garnished with spring onion and sesame seeds.
tips: Perilla seed powder gives gamjatang its distinctive nutty, herbal flavor.
The meat should be so tender it falls off the bone when prodded.

name: Kongnamul Bap (Soybean Sprout Rice)
description: Rice cooked with soybean sprouts and seasoned with a spicy soy scallion sauce, a comforting one-pot dish.
instructions: Wash rice and soak for 30 minutes; drain.
Place rice in a heavy pot with the standard amount of water.
Season ground pork with 1 tbsp soy sauce and spread over the rice.
Top with washed and trimmed soybean sprouts — do not stir.
Cook covered on medium heat until boiling, then reduce to lowest heat for 15 minutes.
Make sauce by mixing remaining soy sauce, gochugaru, sesame oil, garlic, spring onion, and sesame seeds; stir into the finished rice.
tips: Do not open the lid during cooking — steam must stay trapped for fluffy rice and tender sprouts.
The crispy rice at the bottom (nurungji) is a prized part of this dish.

name: Haemul Pajeon (Seafood Pancake)
description: Crispy Korean pancake loaded with scallions, shrimp, and squid, served with a soy dipping sauce.
instructions: Cut spring onions into lengths matching the diameter of your pan; clean and slice shrimp and squid.
Mix flour, cornstarch, salt, egg, and ice water into a thin batter — leave some lumps.
Heat a generous amount of oil in a large non-stick pan over medium-high heat.
Lay spring onions in the pan in a single layer, then scatter seafood over them.
Pour batter evenly over the scallions and seafood; press gently with a spatula.
Fry for 4-5 minutes until bottom is golden and crispy, flip, and fry 3-4 more minutes; serve with soy-vinegar dipping sauce.
tips: Press the pancake flat while cooking for maximum crispiness.
Serve immediately — pajeon loses its crunch quickly.

name: Dakdoritang (Spicy Chicken Stew)
description: Bone-in chicken pieces braised with potatoes, carrots, and onions in a spicy gochugaru-gochujang sauce.
instructions: Cut chicken into large pieces and score the thick parts for even cooking.
Quarter potatoes, cut carrots into thick rounds, and cut onion into large wedges.
Mix gochugaru, gochujang, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, and sugar into a sauce.
Place chicken in a pot, pour sauce over, add water, and bring to a boil.
Add potatoes, carrots, and onion; cover and simmer for 25-30 minutes until chicken is cooked and potatoes are tender.
Garnish with spring onion and serve with steamed rice.
tips: The sauce should be thick and coat the chicken by the end — uncover if too liquidy.
Dakdoritang is perfect cold-weather comfort food and reheats beautifully.

name: Jajangmyeon (Black Bean Noodles)
description: Thick wheat noodles smothered in a savory black bean paste sauce with diced pork and vegetables.
instructions: Dice pork, onion, zucchini, potato, and cabbage into 1 cm cubes.
Heat oil in a wok, fry chunjang paste for 2 minutes to remove raw taste.
Add diced pork and stir-fry until cooked through, about 3 minutes.
Add all vegetables and stir-fry 3 minutes, then add 300 ml water and sugar.
Simmer 10 minutes until vegetables are tender; thicken with cornstarch slurry.
Boil noodles, drain, and serve topped with the glossy black bean sauce; garnish with cucumber.
tips: Frying the chunjang paste first mellows its bitterness significantly.
Jajangmyeon is Korea's most popular delivery food — eaten on moving day tradition.

name: Kimbap (Korean Rice Rolls)
description: Seasoned rice and colorful fillings rolled in roasted seaweed, sliced into bite-sized rounds.
instructions: Cook rice and season with sesame oil and salt while still hot.
Blanch spinach, squeeze dry, season with sesame oil and salt.
Julienne carrot and saute briefly; make thin egg omelet strips.
Place seaweed sheet shiny-side down on a bamboo mat; spread rice thinly leaving 2 cm at the top edge.
Arrange fillings in a line across the center: spinach, carrot, egg, crab stick, and pickled radish.
Roll tightly using the mat, seal the edge with a grain of rice, brush with sesame oil, and slice into 1.5 cm rounds.
tips: Spread rice thinly — too much rice makes a heavy, unbalanced kimbap.
Brush the knife with sesame oil between cuts for clean slices.

name: Ojingeo Bokkeum (Spicy Squid Stir-Fry)
description: Tender squid stir-fried with vegetables in a fiery gochujang and gochugaru sauce.
instructions: Clean squid, score the body in a crosshatch pattern, and cut into pieces.
Slice onion, carrot, and cabbage; chop spring onion.
Mix gochujang, gochugaru, soy sauce, sugar, and minced garlic into a sauce.
Heat sesame oil in a wok over high heat and stir-fry squid for 2 minutes.
Add vegetables and sauce; stir-fry 3-4 minutes until vegetables are crisp-tender.
Finish with spring onion and sesame seeds; serve over steamed rice.
tips: Score the squid body so it curls attractively and absorbs more sauce.
Do not overcook squid — it becomes rubbery if stir-fried more than 3-4 minutes.

name: Yachae Jeon (Vegetable Pancake)
description: Crispy Korean vegetable pancake with zucchini, scallion, and chili in a light batter.
instructions: Julienne zucchini and carrot; cut spring onion into 5 cm lengths; slice chili.
Mix flour, cornstarch, salt, egg, and ice water into a thin batter.
Fold vegetables into the batter until just combined.
Heat oil generously in a non-stick pan over medium-high heat.
Spread vegetable batter in a thin, even layer and fry 3-4 minutes until bottom is golden and crisp.
Flip and fry 3 more minutes; serve cut into wedges with soy dipping sauce.
tips: A thin pancake fries crispier than a thick one — spread the batter wide.
Add a splash of soju to the batter for extra crispiness.

name: Kongbiji Jjigae (Ground Soybean Stew)
description: Thick, creamy stew of ground soybeans with kimchi and pork, a traditional Korean comfort dish.
instructions: Dice pork belly and chop kimchi into small pieces.
Heat sesame oil in a pot and saute pork belly until fat renders.
Add chopped kimchi, diced onion, and minced garlic; stir-fry 2 minutes.
Add ground soybean paste and water; stir well to combine.
Simmer on medium-low for 15 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking.
Season with gochugaru and salt; serve in stone bowls with spring onion.
tips: Fresh ground soybean paste can be made by blending soaked soybeans with water.
Stir frequently as the thick soybean base scorches easily on the bottom.

name: Budae Jjigae (Army Stew)
description: Spicy Korean hot pot with kimchi, spam, sausages, ramen noodles, and cheese in a gochugaru broth.
instructions: Slice spam and sausages into bite-sized pieces; cube tofu and chop kimchi.
Arrange all ingredients in sections in a wide shallow pot: kimchi, spam, sausages, tofu, baked beans.
Mix gochugaru, gochujang, and minced garlic into a paste; add to the center of the pot.
Pour anchovy-kelp stock over everything and bring to a rolling boil.
Once boiling, add ramen noodles and cook until softened, about 3 minutes.
Top with mozzarella cheese, cover briefly to melt, garnish with spring onion, and serve communally.
tips: Budae jjigae is a communal dish — serve in the center of the table for everyone to share.
This stew was created post-Korean War using surplus army base ingredients.

name: Bossam (Boiled Pork Wraps)
description: Tender boiled pork belly sliced thin, wrapped in napa cabbage with garlic, chili, and ssamjang.
instructions: Place whole pork belly in a large pot with halved onion, ginger, spring onion, doenjang, and instant coffee.
Cover with water, bring to a boil, then simmer for 60-70 minutes until pork is very tender.
Meanwhile, prepare cabbage by lightly salting leaves and letting them wilt for 20 minutes.
Remove pork from broth, let rest 10 minutes, then slice against the grain into 5 mm thick pieces.
Arrange pork slices on a platter with cabbage leaves, ssamjang, sliced garlic, and salted shrimp.
Wrap pork in cabbage leaves with desired condiments and eat in one bite.
tips: Instant coffee in the boiling liquid removes pork smell — a common Korean cooking trick.
Slice pork while warm for the cleanest cuts; cold pork crumbles.

name: Dwaeji Gukbap (Pork Rice Soup)
description: Milky pork bone broth soup with sliced pork and rice, a Busan specialty eaten with salted shrimp.
instructions: Blanch pork bones and shoulder in boiling water for 10 minutes to remove impurities; drain and rinse.
Place bones and shoulder in a fresh pot with water, spring onion, ginger, and garlic; boil vigorously for 90 minutes.
The vigorous boil emulsifies the fat into the broth, creating the milky white color.
Remove pork shoulder, slice thinly; strain broth and season with salt.
Place steamed rice in bowls, arrange sliced pork over rice.
Pour piping hot milky broth over, garnish with chives, and serve with salted shrimp and chili flakes on the side.
tips: The broth must boil vigorously (not simmer) to achieve the signature milky white color.
Dwaeji gukbap is Busan's most famous soul food — found at 24-hour restaurants citywide.

name: Sigeumchi Namul (Spinach Side)
description: Blanched spinach seasoned with sesame oil, garlic, and soy sauce, the most common Korean banchan.
instructions: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil.
Blanch spinach for 30-40 seconds until just wilted — do not overcook.
Immediately transfer to ice water to stop cooking and preserve bright green color.
Squeeze out as much water as possible from the spinach.
Toss with sesame oil, soy sauce, minced garlic, salt, and sesame seeds.
Serve at room temperature as a banchan side dish.
tips: Squeeze spinach very thoroughly — excess water dilutes the seasoning.
Sigeumchi namul keeps refrigerated for 3-4 days and is a staple in Korean lunch boxes.

name: Hobak Jeon (Zucchini Pancake)
description: Thin zucchini rounds dipped in egg and lightly pan-fried until golden, a delicate Korean side dish.
instructions: Slice zucchini into 5 mm thick rounds and lightly salt; let sit 5 minutes.
Pat zucchini slices dry with paper towels.
Dust each slice lightly with flour, then dip in beaten egg.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan over medium heat.
Fry zucchini slices for 2 minutes per side until egg coating is golden.
Serve with a dipping sauce of soy sauce and rice vinegar.
tips: Salting the zucchini first draws out moisture for better frying.
These are simple enough for children to help make — a popular family cooking activity in Korea.

name: Dubu Jorim (Braised Tofu)
description: Firm tofu braised in a spicy, savory soy-gochugaru sauce, a popular Korean side dish.
instructions: Slice tofu into 1 cm thick rectangular pieces and pat dry.
Heat vegetable oil in a pan and pan-fry tofu slices for 2 minutes per side until lightly golden.
Mix soy sauce, gochugaru, minced garlic, sugar, sesame oil, and water into a braising sauce.
Pour sauce over the fried tofu in the pan.
Simmer on medium-low for 5-7 minutes, spooning sauce over the tofu occasionally.
Garnish with sliced spring onion and sesame seeds; serve as a side dish.
tips: Pan-frying first gives the tofu a firm exterior that holds up during braising.
Dubu jorim is one of the easiest and most common Korean banchan dishes.

name: Kimchi Bokkeumbap (Kimchi Fried Rice)
description: Wok-fried rice with aged kimchi, gochujang, and sesame oil, topped with a fried egg.
instructions: Chop kimchi into small pieces; reserve any kimchi juice.
Heat vegetable oil in a wok over high heat and stir-fry kimchi for 2-3 minutes until fragrant.
Add day-old rice, breaking up clumps, and stir-fry for 3 minutes.
Add gochujang, soy sauce, kimchi juice, and sesame oil; toss until rice is evenly coated and slightly crispy.
Fry eggs sunny-side up in a separate pan.
Plate rice, top with fried egg, spring onion, sesame seeds, and crumbled seaweed.
tips: Day-old refrigerated rice fries much better than freshly cooked rice.
The kimchi juice adds the sour depth that makes this dish irresistible.

name: Galbitang (Short Rib Soup)
description: Clear beef short rib soup simmered until the meat is tender, with glass noodles and radish.
instructions: Soak short ribs in cold water for 2 hours to remove blood; blanch in boiling water for 5 minutes and rinse.
Place ribs in a large pot with fresh water, garlic, ginger, and spring onion stems; bring to a boil.
Skim foam continuously for 10 minutes, then simmer on low for 90 minutes until meat is tender.
Add large chunks of daikon radish and cook 20 more minutes.
Cook glass noodles separately, drain, and divide into bowls.
Ladle soup and ribs over noodles; season with salt, pepper, and soy sauce; garnish with beaten egg drop and spring onion.
tips: Soaking the ribs is essential for a clear, clean-tasting broth.
Galbitang is a celebratory soup served at Korean weddings and holidays.

name: Pho Bo (Beef Pho)
description: Aromatic Vietnamese beef noodle soup with rice noodles, thinly sliced beef, and fragrant star anise broth
instructions: Char onion and ginger under a broiler until blackened on all sides, about 10 minutes.
Blanch beef bones in boiling water for 5 minutes, drain, and rinse clean.
Simmer bones with charred onion, ginger, star anise, cinnamon, and 2.5 liters of water for 60 minutes, skimming impurities.
Season broth with fish sauce and a pinch of sugar, strain through a fine sieve.
Cook rice noodles according to package directions, drain and divide among bowls.
Slice beef sirloin paper-thin against the grain.
Ladle boiling broth over noodles and raw beef slices — the hot broth cooks the beef instantly.
Serve with bean sprouts, Thai basil, lime wedges, and hoisin sauce on the side.
tips: Freeze beef for 20 minutes before slicing for paper-thin cuts
Toast spices in a dry pan for deeper flavor

name: Pho Ga (Chicken Pho)
description: Light and fragrant Vietnamese chicken noodle soup with tender poached chicken and fresh herbs
instructions: Place whole chicken in a large pot, cover with cold water, and bring to a boil.
Skim foam, then add charred onion, ginger, and coriander seeds. Simmer for 35 minutes.
Remove chicken and let cool. Shred the meat and discard bones.
Strain broth and season with fish sauce and sugar.
Cook rice noodles, drain, and divide among bowls.
Top noodles with shredded chicken, ladle hot broth over.
Garnish with bean sprouts, cilantro, green onion, and lime wedges.
tips: Do not boil the chicken vigorously — gentle simmering keeps the broth clear

name: Bun Bo Hue (Spicy Beef Noodle Soup)
description: Bold and spicy noodle soup from Hue with lemongrass-infused broth, beef shank, and thick round noodles
instructions: Blanch beef shank and pork bones, rinse, and return to a clean pot with fresh water.
Bruise lemongrass stalks and add to the pot. Simmer for 90 minutes until beef is tender.
Heat annatto oil in a small pan, fry chili flakes and shrimp paste until fragrant.
Stir the chili-shrimp paste mixture into the broth. Season with fish sauce.
Remove beef shank and slice into thin rounds.
Cook thick rice noodles, drain, and place in bowls.
Top with sliced beef, ladle broth over, and serve with banana blossom, mint, and lime.
tips: Annatto oil gives the signature red color — find it at Asian grocery stores
Adjust shrimp paste gradually as it is very pungent

name: Bun Cha (Grilled Pork with Noodles)
description: Hanoi-style grilled pork patties and sliced pork belly served with rice vermicelli and tangy dipping broth
instructions: Marinate ground pork and sliced pork belly with garlic, 2 tbsp fish sauce, and 1 tbsp sugar for 30 minutes.
Shape ground pork into small flat patties.
Grill pork patties and pork belly slices over charcoal or high heat until charred and cooked through.
Make dipping broth: combine remaining fish sauce, sugar, lime juice, rice vinegar, and warm water. Stir until sugar dissolves.
Cook rice vermicelli, rinse in cold water, and drain.
Julienne carrot and add to the dipping broth along with the grilled pork.
Serve vermicelli on the side with lettuce, herbs, and the bowl of pork in broth for dipping.
tips: Charcoal grilling gives the authentic smoky flavor that defines this dish

name: Banh Mi Thit (Vietnamese Baguette Sandwich)
description: Crispy Vietnamese baguette stuffed with savory pork, pate, pickled vegetables, and fresh cilantro
instructions: Pickle julienned daikon and carrot in rice vinegar, sugar, and a pinch of salt for 20 minutes.
Season pork loin with soy sauce. Pan-sear or roast until cooked through, about 15 minutes. Slice thinly.
Split baguettes lengthwise without cutting all the way through.
Spread pate on one side and mayonnaise on the other.
Layer sliced pork, pickled vegetables, cucumber slices, cilantro, and jalapeno slices.
Press gently and serve immediately while the bread is still crispy.
tips: Use a light airy baguette — dense bread ruins the texture balance
Quick-pickle the vegetables while the pork cooks to save time

name: Com Tam (Broken Rice with Pork)
description: Saigon-style broken rice topped with grilled marinated pork chop, fried egg, and sweet fish sauce
instructions: Mince lemongrass, garlic, and shallot. Mix with fish sauce, sugar, and oil to make a marinade.
Marinate pork chops for at least 30 minutes, or overnight for best flavor.
Cook broken rice in a rice cooker or pot with slightly less water than regular rice.
Grill or pan-fry pork chops over high heat until charred and cooked through, about 5 minutes per side.
Fry eggs sunny-side up in a little oil.
Make sweet fish sauce (nuoc cham): combine fish sauce, sugar, lime juice, water, and minced chili.
Plate rice with pork chop, fried egg, sliced cucumber, and tomato. Drizzle with nuoc cham.
tips: Broken rice cooks faster than regular rice — reduce water by 10%

name: Goi Cuon (Fresh Spring Rolls)
description: Translucent rice paper rolls filled with shrimp, pork, vermicelli, and fresh herbs with peanut dipping sauce
instructions: Boil pork belly until cooked through, about 20 minutes. Slice thinly.
Cook rice vermicelli, rinse in cold water, and drain.
Halve shrimp lengthwise.
Dip one rice paper wrapper in warm water for 5 seconds until pliable.
Layer lettuce, herbs, vermicelli, pork, and shrimp on the wrapper. Fold sides in and roll tightly.
Make peanut sauce: warm hoisin sauce with peanut butter, garlic, and a splash of water until smooth. Add chili sauce.
Serve rolls with peanut dipping sauce.
tips: Do not over-soak rice paper — it should still be slightly firm when you start rolling

name: Cha Gio (Fried Spring Rolls)
description: Crispy golden Vietnamese fried spring rolls filled with pork, shrimp, taro, and glass noodles
instructions: Soak glass noodles and wood ear mushrooms in warm water for 15 minutes. Drain and chop finely.
Mince shrimp. Grate taro and carrot.
Combine ground pork, shrimp, taro, carrot, noodles, mushrooms, fish sauce, pepper, and egg.
Briefly dip rice paper in water, place filling in the center, fold in sides and roll tightly.
Heat oil to 170°C. Fry spring rolls in batches until golden and crispy, about 5 minutes.
Drain on paper towels and serve with lettuce, herbs, and nuoc cham dipping sauce.
tips: Double-wrap with rice paper for extra crispiness
Fry at moderate heat to cook filling through without burning the wrapper

name: Bo Luc Lac (Shaking Beef)
description: Tender cubes of beef seared in a hot wok with garlic, soy sauce, and served over peppery watercress
instructions: Cut beef into 2cm cubes. Marinate with garlic, soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar, and black pepper for 15 minutes.
Heat oil in a wok over very high heat until smoking.
Sear beef cubes in a single layer without stirring for 1 minute to get a hard sear.
Shake and toss the wok (hence the name) and cook for another minute for medium-rare.
Remove beef. Quickly toss sliced onion and tomato in the same wok for 30 seconds.
Arrange watercress on a plate, top with beef, onion, and tomato. Squeeze lime juice over.
tips: Do not overcrowd the wok — cook in two batches if needed for proper searing

name: Ca Kho To (Caramelized Fish in Clay Pot)
description: Catfish fillets braised in a sweet and savory caramel sauce with fish sauce and black pepper in a clay pot
instructions: Cut catfish into thick steaks. Season lightly with fish sauce and pepper.
In a clay pot or heavy saucepan, make caramel by melting sugar with a splash of water until amber-colored.
Add sliced shallot and garlic to the caramel and stir for 30 seconds.
Add fish pieces, remaining fish sauce, coconut water, and sliced chili.
Simmer uncovered on low heat for 25 minutes, basting fish occasionally, until sauce is thick and glossy.
Garnish with green onion and serve with steamed rice.
tips: Watch the caramel carefully — it goes from amber to burnt in seconds
Coconut water (not coconut milk) gives authentic sweetness

name: Thit Kho (Caramelized Pork Belly)
description: Meltingly tender pork belly slow-braised in caramel sauce with hard-boiled eggs and coconut water
instructions: Cut pork belly into large cubes. Hard-boil eggs and peel.
Make caramel: heat sugar with a splash of water until deep amber.
Add pork cubes to the caramel and toss to coat evenly.
Add garlic, shallot, fish sauce, coconut water, and black pepper.
Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer covered for 40 minutes.
Add peeled hard-boiled eggs and simmer for another 10 minutes until sauce is thick.
Garnish with green onion and serve over steamed jasmine rice.
tips: This dish tastes even better the next day as flavors deepen overnight

name: Goi Ga (Vietnamese Chicken Salad)
description: Shredded poached chicken tossed with cabbage, Vietnamese coriander, crispy shallots, and tangy lime dressing
instructions: Poach chicken breast in simmering water for 15 minutes. Remove and shred finely.
Thinly slice cabbage, julienne carrot, and thinly slice onion. Soak onion in ice water for 5 minutes to soften bite.
Make dressing: whisk lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, and minced chili until sugar dissolves.
Combine chicken, cabbage, carrot, drained onion, and herbs in a large bowl.
Pour dressing over and toss well.
Top with crushed roasted peanuts and fried shallots. Serve immediately.
tips: Shred chicken along the grain for the characteristic texture

name: Canh Chua (Sour Soup with Fish)
description: Vietnamese tamarind-based sour soup with fish, pineapple, tomatoes, okra, and bean sprouts
instructions: Cut fish into large chunks. Season lightly with fish sauce.
Dissolve tamarind paste in 1 cup of warm water, strain out seeds.
Bring 1.5 liters of water to a boil. Add tamarind water, pineapple chunks, and quartered tomatoes.
Simmer for 5 minutes. Add fish pieces and halved okra.
Cook for 8 minutes until fish is done. Season with fish sauce and sugar.
Add bean sprouts, stir briefly, and remove from heat.
Garnish with rice paddy herb and serve with steamed rice.
tips: Do not stir too vigorously after adding fish — the pieces will break apart

name: Bun Rieu (Crab Noodle Soup)
description: Rich tomato-based noodle soup with crab and pork egg froth, tofu puffs, and fresh herbs
instructions: Mix crab paste with ground pork and beaten eggs to form the crab froth mixture.
Bring 2 liters of water to a boil. Gently pour in the crab mixture — it will float and form a frothy raft. Do not stir.
Add quartered tomatoes and annatto oil. Simmer for 20 minutes.
Season broth with fish sauce and shrimp paste.
Halve tofu puffs and add to the soup.
Cook rice vermicelli, drain, and divide among bowls.
Ladle soup with crab froth and tofu over noodles. Serve with lettuce, herbs, and lime.
tips: Let the crab froth form naturally — stirring it breaks the delicate texture

name: Com Ga (Chicken Rice)
description: Vietnamese chicken rice from Hoi An with turmeric-tinted rice, shredded poached chicken, and onion garnish
instructions: Poach chicken thighs in seasoned water with half an onion for 25 minutes. Reserve the broth.
Shred chicken and toss with a splash of fish sauce and pepper.
Cook rice using chicken broth instead of water, adding turmeric and garlic for golden color and flavor.
Thinly slice remaining onion and soak in lime juice for a quick pickle.
Fluff turmeric rice and mound on plates.
Top with shredded chicken, pickled onion, and Vietnamese coriander. Serve with chili sauce and lime.
tips: Using chicken broth to cook the rice is essential — it absorbs all the flavor

name: Banh Xeo (Vietnamese Crispy Pancake)
description: Crispy turmeric-coconut crepe filled with shrimp, pork, bean sprouts, and served with lettuce wraps
instructions: Make batter: whisk rice flour, coconut milk, turmeric, and 1 cup water until smooth. Let rest 15 minutes.
Slice pork belly thinly. Peel and devein shrimp.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan over high heat. Sear a few shrimp and pork slices for 1 minute.
Pour a thin layer of batter over the protein, swirling to coat. Add bean sprouts and sliced onion to one half.
Cover and cook for 3 minutes until edges are crispy and golden.
Fold the pancake in half and slide onto a plate.
Repeat with remaining batter. Serve with lettuce leaves, herbs, and nuoc cham for wrapping.
tips: The key to crispiness is generous oil and high heat — do not skimp

name: Lau (Vietnamese Hot Pot)
description: Communal Vietnamese hot pot with a fragrant lemongrass and tomato broth, assorted seafood, meats, and vegetables
instructions: Prepare broth: simmer lemongrass, quartered tomatoes, and garlic in 2 liters of water for 15 minutes. Season with fish sauce.
Slice beef thinly, clean and score squid, peel shrimp. Arrange on a platter.
Wash and cut napa cabbage, morning glory, and mushrooms. Arrange on another platter.
Bring broth to a rolling boil in a hot pot at the center of the table.
Diners cook ingredients by dipping them into the simmering broth.
Cook rice vermicelli in the broth at the end to soak up all the flavors.
Serve with dipping sauces — satay sauce and nuoc cham.
tips: Keep the broth at a rolling boil throughout the meal for safe cooking

name: Bun Thit Nuong (Grilled Pork Vermicelli)
description: Cool rice vermicelli bowl topped with charcoal-grilled lemongrass pork, fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, and nuoc cham
instructions: Slice pork thinly. Marinate with minced lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, sugar, and a little oil for 30 minutes.
Grill pork over high heat or under a broiler until charred and caramelized, about 3 minutes per side.
Cook rice vermicelli, rinse in cold water, and drain.
Make nuoc cham: mix fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, water, garlic, and chili.
Assemble bowls: vermicelli on the bottom, then lettuce, cucumber, herbs, pickled vegetables, and grilled pork on top.
Drizzle generously with nuoc cham, sprinkle crushed peanuts, and serve.
tips: This is meant to be eaten at room temperature — do not reheat the vermicelli

name: Ga Nuong (Vietnamese Grilled Chicken)
description: Whole chicken pieces marinated in lemongrass, honey, and fish sauce then grilled until golden and juicy
instructions: Mince lemongrass and garlic. Combine with honey, fish sauce, lime juice, chili flakes, oil, and pepper.
Score chicken legs deeply and coat thoroughly with marinade. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour.
Preheat grill or oven to 200°C.
Grill chicken over medium heat, turning occasionally, for 30-35 minutes until charred and cooked through.
Baste with remaining marinade during the last 5 minutes of cooking.
Rest for 5 minutes, garnish with green onion, and serve with steamed rice and pickled vegetables.
tips: Score the chicken deeply so the marinade penetrates the meat thoroughly

name: Rau Muong Xao Toi (Stir-Fried Water Spinach)
description: Crunchy water spinach stir-fried with garlic in a blazing hot wok — a quintessential Vietnamese side dish
instructions: Wash water spinach thoroughly. Cut into 8cm lengths, separating stems from leaves.
Mince garlic.
Heat oil in a wok over maximum heat until smoking.
Add garlic and stir for 10 seconds until fragrant.
Add stems first, stir-fry for 30 seconds, then add leaves.
Add fish sauce, oyster sauce, and sugar. Toss vigorously for 1 minute until just wilted but still crunchy.
Serve immediately — this dish does not hold well.
tips: The wok must be screaming hot — lukewarm stir-frying makes it watery and limp

name: Dau Hu Sot Ca Chua (Tofu in Tomato Sauce)
description: Crispy pan-fried tofu simmered in a savory Vietnamese tomato sauce with green onion
instructions: Press tofu to remove excess water. Cut into triangles.
Pan-fry tofu in oil until golden and crispy on both sides. Remove and set aside.
In the same pan, sauté minced garlic until fragrant.
Add diced tomato and cook until they break down into a sauce, about 5 minutes.
Season with fish sauce, sugar, and pepper.
Return tofu to the pan and simmer in the tomato sauce for 3 minutes.
Garnish with chopped green onion and serve with steamed rice.
tips: Press tofu for at least 15 minutes for the crispiest result

name: Canh Bi Dao (Winter Melon Soup)
description: Light and soothing Vietnamese soup with tender winter melon, shrimp, and a clear savory broth
instructions: Peel winter melon, remove seeds, and cut into bite-sized cubes.
Peel and devein shrimp. Mince garlic.
Sauté garlic in oil until fragrant. Add shrimp and cook for 1 minute.
Add 1 liter of water and bring to a boil.
Add winter melon cubes and simmer for 15 minutes until translucent and tender.
Season with fish sauce and pepper. Garnish with green onion and serve.
tips: Winter melon is done when it turns translucent — do not overcook or it becomes mushy

name: Bo Kho (Vietnamese Beef Stew)
description: Hearty Vietnamese beef stew braised with lemongrass, star anise, and carrots in a rich tomato-spiced broth
instructions: Cut beef chuck into large cubes. Season with fish sauce and five-spice powder.
Heat annatto oil in a heavy pot. Sear beef in batches until browned on all sides.
Sauté garlic, onion, and bruised lemongrass until fragrant.
Add tomato paste, star anise, and cinnamon. Cook for 1 minute.
Return beef to pot. Add coconut water and enough water to cover. Bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer covered for 60 minutes. Add chunked carrots and simmer 20 more minutes.
Adjust seasoning with fish sauce. Serve with crusty bread or rice noodles, garnished with Thai basil.
tips: Bo kho is traditionally eaten with a crusty Vietnamese baguette for dipping

name: Mi Quang (Turmeric Noodles)
description: Central Vietnamese turmeric-tinted noodles with shrimp, pork, peanuts, and a small amount of rich broth
instructions: Slice pork thinly and marinate with turmeric, fish sauce, and garlic.
Sauté pork in annatto oil until cooked. Add shrimp and cook until pink.
Add water and sliced onion. Simmer for 10 minutes to make a concentrated broth.
Cook wide rice noodles and drain.
Place noodles in bowls. Top with pork and shrimp.
Ladle a small amount of broth (this dish is not soupy). Top with crushed peanuts, sesame rice crackers, lettuce, and banana blossom.
tips: Mi Quang uses very little broth compared to pho — just enough to moisten the noodles

name: Hu Tieu Nam Vang (Phnom Penh Noodle Soup)
description: Southern Vietnamese clear pork broth noodle soup with shrimp, pork, and liver, topped with fried garlic
instructions: Simmer pork bones and dried shrimp in water for 40 minutes to make a clear broth. Skim impurities.
Season broth with fish sauce and rock sugar.
Fry sliced garlic in oil until golden and crispy. Set aside.
Form ground pork into small balls. Poach in the broth until cooked.
Cook hu tieu noodles and drain into bowls.
Top with shrimp, pork balls, bean sprouts, and Chinese chives.
Ladle broth over and garnish with fried garlic.
tips: Dried shrimp in the broth is what gives hu tieu its distinctive sweetness

name: Cha Ca La Vong (Turmeric Fish with Dill)
description: Hanoi's iconic dish of turmeric-marinated fish pan-fried with dill and green onion, served with rice noodles
instructions: Cut fish into large chunks. Marinate with turmeric, galangal, shrimp paste, and fish sauce for 20 minutes.
Cook rice vermicelli and drain.
Heat oil in a skillet over medium-high heat.
Pan-fry fish pieces until golden on both sides, about 3 minutes per side.
Add generous amounts of fresh dill and green onion to the pan. Toss gently with the fish for 1 minute.
Serve fish and dill over vermicelli, topped with crushed peanuts and nuoc cham on the side.
tips: Use a generous amount of dill — it is the star of this dish, not just a garnish

name: Com Chien (Vietnamese Fried Rice)
description: Simple Vietnamese fried rice with egg, lap xuong sausage, vegetables, and fish sauce seasoning
instructions: Dice Chinese sausage and carrot. Mince garlic. Chop green onion.
Heat oil in a wok over high heat. Scramble eggs quickly and set aside.
Stir-fry sausage and carrot for 2 minutes until sausage renders its fat.
Add garlic and peas. Stir for 30 seconds.
Add cold rice and break up clumps. Toss over high heat for 3 minutes.
Add fish sauce, white pepper, and scrambled egg. Toss to combine.
Garnish with green onion and serve.
tips: Day-old cold rice is essential — freshly cooked rice will turn mushy

name: Ga Kho Gung (Ginger Chicken)
description: Chicken pieces braised in a sweet-savory ginger and caramel sauce — a comforting everyday Vietnamese dish
instructions: Cut chicken into pieces. Julienne ginger, mince garlic and shallot.
Make caramel: heat sugar with water until amber in a clay pot or heavy pan.
Add chicken pieces and toss in the caramel to coat.
Add ginger, garlic, shallot, fish sauce, and pepper. Stir well.
Add just enough water to partially cover. Bring to a boil.
Simmer covered for 25 minutes until chicken is tender and sauce is thick.
Garnish with green onion and sliced chili. Serve with steamed rice.
tips: Use young ginger for a milder, less fibrous result

name: Canh Rau Mong Toi (Malabar Spinach Soup)
description: Simple and nutritious Vietnamese soup with Malabar spinach and ground pork in a clear broth
instructions: Wash Malabar spinach and pick leaves from stems.
Season ground pork with a splash of fish sauce and pepper.
Sauté garlic in oil until fragrant. Add ground pork and cook, breaking it up, for 2 minutes.
Add 1 liter of water and bring to a boil.
Add Malabar spinach leaves. Cook for 2 minutes until just wilted.
Season with remaining fish sauce and pepper. Serve hot with rice.
tips: Malabar spinach has a slightly mucilaginous texture — this is normal and adds body to the soup

name: Suon Nuong (Grilled Pork Chops)
description: Vietnamese-style grilled pork chops marinated in lemongrass, garlic, and honey served over rice
instructions: Mince lemongrass, garlic, and shallot. Mix with fish sauce, honey, pepper, and oil.
Pound pork chops lightly to even thickness. Coat with marinade and refrigerate for 1 hour.
Cook jasmine rice.
Grill pork chops over medium-high heat for 5 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.
Let rest for 3 minutes before serving.
Serve over rice with pickled vegetables and nuoc cham.
tips: Pound the chops to even thickness so they cook uniformly on the grill

name: Bap Xao (Stir-Fried Corn with Dried Shrimp)
description: Sweet corn kernels stir-fried with butter, dried shrimp, and green onion — a popular Vietnamese street food
instructions: Soak dried shrimp in warm water for 10 minutes. Drain and chop roughly.
Melt butter in a wok over high heat.
Add garlic and dried shrimp. Stir-fry for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add corn kernels and toss over high heat for 3 minutes.
Season with fish sauce and sugar. Stir for another minute.
Add chopped green onion, toss briefly, and serve immediately.
tips: Fresh corn is best, but frozen works in a pinch — just thaw and drain well first

name: Nom Du Du (Green Papaya Salad)
description: Crunchy Vietnamese green papaya salad with beef jerky, herbs, and a sweet-sour-spicy lime dressing
instructions: Peel and julienne green papaya and carrot. Soak in ice water for 10 minutes for crispness.
Shred or tear beef jerky into thin strips.
Make dressing: whisk lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, and minced chili until sugar dissolves.
Drain papaya and carrot well. Toss with dressing, herbs, and beef jerky.
Top with crushed peanuts and fried shallots. Serve immediately.
tips: Soaking the julienned papaya in ice water is the secret to maximum crunch

name: Ca Ri Ga (Vietnamese Chicken Curry)
description: Mild and coconut-rich Vietnamese chicken curry with sweet potato, lemongrass, and warm spices
instructions: Cut chicken into large pieces. Peel and cube sweet potato.
Sauté onion, garlic, and bruised lemongrass in oil until fragrant.
Add curry powder and stir for 30 seconds until aromatic.
Add chicken and sear for 3 minutes until lightly browned.
Pour in coconut milk and add sweet potato. Season with fish sauce and sugar.
Simmer for 30 minutes until chicken and sweet potato are tender.
Garnish with Thai basil. Serve with crusty bread or rice.
tips: Vietnamese curry is intentionally milder than Thai curry — do not add extra chili

name: Chao (Vietnamese Rice Porridge)
description: Silky Vietnamese rice porridge topped with shredded chicken, ginger, fried shallots, and fresh herbs
instructions: Bring 2 liters of water to a boil with sliced ginger. Add chicken thighs and simmer for 20 minutes.
Remove chicken, shred, and set aside. Keep the broth simmering.
Add rinsed rice to the broth. Cook for 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until thick and porridge-like.
Season with fish sauce and pepper.
Ladle porridge into bowls. Top with shredded chicken.
Garnish with fried shallots, green onion, cilantro, and a squeeze of lime.
tips: For silkier porridge, soak rice for 30 minutes before cooking

name: Dau Phu Chien Sa (Lemongrass Tofu)
description: Crispy fried tofu tossed in a fragrant lemongrass and chili sauce — a beloved Vietnamese vegetarian dish
instructions: Press tofu and cut into cubes. Mince lemongrass, garlic, and chili.
Shallow-fry tofu in oil until golden and crispy on all sides. Remove and drain.
In the same pan with 1 tbsp oil, stir-fry lemongrass, garlic, and chili for 1 minute.
Add soy sauce, sugar, and a splash of water. Stir to make a sauce.
Return crispy tofu to the pan and toss to coat evenly in the lemongrass sauce.
Garnish with green onion and pepper. Serve with steamed rice.
tips: Finely mince the lemongrass — large pieces are too fibrous to eat

name: Bun Mam (Fermented Fish Noodle Soup)
description: Intensely flavorful Mekong Delta noodle soup with fermented fish broth, pork, shrimp, and eggplant
instructions: Dissolve fermented fish paste in 2 liters of water. Strain to remove solids.
Simmer the broth with bruised lemongrass and pineapple for 20 minutes.
Boil pork belly until cooked, about 20 minutes. Slice thinly.
Add sugar and adjust fish sauce to balance the broth.
Slice eggplant into rounds and add to the broth. Cook for 5 minutes.
Add shrimp and cook until pink, about 3 minutes.
Cook vermicelli, drain into bowls. Ladle broth with pork, shrimp, and eggplant over. Serve with bean sprouts and basil.
tips: The smell of fermented fish paste is pungent raw but mellows into deep umami when cooked

name: Classic Meatloaf
description: Traditional American meatloaf with ground beef, breadcrumbs, and a sweet tangy ketchup glaze
instructions: Preheat oven to 180°C (350°F). Finely dice onion and mince garlic.
Combine ground beef, breadcrumbs, eggs, milk, diced onion, garlic, 2 tbsp ketchup, Worcestershire sauce, salt, and pepper.
Mix gently until just combined — do not overwork the meat.
Shape into a loaf on a lined baking sheet or press into a loaf pan.
Mix remaining ketchup with brown sugar for the glaze. Spread over the top.
Bake for 55-60 minutes until internal temperature reaches 70°C (160°F).
Rest for 10 minutes before slicing. Serve with mashed potatoes and green beans.
tips: Do not compact the meat too tightly — a gentle hand makes a more tender meatloaf

name: BBQ Pulled Pork
description: Slow-cooked pork shoulder shredded and tossed in smoky barbecue sauce, served on soft buns
instructions: Mix smoked paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, cumin, brown sugar, salt, and pepper. Rub all over the pork shoulder.
Place pork in a slow cooker or Dutch oven with apple cider vinegar.
Cook on low for 3-4 hours (slow cooker) or braise in a 150°C oven covered for 3 hours until fork-tender.
Shred the pork with two forks, discarding excess fat.
Toss shredded pork with BBQ sauce and return to heat for 15 minutes.
Toast buns lightly. Pile pulled pork on buns and top with coleslaw.
tips: Low and slow is the only way — do not rush the cooking or the pork will be tough

name: New England Clam Chowder
description: Thick and creamy New England-style clam chowder with tender potatoes, salt pork, and fresh clams
instructions: Dice salt pork and render in a heavy pot until crispy. Remove cracklings and set aside.
Sauté diced onion and celery in the rendered fat and butter until softened.
Sprinkle flour over vegetables and stir for 2 minutes to make a roux.
Add diced potatoes, clam juice, bay leaf, and thyme. Simmer for 15 minutes until potatoes are tender.
Add chopped clams, cream, and milk. Heat gently for 5 minutes — do not boil.
Season with salt and white pepper. Serve topped with reserved pork cracklings and oyster crackers.
tips: Never boil the chowder after adding cream — it will curdle

name: Southern Fried Chicken
description: Crispy double-dipped Southern fried chicken with a seasoned flour coating and buttermilk brine
instructions: Soak chicken pieces in buttermilk with salt for at least 2 hours, preferably overnight.
Mix flour with paprika, garlic powder, cayenne, salt, and pepper.
Remove chicken from buttermilk. Dredge in seasoned flour, dip back in buttermilk, then dredge again.
Heat oil to 170°C (340°F) in a deep heavy pot.
Fry chicken in batches for 12-15 minutes, turning once, until golden and internal temperature reaches 75°C.
Drain on a wire rack for 5 minutes. Serve with coleslaw and biscuits.
tips: The double dredge is the secret to the thick, craggy crust

name: Jambalaya
description: Spicy Cajun one-pot rice dish with andouille sausage, chicken, shrimp, and the holy trinity of vegetables
instructions: Season chicken with Cajun seasoning. Sear in a large pot until browned. Remove and set aside.
Sauté sliced andouille until browned. Remove.
Cook the holy trinity (diced onion, celery, bell pepper) and garlic in the drippings for 5 minutes.
Add tomatoes, chicken broth, and rice. Return chicken and sausage.
Bring to a boil, cover, reduce heat, and simmer for 20 minutes until rice is cooked.
Add shrimp in the last 5 minutes. Cover and cook until shrimp are pink.
Fluff and serve garnished with chopped green onion.
tips: Do not stir the rice while it simmers — let it cook undisturbed for the best texture

name: Chicken Pot Pie
description: Comforting American chicken pot pie with tender chicken, vegetables, and creamy sauce under a flaky golden crust
instructions: Preheat oven to 200°C (400°F). Dice potato and boil until just tender, about 8 minutes.
Melt butter in a large pan. Sauté diced onion until soft.
Stir in flour and cook for 2 minutes to make a roux.
Gradually add chicken broth and milk, stirring constantly until thick and smooth.
Add diced chicken, cooked potato, peas and carrots, and thyme. Season with salt and pepper.
Pour filling into a baking dish. Top with puff pastry, pressing edges to seal. Cut slits for steam. Brush with beaten egg.
Bake for 25-30 minutes until pastry is golden and filling bubbles.
tips: Let the pot pie rest for 10 minutes before serving — the filling will thicken as it cools slightly

name: Cobb Salad
description: Iconic American composed salad with rows of chicken, bacon, avocado, egg, blue cheese, and tomato
instructions: Season and grill or pan-sear chicken breast until cooked through. Slice.
Cook bacon until crispy. Chop into pieces.
Chop romaine lettuce and spread on a large platter.
Arrange rows of sliced chicken, bacon, diced avocado, halved tomatoes, chopped egg, and crumbled blue cheese over the lettuce.
Whisk vinegar, olive oil, mustard, salt, and pepper for the dressing.
Drizzle dressing over the salad or serve on the side. Garnish with chives.
tips: The composed rows are the signature presentation — do not toss the salad

name: Philly Cheesesteak
description: Thinly sliced ribeye steak with melted provolone, sauteed onions, and peppers on a hoagie roll
instructions: Freeze ribeye for 30 minutes, then slice paper-thin against the grain.
Heat oil on a flat griddle or large skillet over high heat.
Sauté sliced onion, pepper, and mushrooms until softened. Set aside.
Spread steak slices on the hot griddle. Season with salt and pepper. Chop and flip as it cooks.
Lay provolone slices over the steak and let it melt.
Split hoagie rolls and fill with cheesy steak and sauteed vegetables. Serve immediately.
tips: A flat griddle gives the most authentic result — the steak should sizzle hard when it hits the surface

name: Mac and Cheese
description: Creamy baked American macaroni and cheese with a sharp cheddar sauce and golden breadcrumb topping
instructions: Preheat oven to 190°C (375°F). Cook macaroni until al dente and drain.
Melt butter in a saucepan. Whisk in flour and cook for 2 minutes.
Gradually add milk, whisking constantly, until thick and smooth.
Remove from heat. Stir in grated cheddar (reserve a handful), mustard, paprika, garlic powder, and salt.
Fold in cooked macaroni and pour into a greased baking dish.
Top with remaining cheese and breadcrumbs tossed with melted butter.
Bake for 20 minutes until golden and bubbling.
tips: Use a mix of sharp and mild cheddar for the best balance of flavor and meltability

name: Cajun Blackened Catfish
description: Catfish fillets coated in bold Cajun spices and seared in a cast-iron skillet until blackened and flaky
instructions: Mix Cajun seasoning, paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, and cayenne.
Pat catfish fillets dry and coat generously with the spice mixture on both sides.
Heat a cast-iron skillet over very high heat until smoking.
Add butter and immediately place fillets in the pan. Cook for 3 minutes per side until a dark crust forms.
Squeeze lemon juice over the fish.
Serve over rice, garnished with parsley and lemon wedges.
tips: Open a window — blackening creates dramatic smoke, which is exactly what you want

name: Texas Chili Con Carne
description: Thick and hearty Texas-style chili with chunks of beef, dried chiles, and no beans — as tradition demands
instructions: Toast dried ancho chiles in a dry pan, then soak in hot water for 15 minutes. Blend into a smooth paste.
Cut beef chuck into small cubes (not ground). Season with salt, cumin, and oregano.
Brown beef in oil in batches in a heavy pot. Remove and set aside.
Sauté diced onion and garlic in the same pot until softened.
Add chile paste, tomato paste, and cayenne. Cook for 2 minutes.
Return beef to pot. Add broth. Bring to a boil, then simmer covered for 90 minutes until beef is tender.
Adjust seasoning. Serve with cornbread, sour cream, and shredded cheese.
tips: Real Texas chili uses cubed beef, not ground — and absolutely no beans

name: Shrimp and Grits
description: Creamy Southern stone-ground grits topped with sauteed shrimp, bacon, and a savory pan sauce
instructions: Cook grits in milk and 2 cups water, stirring frequently, for 20 minutes until creamy. Stir in cheese and 1 tbsp butter.
Cook bacon until crispy. Chop and set aside. Reserve the drippings.
Season shrimp with Cajun seasoning. Sauté in bacon drippings for 2 minutes per side until pink. Remove.
Add garlic to the pan, cook 30 seconds. Deglaze with chicken broth and lemon juice.
Return shrimp to the pan and toss in the sauce.
Spoon creamy grits into bowls. Top with shrimp and pan sauce, chopped bacon, and green onion.
tips: Stone-ground grits take longer but have far better texture and flavor than instant

name: Buffalo Chicken Wings
description: Crispy fried chicken wings tossed in spicy cayenne-butter Buffalo sauce, served with celery and blue cheese dip
instructions: Pat chicken wings completely dry. Season with salt and garlic powder.
Heat oil to 190°C (375°F) in a deep pot. Fry wings in batches for 10-12 minutes until golden and crispy.
Drain on a wire rack.
Melt butter and whisk in hot sauce to make Buffalo sauce.
Toss fried wings in Buffalo sauce until evenly coated.
Make blue cheese dip: crumble blue cheese into sour cream and mayonnaise, mix well.
Serve wings with celery sticks and blue cheese dip.
tips: Drying the wings thoroughly before frying is the single most important step for crispiness

name: BLT Club Sandwich
description: Triple-decker club sandwich stacked with crispy bacon, fresh lettuce, tomato, turkey, and mayonnaise
instructions: Cook bacon until very crispy. Drain on paper towels.
Toast all bread slices until golden.
Spread mayonnaise on one side of each toast.
First layer: toast, lettuce, tomato slices, bacon.
Second layer: toast, sliced turkey, avocado slices, more bacon.
Top with third toast. Secure with toothpicks, cut diagonally, and serve with chips or a pickle.
tips: Use thick-cut bacon and cook it very crispy so it does not pull out of the sandwich when bitten

name: Cornbread with Chili
description: Sweet golden Southern cornbread baked in a cast-iron skillet, served alongside a rich bean chili
instructions: Preheat oven to 200°C (400°F). Place a cast-iron skillet with 2 tbsp butter in the oven.
Mix cornmeal, flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt. Whisk in egg, buttermilk, and 2 tbsp melted butter.
Pour batter into the hot skillet. Bake for 20-25 minutes until golden.
Meanwhile, brown ground beef and diced onion. Add chili powder, diced tomatoes, and drained beans.
Simmer chili for 20 minutes until thick. Season to taste.
Slice warm cornbread and serve alongside the chili with sour cream and cheddar.
tips: The sizzling hot skillet creates the coveted crispy bottom crust on the cornbread

name: Gumbo
description: Rich Louisiana gumbo with a dark roux, andouille sausage, chicken, okra, and the holy trinity over rice
instructions: Make a dark roux: whisk oil and flour in a heavy pot over medium heat, stirring constantly for 30-40 minutes until chocolate-brown.
Add the holy trinity (diced onion, celery, bell pepper) and garlic. Cook for 5 minutes.
Gradually stir in chicken broth. Add seasoned chicken pieces and sliced sausage.
Bring to a boil, then simmer for 45 minutes until chicken is tender.
Remove chicken, shred, and return to the pot. Add sliced okra.
Simmer for 15 more minutes. Season with Cajun seasoning and file powder.
Serve over steamed white rice.
tips: A dark roux is the soul of gumbo — do not rush it, and stir constantly to prevent burning

name: Pot Roast with Root Vegetables
description: Fork-tender braised beef chuck roast with carrots, potatoes, and onions in a rich gravy
instructions: Preheat oven to 160°C (325°F). Season the roast generously with salt and pepper.
Sear the roast in oil in a Dutch oven on all sides until deeply browned. Remove.
Sauté quartered onion, garlic, and celery for 3 minutes. Stir in tomato paste.
Deglaze with beef broth, scraping up browned bits. Add thyme.
Return roast to the pot. Cover and braise in the oven for 2 hours.
Add chunked carrots and potatoes around the roast. Cover and cook for 45 more minutes.
Slice roast against the grain. Serve with vegetables and spoon pan gravy over everything.
tips: A proper hard sear on the roast builds the flavor foundation for the entire dish

name: Baked Salmon with Lemon Dill
description: Oven-baked salmon fillets with a bright lemon-dill butter sauce, tender and flaky
instructions: Preheat oven to 200°C (400°F). Line a baking sheet with parchment paper.
Place salmon fillets on the sheet. Season with salt, pepper, and a brush of olive oil.
Melt butter with minced garlic, lemon juice, Dijon mustard, and chopped dill.
Spoon the lemon-dill butter over each fillet. Lay lemon slices on top.
Arrange trimmed asparagus around the salmon. Drizzle with olive oil.
Bake for 12-15 minutes until salmon flakes easily with a fork.
Serve immediately with remaining pan juices spooned over.
tips: Do not overcook — salmon should be slightly translucent in the very center for the best texture

name: Greek Moussaka
description: Layered eggplant and spiced lamb casserole topped with creamy bechamel sauce
instructions: Slice eggplant into 1cm rounds, salt and let drain for 30 minutes, then pat dry.
Brush eggplant slices with olive oil and roast at 200°C for 20 minutes until golden.
Sauté onion and garlic in olive oil, add ground lamb and cook until browned.
Stir in crushed tomatoes, cinnamon, salt, and pepper; simmer for 15 minutes.
Make bechamel: melt butter, whisk in flour, gradually add milk, stirring until thick.
Remove bechamel from heat, stir in egg and nutmeg.
Layer roasted eggplant, meat sauce, and bechamel in a baking dish.
Bake at 180°C for 40 minutes until top is golden brown. Let rest 15 minutes before serving.
tips: Salting the eggplant removes bitterness and excess moisture.
Let it rest after baking so layers hold together when sliced.

name: Chicken Souvlaki with Tzatziki
description: Herb-marinated grilled chicken skewers served with cool cucumber yogurt sauce
instructions: Cut chicken into 2cm cubes and marinate in olive oil, lemon juice, garlic, and oregano for at least 20 minutes.
Grate cucumber, squeeze out excess water, and mix with yogurt, minced garlic, and salt for tzatziki.
Thread marinated chicken onto skewers.
Grill skewers on high heat for 4-5 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.
Warm pita bread on the grill for 30 seconds per side.
Slice tomato and red onion thinly.
Serve chicken skewers in pita with tzatziki, tomato, and onion.
tips: Marinate overnight for deeper flavor.
Soak wooden skewers in water for 30 minutes before grilling to prevent burning.

name: Spanakopita
description: Flaky phyllo pastry filled with spinach, feta, and herbs
instructions: Wilt spinach in a pan, squeeze out all excess water, and chop roughly.
Sauté onion and scallions in olive oil until soft.
Mix spinach, onion mixture, crumbled feta, eggs, dill, nutmeg, salt, and pepper in a bowl.
Brush a baking dish with olive oil and layer 6 sheets of phyllo, brushing each with oil.
Spread the spinach-feta filling evenly over the phyllo layers.
Top with remaining 6 phyllo sheets, brushing each with oil.
Score the top layers into squares and bake at 180°C for 35-40 minutes until golden and crisp.
tips: Keep phyllo covered with a damp towel while working to prevent drying.
Let rest 10 minutes before cutting for cleaner slices.

name: Falafel with Tahini Sauce
description: Crispy chickpea fritters with herbs served with creamy tahini dressing
instructions: Soak dried chickpeas overnight (do not use canned), then drain well.
Blend chickpeas, onion, garlic, parsley, cilantro, cumin, and coriander in a food processor until coarsely ground.
Add baking powder and salt, pulse to combine. Refrigerate mixture for 1 hour.
Shape mixture into small patties or balls.
Deep fry in hot oil (180°C) for 3-4 minutes until deep golden brown.
Mix tahini with lemon juice, garlic, and water to make sauce.
Serve falafel with tahini sauce, pita, and fresh vegetables.
tips: Never use canned chickpeas for falafel — they are too wet and will fall apart.
If mixture is too wet, add a tablespoon of flour.

name: Shakshuka
description: Eggs poached in a spiced tomato and pepper sauce with cumin and paprika
instructions: Heat olive oil in a deep skillet and sauté diced onion and bell pepper until soft, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic, cumin, paprika, and chili flakes; cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
Pour in crushed tomatoes, season with salt and pepper, and simmer for 10 minutes.
Make 4 wells in the sauce and crack an egg into each one.
Cover and cook on low heat for 6-8 minutes until egg whites are set but yolks are still runny.
Garnish with fresh cilantro and serve directly from the skillet with crusty bread.
tips: For creamier shakshuka, crumble feta cheese over the top before serving.
Cover tightly so the eggs steam-cook evenly.

name: Greek Lemon Chicken
description: Roasted chicken with lemon, garlic, oregano, and golden potatoes in the Greek tradition
instructions: Cut potatoes into wedges and place in a large roasting pan.
Nestle chicken thighs among the potatoes.
Whisk together olive oil, lemon juice, minced garlic, oregano, salt, and pepper.
Pour the lemon mixture over chicken and potatoes, tossing to coat evenly.
Add chicken broth to the pan and tuck lemon halves between pieces.
Roast at 200°C for 60-65 minutes, basting halfway, until chicken is golden and potatoes are crisp.
Let rest 5 minutes before serving with pan juices spooned over the top.
tips: Use bone-in, skin-on thighs for the juiciest result.
The pan juices mixed with lemon make a natural sauce — don't waste them.

name: Lamb Kofta
description: Spiced ground lamb skewers grilled until charred, served with yogurt and flatbread
instructions: Grate onion and squeeze out excess moisture.
Mix ground lamb with grated onion, minced garlic, parsley, cumin, coriander, paprika, cinnamon, and salt.
Knead the mixture well and shape into elongated sausage shapes around skewers.
Refrigerate for 15 minutes to firm up.
Grill kofta over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.
Serve with Greek yogurt, warm pita bread, and a simple salad.
tips: Grating the onion instead of chopping gives a smoother texture.
Don't over-handle the meat or it becomes tough.

name: Fattoush Salad
description: Crispy pita chip salad with fresh vegetables and tangy sumac dressing
instructions: Tear pita bread into bite-sized pieces, brush with olive oil, and toast at 200°C until crispy.
Chop lettuce, dice tomatoes and cucumber, and thinly slice radishes and green onions.
Tear fresh mint leaves.
Whisk together olive oil, lemon juice, pomegranate molasses, sumac, salt, and pepper for the dressing.
Combine all vegetables in a large bowl.
Add crispy pita chips and dressing just before serving, tossing gently.
tips: Add pita chips at the last moment so they stay crispy.
Sumac is essential — it gives the signature tangy flavor.

name: Baba Ganoush with Pita
description: Smoky roasted eggplant dip blended with tahini, lemon, and garlic
instructions: Prick eggplants all over with a fork and roast directly over a gas flame or under the broiler, turning, until skin is charred and flesh is completely soft.
Let eggplants cool, then scoop out the soft flesh and discard skin.
Drain excess liquid from the eggplant flesh.
