Mash eggplant with tahini, lemon juice, minced garlic, cumin, and salt.
Transfer to a serving bowl, drizzle with olive oil, and sprinkle with smoked paprika and parsley.
Serve with warm pita bread for dipping.
tips: Charring over an open flame gives the best smoky flavor.
Let the eggplant drain well or the dip will be watery.

name: Grilled Halloumi Salad
description: Warm grilled halloumi cheese over a bed of fresh vegetables with herb vinaigrette
instructions: Slice halloumi into 1cm thick slabs and pat dry.
Grill or pan-sear halloumi on high heat for 2 minutes per side until golden.
Halve cherry tomatoes, dice cucumber, and thinly slice red onion.
Arrange salad greens on plates and top with tomatoes, cucumber, onion, and olives.
Whisk olive oil, lemon juice, oregano, salt, and pepper for the dressing.
Place warm grilled halloumi on top of the salad, drizzle with dressing, and garnish with mint.
tips: Halloumi is best eaten warm while still soft inside.
Pat halloumi dry before grilling for a better sear.

name: Stuffed Grape Leaves (Dolma)
description: Tender grape leaves filled with herbed rice, pine nuts, and currants
instructions: If using jarred grape leaves, rinse well and pat dry. Blanch fresh leaves in boiling water for 1 minute.
Sauté diced onion in olive oil until translucent, then add pine nuts and toast lightly.
Mix rice, sautéed onion, pine nuts, currants, dill, mint, allspice, salt, and a tablespoon of olive oil.
Place a grape leaf shiny-side down, add a tablespoon of filling near the stem end, fold sides in, and roll tightly.
Pack rolls snugly seam-side down in a pot lined with extra grape leaves.
Add lemon juice, remaining olive oil, and enough water to just cover the rolls.
Place a plate on top to weigh them down, cover, and simmer on low for 45 minutes until rice is cooked.
tips: Pack the dolma tightly so they don't unravel during cooking.
Serve cold with lemon wedges for the traditional meze presentation.

name: Mediterranean Fish Stew
description: Hearty tomato-saffron broth with mixed white fish, shrimp, and fennel
instructions: Bloom saffron threads in 2 tablespoons of warm water for 10 minutes.
Sauté sliced fennel and diced onion in olive oil until softened, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic and cook 1 minute, then pour in white wine and let it reduce by half.
Add crushed tomatoes, saffron water, salt, and pepper; simmer for 15 minutes.
Cut fish into large chunks, add to the stew, and cook for 5 minutes.
Add shrimp and cook 3-4 minutes more until shrimp are pink and fish flakes easily.
Ladle into bowls, garnish with fresh parsley, and serve with crusty bread.
tips: Don't stir aggressively or the fish will break apart.
A splash of Pernod or ouzo at the end adds an authentic anise note.

name: Tabbouleh
description: Fresh parsley and bulgur wheat salad with tomatoes, mint, and lemon dressing
instructions: Soak bulgur in boiling water for 15 minutes, then drain well and squeeze out excess water.
Finely chop parsley and mint leaves — the herbs should dominate, not the bulgur.
Dice tomatoes and finely slice green onions.
Combine bulgur, herbs, tomatoes, and onions in a large bowl.
Whisk together lemon juice, olive oil, salt, and pepper.
Pour dressing over salad and toss. Let sit 10 minutes before serving for flavors to meld.
tips: Traditional tabbouleh is mostly herbs with just a little bulgur.
Use flat-leaf parsley for the most authentic flavor.

name: Hummus Bowl with Roasted Vegetables
description: Creamy hummus base topped with roasted seasonal vegetables, chickpeas, and olive oil
instructions: Blend chickpeas (reserve a few for topping), tahini, lemon juice, garlic, cumin, and 2 tbsp ice water until very smooth.
Cut zucchini, bell pepper, and red onion into chunks; halve the cherry tomatoes.
Toss vegetables with olive oil, paprika, salt, and pepper.
Roast vegetables at 200°C for 20-25 minutes until caramelized.
Spread hummus generously on plates, top with roasted vegetables and reserved whole chickpeas.
Drizzle with olive oil and scatter toasted pine nuts over the top.
tips: Add ice water to the hummus for an ultra-creamy texture.
A pinch of baking soda in the chickpeas before blending makes it even silkier.

name: Greek Gigantes (Baked Giant Beans)
description: Giant white beans slow-baked in a rich tomato sauce with herbs and olive oil
instructions: If using dried beans, soak overnight and boil until just tender (about 45 minutes). Drain.
Sauté diced onion, carrot, and celery in olive oil until soft.
Add garlic and cook 1 minute, then add crushed tomatoes, sugar, salt, and pepper.
Simmer sauce for 10 minutes, then stir in the beans.
Transfer to a baking dish and drizzle generously with olive oil.
Bake uncovered at 180°C for 45-50 minutes until sauce is thick and top is slightly caramelized.
Garnish with fresh parsley and serve warm with crusty bread.
tips: The long bake is key — it concentrates the sauce and makes the beans creamy inside.
Use good quality olive oil as it is a starring ingredient here.

name: Imam Bayildi (Stuffed Eggplant)
description: Whole eggplant stuffed with onions, tomatoes, and garlic braised in olive oil
instructions: Cut a deep slit lengthwise in each eggplant without cutting through. Salt the insides and let drain for 20 minutes.
Sauté thinly sliced onions in olive oil over low heat until very soft and golden, about 15 minutes.
Add sliced garlic, diced tomatoes, parsley, sugar, salt, and pepper to the onions; cook 5 minutes.
Rinse and pat dry the eggplants, then stuff the filling into each slit.
Arrange stuffed eggplants in a deep pan, pour any remaining filling around them.
Add lemon juice and enough water to come halfway up the eggplants.
Cover and braise on low heat for 40 minutes until eggplant is completely tender. Serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: This dish is traditionally served at room temperature.
The generous olive oil is essential — do not reduce it.

name: Moroccan Tagine with Chickpeas
description: Aromatic North African stew with chickpeas, apricots, and warm spices
instructions: Sauté diced onion in olive oil until golden, then add garlic, cumin, cinnamon, and turmeric.
Add cubed sweet potato and stir to coat with the spices.
Pour in diced tomatoes and enough water to just cover. Bring to a simmer.
Add drained chickpeas and chopped dried apricots.
Cover and simmer for 30 minutes until sweet potato is tender and sauce is thick.
Meanwhile, prepare couscous according to package directions.
Serve the tagine over fluffy couscous, garnished with fresh cilantro.
tips: The apricots add essential sweetness — don't skip them.
A drizzle of honey and a handful of toasted almonds elevate this dish further.

name: Mediterranean Lentil Soup
description: Warming red lentil soup with cumin, lemon, and a swirl of olive oil
instructions: Sauté diced onion and carrot in olive oil until softened, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic, cumin, and turmeric; cook for 1 minute until fragrant.
Add rinsed red lentils and vegetable broth. Bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer for 20-25 minutes until lentils are completely soft.
Blend half the soup for a creamy texture while keeping some lentils whole.
Stir in lemon juice, season with salt and pepper.
Serve drizzled with olive oil and a lemon wedge on the side.
tips: Red lentils don't need soaking and cook very quickly.
The lemon juice at the end brightens the whole soup — add it generously.

name: Grilled Swordfish with Capers
description: Thick swordfish steaks grilled and topped with a lemon-caper-olive relish
instructions: Pat swordfish steaks dry, brush with olive oil, and season with salt and pepper.
Grill swordfish on high heat for 4 minutes per side until charred outside and opaque inside.
Meanwhile, make the relish: combine halved cherry tomatoes, chopped olives, capers, minced garlic, oregano, lemon zest, and olive oil.
Let the swordfish rest for 2 minutes after grilling.
Spoon the caper-olive relish generously over the fish.
Serve with a squeeze of fresh lemon juice.
tips: Don't overcook swordfish — it dries out quickly past medium.
Oil the grill well to prevent sticking.

name: Panzanella (Tuscan Bread Salad)
description: Rustic Italian bread salad with ripe tomatoes, basil, and red wine vinaigrette
instructions: Tear stale bread into rough chunks and drizzle with a little water and olive oil if very hard.
Cut tomatoes into wedges, dice cucumber, and thinly slice red onion.
Whisk together olive oil, red wine vinegar, salt, and pepper.
Combine bread, tomatoes, cucumber, onion, and capers in a large bowl.
Pour dressing over and toss well. Let sit for 15 minutes so the bread absorbs the juices.
Tear fresh basil over the top and toss once more before serving.
tips: Stale bread is essential — fresh bread gets too mushy.
Make this at peak tomato season for the best flavor.

name: Pastitsio (Greek Baked Pasta)
description: Greek-style baked pasta with spiced meat sauce and creamy bechamel topping
instructions: Cook penne until al dente, drain and toss with a little olive oil.
Brown ground beef with onion and garlic, then add crushed tomatoes, cinnamon, salt, and pepper. Simmer 15 minutes.
Make bechamel: melt butter, whisk in flour, gradually add milk, stirring until thick and smooth.
Remove bechamel from heat, stir in beaten eggs and nutmeg.
Layer half the pasta in a greased baking dish, spread all the meat sauce over it, then top with remaining pasta.
Pour bechamel evenly over the top.
Bake at 180°C for 40-45 minutes until golden brown. Let rest 20 minutes before cutting.
tips: The resting time is crucial — it lets layers set for clean slices.
The cinnamon in the meat sauce is what makes it distinctly Greek.

name: Shrimp Saganaki
description: Plump shrimp baked in a spiced tomato sauce with crumbled feta cheese
instructions: Sauté diced onion in olive oil until soft, add garlic and cook 1 minute.
Add crushed tomatoes, oregano, chili flakes, salt, and pepper; simmer for 10 minutes.
Add ouzo and stir to combine.
Nestle peeled shrimp into the sauce in a single layer.
Crumble feta cheese over the top.
Bake at 200°C for 10-12 minutes until shrimp are pink and feta is slightly melted.
Garnish with parsley and serve with crusty bread to soak up the sauce.
tips: Ouzo adds authentic flavor, but you can substitute with a splash of white wine.
Don't overcook the shrimp — they cook quickly in the hot sauce.

name: Roasted Red Pepper and Walnut Dip (Muhammara)
description: Smoky-sweet Syrian dip of roasted red peppers, walnuts, and pomegranate molasses
instructions: Toast walnuts in a dry pan over medium heat until fragrant, about 3 minutes.
Pulse roasted red peppers, toasted walnuts, breadcrumbs, garlic, and cumin in a food processor.
Add pomegranate molasses, olive oil, Aleppo pepper, lemon juice, and salt.
Blend until you have a coarse, spreadable consistency — not completely smooth.
Transfer to a serving bowl, drizzle with olive oil, and sprinkle with extra Aleppo pepper.
Serve with warm pita bread for dipping.
tips: Aleppo pepper is milder than regular chili flakes — it adds warmth without harsh heat.
This dip tastes even better the next day after flavors meld.

name: Za'atar Roasted Chicken
description: Whole spatchcocked chicken rubbed with za'atar and roasted until golden and crispy
instructions: Spatchcock the chicken by removing the backbone and pressing flat.
Mix za'atar, olive oil, lemon juice, sumac, minced garlic, salt, and pepper into a paste.
Rub the za'atar paste all over the chicken, including under the skin.
Place sliced onion and lemon halves in a roasting pan and set chicken on top, skin-side up.
Roast at 220°C for 45-50 minutes until skin is golden and crisp, and internal temperature reaches 75°C.
Rest for 10 minutes before carving. Spoon pan juices over the chicken.
tips: Spatchcocking ensures even cooking and crispier skin.
Za'atar quality varies — use a fresh, well-made blend with lots of sumac and sesame.

name: Mediterranean Orzo Salad
description: Orzo pasta tossed with sun-dried tomatoes, olives, feta, and herb vinaigrette
instructions: Cook orzo according to package directions. Drain, rinse with cold water, and drain again.
Chop sun-dried tomatoes, halve olives, dice cucumber and bell pepper, and finely slice red onion.
Whisk together olive oil, lemon juice, oregano, salt, and pepper.
Toss cooled orzo with all the vegetables and dressing.
Crumble feta cheese over the top and tear fresh basil leaves over it.
Toss gently and serve at room temperature.
tips: Rinse orzo well with cold water to prevent clumping.
This salad improves after 30 minutes in the fridge as flavors meld.

name: Harissa Grilled Lamb Chops
description: Tender lamb chops marinated in fiery harissa paste and grilled until charred
instructions: Mix harissa paste, olive oil, minced garlic, lemon juice, cumin, honey, and salt into a marinade.
Coat lamb chops thoroughly in the marinade and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes.
Bring lamb to room temperature before grilling.
Grill on high heat for 3-4 minutes per side for medium-rare.
Let rest for 5 minutes before serving.
Mix yogurt with chopped mint for a cooling sauce and serve alongside the chops.
tips: Don't skip resting the meat — it redistributes juices for better flavor.
Harissa varies in heat — taste first and adjust quantity.

name: Turkish Lentil Soup (Mercimek)
description: Velvety smooth red lentil soup with a drizzle of paprika butter, a Turkish staple
instructions: Sauté diced onion, carrot, and potato in a splash of oil until slightly softened.
Add garlic, tomato paste, and cumin; cook for 1 minute.
Add rinsed red lentils and vegetable broth. Bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer for 20-25 minutes until lentils and vegetables are very soft.
Blend the soup until completely smooth with an immersion blender.
Melt butter in a small pan with paprika to make a paprika butter drizzle.
Serve soup in bowls with paprika butter swirled on top and lemon wedges on the side.
tips: The paprika butter drizzle on top is traditional and essential.
Blend until very smooth — Mercimek should be velvety, not chunky.

name: Greek Bean Soup (Fasolada)
description: Hearty national dish of Greece — white beans simmered with tomatoes, celery, and olive oil
instructions: Soak beans overnight in plenty of water. Drain and boil in fresh water for 20 minutes, then drain.
In a large pot, sauté diced onion, carrots, and celery in olive oil for 5 minutes.
Add garlic and cook 1 minute.
Add par-boiled beans, crushed tomatoes, bay leaves, and enough water to cover by 2 inches.
Bring to a boil, reduce heat, and simmer for 45-60 minutes until beans are creamy and tender.
Season with salt and pepper, remove bay leaves.
Serve in bowls with a generous drizzle of olive oil and fresh parsley.
tips: The generous olive oil stirred in at the end is what makes this dish special.
Fasolada is even better reheated the next day.

name: Grilled Octopus with Lemon
description: Tender braised then grilled octopus with lemon, olive oil, and oregano
instructions: Clean octopus and place in a pot with bay leaf — do not add water. Cover tightly.
Simmer over low heat for 45-60 minutes until tender (octopus releases its own liquid).
Drain and let cool slightly, then cut into portions.
Toss octopus pieces with olive oil, garlic, oregano, salt, and pepper.
Grill on very high heat for 2-3 minutes per side until charred at the edges.
Dress with lemon juice, red wine vinegar, capers, and fresh parsley.
Serve immediately while warm.
tips: The braising step is essential — without it the octopus will be rubbery.
No water needed when braising — the octopus releases enough liquid.

name: Caponata (Sicilian Eggplant Stew)
description: Sweet and sour Sicilian eggplant stew with tomatoes, capers, olives, and pine nuts
instructions: Cut eggplant into 2cm cubes, salt, and let drain for 20 minutes. Pat dry.
Fry eggplant cubes in olive oil until golden on all sides. Set aside on paper towels.
In the same pan, sauté diced celery and onion until soft.
Add crushed tomatoes, olives, capers, and pine nuts; simmer for 10 minutes.
Stir in vinegar and sugar to create the sweet-sour balance.
Return eggplant to the sauce, fold gently, and cook 5 more minutes.
Let cool to room temperature, garnish with fresh basil, and serve.
tips: Caponata is best served at room temperature or cold.
The sweet-sour balance is the signature — adjust vinegar and sugar to taste.

name: Couscous with Seven Vegetables
description: Traditional North African couscous with a fragrant seven-vegetable stew and chickpeas
instructions: Sauté diced onion in olive oil until golden, then add ras el hanout and stir 1 minute.
Add chopped carrots, turnip, and sweet potato — the harder vegetables first.
Add enough water to cover generously, bring to a boil, and simmer for 15 minutes.
Add zucchini, cabbage, diced tomatoes, and drained chickpeas. Simmer 15 more minutes.
Meanwhile, prepare couscous: pour 2 cups boiling broth over couscous, cover, and let sit 5 minutes, then fluff.
Season the stew with salt and pepper. The broth should be rich but thin enough to ladle.
Mound couscous on a platter, make a well in the center, and ladle the vegetable stew over it. Garnish with cilantro.
tips: Ras el hanout is essential — it is a complex spice blend that defines this dish.
Traditionally the broth is served alongside in a bowl for adding to taste.

name: Lahmacun (Turkish Flatbread Pizza)
description: Paper-thin crispy flatbread topped with spiced lamb, tomatoes, and fresh herbs
instructions: Mix flour, yeast, salt, and warm water to form a soft dough. Knead 5 minutes and let rest 30 minutes.
Finely mince (or process) the lamb, onion, tomatoes, and bell pepper together until almost paste-like.
Mix the meat mixture with tomato paste, Aleppo pepper, cumin, and salt.
Divide dough into 8 portions and roll each paper-thin into rounds.
Spread a thin layer of the meat mixture over each round, all the way to the edges.
Bake at 250°C on a preheated baking stone for 5-7 minutes until edges are crisp.
Serve with fresh parsley, a squeeze of lemon, and roll up to eat.
tips: Roll the dough as thin as possible — it should be see-through almost.
The meat topping should be spread very thin, like a paste, not a chunky layer.

name: Gemista (Greek Stuffed Tomatoes)
description: Ripe tomatoes stuffed with herbed rice and baked until caramelized
instructions: Cut tops off tomatoes and scoop out the pulp. Reserve pulp and chop it.
Sprinkle a little sugar inside each tomato shell.
Sauté onion in olive oil until soft, add garlic, rice, pine nuts, herbs, reserved tomato pulp, salt, and pepper.
Cook for 2 minutes, then stuff each tomato with the rice mixture, leaving room for rice to expand.
Place caps back on tomatoes and arrange in a baking dish.
Tuck potato wedges between tomatoes, drizzle everything with olive oil, and add a little water to the dish.
Bake at 180°C for 55-60 minutes until rice is cooked and tomatoes are soft and caramelized.
tips: Leave space in the tomatoes — the rice expands significantly.
The potatoes cook in the tomato juices and become incredibly flavorful.

name: Mediterranean Chickpea Stew
description: Comforting chickpea stew with spinach, tomatoes, and warm spices
instructions: Sauté diced onion in olive oil until golden, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic, cumin, and smoked paprika; cook 1 minute.
Add diced tomatoes, drained chickpeas, and vegetable broth.
Simmer for 15 minutes until sauce thickens.
Stir in spinach and cook until wilted, about 2 minutes.
Finish with lemon juice, salt, and pepper.
Serve drizzled with olive oil and warm bread on the side.
tips: Smoked paprika gives a depth that regular paprika can't match.
Mash a few chickpeas against the pot to thicken the stew naturally.

name: Grilled Sea Bass with Herbs
description: Whole sea bass grilled with lemon, olive oil, and Mediterranean herbs
instructions: Score each sea bass 3 times on each side with a sharp knife.
Rub fish inside and out with olive oil, salt, and pepper.
Stuff the cavity of each fish with lemon slices, garlic, rosemary, and thyme.
Grill on medium-high heat for 5-6 minutes per side until skin is crispy and flesh flakes easily.
Meanwhile, blister cherry tomatoes in a hot pan with a drizzle of olive oil.
Serve fish whole with blistered tomatoes, fresh parsley, and extra lemon wedges.
tips: Scoring helps the fish cook evenly and lets flavors penetrate.
Oil the grill grates generously to prevent skin from sticking.

name: Horiatiki (Greek Village Salad)
description: Classic Greek salad with chunky tomatoes, cucumber, olives, and a slab of feta
instructions: Cut tomatoes into large wedges — do not dice them small.
Peel and cut cucumber into thick half-moons.
Slice green pepper into rings and red onion into thin slices.
Arrange vegetables on a plate or shallow bowl.
Scatter Kalamata olives over the vegetables.
Place a thick slab of feta cheese on top — do not crumble it.
Drizzle generously with olive oil, sprinkle with oregano, add a splash of vinegar, and season with salt.
tips: A true Horiatiki has no lettuce — just chunky vegetables.
Place the feta as a whole slab on top — crumbling it is not traditional.

name: Karniyarik (Stuffed Eggplant with Meat)
description: Turkish split eggplant filled with spiced ground beef, tomatoes, and peppers
instructions: Peel eggplants in stripes (leaving some skin) and fry in oil until golden. Drain on paper towels.
Sauté diced onion until golden, add ground beef and cook until browned.
Add garlic, diced pepper, one chopped tomato, tomato paste, allspice, and salt. Cook 5 minutes.
Make a deep slit in each fried eggplant and open like a pocket.
Fill each eggplant with the meat mixture and place in a baking dish.
Slice remaining tomato and place a slice on top of each stuffed eggplant.
Add a little water to the dish, cover with foil, and bake at 180°C for 25 minutes.
tips: Frying the eggplant first gives it a silky texture inside.
The name means 'split belly' — the slit should be generous to hold plenty of filling.

name: Kibbeh (Bulgur and Lamb Croquettes)
description: Crispy fried bulgur and lamb shells stuffed with spiced meat, pine nuts, and onions
instructions: Soak bulgur in warm water for 15 minutes, then drain and squeeze dry.
For the shell: blend half the lamb with the soaked bulgur, grated onion, allspice, salt, and pepper until smooth and paste-like.
For the filling: sauté diced onion, remaining ground lamb, pine nuts, cinnamon, cumin, and salt until cooked through.
Take a handful of shell mixture, form into an egg shape, hollow it out, stuff with filling, and pinch closed into a torpedo shape.
Repeat until all mixture is used.
Deep fry in hot oil (180°C) for 4-5 minutes until deep golden brown and crispy.
Drain on paper towels and serve hot with yogurt and fresh mint.
tips: Keep your hands wet while shaping to prevent the mixture from sticking.
The shell mixture must be very smooth — process it well or the kibbeh will crack when frying.

name: Mediterranean Roasted Vegetable Platter
description: Assorted seasonal vegetables roasted with olive oil, garlic, and za'atar
instructions: Cut all vegetables into large, similar-sized pieces for even roasting.
Toss vegetables with olive oil, za'atar, whole garlic cloves, and salt.
Spread in a single layer on a large baking sheet — don't overcrowd.
Roast at 220°C for 30-35 minutes, stirring once halfway, until charred at the edges.
Squeeze lemon juice over the hot roasted vegetables.
Arrange on a platter, garnish with fresh parsley, and drizzle with extra olive oil.
Serve warm or at room temperature with hummus and pita.
tips: Don't overcrowd the pan — the vegetables need space to roast, not steam.
Za'atar gives a wonderful earthy, tangy crust to the vegetables.

name: Turkish Pide (Stuffed Flatbread)
description: Boat-shaped Turkish flatbread filled with spiced ground meat, peppers, and tomatoes
instructions: Mix flour, yeast, salt, and warm water to form a soft dough. Knead 8 minutes and let rise 45 minutes.
Mix ground beef with finely diced onion, pepper, tomato, tomato paste, cumin, Aleppo pepper, and salt.
Divide dough into 4 portions and roll each into an oval shape.
Spread meat filling down the center of each oval, leaving borders.
Fold and pinch the long edges up to form a boat shape with pointed ends.
Brush edges with egg yolk and bake at 230°C for 12-15 minutes until golden.
Brush hot pide with melted butter immediately after removing from the oven.
tips: The buttering at the end is traditional and makes the crust incredibly tender.
Pinch the ends tightly to maintain the boat shape during baking.

name: Saag-Style Mediterranean Greens with Feta
description: Slow-cooked mixed greens with garlic, olive oil, and tangy crumbled feta
instructions: Wash and roughly chop spinach and Swiss chard, removing tough stems.
Heat olive oil in a large pan and sauté sliced garlic until golden, about 1 minute.
Add the greens in batches, stirring as they wilt down.
Season with salt, chili flakes, and nutmeg. Cook on low for 15 minutes until very tender.
Squeeze in lemon juice and stir.
Transfer to a serving dish and crumble feta generously over the top.
Serve warm with crusty bread for scooping.
tips: Mixing different greens gives more complex flavor than spinach alone.
Cook the greens down well — they should be silky, not just wilted.

name: Grilled Chicken Shawarma Plate
description: Spiced grilled chicken shawarma served with garlic sauce, pickles, and warm flatbread
instructions: Marinate chicken thighs in yogurt, cumin, turmeric, paprika, cinnamon, minced garlic, lemon juice, and olive oil for at least 1 hour.
Grill chicken on medium-high heat for 6-7 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.
Let chicken rest for 5 minutes, then slice thinly against the grain.
Make garlic sauce: whisk tahini with lemon juice, minced garlic, and water until smooth.
Warm pita bread on the grill for 30 seconds per side.
Arrange sliced chicken on plates with warm pita, pickled turnips, and garlic tahini sauce.
tips: The yogurt marinade tenderizes the chicken and helps the spices adhere.
Bone-in, skin-on thighs give the most flavorful shawarma.

name: White Bean and Tomato Stew
description: Rustic one-pot stew of creamy white beans in a garlicky tomato broth with herbs
instructions: Sauté diced onion, carrot, and celery in olive oil until softened, about 5 minutes.
Add garlic and rosemary sprig; cook 1 minute until fragrant.
Add crushed tomatoes, vegetable broth, and drained beans.
Bring to a simmer and cook for 20 minutes until sauce thickens.
Mash a few beans against the side of the pot to thicken the stew.
Remove rosemary sprig, season with salt and pepper.
Serve in bowls with a generous drizzle of olive oil and fresh parsley.
tips: Mashing some of the beans creates a naturally creamy stew without any dairy.
A drizzle of your best olive oil at the end makes all the difference.

name: Olive Oil Poached Fish with Tomatoes
description: Delicate white fish gently poached in olive oil with cherry tomatoes, garlic, and capers
instructions: Heat olive oil in a wide, deep skillet over medium-low heat.
Add sliced garlic and cook gently until golden, about 1 minute.
Add halved cherry tomatoes, capers, and olives; cook until tomatoes begin to soften.
Pour in white wine and let it simmer for 2 minutes.
Season fish fillets with salt and pepper, then nestle them into the tomato mixture.
Spoon the olive oil and tomato mixture over the fish, cover, and poach on low for 8-10 minutes until fish is opaque and flakes easily.
Garnish with fresh basil and lemon zest, and serve with the poaching oil spooned over the fish.
tips: Low and slow is the key — high heat will toughen the fish.
The infused olive oil is part of the dish — serve it as a sauce.

name: Chicken Shawarma
desc: Marinated spiced chicken carved from a vertical spit, served with garlic sauce and pickles
steps: Combine yogurt, cumin, paprika, turmeric, cinnamon, minced garlic, lemon juice, and olive oil to make the marinade.
Coat chicken thighs in the marinade and refrigerate for at least 2 hours.
Preheat oven to 220C and arrange chicken on a baking tray.
Roast for 25-30 minutes until charred at the edges and cooked through.
Slice chicken thinly against the grain.
Serve in warm pita with garlic sauce, pickled turnips, and fresh vegetables.
tips: Marinating overnight gives the best flavor.
Broil for the last 3 minutes for extra char.

name: Lamb Mansaf
desc: Jordanian national dish of lamb cooked in fermented dried yogurt sauce served over rice
steps: Soak jameed in warm water for several hours, then blend until smooth.
Boil lamb shoulder with onion, cardamom, and turmeric until tender, about 90 minutes.
Cook basmati rice in seasoned lamb broth until fluffy.
Simmer the jameed sauce on low heat, stirring constantly to prevent curdling.
Add cooked lamb to the jameed sauce and warm through.
Layer rice on a platter, top with lamb and sauce, garnish with toasted pine nuts and almonds.
tips: Stir jameed sauce constantly on low heat to prevent splitting.
Traditional mansaf is eaten with the right hand from a communal platter.

name: Maklouba (Upside Down Rice)
desc: Palestinian layered rice dish flipped upside down to reveal fried vegetables and tender meat
steps: Slice eggplant and fry until golden on both sides, drain on paper towels.
Break cauliflower into florets and fry until golden, set aside.
Brown chicken pieces with onion, turmeric, allspice, and cinnamon.
Layer fried vegetables on the bottom of a heavy pot, place chicken on top.
Add washed rice as the top layer, pour in seasoned broth to cover by 1 cm.
Cook on low heat 25 minutes, then flip the pot onto a large platter in one motion.
tips: Let the pot rest 5 minutes before flipping for a clean release.
Serve with yogurt and a fresh cucumber-tomato salad.

name: Fatteh (Chickpea and Yogurt Dish)
desc: Levantine layered dish of toasted pita, warm chickpeas, and garlicky yogurt sauce with pine nuts
steps: Toast or fry torn pita pieces until golden and crispy.
Warm chickpeas in their liquid with a pinch of cumin.
Mix yogurt with tahini, crushed garlic, lemon juice, and salt.
Layer crispy pita on a serving dish, spoon warm chickpeas over the bread.
Pour the yogurt-tahini sauce generously over the chickpeas.
Toast pine nuts in olive oil with paprika and drizzle over the top.
tips: Assemble just before serving so the pita stays slightly crispy.
The contrast of warm chickpeas and cool yogurt is key.

name: Kibbeh Bil Sanieh (Baked Kibbeh)
desc: Lebanese baked kibbeh with layers of spiced ground meat, bulgur, and toasted pine nuts
steps: Soak bulgur in water for 30 minutes, then squeeze dry.
Mix half the lamb with soaked bulgur, onion, allspice, and salt to form the kibbeh shell dough.
Saute remaining lamb with diced onion, pine nuts, cinnamon, and pepper for the filling.
Press half the shell mixture into an oiled baking dish as the bottom layer.
Spread the cooked meat filling evenly, then top with remaining shell mixture.
Score into diamond shapes, drizzle with olive oil, and bake at 180C for 35-40 minutes.
tips: Keep hands wet when pressing the kibbeh layers to prevent sticking.
Score deeply before baking for clean serving pieces.

name: Maqluba with Lamb
desc: Upside-down rice casserole with tender lamb, fried eggplant, and aromatic spices
steps: Boil lamb shoulder with spices and onion for 60-75 minutes until very tender, reserve broth.
Slice eggplant thickly and fry in oil until golden brown on both sides.
Layer fried eggplant in the bottom of a heavy pot.
Place tender lamb pieces over the eggplant.
Add washed rice on top and pour in 600ml of strained lamb broth.
Cook covered on low heat 25 minutes, rest 5 minutes, then invert onto a platter.
tips: The lamb broth is what makes the rice incredibly flavorful.
Press down gently on the pot when flipping for a clean release.

name: Bamia (Okra Stew)
desc: Middle Eastern stew of tender okra and lamb in a tomato-based sauce with warm spices
steps: Brown lamb pieces in olive oil, then remove and set aside.
Saute onion and garlic until golden, add tomato paste and cook 1 minute.
Add canned tomatoes, coriander, allspice, and return lamb to the pot.
Simmer covered for 40 minutes until lamb is nearly tender.
Add trimmed okra on top, do not stir, cover and cook 20 more minutes.
Finish with lemon juice and serve over white rice or with bread.
tips: Do not stir after adding okra to prevent sliminess.
Trim okra stems carefully without piercing the pod.

name: Shish Tawook (Grilled Chicken)
desc: Lebanese marinated chicken skewers with yogurt, garlic, and lemon, grilled until charred
steps: Cut chicken into large cubes and place in a bowl.
Mix yogurt, crushed garlic, lemon juice, tomato paste, paprika, oregano, and olive oil.
Coat chicken thoroughly in the marinade and refrigerate at least 1 hour.
Thread marinated chicken onto metal skewers.
Grill over medium-high heat for 4-5 minutes per side until charred and cooked through.
Serve with garlic sauce (toum), pickles, and warm pita.
tips: Do not skip the tomato paste — it helps the char develop.
Wooden skewers must be soaked in water for 30 minutes before grilling.

name: Stuffed Zucchini (Kousa Mahshi)
desc: Hollowed zucchini stuffed with spiced rice and ground meat, braised in tomato broth
steps: Core the zucchini using a vegetable corer, leaving a thin shell.
Mix ground beef with rice, diced onion, allspice, cinnamon, mint, and salt.
Stuff each zucchini loosely with the meat-rice mixture, leaving room for rice to expand.
Arrange stuffed zucchini snugly in a pot, seam-side up.
Pour crushed tomatoes mixed with water over the zucchini to just cover.
Simmer covered on low heat for 60-70 minutes until rice is tender and zucchini is soft.
tips: Do not overstuff — rice expands and can burst the zucchini.
Choose straight, uniform zucchini for easier coring.

name: Lebanese Kafta with Potatoes
desc: Spiced ground meat patties baked with sliced potatoes and tomatoes in a tahini or tomato sauce
steps: Mix ground beef with finely chopped onion, parsley, allspice, cinnamon, and salt.
Shape meat into flat patties or press into a baking dish as a flat layer.
Slice potatoes and tomatoes into rounds.
Layer potato slices over the meat, then tomato slices on top.
Mix tomato paste with water and pour over the layers, drizzle with olive oil.
Bake at 200C for 45 minutes until potatoes are tender and edges are golden.
tips: Slice potatoes thinly so they cook through evenly.
Some versions add a tahini sauce instead of tomato — both are traditional.

name: Persian Zereshk Polo (Barberry Rice with Chicken)
desc: Saffron-infused basmati rice studded with tart barberries, served with braised chicken
steps: Soak rice in salted water for at least 1 hour, then parboil until just al dente and drain.
Braise chicken with onion, turmeric, and salt until cooked through, about 35 minutes.
Bloom saffron in 2 tbsp hot water.
Rinse barberries, saute briefly in butter with sugar until plump.
Layer parboiled rice in a pot with saffron water drizzled between layers, cook on low for tahdig.
Serve rice topped with saffron barberries and braised chicken.
tips: Do not overcook barberries — they burn quickly due to their sugar content.
The crispy tahdig bottom is the most prized part of the dish.

name: Iraqi Dolma
desc: Iraqi-style grape leaves, onions, and vegetables stuffed with herbed rice and lamb
steps: Mix rice, ground lamb, chopped parsley, dill, tomato paste, and seasoning for the stuffing.
Blanch grape leaves briefly, then place a spoonful of filling on each leaf and roll tightly.
Hollow out onions and stuff with the same mixture.
Slice tomatoes and layer on the bottom of a heavy pot.
Arrange stuffed grape leaves and onions tightly in the pot.
Add water with lemon juice and olive oil, place a plate on top to weigh down, and simmer 90 minutes.
tips: Pack the rolls very tightly in the pot so they hold their shape.
The lemon juice brightens the rich, meaty filling.

name: Ful Medames (Egyptian Fava Beans)
desc: Slow-cooked fava beans mashed with garlic, lemon, cumin, and olive oil — Egypt's beloved staple
steps: Warm fava beans in a pot with some of their liquid over medium heat.
Mash about half the beans with a fork, leaving some whole for texture.
Stir in crushed garlic, cumin, lemon juice, and salt.
Drizzle generously with olive oil and tahini.
Top with diced tomatoes and chopped parsley.
Serve warm with Egyptian baladi bread for scooping.
tips: The beans should be creamy but not a smooth paste.
Ful tastes better the next day as flavors meld overnight.

name: Chicken Freekeh Soup
desc: Hearty Levantine soup of smoky roasted green wheat and tender chicken in aromatic broth
steps: Rinse freekeh well and soak for 15 minutes, then drain.
Brown chicken pieces in olive oil, add onion and garlic, cook until softened.
Add water, cinnamon stick, allspice, cardamom, and bring to a boil.
Simmer chicken for 25 minutes, then remove and shred the meat.
Add freekeh to the broth and cook 20-25 minutes until tender.
Return shredded chicken to the soup, adjust seasoning and serve hot.
tips: Freekeh has a distinct smoky flavor that deepens with slow cooking.
This soup is traditionally served during Ramadan.

name: Lamb and Spinach Stew
desc: Persian-inspired stew of tender lamb simmered with spinach, dried lime, and warm spices
steps: Brown lamb pieces in olive oil in a heavy pot, then remove.
Saute onions and garlic until golden, add turmeric, cumin, and tomato paste.
Return lamb to the pot, add water and dried limes, bring to a simmer.
Cook covered for 60 minutes until lamb is very tender.
Add chopped spinach and cook 10 more minutes until wilted.
Serve over steamed basmati rice.
tips: Dried limes add a unique tangy depth that cannot be substituted.
The stew improves greatly when reheated the next day.

name: Persian Ghormeh Sabzi (Herb Stew)
desc: Iran's national dish of slow-cooked herbs, kidney beans, dried lime, and lamb in a deeply savory stew
steps: Finely chop all fresh herbs and fry in oil on medium-high until dark green, about 15 minutes.
Brown lamb pieces separately, then saute onion with turmeric.
Combine fried herbs, lamb, and onion in a pot with enough water to cover.
Add pierced dried limes and soaked kidney beans.
Simmer on very low heat for at least 90 minutes until everything is deeply melded.
Serve over steamed basmati rice with tahdig if desired.
tips: The herbs must be fried until very dark — this is what creates the signature flavor.
Dried fenugreek leaves are essential and cannot be substituted.

name: Fatayer (Spinach Pies)
desc: Lebanese triangle-shaped hand pies filled with spinach, onion, lemon, and sumac
steps: Make dough by mixing flour, yeast, sugar, salt, oil, and warm water; knead and let rise 45 minutes.
Chop spinach finely, mix with diced onion, lemon juice, sumac, olive oil, and salt.
Squeeze out excess liquid from the spinach filling.
Divide dough into small balls, roll each into a circle, place filling in the center.
Fold into triangles, pinching edges tightly closed.
Bake at 200C for 15-18 minutes until golden brown.
tips: Squeeze the spinach very dry to prevent soggy dough.
The sumac and lemon combination gives the characteristic tangy filling.

name: Chicken Muhammar (Sweet Rice)
desc: Gulf-style sweet spiced rice with dates, rose water, and roasted chicken
steps: Season chicken with salt, turmeric, and cardamom, then roast at 200C for 35 minutes.
Parboil rice until just al dente, drain well.
Melt date paste with ghee and sugar in a large pot.
Add rice to the date mixture, stir gently to coat.
Drizzle saffron water and rose water over the rice, cover and steam on low for 20 minutes.
Serve the sweet rice topped with roasted chicken pieces.
tips: Use soft Medjool dates for the richest paste.
The rice should be sticky-sweet, contrasting with the savory chicken.

name: Sayyadieh (Lebanese Fish and Rice)
desc: Lebanese fisherman's dish of spiced rice cooked in fish stock with caramelized onions and cumin
steps: Deeply caramelize sliced onions in oil until very dark brown, reserve half for garnish.
Add cumin and turmeric to remaining onions, stir in flour, then add water to make fish stock.
Simmer the onion broth for 15 minutes, then strain.
Cook rice in the strained onion broth until fluffy and golden.
Season fish fillets and pan-fry until golden on both sides.
Serve rice topped with fried fish, caramelized onions, and toasted pine nuts.
tips: The onions must be very dark for the stock to have authentic deep flavor and color.
Tahini sauce on the side is the traditional accompaniment.

name: Fasolia (White Bean Stew)
desc: Comforting Middle Eastern white bean stew in a rich tomato sauce with warm spices
steps: Saute diced onion and garlic in olive oil until softened.
Add tomato paste and cook for 1 minute until darkened.
Add canned tomatoes, allspice, cinnamon, salt, and pepper.
Simmer the sauce for 10 minutes until thickened.
Add drained white beans and enough water to create a stew consistency.
Cook for 20 minutes until flavors meld, serve over rice or with bread.
tips: This stew can also be made with lamb chunks for a heartier version.
The cinnamon-allspice combination is signature to Levantine cooking.

name: Persian Tahdig (Crispy Rice)
desc: Saffron-scented basmati rice with a prized golden crispy crust on the bottom
steps: Soak rice in salted water for at least 1 hour.
Parboil rice in a large pot of boiling water until just al dente, about 5 minutes, then drain.
Mix a cup of parboiled rice with yogurt, saffron water, and a pinch of turmeric.
Heat butter and oil in a non-stick pot, spread the yogurt-rice mixture on the bottom.
Pile remaining rice on top in a mound, poke several holes with a spoon handle.
Cover with a wrapped lid, cook on medium 5 minutes, then very low for 40 minutes.
tips: Wrapping the lid in a towel absorbs steam and makes the crust crispier.
Do not peek — lifting the lid releases precious steam.

name: Harira (Moroccan Lentil Soup)
desc: Morocco's iconic Ramadan soup with lentils, chickpeas, tomatoes, and warming spices thickened with flour
steps: Saute diced onion and celery in olive oil until softened.
Add ginger, turmeric, and tomatoes, cook 5 minutes.
Add lentils and enough water to cover generously, simmer 25 minutes.
Add chickpeas and simmer another 10 minutes.
Mix flour with a little water to form a paste, stir into the soup to thicken.
Finish with chopped cilantro, adjust seasoning, and serve with lemon wedges and dates.
tips: Harira is traditionally the soup that breaks the fast during Ramadan.
The flour thickener gives it a distinctive silky body.

name: Macarona Bechamel (Egyptian Baked Pasta)
desc: Egypt's beloved baked penne with spiced meat sauce and creamy bechamel topping
steps: Cook penne until al dente and drain.
Brown ground beef with diced onion, add tomato paste, allspice, and salt.
Make bechamel by melting butter, stirring in flour, gradually adding milk and nutmeg.
Layer half the pasta in a greased baking dish, spread meat sauce over it.
Add remaining pasta on top, pour bechamel evenly over everything.
Bake at 190C for 30 minutes until top is golden and bubbly.
tips: Let the dish rest 10 minutes after baking for cleaner slices.
Some families add a beaten egg to the bechamel for extra richness.

name: Kofta Bil Tahina (Meatballs in Tahini)
desc: Egyptian baked meatballs in a creamy tahini sauce with potatoes and tomatoes
steps: Mix ground beef with grated onion, chopped parsley, cumin, and salt, shape into small patties.
Slice potatoes and tomatoes into rounds.
Layer potato slices in a baking dish, place meatball patties on top, then tomato slices.
Blend tahini with lemon juice, garlic, and water to make a pourable sauce.
Pour tahini sauce over the entire dish.
Bake at 200C for 40 minutes until potatoes are tender and top is golden.
tips: The tahini sauce should be thin enough to pour — add water gradually.
Lemon juice prevents the tahini from becoming bitter during baking.

name: Freekeh Pilaf with Lamb
desc: Smoky roasted green wheat pilaf with tender lamb, toasted almonds, and aromatic spices
steps: Brown lamb pieces in ghee, then add diced onion and garlic until softened.
Add cinnamon, allspice, salt, pepper, and enough water to cover the lamb.
Simmer for 35 minutes until lamb is nearly tender.
Add rinsed freekeh and extra water as needed, cover and cook 20 minutes.
Fluff with a fork and let rest 5 minutes off heat.
Serve topped with toasted almonds and a side of yogurt.
tips: Freekeh has a distinct nutty, smoky flavor that pairs beautifully with lamb.
Rinse freekeh well to remove any small stones.

name: Yemeni Saltah
desc: Yemen's national dish — a bubbling stew of meat, fenugreek froth, and vegetables served in a stone bowl
steps: Boil meat with onion, garlic, and turmeric until tender, about 50 minutes.
Add cubed potatoes and chopped tomatoes to the broth, cook 15 minutes.
Whip fenugreek powder with water until a thick, foamy froth forms (hulba).
Bring the stew to a vigorous simmer in a stone or heavy pot.
Top with a generous layer of fenugreek froth and a dollop of zhug chili paste.
Serve immediately while still bubbling, with flatbread for scooping.
tips: The fenugreek froth (hulba) must be whipped until very light and foamy.
Saltah is traditionally served in the same stone bowl it's cooked in.

name: Chicken Bastilla (Moroccan Pie)
desc: Moroccan savory-sweet pie with shredded chicken, spiced almonds, and crispy warqa pastry dusted with cinnamon
steps: Simmer chicken with onion, saffron, ginger, and cinnamon until very tender, about 40 minutes.
Shred chicken finely, reduce cooking liquid, then scramble eggs into the reduced sauce.
Toast almonds and mix with cinnamon and powdered sugar.
Layer buttered phyllo sheets in a round pan, overlapping the edges.
Layer the chicken-egg filling, then the spiced almonds, fold pastry edges over.
Top with more buttered phyllo, tuck edges under, and bake at 190C for 25 minutes until golden.
tips: The sweet-savory contrast of cinnamon sugar and chicken is what makes bastilla special.
Dust the top with powdered sugar and cinnamon in a crosshatch pattern.

name: Adas Bil Hamod (Lentil Lemon Soup)
desc: Tangy Lebanese lentil soup brightened with lemon juice, Swiss chard, and garlic
steps: Cook lentils with cubed potatoes in water until both are tender, about 20 minutes.
Chop Swiss chard stems and leaves separately.
In a small pan, fry garlic and chard stems in olive oil, add flour and stir.
Add the chard-garlic mixture and chard leaves to the lentil pot.
Pour in lemon juice and stir well, simmer 5 more minutes.
Serve hot with a drizzle of olive oil and extra lemon if desired.
tips: The lemon must be generous — this soup should taste distinctly tangy.
Some versions add cilantro instead of or alongside Swiss chard.

name: Shakshouka with Merguez
desc: North African poached eggs in spiced tomato sauce with sliced merguez sausages
steps: Slice merguez sausages and brown in olive oil until cooked through, then set aside.
Saute diced onion and bell pepper until softened, add garlic, cumin, and paprika.
Add canned tomatoes, crush them, and simmer until sauce thickens, about 12 minutes.
Nestle merguez slices into the sauce.
Make wells in the sauce and crack eggs into them.
Cover and cook on low until egg whites are set but yolks are still runny, about 5 minutes.
tips: Merguez adds a spicy lamb flavor that elevates basic shakshouka.
Serve with crusty bread to mop up the sauce and egg yolk.

name: Persian Joojeh Kabab (Saffron Chicken)
desc: Iranian saffron and lemon marinated chicken kababs grilled over charcoal
steps: Bloom saffron in 2 tbsp hot water.
Cut chicken into large pieces and marinate in saffron water, lemon juice, grated onion, yogurt, and olive oil.
Refrigerate for at least 2 hours, ideally overnight.
Thread chicken onto flat metal skewers.
Grill over high heat for 5-6 minutes per side, basting with melted butter.
Serve over saffron rice with grilled tomatoes and sumac-dusted onions.
tips: Flat skewers prevent the chicken from spinning when you turn them.
Basting with butter during grilling creates the signature golden glaze.

name: Kabab Hindi (Iraqi Potato Kabab)
desc: Iraqi crispy potato patties stuffed with spiced ground meat, onions, and parsley
steps: Boil and mash potatoes with turmeric and salt until smooth.
Cook ground beef with diced onion, allspice, pepper, and parsley.
Take a portion of potato mash, flatten in your hand, place filling in the center.
Seal the potato around the filling to form an oval patty.
Dust with flour and shallow fry in hot oil until golden on both sides.
Drain on paper towels and serve hot with pickled vegetables.
tips: Make sure the potato dough is firm enough to hold its shape.
Refrigerating formed patties for 15 minutes before frying helps prevent cracking.

name: Ouzi (Stuffed Pastry with Lamb Rice)
desc: Festive pastry parcels filled with spiced lamb rice, peas, and toasted nuts
steps: Cook rice in seasoned broth until fluffy.
Brown ground lamb with cinnamon, allspice, and salt.
Mix cooked rice with lamb, peas, and toasted nuts.
Cut phyllo into squares, brush each with melted butter.
Place a portion of filling in the center, gather edges up and twist to seal.
Bake at 190C for 20-25 minutes until pastry is golden and crispy.
tips: Keep phyllo sheets covered with a damp towel while working to prevent drying.
Ouzi is traditionally served at celebrations and festive gatherings.

name: Arabian Mixed Grill
desc: A platter of grilled lamb kofta, chicken shish, and lamb chops with Middle Eastern spices
steps: Mix ground lamb with grated onion, parsley, cumin, and salt, shape into kofta around skewers.
Cube chicken breast and marinate with garlic, paprika, olive oil, and lemon juice.
Season lamb chops with salt, pepper, and olive oil.
Grill kofta skewers over high heat for 3-4 minutes per side.
Grill chicken skewers for 5 minutes per side until charred.
Grill lamb chops 3 minutes per side for medium, serve all together with hummus and flatbread.
tips: Varied grill times ensure each meat is perfectly cooked without drying out.
A mixed grill platter is best with complementary dips: hummus, baba ganoush, and toum.

name: Molokhia (Jute Leaf Stew)
desc: Egyptian stew of finely chopped jute leaves in garlicky broth, served over rice with chicken
steps: Boil chicken pieces with onion until tender, reserve the broth.
Thaw molokhia leaves and chop very finely if not already minced.
Bring chicken broth to a boil, add molokhia while stirring constantly.
Make the taqliya by frying crushed garlic and ground coriander in ghee until golden and fragrant.
Pour the sizzling taqliya into the molokhia pot — it should sizzle dramatically.
Serve the molokhia stew over white rice with chicken pieces alongside, squeeze lemon on top.
tips: Never cover the pot after adding molokhia or it will turn dark and lose its green color.
The garlic taqliya is the soul of this dish — do not skip it.

name: Ratatouille Provençale
desc: Provençal summer vegetable stew of eggplant, zucchini, peppers, and tomatoes with herbs de Provence
steps: Dice all vegetables into similar-sized pieces.
Saute onion and garlic in olive oil until softened.
Add bell peppers, cook 5 minutes, then add eggplant, cook 5 more minutes.
Add zucchini and diced tomatoes with herbes de Provence.
Simmer uncovered on low heat for 30-35 minutes until all vegetables are tender and flavors meld.
Finish with torn basil and a drizzle of olive oil, serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Each vegetable can be sauteed separately for better texture in the refined version.
Ratatouille improves significantly after resting overnight.

name: Cassoulet
desc: Rich slow-baked casserole from Languedoc with white beans, duck confit, sausages, and pork
steps: Soak beans overnight, then simmer with onion, garlic, and thyme until almost tender.
Brown pork belly cubes and Toulouse sausages, set aside.
Layer beans in a deep casserole, nestle in the meats and duck confit legs.
Pour bean cooking liquid over to just cover.
Sprinkle breadcrumbs over the top.
Bake at 150C for 2-3 hours, breaking the breadcrumb crust and reforming it 2-3 times during cooking.
tips: Breaking and reforming the crust multiple times is the traditional technique that creates layers of flavor.
Each city in Languedoc argues their version is the authentic one.

name: Bouillabaisse
desc: Marseille's legendary saffron-scented fish stew with mixed seafood and rouille
steps: Saute onion, fennel, and garlic in olive oil until softened.
Add chopped tomatoes, saffron, orange peel, and enough water to cover generously.
Simmer the broth for 20 minutes to develop flavors.
Add firm fish pieces first, cook 5 minutes, then add shrimp.
Add Pernod and cook 3 more minutes until all seafood is just done.
Serve in wide bowls with the broth, accompanied by toasted bread and rouille.
tips: Use at least 3 different varieties of firm-fleshed fish for authenticity.
Rouille — a saffron-garlic mayonnaise — is spread on croutons and floated on the broth.

name: Duck Confit
desc: Duck legs slow-cooked in their own fat until meltingly tender, then crisped under high heat
steps: Rub duck legs generously with coarse salt, garlic, thyme, and peppercorns, refrigerate overnight.
Rinse off the cure and pat legs completely dry.
Submerge duck legs in melted duck fat in a deep oven-safe pot.
Cook in a 140C oven for 2.5-3 hours until the meat is very tender and almost falling off the bone.
To serve, remove from fat and crisp skin-side down in a hot skillet or under the broiler.
Serve with roasted potatoes cooked in the duck fat.
tips: The salt cure overnight is essential for proper confit texture and flavor.
Strained duck fat can be reused for the next confit or for roasting potatoes.

name: Blanquette de Veau
desc: Classic French creamy white veal stew with mushrooms and pearl onions in a velouté sauce
steps: Cut veal into large cubes, blanch briefly in boiling water, then drain and rinse.
Place veal in a pot with carrots, bouquet garni, and cold water to cover, simmer gently 90 minutes.
Cook pearl onions and mushrooms in butter separately, set aside.
Strain the veal broth, make a velouté by whisking butter and flour roux with the broth.
Mix egg yolks with cream and lemon juice, temper with hot sauce, then stir back in (do not boil).
Combine veal, onions, mushrooms, and sauce, serve over steamed rice.
tips: Never boil after adding the egg yolk liaison or it will curdle.
The veal must be blanched first to keep the sauce white.

name: French Onion Soup
desc: Deeply caramelized onion soup topped with a crusty bread crouton and melted Gruyère
steps: Slice onions thinly and cook slowly in butter over low heat for 40-45 minutes until deeply caramelized.
Add a pinch of sugar to help caramelization in the final minutes.
Deglaze with white wine and cook until evaporated.
Add beef broth, thyme, and bay leaf, simmer 20 minutes.
Ladle soup into oven-safe crocks, place a baguette slice on top, pile with Gruyère.
Broil until cheese is bubbly and golden brown.
tips: Patience with the onions is everything — rushing this step ruins the soup.
The onions should be a deep mahogany color before adding broth.

name: Salade Niçoise
desc: Composed salad from Nice with tuna, hard-boiled eggs, olives, green beans, and anchovies
steps: Boil potatoes until tender and eggs until hard-boiled, let cool, then slice.
Blanch green beans in salted water for 3 minutes, then shock in ice water.
Arrange all ingredients beautifully on a large platter rather than tossing.
Whisk olive oil with red wine vinegar and salt for the dressing.
Drizzle dressing over the composed salad.
Serve immediately as a main course lunch.
tips: A true Niçoise never uses lettuce — it is a Provençal tradition.
Arrange ingredients in separate sections for visual impact.

name: Pot-au-Feu
desc: The quintessential French boiled dinner of beef, marrow bones, and root vegetables in clear broth
steps: Place beef and marrow bones in a large pot, cover with cold water and bring slowly to a boil.
Skim all foam thoroughly — this is what keeps the broth clear.
Add bouquet garni, onion studded with cloves, and peppercorns.
Simmer very gently for 2 hours, never allowing a full boil.
Add carrots, turnips, leeks, and celery for the last 45 minutes.
Serve the broth first as a soup course, then the meat and vegetables with mustard and coarse salt.
tips: The broth must never boil vigorously or it will become cloudy.
Pot-au-feu is traditionally served in two courses: broth first, then meat.

name: Chicken Fricassée
desc: Gentle French braised chicken in a creamy white wine and mushroom sauce
steps: Lightly brown chicken pieces in butter without developing deep color.
Remove chicken, saute onion, carrots, and mushrooms in the same pan.
Sprinkle flour over the vegetables, stir, and cook 1 minute.
Add white wine and enough water to cover, return chicken to the pot.
Simmer gently for 35 minutes until chicken is cooked through.
Stir in cream, adjust seasoning, and serve over rice or with bread.
tips: Unlike coq au vin, fricassée should remain pale — do not deeply brown the chicken.
The sauce should be silky and coating, not thick like a gravy.

name: Gratin Dauphinois
desc: Thinly sliced potatoes baked in cream and garlic until golden and bubbling from the Dauphiné region
steps: Peel and slice potatoes very thinly using a mandoline.
Rub a baking dish with garlic and butter generously.
Layer potato slices overlapping in the dish, seasoning each layer with salt and nutmeg.
Heat cream and milk with crushed garlic until just simmering, then pour over potatoes.
Dot the top with butter.
Bake at 160C for 75 minutes until potatoes are tender and top is deeply golden.
tips: Do not rinse the potatoes after slicing — the starch helps thicken the cream.
True gratin dauphinois has no cheese — the cream does all the work.

name: Tarte Flambée (Flammkuchen)
desc: Paper-thin Alsatian flatbread topped with crème fraîche, thinly sliced onions, and lardons
steps: Make dough by mixing flour, water, oil, and salt; rest 15 minutes.
Roll dough paper-thin into a rectangle on a floured surface.
Mix crème fraîche with fromage blanc and nutmeg, spread evenly over the dough.
Scatter thinly sliced onion rings and lardons over the cream.
Transfer to a very hot oven at 250C on a preheated baking sheet.
Bake 8-10 minutes until edges are charred and base is crispy.
tips: The dough must be rolled as thin as possible — almost translucent.
Maximum oven heat is essential for the charred edges that define this dish.

name: Soupe au Pistou
desc: Provençal vegetable and bean soup finished with a vibrant basil-garlic pistou stirred in at the table
steps: Cook soaked white beans in water until almost tender, about 30 minutes.
Add diced potatoes, zucchini, green beans, tomatoes, and pasta to the bean pot.
Simmer until all vegetables and pasta are cooked, about 15 minutes.
Make the pistou by pounding basil, garlic, Parmesan, and olive oil in a mortar.
Ladle soup into bowls.
Stir a generous spoonful of pistou into each bowl and serve immediately.
tips: The pistou must be added at the table, not cooked, to preserve its fresh flavor.
This is Provence's answer to Italian minestrone — the pistou is similar to pesto.

name: Steak Frites
desc: Pan-seared bavette or entrecôte steak with crispy twice-fried French fries and herb butter
steps: Cut potatoes into batons, soak in cold water 30 minutes, then dry thoroughly.
First fry at 140C for 6-7 minutes until cooked but pale, drain and rest.
Season steaks generously with salt and pepper.
Sear steaks in a very hot pan with a little oil, 3-4 minutes per side for medium-rare.
Rest steaks 5 minutes, then top with garlic-parsley butter.
Second fry potatoes at 190C until golden and crispy, salt immediately and serve alongside.
tips: The two-stage frying method is what gives true French frites their crispy exterior and fluffy interior.
Always rest the steak — cutting too soon loses all the juices.

name: Poulet Rôti (Roast Chicken)
desc: Simple French roast chicken with crispy golden skin, roasted with butter and herbs
steps: Pat chicken dry and season generously inside and out with salt and pepper.
Stuff the cavity with halved lemon, garlic, and thyme.
Rub the outside with softened butter and olive oil.
Place breast-side up in a roasting pan.
Roast at 220C for 60-70 minutes, basting twice with pan juices.
Rest for 15 minutes before carving, make a jus from the pan drippings.
tips: Drying the chicken thoroughly and starting with a hot oven are the secrets to crispy skin.
Let the chicken come to room temperature before roasting for even cooking.

name: Navarin d'Agneau (Lamb Stew)
desc: French spring lamb stew with turnips, carrots, potatoes, and fresh peas in a light sauce
steps: Cut lamb into large cubes and brown well in batches.
Add diced onion and garlic, cook until softened.
Sprinkle flour over the meat, stir, then add tomato paste.
Add enough water to cover, plus bouquet garni, and simmer 60 minutes.
Add carrots, turnips, and potatoes, cook 30 more minutes.
Add peas in the last 5 minutes, adjust seasoning and serve.
tips: Navarin is traditionally made in spring with the freshest young vegetables.
The sauce should be light and brothy, not thick like a British stew.

name: Pissaladière (Onion Tart)
desc: Niçoise flatbread topped with slow-cooked sweet onions, anchovies, and black olives
steps: Cook sliced onions very slowly in olive oil with thyme for 40-45 minutes until meltingly soft and sweet.
Roll out bread dough into a rectangle and place on an oiled baking sheet.
Spread the cooked onions evenly over the dough.
Arrange anchovies in a lattice pattern across the top.
Place olives at the intersections of the anchovy lattice.
Bake at 220C for 15-18 minutes until the dough is golden and crispy.
tips: The onions must cook until completely soft and jammy — they should not have any bite.
Pissaladière is best eaten warm or at room temperature, never hot from the oven.

name: Hachis Parmentier (Shepherd's Pie)
desc: French shepherd's pie with seasoned ground beef topped with creamy mashed potatoes and browned under the broiler
steps: Boil potatoes until tender, mash with butter, milk, nutmeg, and salt.
Brown ground beef with diced onion and garlic.
Add tomato paste and thyme, cook 5 more minutes.
Spread the meat mixture in a baking dish.
Pipe or spread mashed potatoes over the meat, score the top with a fork.
Broil for 8-10 minutes until potato top is golden and crusty.
tips: Named after Antoine-Augustin Parmentier who popularized potatoes in France.
Running a fork across the mashed potato top creates ridges that brown beautifully.

name: Salmon en Croûte
desc: Salmon fillet wrapped in puff pastry with a layer of spinach and cream cheese
steps: Wilt spinach, squeeze dry, mix with cream cheese, lemon zest, and dill.
Roll out puff pastry into a rectangle large enough to wrap the salmon.
Spread spinach mixture in the center, place seasoned salmon on top.
Brush mustard on the salmon, fold pastry over and seal edges tightly.
Brush with beaten egg, score the top decoratively.
Bake at 200C for 25-30 minutes until pastry is golden and puffed.
tips: Squeeze the spinach very dry to prevent a soggy pastry bottom.
Let the wrapped salmon rest in the fridge 15 minutes before baking for crisper pastry.

name: Chicken Provençal
desc: Sun-drenched braised chicken with tomatoes, olives, capers, and herbes de Provence
steps: Season and brown chicken pieces in olive oil on all sides.
Remove chicken, saute onion and garlic until softened.
Add white wine and reduce by half.
Add tomatoes, olives, capers, and herbes de Provence.
Return chicken to the pot, cover and simmer 35 minutes.
Serve with crusty bread or over couscous to soak up the sauce.
tips: Use a mix of green and black olives for visual and flavor variety.
This dish captures the essence of Provençal cooking: tomatoes, olive oil, garlic, herbs.

name: Lentilles du Puy with Sausage
desc: Earthy French green lentils from Le Puy braised with Toulouse sausages and aromatics
steps: Brown sausages in olive oil on all sides, then set aside.
Saute diced onion, carrots, and garlic until softened.
Add lentils, thyme, bay leaf, and enough water to cover by 3 cm.
Simmer for 25 minutes until lentils are tender but not mushy.
Slice sausages and nestle into the lentils for the last 10 minutes.
Finish with Dijon mustard and red wine vinegar, stir gently and serve.
tips: Puy lentils hold their shape beautifully unlike other varieties.
The mustard-vinegar finish brightens the earthy lentils perfectly.

name: Endive au Jambon (Ham-Wrapped Endive)
desc: Belgian endives wrapped in ham, smothered in Mornay sauce, and baked until golden
steps: Braise endives in butter with lemon juice and a splash of water, covered, for 20 minutes until tender.
Make Mornay sauce: melt butter, add flour, gradually whisk in milk, add half the Gruyère and nutmeg.
Wrap each braised endive in a slice of ham.
Arrange wrapped endives in a baking dish.
Pour Mornay sauce over and sprinkle with remaining Gruyère.
Bake at 200C for 20 minutes until bubbly and golden on top.
tips: Braising the endives first removes some bitterness.
The lemon juice while braising helps keep the endives from browning.

name: Tartiflette
desc: Savoyard potato gratin with Reblochon cheese, lardons, and onions from the French Alps
steps: Parboil potatoes until just tender, then slice thickly.
Cook lardons until crispy, add sliced onions and cook until softened.
Deglaze with white wine and reduce slightly.
Layer potatoes and lardon-onion mixture in a baking dish.
Dollop crème fraîche between the layers.
Cut Reblochon in half horizontally and place cut-side down on top, bake at 200C for 25 minutes.
tips: Reblochon must go cut-side down so it melts into the potatoes.
If Reblochon is unavailable, Brie or Camembert can substitute but the flavor will differ.

name: Boeuf aux Carottes (Beef with Carrots)
desc: Homestyle French braised beef with sweet carrots in a rich wine-enriched sauce
steps: Cut beef into large chunks, season, dust with flour, and brown in olive oil.
Add diced onions and garlic, cook until softened.
Add tomato paste and cook 1 minute.
Pour in red wine, scrape up the fond, add water to cover and bouquet garni.
Simmer covered for 90 minutes, then add large carrot chunks and cook 45 more minutes.
The carrots should be meltingly sweet and the beef fork-tender.
tips: Use large carrot pieces — they become the star alongside the beef.
This is a grandmother's dish that rewards patience above all else.

name: Moules Marinières (Mussels)
desc: Classic French mussels steamed in white wine with shallots, garlic, and parsley
steps: Clean and debeard mussels, discarding any that do not close when tapped.
Saute diced shallots and garlic in butter until softened.
Pour in white wine and bring to a vigorous boil.
Add mussels, cover tightly, and cook 3-4 minutes until all shells open.
Remove mussels to serving bowls, discard any that remain closed.
Stir cream and parsley into the broth, pour over the mussels and serve with frites.
tips: Never overcook mussels — they become rubbery within seconds of opening.
Serve with crusty bread or frites to soak up the broth.

name: Pan-Seared Duck Breast
desc: Magret de canard with crispy skin scored in a crosshatch, served pink with a fruit sauce
steps: Score the duck skin in a tight crosshatch pattern, being careful not to cut into the meat.
Season generously with salt and pepper.
Place duck skin-side down in a cold pan, then turn heat to medium and render for 12-15 minutes.
Flip and cook flesh-side for 3-4 minutes for medium-rare.
Rest for 8 minutes, then slice on the bias.
Make a sauce in the same pan with orange juice, vinegar, honey, and butter.
tips: Starting in a cold pan and rendering slowly is the secret to perfectly crispy skin.
Duck breast should be served pink — well-done duck is tough and dry.

name: Raclette
desc: Melted raclette cheese scraped over boiled potatoes with charcuterie, cornichons, and pickled onions
steps: Boil potatoes in their skins until tender, keep warm.
Slice raclette cheese into portions for melting.
Arrange charcuterie, cornichons, and pickled onions on a platter.
Melt cheese portions under a raclette grill or broiler.
Scrape the melted cheese over hot potatoes.
Serve immediately with the accompaniments and mustard.
tips: Raclette is a communal meal — everyone melts and scrapes their own cheese.
The cheese must bubble and brown slightly for the best flavor.

name: Piperade (Basque Pepper Dish)
desc: Basque Country stewed peppers and tomatoes with espelette pepper, often served with eggs or ham
steps: Roast peppers, peel and cut into strips.
Saute onion and garlic in olive oil until softened.
Add pepper strips, diced tomatoes, and espelette pepper.
Cook slowly for 20 minutes until everything is soft and jammy.
Stir in lightly beaten eggs and cook gently until just set.
Serve with slices of Bayonne ham draped on top.
tips: Espelette pepper gives a gentle warmth — you can substitute mild paprika.
The peppers should be cooked until completely melting and sweet.

name: Flamiche (Leek Tart)
desc: Rich Picardy tart filled with slowly cooked leeks in butter and cream in a flaky pastry shell
steps: Slice leeks thinly and wash thoroughly.
Cook leeks very slowly in butter for 20 minutes until completely soft and silky.
Mix cooked leeks with cream, egg yolks, nutmeg, salt, and pepper.
Line a tart tin with puff pastry, prick the base.
Pour the leek filling into the pastry shell.
Bake at 190C for 30-35 minutes until pastry is golden and filling is set.
tips: The leeks must be cooked until completely melting — any bite ruins the texture.
Some versions enclose the filling entirely in pastry like a pie.

name: Aligot (Cheesy Mashed Potatoes)
desc: Stretchy, elastic mashed potatoes from Aubrac blended with fresh tomme cheese and garlic
steps: Boil potatoes until very tender, then mash or rice them until perfectly smooth.
Heat butter and cream with crushed garlic.
Combine with the mashed potatoes over medium heat.
Add grated tomme cheese in handfuls, stirring vigorously with a wooden spoon.
Keep stirring until the mixture becomes elastic and stretchy, pulling away from the pot in long strings.
Serve immediately — aligot hardens as it cools.
tips: The vigorous stirring is what develops the signature elastic texture.
If tomme is unavailable, a mix of mozzarella and Cantal can approximate the stretch.

name: Poulet Basquaise (Basque Chicken)
desc: Basque Country braised chicken with peppers, tomatoes, ham, and espelette pepper
steps: Brown chicken pieces in olive oil on all sides, then remove.
Saute onions and garlic until softened.
Add sliced peppers and diced ham, cook 5 minutes.
Add chopped tomatoes, espelette pepper, and white wine.
Return chicken, cover and braise 40 minutes until chicken is tender and sauce is thick.
The peppers should be melting and the sauce deeply flavorful.
tips: Bayonne ham adds a distinctive smoky-salty backbone to the dish.
Espelette pepper is the signature spice of Basque cuisine — mild but flavorful.

name: Sole Meunière
desc: Delicate sole fillets dredged in flour and pan-fried in brown butter with lemon and parsley
steps: Pat sole fillets dry and season with salt and pepper.
Dredge lightly in flour, shaking off excess.
Melt half the butter in a large pan and cook sole 2-3 minutes per side until golden.
Remove sole to plates.
Add remaining butter to the pan, cook until nutty brown and foaming.
Add lemon juice and parsley, swirl and pour the brown butter over the fish immediately.
tips: The butter must be beurre noisette — hazelnut-brown, not burnt.
This is the dish that made Julia Child fall in love with French food in 1948.

name: Choucroute Garnie (Alsatian Sauerkraut)
desc: Alsatian platter of sauerkraut braised in wine with assorted sausages, pork, and potatoes
steps: Rinse sauerkraut and squeeze dry.
Saute onion in goose fat, add sauerkraut, juniper berries, and bay leaves.
Pour in Riesling and enough water to barely cover.
Nestle bacon into the sauerkraut, cover and braise at 160C for 90 minutes.
Add sausages and pork chops for the last 30 minutes.
Boil potatoes separately and serve the choucroute on a large platter with mustard.
tips: Rinsing the sauerkraut removes excess sourness for a more balanced flavor.
Alsatian Riesling is the traditional wine — its acidity complements the sauerkraut.

name: Confit Byaldi
desc: Refined ratatouille with thinly sliced vegetables layered over piperade and baked until silky
steps: Make piperade by slowly cooking diced peppers, onion, and garlic until very soft.
Spread piperade on the bottom of a baking dish.
Slice zucchini, squash, eggplant, and tomatoes into very thin uniform rounds.
Arrange vegetable slices alternating in tight overlapping rows over the piperade.
Drizzle with olive oil, season with thyme, salt, and pepper.
Cover with parchment paper and bake at 130C for 2 hours until vegetables are silky and concentrated.
tips: This is the dish from the movie Ratatouille, refined by Thomas Keller.
Slicing vegetables uniformly thin on a mandoline is essential for even cooking.

name: Vichyssoise
desc: Classic chilled leek and potato soup finished with cream, served cold and silky smooth
steps: Slice leeks thinly and sweat in butter over low heat until completely soft, about 10 minutes.
Add diced potatoes and chicken broth, simmer 20 minutes until potatoes are tender.
Blend until perfectly smooth using a blender.
Stir in cream, season with salt and white pepper.
Refrigerate until thoroughly chilled, at least 3 hours.
Serve cold in chilled bowls, garnished with snipped chives.
tips: Vichyssoise must be served ice-cold — this is not a hot soup served cold.
Use white pepper only — black pepper specks ruin the elegant appearance.

name: Gratin de Courgettes (Zucchini Gratin)
desc: Provençal baked zucchini gratin with eggs, cream, herbs, and a golden breadcrumb-cheese crust
steps: Slice zucchini into rounds and saute in olive oil with garlic until just softened.
Whisk eggs with cream, thyme, salt, and pepper.
Place sauteed zucchini in a baking dish.
Pour the egg-cream mixture over the zucchini.
Top with grated Gruyère and breadcrumbs.
Bake at 190C for 25-30 minutes until set and golden on top.
tips: Do not overcook the zucchini before baking — they cook further in the oven.
Pressing moisture from the zucchini first prevents a watery gratin.

name: Boeuf en Daube (Provençal Beef Stew)
desc: Slow-braised Provençal beef stew with red wine, orange peel, olives, and herbes de Provence
steps: Marinate beef chunks in red wine with garlic, orange peel, and herbes de Provence overnight.
Remove beef from marinade, pat dry, and brown in olive oil.
Saute onions and carrots, add tomato paste.
Pour in the reserved marinade, add olives and enough water to cover.
Cover tightly and braise at 150C for 3 hours until beef is falling apart.
Serve over fresh egg noodles or with crusty bread.
tips: The overnight marinade is what distinguishes a daube from a simple beef stew.
Orange peel is the Provençal secret ingredient that gives this dish its unique character.

name: Tian Provençal (Layered Vegetable Bake)
desc: Provençal layered vegetable gratin of tomatoes, zucchini, and eggplant with herbs and olive oil
steps: Saute sliced onion and garlic in olive oil until soft, spread in a baking dish.
Slice zucchini, eggplant, and tomatoes into thin uniform rounds.
Arrange alternating slices upright in tight rows over the onion base.
Drizzle generously with olive oil and sprinkle with herbes de Provence and salt.
Bake at 180C for 40 minutes until vegetables are tender.
Sprinkle with Parmesan and broil briefly until golden.
tips: The vegetables should be sliced the same thickness for even cooking.
This is essentially a composed vegetable gratin, not a casserole — presentation matters.

name: Salade Lyonnaise
desc: Lyon's classic frisée salad with warm lardons, a poached egg, and tangy mustard vinaigrette
steps: Cook lardons until crispy, reserve the rendered fat.
Make vinaigrette by whisking mustard, vinegar, olive oil, diced shallot, salt, and pepper.
Poach eggs in simmering vinegar water until whites are set but yolks remain runny.
Toss frisée with the warm vinaigrette and croutons.
Divide salad among plates, top each with crispy lardons.
Crown each salad with a poached egg — break the yolk at the table.
tips: The warm dressing slightly wilts the frisée, which is intentional.
The runny poached egg yolk becomes part of the dressing when broken.

name: Poireaux Vinaigrette (Leeks in Vinaigrette)
desc: Tender poached leeks served cold with a classic Dijon mustard vinaigrette and chopped eggs
steps: Trim leeks, cut to uniform length, and wash thoroughly.
Poach leeks in salted simmering water until tender, about 10-12 minutes.
Drain and cool the leeks, gently pressing out excess water.
Whisk mustard, vinegar, olive oil, diced shallot, and salt for the vinaigrette.
Arrange leeks on a platter, spoon vinaigrette over generously.
Garnish with chopped hard-boiled eggs, capers, and parsley.
tips: Test leeks with a knife — they should offer no resistance when tender.
This is a classic French bistro starter that also works as a light main course.

name: Croûte aux Champignons (Mushroom Toast)
desc: Rich sautéed mushrooms in cream and garlic served on thick toasted bread with herbs
steps: Slice mushrooms and cook in butter over high heat until deeply golden, about 8 minutes.
Add garlic and thyme, cook 1 more minute.
Deglaze with lemon juice, then add cream and reduce until thick.
Season with salt, pepper, and chopped parsley.
Toast bread slices until golden and crispy.
Pile the creamy mushrooms generously on the toast and serve immediately.
tips: Cook mushrooms in a single layer without stirring to get proper browning.
Use a mix of mushrooms — cremini, oyster, chanterelle — for depth of flavor.

name: Pistou Vegetable Soup
desc: Light Provençal summer vegetable soup brimming with beans, zucchini, and tomatoes, finished with basil pistou
steps: Dice potatoes, zucchini, and tomatoes, trim green beans.
Bring water to a boil, add potatoes first, cook 10 minutes.
Add green beans, zucchini, tomatoes, and white beans, simmer 15 minutes.
Meanwhile, pound basil, garlic, and Parmesan in a mortar, gradually adding olive oil.
Season the soup with salt and pepper.
Ladle into bowls and stir a generous spoonful of pistou into each serving.
tips: The pistou must never be cooked — stir it in at the very end or at the table.
This soup is best in summer when vegetables are at their peak.
